We argue thatalignment sharing is fundamentally a geometric problem of overlapping optimization trajectories within shared low-rank subspaces. Grounded in this insight, we propose iGSP, a novel framework that achieves efficient adaptation via implicit gradient subspace projection. Leveraging the early convergence of MoE routers to establish the subspace basis, iGSP bifurcates the adaptation process into two phases. First, the Subspace Identification phase introduces candidate experts via basis pre-expansion, applies a novel subspace-constrained regularization to implicitly project new task gradients onto the historical subspace, and precisely prunes redundant dimensions by treating routing probabilities as gradient flow indicators, ultimately to maximize knowledge reuse. Second, the Orthogonal Subspace Fine-Tuning phase fixes this structural basis and removes the regularization to rapidly fit the task-specific residual loss. Extensive experiments on the MTIL benchmark demonstrate that iGSP achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while significantly improving training efficiency, reducing the average trainable parameters by 42.7\% compared to current SOTA methods, and decreasing the final total parameters by 86.9\% relative to counterparts. The source code is available at https://github.com/GeoX-Lab/iGSP.

</details>


### [27] [MMGS: 10$\times$ Compressed 3DGS through Optimal Transport Aggregation based on Multi-view Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19304)
*Beizhen Zhao,Sicheng Yu,Ziran Yin,Dongxu Shen,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A global geometric distribution matching framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting compression that uses multi-view Gaussian contributions ranking, a global OT-based aggregation to merge redundant primitives, and an OT-based densification operator, achieving high rendering fidelity with only 10% of primitives and 10x faster training compared to vanilla 3DGS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) incurs heavy overhead from numerous redundant primitives. Existing compression methods rely on local sampling or fixed pruning thresholds, which often trade off redundancy removal against rendering fidelity. A global, geometry-aware compression approach could better preserve scene geometry while reducing primitives.

Method: 1) Multi-view 3D Gaussian contribution ranking to filter primitives using geometric consistency rather than local heuristics. 2) Global Optimal Transport (OT)-based aggregation to merge redundant primitives while preserving geometry. 3) OT-based densification operator to maintain Gaussian distributional properties during optimization.

Result: The method achieves state-of-the-art rendering quality using only 10% of the primitives and accelerates training by about 10x compared to vanilla 3DGS.

Conclusion: A globally optimized, OT-guided compression framework effectively reduces primitive count without sacrificing fidelity and substantially speeds up training, indicating strong potential for geometry-driven neural rendering pipelines and broader applicability beyond 3DGS.

Abstract: While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has revolutionized 3D reconstruction, it suffers from significant overhead due to massive redundant primitives. Existing compression methods typically rely on local sampling or fixed pruning thresholds, which often struggle to balance redundancy reduction with high-fidelity rendering. To address this, we propose a novel framework that formulates Gaussian optimization as a global geometric distribution matching problem. Specifically, our approach integrates three components: (1) we introduce a multi-view 3D Gaussian contribution ranking mechanism that filters primitives using geometric consistency instead of local heuristics; (2) we propose a global Optimal Transport (OT)-based aggregation algorithm that merges redundant primitives while preserving the underlying geometry; and (3) we design an OT-based densification operator that maintains the Gaussian's distributional properties for stable optimization. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art rendering quality with only \textbf{10$\%$} primitives and \textbf{10$\times$} accelerated training speeds compared to vanilla 3DGS.

</details>


### [28] [MetaRA: Metamorphic Robustness Assessment for Multimodal Large Language Model-based Visual Question Answering Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19307)
*Quanxing Xu,Yuhao Tian,Ling Zhou,Xian Zhong,Xiaohua Huang,Rubing Huang,Chia-Wen Lin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MetaRA uses metamorphic testing to assess robustness of MLLM-based VQA, revealing failure modes beyond accuracy and offering a model-agnostic diagnostic framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VQA evaluation relies on static accuracy metrics, which miss robustness, consistency, and generalization. There's a need for systematic robustness assessment in multimodal AI.

Method: Apply metamorphic relations to generate controlled input variations (images and questions) and test multiple MLLM-based VQA models across tasks; compare results to standard accuracy.

Result: Uncovers nuanced failure patterns: sensitivity to linguistic perturbations, reliance on superficial visual cues, and deeper multimodal reasoning weaknesses; provides richer diagnostics than accuracy alone; framework is model-agnostic and scalable.

Conclusion: Metamorphic robustness testing offers a scalable, model-agnostic approach to trustworthy multimodal AI, highlighting the importance of robustness evaluation beyond traditional benchmarks.

Abstract: Visual Question Answering (VQA), as the representative multimodal task, serves as a key benchmark for evaluating the reasoning capabilities of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). However, existing evaluations largely rely on static datasets and accuracy-based metrics, which fail to capture robustness, consistency, and generalization. Inspired by Metamorphic Testing (MT), we propose Metamorphic Robustness Assessment (MetaRA), a testing framework that employs Metamorphic Relations (MRs) to systematically probe vulnerabilities in MLLM-based VQA systems. MetaRA generates controlled variations of image-question inputs based on specific MRs and evaluates models across diverse conditions. Applying MetaRA to multiple MLLM-based VQA models across different tasks reveals nuanced failure patterns, including sensitivity to linguistic perturbations, over-reliance on superficial visual cues, and deeper weaknesses in multimodal reasoning. Experimental results demonstrate that MetaRA provides richer diagnostic insights than conventional accuracy metrics, exposing failure modes that remain hidden under standard benchmarks. Overall, this work highlights the need for systematic robustness evaluation in VQA and positions metamorphic assessment as a scalable, model-agnostic approach toward trustworthy multimodal AI.

</details>


### [29] [SWEET: Sparse World Modeling with Image Editing for Embodied Task Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19319)
*Yiren Song,Yihan Wang,Xiyao Deng,Zhuoran Yan,Mike Zheng Shou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Image editing-based sparse visual planning (SWEET) reduces reliance on dense video rollout for robot manipulation by predicting task-level keyframes via iterative image editing guided by language/spatial cues, with a diffusion-based action predictor and mixed-training to mitigate edits/real-data mismatch; validated on DROID and RoboMimic, showing improved keyframe fidelity and end-to-end action execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense video generation is computationally expensive and often unnecessary for manipulation tasks that can be summarized by a few task-relevant states; investigate whether image editing can serve as an efficient, lighter-weight world model for embodied control.

Method: 1) Controlled comparison between Wan2.2 (video generation) and FLUX-Kontext (image editing) under the same robotic dataset to assess task-level keyframes. 2) SWEET: a one-shot sparse planning framework that progressively generates task-relevant keyframes through successive image editing, conditioned on language instructions and optional spatial guidance. 3) A goal-conditioned diffusion action predictor converts adjacent keyframes into executable action chunks. 4) Mixed-training with filtered edited targets to reduce mismatch between real and edited subgoals.

Result: Image editing produced more reliable task-level keyframes with better visual fidelity and substantially lower inference cost than video generation. SWEET improves keyframe prediction across seen and unseen scenes and enables a full pipeline from keyframe planning to robot actions. Demonstrated on DROID and RoboMimic datasets.

Conclusion: Image editing is a promising and underexplored approach for embodied visual prediction. Sparse planning via keyframes, coupled with a diffusion-based action predictor, can match or exceed dense video-based methods while reducing computation, enabling practical end-to-end manipulation pipelines.

Abstract: Visual prediction has emerged as a promising paradigm for embodied control, where future observations are generated and then translated into actions. However, dense video generation is computationally expensive and often unnecessary for many manipulation tasks, whose progress can be summarized by a small number of task-relevant visual states. In this work, we study whether image editing models can serve as sparse visual world models for robot manipulation by predicting task-level future states without dense video rollout. We first conduct a controlled comparison between the video generation model Wan2.2 and the image editing model FLUX-Kontext under the same robotic data setting, and find that image editing produces more reliable task-level keyframes with better visual fidelity and substantially lower inference cost. Motivated by this observation, we propose SWEET, a one-shot sparse visual planning framework that progressively generates a sequence of task-relevant manipulation keyframes through successive image editing, conditioned on language instructions and optional arrow-based spatial guidance. A goal-conditioned diffusion action predictor then converts adjacent imagined keyframes into executable action chunks. To reduce the mismatch between real and edited visual subgoals, we further introduce a mixed-training strategy with filtered edited targets. Experiments on DROID and RoboMimic show that SWEET improves keyframe prediction across seen and unseen scenes and enables a full pipeline from sequential keyframe planning to executable robot actions, suggesting that image editing is a promising and underexplored direction for embodied visual prediction.

</details>


### [30] [TextAlign: Preference Alignment for Text Rendering with Hierarchical Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19320)
*Mingxuan Cui,Jingpu Yang,Fengxian Ji,Qian Jiang,Zhecheng Shi,Jiaming Wang,Zirui Song,Fajri Koto,Xiuying Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TextAlign is a post-training, non-invasive framework that aligns large text-to-image generators to render text accurately by learning a hierarchical VLM-based reward that decomposes errors into global, word, and glyph levels, usable with GRPO or DPO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Faithful text rendering is a persistent weakness in T2I models, and prior fixes require architecture changes; a scalable, non-invasive post-training approach is desirable to deploy across foundation models.

Method: Propose a hierarchical vision-language model (VLM)–based reward that converts binary defect judgments into scalar preferences. Use this reward to optimize via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) or Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) without changing the generator architecture. Evaluate on FLUX.1-dev and Z-Image-Turbo.

Result: The method yields consistent improvements in OCR-based text accuracy without compromising overall image quality. Outperforms baselines (SD3.5, Qwen-Image, AnyText, TextDiffuser), suggesting reward design as a scalable alternative to model redesign for improving text rendering.

Conclusion: Reward-based post-training alignment offers a scalable, non-invasive route to enhance text rendering in text-to-image models without architectural changes.

Abstract: Faithful text rendering remains a persistent weakness of large text-to-image generative models, as it requires both semantic instruction following and fine-grained glyph-level structure. Prior methods often improve this ability through architecture-specific modules or encoder modifications, which complicate deployment across foundation models. We study text rendering as a post-training preference-alignment problem and propose TextAlign, a non-invasive framework that keeps the generator architecture unchanged. The key component is a hierarchical vision-language model (VLM)-based reward that decomposes rendering errors into global, word, and glyph levels, then converts binary defect judgments into a scalar preference signal. The resulting signal supports both Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and Z-Image-Turbo show consistent gains in OCR-based text accuracy without degrading general generation quality. Compared with strong foundation and text-rendering baselines, including SD3.5, Qwen-Image, AnyText, and TextDiffuser, these results indicate that reward design offers a scalable alternative to model redesign for improving text rendering.

</details>


### [31] [DynaTok: Temporally Adaptive and Positional Bias-Aware Token Compression for Video-LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19322)
*Minyoung Park,Taehun Kong,Sangjun Ahn*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DynaTok is a training-free, temporally adaptive token compression framework for Video-LLMs that allocates tokens across time and space using an EMA-based Temporal Budget Allocation (TBA) and a spatial activation-based Budget Allocation (SBA) with memory, preserving semantic coverage under aggressive compression (up to 90% token reduction) and enabling seamless integration with existing LLaVA variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the computational burden of processing long videos in Video-LLMs by selecting semantically important tokens without retraining, addressing redundancy from long-term temporal variation and spatial overlap while mitigating positional bias.

Method: Two modular design: Temporal Budget Allocation (TBA) uses a lightweight exponential moving average (EMA) memory to allocate fewer tokens to redundant frames and more to novel frames, capturing long-term temporal variation. Spatial Budget Allocation (SBA) uses activation-based attention maps to select diverse and semantically important spatial features, plus a spatial memory to reduce redundancy from previously selected regions and mitigate positional bias. The approach is training-free and integrates with existing Video-LLMs such as LLaVA-OneVision and LLaVA-Video without retraining.

Result: Empirical results on four VideoQA benchmarks (MVBench, LongVideoBench, MLVU, VideoMME) show DynaTok retains over 95% of baseline accuracy even with a 90% token reduction, outperforming recent training-free token compression methods.

Conclusion: DynaTok provides a principled, training-free foundation for efficient and robust video reasoning, enabling real-time streaming video understanding with future Video-LLMs and offering strong semantic coverage under aggressive compression.

Abstract: Recent advances in Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) have greatly expanded multimodal reasoning capabilities. However, the massive number of visual tokens extracted from long video sequences incurs prohibitive computational costs, limiting their deployment in real-world scenarios. Existing training-free token compression methods select tokens based on attention magnitude as a proxy for semantic importance, but often overlook positional bias and rely only on short-term temporal locality, leading to redundant spatio-temporal coverage and inefficient token usage. We present DynaTok, a training-free, temporally adaptive and bias-aware token compression framework that allocates token budgets across both temporal and spatial dimensions. Through a lightweight exponential moving average (EMA) memory, the Temporal Budget Allocation (TBA) module dynamically assigns fewer tokens to redundant frames and more to novel frames, capturing long-term temporal variation. The Spatial Budget Allocation (SBA) module complements this by selecting spatially diverse and semantically important features using activation-based attention maps, while leveraging a spatial memory to reduce redundancy from previously selected regions and mitigate positional bias. DynaTok integrates seamlessly with existing Video-LLMs such as LLaVA-OneVision and LLaVA-Video without retraining, and effectively preserves semantic coverage under aggressive compression. Experiments on four representative VideoQA benchmarks-MVBench, LongVideoBench, MLVU, and VideoMME-show that DynaTok retains over 95% of baseline accuracy even with a 90% token reduction, surpassing recent training-free approaches. These results demonstrate that DynaTok provides a principled foundation for efficient and robust video reasoning, paving the way toward real-time streaming video understanding with future Video-LLMs.

</details>


### [32] [RE-VLM: Event-Augmented Vision-Language Model for Scene Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19329)
*Hanqing Liu,Mingjie Liu,Luoping Cui,Endian Lin,Donghong Jiang,Chuang Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RE-VLM: a dual-stream RGB + event vision-language model with graph-based supervision for robust captioning and VQA under challenging illumination and motion, with new datasets PEOD-Chat and RGBE-Chat.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB degradation under adverse conditions reduces VLM performance; event cameras offer high dynamic range and motion cues; jointly leveraging both modalities can enhance robustness.

Method: Parallel encoders for RGB and event streams; progressive cross-modal alignment for language; graph-driven pipeline converts synchronized streams into scene graphs to generate captions and QA pairs; new datasets PEOD-Chat and RGBE-Chat; training with synthesized supervision.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art RGB-only and event-only models with similar parameter counts, especially under challenging conditions; code and datasets released.

Conclusion: Event-augmented VLMs enable robust scene understanding across normal and adverse environments; the approach provides scalable data generation via scene graphs for supervision.

Abstract: Conventional vision-language models (VLMs) struggle to interpret scenes captured under adverse conditions (e.g., low light, high dynamic range, or fast motion) because standard RGB images degrade in such environments. Event cameras provide a complementary modality: they asynchronously record per-pixel brightness changes with high temporal resolution and wide dynamic range, preserving motion cues where frames fail. We propose RE-VLM, the first dual-stream vision-language model that jointly leverages RGB images and event streams for robust scene understanding across both normal and challenging conditions. RE-VLM employs parallel RGB and event encoders together with a progressive training strategy that aligns heterogeneous visual features with language. To address the scarcity of RGB-Event-Text supervision, we further propose a graph-driven pipeline that converts synchronized RGB-Event streams into verifiable scene graphs, from which we synthesize captions and question-answer (QA) pairs. To develop and evaluate RE-VLM, we construct two datasets: PEOD-Chat, targeting illumination-challenged scenes, and RGBE-Chat, covering diverse scenarios. On captioning and VQA benchmarks, RE-VLM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art RGB-only and event-only models with comparable parameter counts, with particularly large gains under challenging conditions. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of event-augmented VLMs in achieving robust vision-language understanding across a wide range of real-world environments. Code and datasets are available at https://github.com/bupt-ai-cz/RE-VLM.

</details>


### [33] [Selective, Regularized, and Calibrated: Harnessing Vision Foundation Models for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19340)
*Junyuan Ma,Xunzhi Xiang,Wenbin Li,Qi Fan,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A three-stage selective adaptation framework (HERA) for cross-domain few-shot segmentation using frozen vision foundation models, achieving state-of-the-art mIoU with minimal test-time fine-tuning (<2.7% parameters updated).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CD-FSS with VFMs is hindered by limited labeled exemplars and domain shifts; pretraining data underrepresents target domains, causing cross-domain mismatch and sensitivity to layers. A solution should adapt representations without source-data retraining and with minimal parameter updates.

Method: Hierarchical Layer Selection (HLS) selects the most informative VFM layer via Exemplar Transfer Risk (ETR); Prior-Guided Regularization (PGR) regularizes interactions on the chosen representation; Pixelwise Adaptive Calibration (PAC) calibrates pixel-level predictions by combining the selected representation and refined interaction maps. The pipeline operates with frozen VFM features and updates under 2.7% of parameters at test time.

Result: HERA surpasses state-of-the-art by more than 4.1 mIoU across multiple CD-FSS benchmarks, demonstrating robust adaptation to target domains without source-data retraining.

Conclusion: The Hierarchical Exemplar Representation Adaptation framework effectively guides frozen VFM features in new domains with minimal parameter tuning, delivering strong cross-domain few-shot segmentation performance.

Abstract: Vision foundation models (VFMs) have achieved strong performance across various vision tasks. However, it still remains challenging to apply VFMs for cross-domain few-shot segmentation (CD-FSS), which segments objects of novel classes under domain shifts using only a few labeled exemplars. The challenge is mainly driven by two factors: (1) limited labeled exemplars per novel class relative to the scale of VFM pre-training, making the model prone to overfitting during retraining, and (2) target-domain shifts underrepresented during pre-training, inducing cross-domain inconsistency and layer-wise sensitivity. To address these issues, we propose Hierarchical Exemplar Representation Adaptation (HERA), a three-stage select-regularize-calibrate VFM-based segmentation framework that learns effectively from limited labels and adapts to novel domains without source-data retraining. We first design Hierarchical Layer Selection (HLS) to adaptively identify the most informative VFM layer using a data-dependent Exemplar Transfer Risk (ETR) computed for each candidate layer. Then, Prior-Guided Regularization (PGR) regularizes interactions on the selected representation, yielding well-structured local signals for the subsequent stage. Furthermore, Pixelwise Adaptive Calibration (PAC) combines the selected representation with the refined interaction maps to calibrate pixel-wise predictions, producing consistent masks. Together, these stages form a hierarchical select-regularize-calibrate pipeline that guides frozen VFM features in new domains while fine-tuning less than 2.7% of parameters at test time. Extensive experiments show that HERA surpasses the state of the art by more than 4.1 mIoU across multiple CD-FSS benchmarks.

</details>


### [34] [Semantic-Enriched Latent Visual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19342)
*Tianrun Xu,Yue Sun,Qixun Wang,Jingyi Lu,Yuan Wang,Tianren Zhang,Longteng Guo,Fengyun Rao,Jing Lyu,Feng Chen,Jing Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SLVR, a two-stage framework to enrich latent visual reasoning with semantic attribute supervision and cross-query alignment, aided by new SLV-Set and SV-QA benchmarks; shows improved robustness and semantic consistency versus baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current latent visual reasoning often relies on shallow latent representations trained with visual supervision, lacking rich semantic attributes needed for diverse region-level reasoning and robust generalization to semantic variation.

Method: Stage 1 learns semantically enriched region-centric latent representations under fine-grained attribute supervision. Stage 2 proposes Multi-query Group Relative Policy Optimization (M-GRPO) to align latent representations across multiple queries grounded in the same region. Dataset creation includes SLV-Set (~400k region-level attribute annotations) and SV-QA benchmark for evaluating latent reasoning under semantic variation.

Result: Empirical results show that SLVR improves robustness and semantic consistency of latent visual reasoning compared with existing baselines across targeted tasks.

Conclusion: SLVR demonstrates that semantic enrichment and cross-query alignment in latent spaces can enhance region-level visual reasoning, supported by new large-scale annotations and a benchmark for semantic variation.

Abstract: Multimodal latent-space reasoning aims to replace explicit thinking with images by performing visual reasoning directly in a compact latent space. However, existing approaches largely rely on visual supervision and produce latent representations that lack sufficient semantic richness, limiting their ability to support diverse region-level reasoning tasks. In this work, we introduce Semantic-Enriched Latent Visual Reasoning (SLVR), a two-stage learning framework that enriches latent representations with attribute-level visual semantics and aligns them with diverse reasoning objectives. In the first stage, SLVR learns semantically enriched region-centric latents under fine-grained attribute supervision. In the second stage, we design Multi-query Group Relative Policy Optimization (M-GRPO) to align latent representations across multiple queries grounded in the same region. To support this framework, we construct SLV-Set, comprising approximately 400K region-level attribute annotations and 800K multi-query question answering samples, and introduce SV-QA, a benchmark that evaluates latent reasoning under semantic variation. Experiments demonstrate that SLVR improves the robustness and semantic consistency of latent visual reasoning compared to existing baselines.

</details>


### [35] [MAM-CLIP: Vision-Language Pretraining on Mammography Atlases for BI-RADS Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19359)
*Halil Ibrahim Gulluk,Olivier Gevaert*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-modal vision-language approach pretrains a vision encoder on image-caption pairs from mammography atlases using PubMedBERT as the language model and contrastive learning, then fine-tunes for BI-RADS prediction. This yields improved 3-class F1 scores, especially with scarce labels; 2K image-text pairs can be more informative than 2K labeled samples at larger data scales. The authors release code, models, and a TEKNOFEST dataset preprocessed data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address label scarcity and inter-radiologist variability in BI-RADS prediction from mammograms by leveraging rich textual captions from domain-specific atlases to augment visual representations.

Method: Construct a two-branch vision-language model: a vision encoder and PubMedBERT as the language encoder. Train with contrastive learning on 2313 image-caption pairs sourced from two mammography atlases. Fine-tune the vision encoder on two BI-RADS prediction datasets. Compare against image-label-only baselines and examine sample efficiency (varying labeled data).

Result: 3-class average F1 improvement between +1% and +14% depending on training size; +1% with 40K samples, +14% with 1K samples. 2K image-text pairs can outperform 2K labeled samples for label prediction when >10K training samples are available (average margin ~+1.1%). Public release of preprocessed TEKNOFEST data, training code, and models.

Conclusion: Vision-language pretraining using mammography captions enhances BI-RADS prediction performance and data efficiency, validating the value of textual atlas-derived information for medical imaging tasks.

Abstract: Deep learning methods have demonstrated promising results in predicting BI-RADS scores from mammography images. However, the interpretation of these images can vary, leading to discrepancies even among radiologists. Given the inherent complexity of mammograms, training classification models solely on image labels often yields limited performance. To address this challenge, we curated 2313 mammogram images and their corresponding captions from two mammography atlases. Our proposed approach employs a multi-modal model that uses a pretrained PubMedBERT as the language component. By training this model on image-text pairs with contrastive learning, we enable the vision encoder to absorb the rich information contained in the captions, thereby improving its understanding of mammography findings. We then fine-tune the vision encoder on two datasets for BI-RADS prediction, achieving superior performance compared with models trained without this pretraining, particularly when labeled samples are scarce. The improvement in the 3-class average F1 score ranges from +1% to +14%: a +1% increase with 40K training samples, and a +14% increase with 1K samples. Furthermore, our experiments reveal that 2K image-text pairs from mammography atlases can be more informative than 2K labeled samples for label prediction, with an average margin of +1.1% when more than 10K training samples are available. Overall, our work provides a vision-language model for mammography and highlights the value of textual information from mammography atlases. In addition, we publicly release preprocessed mammography images of the TEKNOFEST dataset. The training code, pre-trained model weights, data extraction scripts, and the released dataset are publicly available at: https://github.com/igulluk/MAM-CLIP

</details>


### [36] [Scalable, Energy-Efficient Optical-Neural Architecture for Multiplexed Deepfake Video Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19360)
*Parnian Ghapandar Kashani,Shiqi Chen,Aydogan Ozcan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hybrid digital-analog deepfake video detector using a lightweight digital front-end and a spatially multiplexed optical back-end processes 15+ streams in one optical pass, achieving high accuracy and robustness with lower computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the scalability and energy demands of deepfake detection by enabling parallel, low-power optical inference that complements digital processing.

Method: A two-stage system: a compact digital front-end followed by a programmable spatial light modulator-based optical decoder that multiplexes and processes multiple video streams in parallel within a single optical propagation pass, validated with datasets including Celeb-DF.

Result: Achieves 97.79% accuracy, 99.86% sensitivity, 95.72% specificity on Celeb-DF with 15 videos in parallel; demonstrates resilience to degradation, noise, compression, misalignment, and some adversarial attacks; enables higher throughput and energy efficiency than purely digital methods.

Conclusion: Optical computation can complement AI inference to attain simultaneous gains in throughput, energy efficiency, and robustness against adversarial and environmental perturbations, addressing key limitations of current digital-only deepfake detectors.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of AI-generated visual media has created an urgent need for efficient, trustworthy deepfake detection systems. However, existing deep learning-based detection methods rely on computationally intensive and energy-demanding inference algorithms, limiting their scalability. Here, we present a hybrid digital-analog deepfake video detection framework that combines a lightweight digital front-end with a spatially multiplexed optical decoding back-end for massively parallel analog inference through a programmable spatial light modulator. By simultaneously processing 15 or more video streams within a single optical propagation pass, the system enables high-throughput and accurate video-level authenticity prediction at reduced computational cost compared with purely digital methods. We validated this hybrid deepfake video processor using different datasets spanning classical face-swapping, real-world deepfake recordings, and fully AI-generated videos. Using a spatially multiplexed experimental set-up operating in the visible spectrum, we achieved average deepfake detection accuracy, sensitivity and specificity of 97.79%, 99.86% and 95.72%, respectively, on the Celeb-DF video dataset with 15 videos tested in parallel in a single optical pass per inference. The multiplexed optical decoder also demonstrates resilience against various types of video degradation, noise, compression, experimental misalignments and black-box adversarial attacks. Our results show that integrating optical computation into AI inference enables simultaneous gains in throughput, energy efficiency, and adversarial robustness - three properties that are difficult to achieve together in purely digital systems.

</details>


### [37] [Multi-Scale Generative Modeling with Heat Dissipation Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19371)
*Jun Ma,Hanquan Zhang,Yanjun Qin,Haoyuan Guan,Ke Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Heat Dissipation Flow Matching (HDFM): an ODE-based diffusion model variant that injects a continuous heat-dissipation blur process into Flow Matching, uses an interpolated path to address ill-posedness, and employs x-prediction to ease high-dimensional regression; shows improvements across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Blur-based diffusion models preserve multi-scale priors and color budgets but are typically in SDE frameworks; Flow Matching is an ODE-based approach, leaving a gap in integrating blur priors. Ill-posed inverse heat-dissipation (IHD) and high-dimensional regression under data-manifold assumptions hinder blur-based methods.

Method: Introduce a continuous blurred (heat-dissipation) process into Flow Matching. Align an interpolated heat-dissipation path to address ill-posedness and adopt x-prediction to mitigate high-dimensional regression difficulty.

Result: Toy experiments and ablation studies indicate HDFM consistently benefits from both blur and x-prediction, with performance outperforming most baseline methods on all datasets.

Conclusion: HDFM successfully integrates blur-based priors into an FM (ODE) framework, addressing ill-posedness and high-dimensional regression, and achieving strong empirical performance across datasets.

Abstract: Diffusion models are widely used in image generation, with most relying on noise-based corruption and denoising. A distinct branch instead uses blur as the main corruption, preserving better color budgets and multi-scale detail by providing multi-scale priors. However, blur-based models remain in SDE-based frameworks and are not integrated into ODE-based frameworks, such as Flow Matching (FM). Meanwhile, in the blur-based formulation, the classical inverse heat-dissipation (IHD) process faces an ill-posed challenge. Moreover, under the data-manifold assumption, regressing blurred images from high-dimensional noise (or velocity) space is also difficult. We propose Heat Dissipation Flow Matching (HDFM), which introduces a continuous blurred (heat-dissipation) process into FM to inject multi-scale priors. HDFM aligns an interpolated heat-dissipation path to address ill-posedness and adopts $x$-prediction to mitigate high-dimensional regression difficulty. Toy experiments and ablation studies show that HDFM consistently benefits from both blur and $x$-prediction. The performance of HDFM outperforms most baseline methods on all datasets.

</details>


### [38] [Concept-Guided Noisy Negative Suppression for Zero-Shot Classification and Grounding of Chest X-Ray Findings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19374)
*Chenyu Lian,Hong-Yu Zhou,Chun-Ka Wong,Jing Qin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoNNS introduces a concept-guided noisy-negative suppression framework for vision-language alignment in chest X-rays, using a hierarchical clinical concept ontology and a cross-patient relabeling strategy to mitigate noisy negatives in contrastive learning, yielding improved zero-shot grounding and classification; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard contrastive V-L learning treats cross-patient radiographs/reports as negatives, but similar findings across patients create noisy negatives that degrade zero-shot performance; a method to suppress these negatives can improve alignment and grounding.

Method: Construct a 41-concept hierarchical ontology with presence, attributes (location, characteristics), and textual evidences. Implement cross-patient relabeling: (1) Fine-Grained Breakdown by presence, (2) Noisy Negative Filtering to remove semantic conflicts, (3) Hard Negative Mining using a lightweight LM. Train with a Concept-Aware NCE loss that aligns visuals and text while suppressing noisy negatives.

Result: Extensive experiments on multi-granularity zero-shot grounding and five zero-shot classification datasets show CoNNS outperforms existing SOTA methods.

Conclusion: Ontology-guided relabeling and concept-aware contrastive loss effectively suppress noisy negatives and improve zero-shot vision-language understanding in chest X-ray tasks; code is available on GitHub.

Abstract: Vision-language alignment using chest X-rays and radiology reports has emerged as an advanced paradigm for zero-shot classification and grounding of chest X-ray findings. However, standard contrastive learning typically treats radiographs and reports from different patients simply as negative pairs. This assumption introduces noisy negatives, as different patients frequently exhibit similar findings. Such noisy negatives cause semantic ambiguity and degrade performance in zero-shot understanding tasks. To address this challenge, we propose CoNNS, a concept-guided noisy-negative suppression framework. To support the negative suppression mechanism, unlike previous methods that use raw reports or templatized texts, we construct a hierarchical concept ontology using large language models. The ontology structures 41 key clinical concepts by explicitly modeling presence, attributes (location and characteristics), and texts (evidential segment and presence statement). Leveraging this ontology, we implement a cross-patient pair relabeling strategy comprising three steps: (1) Fine-Grained Breakdown to categorize pairs based on finding presence; (2) Noisy Negative Filtering to resolve semantic conflicts by removing false negatives; and (3) Hard Negative Mining to identify subtle attribute discrepancies using a lightweight language model. Finally, we propose a Concept-Aware NCE loss to align visual features with text while suppressing the identified noisy negatives. Extensive experiments across multi-granularity zero-shot grounding tasks and five zero-shot classification datasets validate that CoNNS outperforms existing state-of-the-art models. The code is available at https://github.com/DopamineLcy/conns.

</details>


### [39] [Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Routing in Visual Diffusion Transformers:Diagnosis, Boundary Calibration and Evolutionary Roadmap from Routing Collapse to Selective Deadlock](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19378)
*Haiying Sha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Comprehensive diagnosis of training failure modes in Token-Choice sparse MoE for video Diffusion Transformers; identifies five failure modes across routing mechanisms, proposes Functional Redundancy Hypothesis and Three Laws for dense-to-MoE conversion, and offers a bfloat16 fix and a roadmap toward a world-model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand and stabilize training when converting a large dense model to sparse MoE, enabling reliable scaling and performance in video diffusion architectures.

Method: Convert a pretrained ~5B dense model to an MoE using three conversion laws; evaluate different router types (linear, MLP, cross-attention); analyze routing-time series over 65M tokens across 5,000 training steps; diagnose failure modes; propose a hypothesis and practical remedies; provide guidelines (Three Laws) and a bfloat16 precision fix; outline capability boundary and roadmap.

Result: Identified five failure modes: (1) linear routers cause global soft saturation and complete expert homogenization; (2) MLP routers yield selective deadlock, with ~1/3 layers degenerating into a single-expert mode despite auxiliary loss; (3) cross-attention routers show partial self-recovery but ~9 layers remain deadlocked; (4) deadlocked layers concentrate in shallow visual processing and deep semantic integration, following a U-shaped distribution; (5) bfloat16 mixed precision truncates tiny weight updates to zero. Proposes Functional Redundancy Hypothesis linking deadlock to a rational waiting strategy before shared experts mature; supports it with theory of functional redundancy from systems biology. Delivers three conversion laws, a complete bfloat16 remedy, and a roadmap from visual unification to world modeling.

Conclusion: The work provides a diagnostic framework and actionable guidelines for stable dense-to-MoE conversion in Token-Choice models, offering both theoretical insight (Functional Redundancy) and practical fixes (Three Laws, bfloat16 solution) alongside a strategic roadmap for scaling toward integrated world-model capabilities.

Abstract: This paper systematically diagnoses the training failure modes of Token-Choice sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) on video Diffusion Transformers. Starting from a pretrained dense model of about 5 billion parameters, we convert it into an MoE architecture following three laws: routed experts exactly clone the original FFN weights, shared experts are initialized to zero for verification and then to extremely small non-zero noise for actual training, while only the gating networks start from random initialization.
  Experiments reveal a hierarchy of five failure modes: (1) linear routers suffer global soft saturation with complete expert homogenization; (2) MLP routers introduce selective deadlock, where roughly one-third of layers degenerate into a single-expert mode that cannot be prevented by increasing the auxiliary loss; (3) cross-attention routers exhibit preliminary self-recovery, yet about nine layers remain stubbornly deadlocked; (4) deadlocked layers display a U-shaped distribution, concentrated in shallow visual processing layers and deep semantic integration layers; (5) bfloat16 mixed precision causes tiny weight updates to be truncated to zero by hardware.
  Based on routing decision time series over 65 million tokens across 5,000 training steps, we propose the Functional Redundancy Hypothesis: deadlock is a rational waiting strategy before the shared expert matures within the gate-shared expert-routed expert triadic system. This hypothesis is supported by the theory of functional redundancy in systems biology. On the engineering side, we summarize the Three Laws of dense-to-MoE conversion and provide a complete solution for the bfloat16 precision trap. We calibrate the current capability boundary of the Token-Choice paradigm and outline a three-step evolutionary roadmap from visual unification to a world model.

</details>


### [40] [MatPhys: Learning Material-Aware Physics Parameters for Deformable Object Simulation from Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19386)
*Yang Yang,Yiyan Wang,Zheming Liu,Naoya Iwamoto*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Part-aware, feed-forward prediction of spring-mass parameters from a single-view video (MatPhys) using DINO-based part decomposition and a shared material codebook to achieve cross-scene consistency and better generalization, matching optimization baselines on reconstruction and future prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing physics-driven methods struggle due to (1) the homogeneous-material assumption across an object, and (2) scene-specific inverse optimization under monocular observation, which yields inconsistent material estimates across scenes and interactions.

Method: Two coupled designs: (1) Use DINO features to decompose the object into semantically meaningful parts and query a part-level material prior, assigning each part its own physical behavior. (2) Learn a material codebook of shared material embeddings to bridge appearance and physics; use the part-level prior as a reference distribution to constrain the decoder so that the same material yields consistent parameters across scenes and interactions.

Result: Experiments show that MatPhys matches per-scene optimization baselines for reconstruction and future prediction, while generalizing better to unseen interactions and objects, and yielding more consistent physical parameters across scenes.

Conclusion: The approach turns monocular, under-constrained inference into a feed-forward pipeline grounded in reusable material concepts, enabling robust, cross-scene generalizable estimation of deformable-object physics from video.

Abstract: Reconstructing simulation-ready deformable objects is important for vision, graphics, and robotics. Existing physics-driven methods can recover physical digital twins from videos, but they suffer from two fundamental limitations: they typically assume a homogeneous material across the whole object, and their scene-specific inverse optimization, combined with the inherent ambiguity of monocular observation, yields inconsistent parameters for the same material across different scenes or interactions. We propose MatPhys, a material-aware feed-forward framework that predicts spring-mass parameters from a single-view video, addressing these two issues with two coupled designs. To relax the homogeneous material assumption, we use DINO features to decompose the object into semantically meaningful parts and to query a part-level material prior, assigning each part its own physical behavior. To enforce cross-scene consistency, we introduce a learned material codebook of shared material embeddings as the bridge between appearance and physics, and further use the part-level prior as a reference distribution that constrains the decoder so that the same material yields consistent parameters across scenes and interactions. Together, these designs turn an under-constrained monocular problem into feed-forward inference grounded on shared, reusable material concepts. Experiments show that our method matches per-scene optimization baselines in reconstruction and future prediction, while achieving stronger generalization to unseen interactions and objects with more consistent physical parameters.

</details>


### [41] [LMM-Track4D: Eliciting 4D Dynamic Reasoning in LMMs via Trajectory-Grounded Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19390)
*Chaoyue Li,Yongxue Xu,Jie Feng,Jiayu Ding*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Track4D-Bench and LMM-Track4D for trajectory-grounded 4D dynamic reasoning in LMMs; shows improved performance via explicit dynamic state modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 4D continuous spatiotemporal reasoning remains challenging for large multimodal models; a dedicated benchmark and architecture are needed to study and improve long-horizon dynamic interpretation and trajectory extraction under occlusion and viewpoint changes.

Method: Define trajectory-grounded multi-turn spatiotemporal dialogue task; Build Track4D-Bench with 526 clip-level dialogues, 23.5k frames, 7.5k object annotations; Propose LMM-Track4D with RTGE (Ray-Time Geometry Encoding), a streaming state token TRK for long-horizon propagation, and an OSK-RA decoder for stable 4-step 3D state estimation under occlusion/viewpoint variation.

Result: On Track4D-Bench, LMM-Track4D achieves consistent improvements over strong baselines, indicating explicit dynamic state modeling benefits 4D dynamic reasoning.

Conclusion: Dynamic state modeling is a valuable principle to elicit 4D dynamic reasoning in LMMs; code and data will be released to foster further research.

Abstract: Recent large multimodal models (LMMs) have become increasingly capable on image and video understanding, yet still struggle to sustain 4D continuous spatiotemporal dynamic reasoning. To study this capability gap, we formulate trajectory-grounded multi-turn spatiotemporal dialogue, a new task in which a model must answer spatiotemporal queries while returning structured 3D target trajectories over an entire short clip or a specified segment of a longer clip, and introduce Track4D-Bench, a benchmark with 526 clip-level dialogue samples spanning 23.5k frames and 7.5k object annotations, for training and evaluation. Building on this task, we propose LMM-Track4D, which combines RTGE (Ray--Time Geometry Encoding), a dedicated streaming state token TRK for long-horizon dynamic propagation, and an Object-Slot Kinematic, Residual-Anchor (OSK-RA) decoder for stable 4-step 3D state estimation under occlusion and viewpoint variation. Experiments on Track4D-Bench show consistent improvements over strong baselines, suggesting that explicit dynamic state modeling is a useful design principle for eliciting 4D dynamic reasoning in LMMs. Our code and dataset will be publicly available at https://github.com/mikubaka88/LMM-Track4D.

</details>


### [42] [Neuron Incidence Redistribution for Fairness in Medical Image Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19393)
*Abin Shoby,Lyle John Palmer,Nikhil Cherian Kurian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight regularizer (NIR) redistributes latent disease evidence across the penultimate layer to reduce demographic disparities in medical image classification without using demographic labels, achieving substantial fairness gains on HAM10000 and Harvard OCT-RNFL with minimal AUC impact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Demographic disparities arise from latent channel activations that co-activate with privileged/demographic groups; a method is needed to mitigate this without requiring demographic annotations.

Method: Penultimate-layer neuron activation variance is weighted by predicted probabilities; NIR penalizes variance across neurons, encouraging distributed evidence across neurons; training-time no demographic labels.

Result: On HAM10000: TPR disparity by age reduced from 10.81% to 0.93%; by gender 12.04% to 0.74%; AUC up by 0.51. On Harvard OCT-RNFL: race disparity FPR reduced from 15.68% to 10.66%; age disparity from 12.69% to 1.80%.

Conclusion: NIR demonstrates a principled, effective approach to fairness in medical AI by distributing latent disease evidence across the full penultimate layer and requiring no demographic labels, with minimal performance trade-offs.

Abstract: Deep learning models for medical image classification are susceptible to subgroup performance disparities across demographic attributes such as age, gender, and race. We identify a latent representational mechanism underlying these disparities: in transfer-learned models, the dominant penultimate-layer activation channel under positive predictions is co-activated by both disease-positive samples and privileged demographic groups (male, older patients), producing over-diagnosis; conversely, the dominant channel under negative predictions is co-activated by disadvantaged groups (female, younger patients), producing systematic under-diagnosis. To address this, we propose Neuron Incidence Redistribution (NIR), a lightweight regularization method that penalizes the variance of predicted-probability-weighted mean activations across penultimate-layer neurons, requiring no demographic labels at training time. On HAM10000, TPR disparity drops from 10.81% to 0.93% across age groups and from 12.04% to 0.74% across gender, with a marginal AUC improvement of 0.51 points. On Harvard OCT-RNFL, NIR reduces FPR disparity for race (from 15.68% to 10.66%) and age (from 12.69% to 1.80%), demonstrating that distributing latent disease evidence across the full penultimate layer is a principled and effective strategy for improving demographic fairness in medical AI.

</details>


### [43] [Rebalancing Reference Frame Dominance to Improve Motion in Image-to-Video Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19398)
*Wooseok Jeon,Seungho Park,Seunghyun Shin,Sangeyl Lee,Hyeonho Jeong,Hae-Gon Jeon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DyMoS (Dynamic Motion Slider) is a training-free, model-agnostic method that addresses motion suppression in image-to-video (I2V) models by rebalancing the attention from generated frames to the reference frame during the early denoising steps. It introduces a single scalar parameter to continuously control motion strength, preserving fidelity to the reference image while improving inter-frame dynamics across multiple backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: I2V models often produce overly static videos. Prior solutions weaken or modify the image-conditioning signal, but require retraining or reduce fidelity. The authors identify reference-frame dominance as the root cause: non-reference frames over-attend to reference-frame tokens, propagating reference information across time and suppressing inter-frame motion.

Method: DyMoS operates without training or modifying model weights. It reweights the attention pathway from generated frames to the reference frame during the initial denoising steps by a single scalar parameter, leaving input and weights unchanged. It is designed to be training-free and model-agnostic.

Result: Across multiple state-of-the-art I2V backbones, DyMoS consistently enhances motion dynamics while maintaining visual quality and fidelity to the reference image.

Conclusion: DyMoS provides a simple, effective, and general solution to motion suppression in I2V models, offering continuous motion control via a single parameter without additional training or architectural changes.

Abstract: Image-to-video models often generate videos that remain overly static, compared to text-to-video models. While prior approaches mitigate this issue by weakening or modifying the image-conditioning signal, they often require additional training or sacrifice fidelity to the reference image. In this work, we identify \emph{reference-frame dominance} as a key mechanism behind motion suppression. We observe that non-reference frames in I2V models allocate excessive self-attention to reference-frame key tokens, causing reference information to be over-propagated across time and suppressing inter-frame dynamics. Based on this finding, we propose DyMoS~(Dynamic Motion Slider), a training-free and model-agnostic method that rebalances the attention pathway from generated frames to the reference frame during initial denoising steps. DyMoS leaves both the input image and model weights unchanged and introduces a single scalar parameter for continuous control over motion strength. Experiments across multiple state-of-the-art I2V backbones demonstrate that DyMoS consistently improves motion dynamics while maintaining visual quality and fidelity to the reference image.

</details>


### [44] [Vision Harnessing Agent for Open Ad-hoc Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19410)
*Zilin Wang,Stella X. Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: We present VASA, a training-free vision-guided agent for open ad-hoc segmentation, and PARS, a benchmark converting PartImageNet part labels into open ad-hoc concepts. VASA uses a persistent working mask and planning-based visual operations to construct and validate segmentation, outperforming baselines on PARS and RefCOCOm. The work advocates agentic visual construction as a path beyond tool-wrapping of foundation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open ad-hoc segmentation requires grounding concepts that may not have a pre-learned mask; current prompts and fixed models struggle with parts, relations, exclusions, and collections. A persistent, memory-driven agent that can plan, execute, and correct visual actions is needed.

Method: Introduce VASA, a training-free agent that couples a vision-language model (VLM) agent, a segmentation foundation model, and a visually grounded workflow. It maintains a persistent working mask, plans visual operations, calls segmentation tools, inspects results, edits the mask, and performs failure recovery. They also construct PARS by turning PartImageNet part-level labels into open ad-hoc concepts via long-form definition queries.

Result: On PARS, VASA outperforms open-vocabulary, reasoning-based, and agentic baselines, surpassing SAM3 Agent by 14-25%. On RefCOCOm, VASA improves over SAM3 Agent by 5-9% and over other agentic baselines by up to 20%.

Conclusion: Agentic visual construction is a viable path for open ad-hoc segmentation, illustrating how programming of task knowledge, VLM behavior, visual routines, working memory, and failure-aware workflows can enable AI agents beyond mere tool-wrapping of foundation models.

Abstract: Segmentation has become easy when the concept is known, requiring retrieval of a learned visual grounding from text. It remains hard for open ad-hoc concepts, where the grounding may not exist as one learned mask and must often be constructed from image evidence through parts, relations, exclusions, and collections. We propose a Vision-guided Ad-hoc Segmentation Agent (VASA), the first vision harnessing agent for open ad-hoc segmentation. VASA is training-free and couples a VLM agent, a segmentation foundation model, and a visually grounded workflow. Rather than revising text prompts alone, VASA uses a persistent working mask to reason, construct, and validate a solution. It plans visual operations, invokes segmentation tools, inspects results, edits the mask, and recovers from errors. We construct PARS, a new benchmark that turns part-level labels in PartImageNet into open ad-hoc concepts through long-form definition queries. On PARS, VASA outperforms open-vocabulary, reasoning-based, and agentic baselines, surpassing SAM3 Agent by 14-25%. On RefCOCOm, a standard multi-granularity referring segmentation benchmark, VASA improves over SAM3 Agent by 5-9% and over other agentic baselines by up to 20%. These results validate agentic visual construction for open ad-hoc segmentation. Our work points to a path for AI agents beyond wrapping foundation models as tools: Programming them with task knowledge, VLM behavior, visual routines, working memory, and failure-aware workflows.

</details>


### [45] [KappaPlace: Learning Hyperspherical Uncertainty for Visual Place Recognition via Prototype-Anchored Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19435)
*Maya Yanko,Yoli Shavit*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes KappaPlace, an uncertainty-aware VPR framework using prototype-anchored supervision and von Mises-Fisher descriptors, achieving markedly better uncertainty calibration (ECE@K) on five benchmarks with optional joint-training or post-training variants; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VPR systems need reliable uncertainty signals to detect ambiguous queries and incorrect matches, crucial for safety-critical robotics. Existing uncertainty methods are largely query-centric and lack accurate, match-level reliability, hindering robust decision-making.

Method: Introduce Prototype-Anchored supervision where latent class prototypes serve as targets for a probabilistic objective. Model image descriptors as von Mises-Fisher (vMF) distributions and train a lightweight module to predict the concentration parameter, providing direct aleatoric uncertainty. Derive a novel match-level reliability formulation for pairwise query-reference confidence. Provide both a joint-training variant (with backbone) and a post-training extension (frozen backbone).

Result: Across five diverse benchmarks, KappaPlace reduces Expected Calibration Error (ECE@K) by up to 50% vs. existing methods, while maintaining or improving retrieval recall. Demonstrates robust, stable, and well-calibrated uncertainty signals suitable for decision-making in VPR pipelines. Code available at the provided URL.

Conclusion: KappaPlace delivers calibrated, uncertainty-aware VPR representations, enabling safer and more reliable autonomous navigation. The approach offers flexible training options and tangible calibration gains without sacrificing retrieval performance.

Abstract: Visual Place Recognition (VPR) is critical for autonomous navigation, yet state-of-the-art methods lack well-calibrated uncertainty estimation. Standard pipelines cannot reliably signal when a query is ambiguous or a match is likely incorrect, posing risks in safety-critical robotics. We propose KappaPlace, a principled framework for learning uncertainty-aware VPR representations. Our core contribution is a Prototype-Anchored supervision strategy that leverages latent class representatives as targets for a probabilistic objective. By modeling image descriptors as von Mises-Fisher (vMF) variables, we learn a lightweight module to predict the concentration parameter as a direct proxy for aleatoric uncertainty. While existing VPR uncertainty methods are typically restricted to a query-centric view, we derive a novel match-level formulation to quantify the reliability of specific query-reference pairs. Across five diverse benchmarks, KappaPlace reduces Expected Calibration Error (ECE@K) by up to 50% compared to existing methods while maintaining or improving retrieval recall. We provide both a joint-training variant and a post-training extension for frozen backbones. Our results demonstrate that KappaPlace provides a robust, stable, and well-calibrated signal that enables reliable decision-making within the VPR pipeline. Our code is available at: https://github.com/mayayank95/UncertaintyAwareVPR

</details>


### [46] [Targeted Downstream-Agnostic Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19446)
*Zhuxin Lei,Ziyuan Yang,Yi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces a targeted downstream-agnostic adversarial attack on pre-trained encoders using per-example perturbations guided by a threat image to force identical features between adversarial inputs and the threat image, showing strong effectiveness across SSL methods and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pre-trained encoders are widely used but vulnerable to downstream-agnostic attacks. Existing DAA methods are non-targeted and assume knowledge of downstream tasks, which is unrealistic since encoders do not output predictions. A stricter threat model (targeted DAA) is needed to assess robustness of SSL representations.

Method: Propose a threat image as a target and train an example-specific perturbation generator to make the features of downstream-agnostic adversarial inputs match the threat image's features under the victim encoder. Unlike shared perturbations, perturbations are tailored per example to cope with input diversity and maintain invisibility. The attack operates at the feature level and does not require knowledge of the downstream task.

Result: Empirical evaluation across 10 self-supervised learning methods on 3 benchmark datasets shows the approach achieves high attack effectiveness and reveals strong vulnerabilities of pre-trained encoders. The authors plan to release code after review.

Conclusion: Targeted downstream-agnostic attacks with a threat image reveal pronounced vulnerabilities in pre-trained encoders and highlight the need for defense/robustness strategies under stricter threat models. The approach emphasizes the importance of example-specific perturbations and feature-level alignment in evaluating encoder robustness.

Abstract: Recently, pre-trained encoders have gained widespread use due to their strong capability in representation extraction. However, they are vulnerable to downstream-agnostic attacks (DAAs). Existing DAA methods operate under a permissive threat model, where an attack is successful if the generated downstream-agnostic adversarial examples (DAEs) change the original prediction, without requiring a specific target. In this paper, we propose a Targeted DAA (TDAA) method under a stricter threat model requiring the attack to be both targeted and downstream-agnostic. Since the downstream task is unknown and encoders do not directly produce predictions, achieving a targeted attack is particularly challenging. To address this, we introduce a novel component termed the 'threat image', pre-selected by the attacker as the target. Specifically, a generator is designed to produce example-specific adversarial perturbations that compel the victim encoder to output identical features for both the DAEs and the threat image. Unlike previous DAA methods that generate a single shared perturbation for all samples, which often fails due to image diversity, our method adopts an example-specific paradigm. This generates tailored perturbations for each image to ensure a high attack success rate and invisibility. By leveraging the threat image as a feature-level anchor, our method builds a task-agnostic bridge to reveal the vulnerabilities of the victim encoder. Extensive experiments on 10 self-supervised methods across 3 benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach and reveal the pronounced vulnerability of pre-trained encoders. The code will be made publicly available after the review period.

</details>


### [47] [CutVerse: A Compositional GUI Agents Benchmark for Media Post-Production Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19484)
*Haobo Hu,Xiangwu Guo,Zhiheng Chen,Difei Gao,Haotian Liu,Libiao Jin,Qi Mao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Cutverse is a benchmark to evaluate autonomous GUI agents in professional media post-production, covering 7 apps and 186 long-horizon tasks; introduces a parser to convert screen recordings and logs into structured GUI action trajectories; initial results show 36% task success, highlighting challenges in long-horizon domain-specific planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Professional creative workflows involve dense multimodal interfaces and long-horizon planning; current GUI agents underperform in such settings; need a systematic benchmark to measure and drive progress in post-production environments.

Method: Assemble expert demonstrations across seven applications (e.g., Premiere Pro, Photoshop) for 186 complex tasks; develop a lightweight parser to transform raw screen recordings and low-level logs into structured, compositional GUI action trajectories with precise grounding.

Result: Existing agents achieve 36.0% task success on realistic media editing tasks; demonstrates limited long-horizon reliability and domain-specific planning, although they show some spatial grounding and multimodal alignment.

Conclusion: Cutverse exposes substantial gaps in long-horizon planning for GUI agents in professional workflows and provides a scalable evaluation framework to benchmark and improve future models in media post-production tasks.

Abstract: While GUI agents have made significant progress in web navigation and basic operating system tasks, their capabilities in professional creative workflows remain largely underexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce Cutverse, a benchmark designed to systematically evaluate autonomous GUI agents in realistic media post-production environments. We curate expert demonstrations across 7 professional applications (e.g., Premiere Pro, Photoshop), covering 186 complex, long-horizon tasks grounded in authentic editing workflows, involving dense multimodal interfaces and tightly coupled interaction sequences. To support scalable evaluation, we develop a lightweight parser that transforms raw screen recordings and low-level interaction logs into structured, compositional GUI action trajectories with precise grounding. Extensive evaluations reveal that existing agents achieve only 36.0\% task success on realistic media editing tasks, underscoring the challenges posed by complex, long-horizon media post-production workflows in our benchmark.While current models demonstrate promising spatial grounding, multimodal alignment, and coordinated action execution, they remain limited in long-horizon reliability and domain-specific planning.

</details>


### [48] [CADENet: Condition-Adaptive Asynchronous Dual-Stream Enhancement Network for Adverse Weather Perception in Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19837)
*Sherif Khairy,Catherine M. Elias*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Three-thread, training-free CADENet for weather-robust detection; asynchronous S (YOLOv11n) for real-time detections, Q (CAPE) for condition-adaptive enhancement with EG-NMS fusion, E (CLIP zero-shot weather classification); aims to satisfy hard real-time constraints without retraining. Evaluation on DAWN (1327 images) reports very low F1 gains (snow F1 0.0230, rain F1 0.0038; micro-recall 0.0103) and highlights annotation-completeness bias; S runs ~44 FPS, no extra sensors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for real-time, generalizable enhancement for degraded-weather detection while avoiding training-time costs and undercounting of gains due to annotation bias.

Method: Three-thread pipeline: Thread S runs detections at full frame rate with YOLOv11n; Thread Q applies condition-adaptive enhancement CAPE and fuses results via entropy-guided NMS (EG-NMS) without blocking Thread S; Thread E uses CLIP for zero-shot weather classification to enable new weather categories via text prompts, with no labeled data or retraining.

Result: Evaluated on 1327 DAWN images (IoU 0.5, conf 0.25). Reported metrics: snow recall 0.0103 (micro), F1 0.0230; rain F1 0.0038; S maintains ~44 FPS irrespective of enhancement load; annotation-completeness bias formalized, so reported F1 are lower bounds; no model retraining or additional sensors required.

Conclusion: CADENet demonstrates a training-free, low-latency enhancement framework that adheres to real-time constraints and uses an annotation-bias-aware evaluation. However, the observed gains are small and bounded by annotation completeness, indicating limited practical improvement under degraded conditions; further work should address evaluation design and compare against stronger baselines.

Abstract: Adverse weather (rain, fog, sand, and snow) degrades camera-based object detection in autonomous vehicles. Existing enhancement-then-detect approaches stall the safety-critical perception loop, violating hard real-time requirements. Progress on this problem is also constrained by an under-recognized evaluation ceiling: ground truth annotated on degraded images cannot credit a detector that recovers objects the annotators themselves could not see, so a genuinely useful enhancement can register as a near-flat F1 gain. This paper presents CADENet (Condition-Adaptive Asynchronous Dual-stream Enhancement Network), a training-free three-thread system: Thread S (YOLOv11n) delivers detections at full frame rate with zero added latency; Thread Q applies condition-adaptive enhancement (CAPE) and fuses results via entropy-guided NMS (EG-NMS) without blocking Thread S; Thread E provides CLIP zero-shot weather classification, so new weather categories require only a new text prompt, with no labeled data and no retraining. Evaluated on 1327 DAWN images (YOLOv11m, IoU = 0.5, confidence = 0.25), CADENet achieves Recall = 0.0103 (micro), F1 = 0.0230 on snow, and F1 = 0.0038 on rain. We formalize the annotation completeness bias on DAWN-class data, so the reported F1 values are lower bounds on the true gain; recall is the annotation-gap-immune headline metric. Thread S sustains approximately 44 FPS regardless of enhancement load. No model retraining or additional sensor hardware is required.

</details>


### [49] [EventPrune: Cascaded Event-Assisted Token Pruning for Efficient First-Person Dynamic Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19506)
*Pengtao Ma,Ziliang Zhou,Ciyu Ruan,Haoyang Wang,Kaiyuan Li,Zihang Gong,Wenhua Ding,Chen Gao,Jingao Xu,Xinlei Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free event-guided token pruning framework (ECP) for video-LLMs using event cameras to reduce tokens by 80%, preserving motion cues, improving accuracy and speed relative to full-token baselines; ESR-Real benchmark introduced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the quadratic attention cost of Transformer-based Video-LLMs and retain dynamic geometric cues in first-person spatial reasoning, overcoming limitations of static token pruning that ignores motion information.

Method: Three-stage framework: (1) Event-Triggered Causal Sampling anchors motion-relevant keyframes; (2) Event-guided Motion Saliency Filtering suppresses tokens with little motion; (3) Event-Attention Ranking Fusion calibrates spatial attention with motion saliency. Training-free, relies on event cameras as a motion prior.

Result: With 80% visual token reduction, ECP outperforms the full-token baseline (37.62% vs. 36.31% accuracy), provides 1.89× inference speedup and 52% GFLOPs reduction. ESR-Real real-world RGB-event benchmark shows ECP improves accuracy by 2.68 percentage points over full-token baselines.

Conclusion: ECP demonstrates effective, training-free token pruning guided by high-frequency motion cues from event cameras, achieving substantial efficiency gains while preserving reasoning performance; introduction of ESR-Real offers a real-world benchmark for first-person spatial reasoning.

Abstract: First-person dynamic spatial reasoning requires models to track continuous motion and precise geometric structure, but the quadratic attention cost of Transformer-based Video-LLMs makes dense visual tokens computationally expensive. Existing token pruning paradigms predominantly rely on discrete static snapshots, failing to preserve the motion and geometric cues essential for reasoning. We propose Event Cascade Pruning (ECP), to our knowledge the first training-free framework that leverages the high-frequency motion cues from event cameras as a continuous event-guided motion prior to guide token selection. ECP combines three stages: Event-Triggered Causal Sampling to anchor motion-informative keyframes, Event-guided Motion Saliency Filtering to suppress event-inactive visual tokens, and Event-Attention Ranking Fusion to calibrate spatial attention with motion-salient dynamics. With 80% visual token reduction, ECP outperforms the full-token baseline (37.62% vs. 36.31%) while achieving 1.89x inference speedup and 52% GFLOPs reduction. We further introduce ESR-Real, the first real-world RGB-event benchmark for first-person spatial reasoning, where ECP improves accuracy by 2.68 percentage points over full-token baselines.

</details>


### [50] [World-Ego Modeling for Long-Horizon Evolution in Hybrid Embodied Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19957)
*Zuyao Lin,Jianhui Zhang,Peidong Jia,Xiaoguang Zhao,Shanghang Zhang,Xingyu Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes World-Ego Modeling to disentangle world and ego evolution in embodied agents, defines boundaries (motion, semantic, intention), analyzes three disentanglement strategies, and presents World-Ego Model (WEM) with a world-ego planner and a cascade-parallel MoE diffusion generator. Also introduces HTEWorld, a long-horizon hybrid navigation-manipulation benchmark, achieving state-of-the-art on it and competitive results on existing benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World-ego entanglement in conventional single-stream models causes degradation in long-horizon embodied tasks, especially hybrid navigation-manipulation scenarios; a principled decomposition of future evolution into world and ego components is needed for better generalization and planning.

Method: Define world-ego boundary from three perspectives (motion-, semantic-, intention-based). Analyze three disentanglement strategies: post-, pre-, and full disentanglement. Instantiate as World-Ego Model (WEM) comprising an implicit separate world-ego planner and a cascade-parallel mixture-of-experts (CP-MoE) diffusion generator. Build HTEWorld benchmark with 125K video clips (over 4.5M frames) and 300 multi-turn evaluation trajectories (over 2K instructions) for long-horizon world modeling in hybrid tasks.

Result: WEM achieves state-of-the-art performance on the HTEWorld benchmark and remains competitive on existing manipulation-only benchmarks.

Conclusion: The World-Ego Modeling paradigm provides a principled way to decouple world and ego dynamics, enabling better long-horizon reasoning in embodied AI. The WEM architecture and the HTEWorld benchmark together validate the approach and offer a foundation for future research in hybrid navigation-manipulation domains.

Abstract: World models are widely explored in embodied intelligence, yet they typically predict distinct evolutions of the world and the ego within a single stream, where the world captures persistent instruction-agnostic scene regularities and the ego captures robot-centric instruction-conditioned dynamics. This world-ego entanglement leads to a degradation in long-horizon embodied scenarios, particularly in hybrid tasks with interleaved navigation and manipulation behaviors. In this paper, we introduce \emph{World-Ego Modeling}, a new conceptual paradigm that decomposes future evolution into world and ego components. We define the world-ego boundary from three perspectives, i.e., motion-, semantic-, and intention-based views, and analyze three disentanglement strategies with post-, pre-, and full disentanglement. Further, we instantiate this paradigm as the World-Ego Model (WEM), a unified embodied world model that couples an implicit separate world-ego planner with a cascade-parallel mixture-of-experts (CP-MoE) diffusion generator. To enable rigorous evaluation, we further construct HTEWorld, the first benchmark for long-horizon world modeling with hybrid navigation-manipulation tasks, providing 125K video clips (over 4.5M frames) with fine-grained action annotations and 300 multi-turn evaluation trajectories (over 2K instructions). Extensive experiments show that WEM achieves state-of-the-art performance on HTEWorld while remaining competitive on existing manipulation-only benchmarks.

</details>


### [51] [Return of Frustratingly Easy Unsupervised Video Domain Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19510)
*Pengfei Wei,Yiqun Sun,Zhiqiang Xu,Yiping Ke,Lawrence B. Hsieh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A simple two-term objective for unsupervised video domain adaptation (MetaTrans) that separates spatial and temporal divergence via a temporal-static subtraction module; achieves strong cross-domain action recognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UVDA is practical but under-explored; addressing spatial and temporal divergence separately can improve cross-domain video recognition.

Method: A concise learning objective with two loss terms, coupled with a model architecture that includes a temporal-static subtraction module to decouple spatial and temporal divergences across domains.
