Result: Significant absolute improvements and superior relative gains over state-of-the-art UVDA baselines on cross-domain action recognition tasks.

Conclusion: Simple objective plus targeted architectural bias effectively addresses video domain shift by disentangling spatial and temporal factors, establishing MetaTrans as a strong UVDA baseline.

Abstract: Unsupervised video domain adaptation (UVDA) is a practical but under-explored problem. In this paper, we propose a frustratingly easy UVDA method, called MetaTrans. Specifically, MetaTrans adopts a concise learning objective that contains only two fundamental loss terms. Despite the simplicity of the learning objective, MetaTrans embodies an advanced UVDA idea, that is, handling the spatial and temporal divergence of cross-domain videos separately, through a subtle model architecture design. By implementing a temporal-static subtraction module, MetaTrans effectively removes spatial and temporal divergence. Extensive empirical evaluations, particularly on various cross-domain action recognition tasks, show substantial absolute adaptation performance enhancement and significantly superior relative performance gain compared with state-of-the-art UVDA baselines.

</details>


### [52] [Are Watermarked Images Editable? SafeMark for Watermark-Preserving Text-Guided Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19511)
*Xiaodong Wu,Qi Li,Xiangman Li,Zelin Zhang,Lingshuang Liu,Jianbing Ni*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SafeMark inserts a thresholded watermark-decoding loss into diffusion-based editors, enabling text-guided edits that preserve the embedded watermark without sacrificing edit quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preserve watermark recoverability in edited watermarked images. Editing can disrupt embedded watermarks; need a method that guarantees watermark integrity during semantic edits is maintained.

Method: Incorporate a thresholded watermark-decoding loss into the editor's training objective; fine-tune the editor to maximize watermark recovery alongside semantic editing quality; no architectural changes; compatible with differentiable diffusion editors.

Result: Demonstrates high watermark bit accuracy across diverse datasets, editing methods, and post-edit distortions, while maintaining high-quality edits and robustness to common distortions.

Conclusion: Semantic editability and watermark integrity are compatible; SafeMark enables trustworthy image provenance in generative editing pipelines.

Abstract: This paper investigates a fundamental yet underexplored question: can watermarked images remain editable without compromising watermark integrity? We propose SafeMark, a framework for watermark-preserving text-guided image manipulation that explicitly integrates watermark integrity into the editing process. Specifically, SafeMark adds a thresholded watermark-decoding loss directly to the diffusion editor's training objective, fine-tuning the editor so that semantically valid edits also preserve the embedded watermark at the final output. This design admits a clean information-theoretic justification: maintaining high bit-accuracy on the edited image lower-bounds the mutual information that the editor channel preserves between watermark and edited output, the quantity that fundamentally controls watermark recoverability. SafeMark is compatible with differentiable diffusion-based editors, and requires no architectural modification. Extensive evaluations across multiple datasets, text-guided editing methods, and post-edit distortion settings demonstrate that SafeMark achieves high watermark bit accuracy across diverse editing settings while maintaining high-quality semantic edits, without sacrificing robustness to common post-edit distortions. These results demonstrate that semantic editability and watermark integrity are fundamentally compatible, enabling trustworthy image provenance in generative editing pipelines.

</details>


### [53] [iDiff: Interpretable Difference-aware Framework for Pairwise Image Quality Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19522)
*Xinli Yue,JianHui Sun,Tao Shao,Liangchao Yao,Fan Xia,Yuetang Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces iDiff with a dual-branch architecture for pairwise IQA that jointly predicts preferences and generates rationale; achieves first place in NTIRE 2026 RAIM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for not only accurate preference prediction in pairwise IQA but also image-grounded, structured explanations; RAIM demands reasoning-based evaluation.

Method: Dual-branch: Answer Model for robust preference prediction by decomposing samples into left/right global/local views, content-aware specialization (person/scene), and backbone ensemble; Thinking Model for rationale generation aided by expert-style templates, multi-source quality features, and answer-aware supervision conditioned on Answer Model outputs.

Result: Demonstrates improved robustness and interpretability; strong performance on accuracy and reasoning metrics; won first place in NTIRE 2026 RAIM.

Conclusion: Validates integrating explicit difference modeling with structured multimodal reasoning for pairwise IQA; potential for broader tasks in interpretable image quality assessment.

Abstract: Pairwise image quality assessment (IQA) in professional photography requires a model not only to identify the preferred image between two candidates, but also to provide convincing and image-grounded reasoning. In the NTIRE 2026 RAIM challenge, this requirement is further emphasized by jointly evaluating preference prediction and rationale generation. To address this task, we propose iDiff, an Interpretable Difference-aware framework for pairwise image quality assessment. Our method adopts a dual-branch design consisting of an Answer Model and a Thinking Model. The Answer Model performs robust preference prediction by explicitly decomposing each sample into left/right global and local views, followed by content-aware specialization for person and scene images and ensemble-based aggregation across backbones. The Thinking Model focuses on rationale generation and is progressively enhanced with expert-style templates, multi-source quality features, and answer-aware supervision conditioned on the Answer Model prediction. In this way, iDiff jointly models discriminative decision making and structured explanation, improving both robustness and interpretability. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework on both accuracy and reasoning-quality metrics. Our method achieved first place in the NTIRE 2026 RAIM challenge, showing the effectiveness of integrating explicit difference modeling with structured multimodal reasoning for pairwise IQA.

</details>


### [54] [Dual-Prompt CLIP with Hybrid Visual Encoders for Occluded Person Re-Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19527)
*Zhangjian Ji,Shaotong Qiao,Kai Feng,Wei Wei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A proposed Dual Prompt Learning ReID (DPL-ReID) for occluded person re-ID that uses dual text prompts, real-world occlusion augmentation, and a weighted gated fusion to improve CLIP-based representations, achieving state-of-the-art results on occluded datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Occlusions disrupt body-region cues in re-ID; existing CLIP-based methods focus on prompts and neglect occluder semantics. Textual cues about occluders can capture complete pedestrian semantics and improve robustness, while realistic occlusion data and feature fusion can enhance representations.

Method: Introduce Dual Prompt Learning (Dual-PL) to leverage textual cues for complete semantics and occlusion robustness; Real-World Occlusion Augmentation (RWOA) to simulate realistic occlusions; Weighted Gated Feature Fusion (WGFF) with LSNet to capture global information and gate features, guiding the CLIP visual encoder.

Result: Extensive experiments on occluded ReID benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: DPL-ReID with Dual-PL, RWOA, and WGFF advances occluded person ReID; an occlusion instance library is provided at the project GitHub.

Abstract: Occluded person re-identification focuses on matching partially visible pedestrians across multiple camera views. However, occlusions disrupt body-region cues, thereby complicating cross-view matching. Most person ReID methods built on pretrained vision-language models only focus on enhancing prompt-based feature learning while ignoring the semantic information of occluders. Based on the success of CLIP-ReID, we propose a novel Dual Prompt Learning ReID (DPL-ReID) model for occluded person ReID. It incorporates a Dual Prompt Learning (Dual-PL) strategy, which can utilize textual cues to capture complete pedestrian semantics and keep robustness against occlusion, and a Real-World Occlusion Augmentation (RWOA) method that realistically simulates occlusion scenarios encountered in real word to enrich occluded samples. In addition, we also design a Weighted Gated Feature Fusion (WGFF) method, which in corporates LSNet to capture global information and act as a feature-gating mechanism. This mechanism can effectively guide the CLIP visual encoder toward generating more comprehensive feature representations. Extensive experiments on several benchmark occluded ReID datasets show that our proposed DPL-ReID achieves the state-of-the art performance. The occlusion instance library are available at https://github.com/stone-qiao/DPL-ReID.

</details>


### [55] [Towards Camera-Robust 3D Localization: Equation-Anchored Tool-Use for MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19528)
*Xueying Jiang,Wenhao Li,Quanhao Qian,Deli Zhao,Shijian Lu,Gongjie Zhang,Ran Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes an equation-anchored tool-use framework to deterministicly inject camera intrinsics into 3D perception in MLLMs, improving 3D object detection and grounding under varying intrinsics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Camera intrinsic ambiguity in multimodal LLMs causes mis-projection of 3D scenes; existing methods either ignore intrinsics or treat depth cues as weak hints, hindering deterministic propagation of camera parameters and robustness to intrinsics shifts.

Method: Retreive camera intrinsics and sample multi-point metric depths; explicitly write the pinhole back-projection equation X_hat = (u_c - c_x) Z_bar / f_x in chain-of-thought; substitute tool outputs into the formula, and regress final 9-DoF bounding boxes; evaluate on 3D object detection and 3D visual grounding under rescaled intrinsics (0.5x–1.5x).

Result: Outperforms RGB-only and tool-augmented baselines on both 3D detection and grounding across intrinsic scales, with larger gains as intrinsics deviate from training scale.

Conclusion: Equation-anchored, tool-assisted reasoning enables deterministic camera information propagation, boosting robustness to intrinsic variations; code and data will be released.

Abstract: 3D localization in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), including 3D object detection and 3D visual grounding, is fundamentally limited by camera intrinsic ambiguity: the same image admits different 3D scenes under different cameras. Existing MLLMs either ignore camera parameters and overfit to a canonical training intrinsic, or retrieve depth and 3D cues from external tools but treat the returned values as reference cues (numerical hints that the model is free to interpret implicitly), both preventing camera information from being deterministically propagated into the prediction. We propose an equation-anchored tool-use framework that re-purposes spatial tools as formula variables. The proposed framework proactively retrieves camera intrinsics and samples multi-point metric depths, writes the pinhole back-projection equation $\hat{X} = (u_c - c_x)\bar{Z}/f_x$ explicitly in Chain-of-Thought (CoT), and substitutes tool outputs into the formula before regressing the final 9-DoF bounding box. On both 3D object detection and 3D visual grounding tasks under rescaled camera intrinsics from $0.5\times$ to $1.5\times$, our method outperforms RGB-only and tool-augmented baselines, with significant gains where the camera deviates most from the training scale. Code and data will be released.

</details>


### [56] [Boosting Text-to-Image Diffusion Models via Core Token Attention-Based Seed Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19532)
*Yunzhe Zhang,Hongfu Liu,Pengyu Hong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes ABSS, a training-free, inference-time method that ranks diffusion seeds via early cross-attention to prompt core tokens, boosting alignment and image quality without changing the initial noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Seed-induced variability in text-to-image diffusion leads to inconsistent quality and prompt–image alignment; a lightweight, non-training, plug-and-play seed selection method is desirable.

Method: During the first few denoising steps, compute cross-attention scores between the latent state and the prompt's core tokens for each candidate seed. Score and rank all seeds, select the top-k, and discard the rest, without finetuning or altering the initial noise. Operates entirely at inference time and can be combined with existing seed-optimization pipelines.

Result: ABSS yields consistent improvements in text–image alignment and visual quality across three benchmarks for Stable Diffusion variants, as supported by human preferences and alignment metrics; it requires no training and acts as a lightweight pre-selection module.

Conclusion: Attention-based early signals provide a simple, generalizable mechanism to stabilize seed-dependent diffusion outputs, enabling reliable quality gains without model changes.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models can synthesize high-quality images, yet the outcome is notoriously sensitive to the random seed: different initial seeds often yield large variations in image quality and prompt-image alignment. We revisit this "seed effect" and show that attention dynamics over prompt core tokens, the content-bearing words, measured during the first few denoising steps, strongly predict final generation quality. Building on this observation, we introduce Attention-Based Seed Selection (ABSS), a training-free, plug-and-play method that ranks seeds for a given prompt by leveraging cross-attention to core tokens during the denoising process. ABSS requires no finetuning and does not alter the initial noise; it scores and ranks all candidate seeds, keeps only the top-k for full generation, and discards the rest, without relying on a fixed accept/reject threshold. Operating purely at inference time, ABSS can serve as a lightweight pre-selection add-on for existing seed-optimization pipelines, enabling additional gains. Across three benchmarks, extensive experiments show that ABSS enables consistent improvements in text-image alignment and visual quality for Stable Diffusion variants, as corroborated by human preference and alignment metrics.

</details>


### [57] [Replacement Learning: Training Neural Networks with Fewer Parameters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19533)
*Yuming Zhang,Peizhe Wang,Tianyang Han,Hengyu Shi,Junhao Su,Dongzhi Guan,Jiabin Liu,Jiaji Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RepL replaces selected blocks with lightweight surrogate operators learned from neighboring blocks, preserving local context while reducing parameters, memory, and compute; demonstrated for CNNs and ViTs across diverse tasks and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Full-depth backpropagation becomes increasingly inefficient for deep networks due to parameter redundancy, high activation-memory costs, and training latency. Reducing depth or skipping layers can hurt representation capacity unless the removed computation is meaningfully approximated.

Method: During training, for each removed block a lightweight computing layer synthesizes a surrogate operator from the parameters of adjacent preceding and succeeding blocks via a learnable transformation and applies the synthesized operator to the preceding activation. This preserves local continuity without full-layer computation. RepL is instantiated for CNNs and ViTs with tailored parameter-fusion blocks that handle channels, resolutions, and transformer submodules.

Result: RepL reduces trainable parameters, GPU memory usage, and training time while matching or surpassing standard end-to-end training across classification, detection, and segmentation tasks. Additional results include WikiText-2, transfer learning, inference throughput, checkpointing, stochastic depth, and INT8 quantization, demonstrating generality and compatibility.

Conclusion: RepL offers a general training-time paradigm to cut full-depth redundancy without sacrificing performance, by learning surrogate operators that replace selected blocks; it is compatible with CNNs and ViTs and broadly applicable to vision-language tasks and beyond.

Abstract: End-to-end training with full-depth backpropagation remains the dominant paradigm for optimizing deep neural networks, but its efficiency deteriorates as models grow deeper. Since every block must be executed and differentiated under a single global objective, full-depth BP introduces substantial parameter redundancy, activation-memory cost, and training latency, especially when neighboring layers exhibit highly correlated learning patterns. Directly skipping or removing layers can reduce cost, but often weakens representation capacity or requires architecture-specific reuse designs. In this paper, we propose Replacement Learning (RepL), a training-time paradigm that reduces full-depth redundancy by replacing selected blocks rather than simply discarding them. For each removed block, RepL inserts a lightweight computing layer that synthesizes a surrogate operator from the parameters of its adjacent preceding and succeeding blocks through a learnable transformation, and applies the synthesized operator to the preceding activation. In this way, RepL preserves local contextual continuity while avoiding unnecessary full-layer computation. We instantiate RepL for CNNs and ViTs with tailored parameter-fusion blocks that handle convolutional channels, feature resolutions, and transformer submodules. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, SVHN, STL-10, ImageNet, COCO, and CityScapes show that RepL reduces trainable parameters, GPU memory usage, and training time while matching or surpassing standard end-to-end training across classification, detection, and segmentation. Additional results on WikiText-2, transfer learning, inference throughput, checkpointing, stochastic depth, and INT8 quantization further demonstrate its generality and compatibility.

</details>


### [58] [CaptchaMind: Training CAPTCHA Solvers via Reinforcement Learning with Explicit Reasoning Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19538)
*Pengcheng Wang,Haoxiang Liu,Yang Dai,Xiangxiang Zeng,Guanhua Chen,Baotian Hu,Longyue Wang,Weihua Luo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: First large-scale CAPTCHA benchmark (CaptchaBench) with 16k programmatically generated samples across eight tasks and region/process annotations; RL-based solver CaptchaMind with explicit reasoning supervision achieves improved performance (82.9% average across tasks, 71.0% on real-world instances).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of large-scale, richly annotated training data for training-based CAPTCHA solvers and to enable systematic evaluation of multi-step visual reasoning and interaction capabilities in end-to-end CAPTCHA solving.

Method: CaptchaBench provides 16k programmatically generated samples over eight task categories with detailed region- and process-level annotations for supervised or weakly supervised training. CaptchaMind is an RL-based solver trained with explicit reasoning process supervision to learn multi-step visual reasoning and actions.

Result: Existing methods consistently fail on tasks requiring fine-grained visual detail capture and region-level comparisons. CaptchaMind achieves 82.9% average success across eight tasks and 71.0% on real-world instances, significantly outperforming all existing methods without relying on closed-source APIs.

Conclusion: The dataset enables scalable training and rigorous evaluation of multi-step CAPTCHA solvers, and the RL-based CaptchaMind demonstrates that explicit reasoning supervision can substantially boost performance. Further work should validate generalization to diverse CAPTCHAs and explore broader applicability of process-aware supervision.

Abstract: CAPTCHAs are widely deployed as human verification mechanisms and frequently block intelligent agents from completing end-to-end automation in real-world web environments. Solving modern CAPTCHAs requires robust multi-step visual reasoning and interaction capabilities, yet training-based approaches have remained absent due to the lack of large-scale training data and process-level annotations. We introduce CaptchaBench, the first CAPTCHA benchmark designed to support large-scale training, comprising 16,000 programmatically generated samples across eight task categories with detailed region and process-level annotations. Systematic evaluation on CaptchaBench reveals that existing methods fail consistently on tasks requiring fine-grained visual detail capture and region-level comparison. We therefore present CaptchaMind, an RL-based solver trained with explicit reasoning process supervision, achieving 82.9% average success rate across eight tasks and 71.0% on real-world instances, substantially outperforming all existing methods without closed-source APIs.

</details>


### [59] [Trust It or Not: Evidential Uncertainty for Feed-Forward 3D Reconstruction with Trust3R](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19539)
*Zihao Zhu,Wenyuan Zhao,Nuo Chen,Chao Tian,Zhiwen Fan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Trust3R introduces a lightweight evidential uncertainty framework for feed-forward 3D reconstruction, yielding per-point multivariate Student-t uncertainty via a Normal-Inverse-Wishart head, improving risk-coverage, sparsification, and generally geometry accuracy across indoor/outdoor benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current feed-forward geometric models output heuristic confidences lacking probabilistic interpretation and fail to indicate where/how much prediction can be trusted; there is a need for principled, per-point uncertainty estimates to enable reliable downstream geometry pipelines.

Method: Integrates gated residual mean refinement with a Normal-Inverse-Wishart evidential head to produce a closed-form multivariate Student-t distribution for per-point uncertainty; lightweight with moderate inference overhead; compares against MASt3R's confidence map and single-pass heteroscedastic, MC dropout, and deep ensembles; evaluates on diverse indoor/outdoor benchmarks with uncertainty metrics (AURC, AUSE) and geometric accuracy.

Result: Trust3R consistently improves risk-coverage and sparsification and generally enhances geometry accuracy; achieves stronger uncertainty ranking across benchmarks, notably 25% lower AURC and 41% lower AUSE on ScanNet++; demonstrates practical reliability signal for uncertainty-aware weighting in downstream pipelines.

Conclusion: The approach provides probabilistically grounded per-point uncertainty with modest overhead, enabling more reliable dense 3D reconstructions; outcomes support integrating uncertainty estimates into geometry pipelines and active weighting strategies; project page and code are available.

Abstract: Geometric foundation models hold promise for unconstrained dense geometry prediction from uncalibrated images. However, in current feed-forward designs, their predicted confidence scores are heuristic, lack probabilistic interpretation, and often fail to indicate where and how much the predicted geometry can be trusted. To address this gap, we present Trust3R, a lightweight evidential uncertainty framework for feed-forward 3D reconstruction. Trust3R combines gated residual mean refinement with a Normal-Inverse-Wishart evidential head, yielding a closed-form multivariate Student-t distribution for per-point geometric uncertainty. This design provides probabilistically grounded pointmap uncertainty estimates while adding moderate inference overhead. We evaluate on diverse indoor and outdoor benchmarks and compare against MASt3R's built-in confidence map as well as common uncertainty-aware baselines spanning single-pass heteroscedastic regression and sampling-based methods such as MC dropout and deep ensembles. Experimental results show that Trust3R consistently improves risk-coverage and sparsification, and generally improves geometric accuracy. These gains are reflected in stronger uncertainty ranking across benchmarks, with 25% lower AURC and 41% lower AUSE on ScanNet++, providing a practical reliability signal for uncertainty-aware weighting in downstream geometry pipelines. The project page and code are available at https://trust3r-z.github.io/.

</details>


### [60] [EpiDiffVO: Geometry-Aware Epipolar Diffusion for Robust Visual Odometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19556)
*Prateeth Rao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A sparse, diffusion-enhanced epipolar matching framework with graph-based subset selection and differentiable SVD for robust relative pose estimation in VO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce correspondence redundancy from dense matching, improve geometric interpretability, and achieve robust, end-to-end differentiable pose estimation across varying baselines and noise.

Method: 1) Predict sparse epipolar correspondences instead of dense matches. 2) Apply an epipolar diffusion process to refine keypoints toward epipolar consistency. 3) Incorporate depth cues and lift correspondences into a Steiner graph that encodes relational structure. 4) Use a graph neural network to select a compact, informative subset of correspondences. 5) Solve for the essential matrix with a differentiable SVD, enabling end-to-end optimization. 6) Recover relative pose in a visual odometry setting; evaluated on TartanAir and KITTI SLAM datasets.

Result: The approach reduces correspondence redundancy while preserving robust pose estimation across challenging baselines, demonstrating effective sparse, diffusion-refined, graph-structured matching compatible with end-to-end optimization.

Conclusion: A principled framework that combines sparse epipolar matching, diffusion-based refinement, and graph-based subset selection to achieve robust, interpretable, and efficient relative pose estimation suitable for VO pipelines.

Abstract: Estimating relative pose from image pairs fundamentally requires only a minimal subset of geometrically consistent correspondences. However, most learning-based approaches rely on dense matching or direct regression, leading to redundancy and reduced geometric interpretability. In this work, we propose a sparse epipolar matching framework that predicts a compact set of correspondences optimized for geometric consistency across varying temporal baselines. To address residual noise and misalignment, we introduce an epipolar diffusion process that models correspondence uncertainty and refines keypoints toward epipolar consistency. The refined correspondences, along with depth cues, are lifted into a graph representation forming a Steiner graph that encodes relational structure between points. A graph neural network learns a compact subset of informative correspondences, which are passed to a differentiable singular value decomposition solver for end-to-end geometric estimation. Relative pose is recovered from the resulting essential matrix and evaluated in a visual odometry setting on the TartanAir and KITTI SLAM datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that combining sparse matching, diffusion-based refinement, and graph-based subset selection reduces correspondence redundancy while maintaining robust pose estimation across challenging baselines.

</details>


### [61] [EgoCoT-Bench: Benchmarking Grounded and Verifiable Operation-Centric Chain of Thought Reasoning for MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19559)
*Yang Dai,Dian Jiao,Tianwei Lin,Wenqiao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EgoCoT-Bench presents a grounded, verifiable, operation-centric reasoning benchmark for egocentric video understanding with explicit step-by-step rationales, built via STSG-guided generation and refined by humans; reveals current MLLMs struggle with fine-grained, grounded reasoning and that explanations may be superficially correct yet inconsistently supported by evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing egocentric benchmarks offer limited evaluation of fine-grained, operation-centric reasoning with explicit spatio-temporal grounding; there is a need for verifiable rationales to assess whether model explanations align with underlying evidence.

Method: Construct EgoCoT-Bench consisting of 3,172 verifiable QA pairs over 351 egocentric videos, organized into 4 task groups and 12 sub-tasks. Use a spatio-temporal scene graph (STSG) guided generation framework, then employ human annotators to ensure correctness, egocentric relevance, and fine-grained quality; provide explicit step-by-step rationales as groundings.

Result: The benchmark exposes persistent difficulties in egocentric fine-grained reasoning; many MLLMs produce explanations that are answer-correct but lack consistent grounding in the provided spatio-temporal evidence; the dataset serves as a testbed for grounded and verifiable reasoning.

Conclusion: EgoCoT-Bench can be a useful resource for evaluating and driving progress in grounded, verifiable reasoning in egocentric video understanding; a project page and supplementary materials are available for researchers to access.

Abstract: The rapid development of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has led to growing interest in egocentric video understanding, specifically the ability for MLLMs to recognize fine-grained hand-object interactions, track object state changes over time, and reason about manipulative processes in dynamic environments from a first-person perspective. However, existing egocentric video benchmarks suffer from \textbf{limited grounded rationale evaluation}, offering limited support for fine-grained operation-centric reasoning and rarely examining whether model rationales are grounded in explicit spatio-temporal evidence. To address this gap, we introduce \textbf{EgoCoT-Bench}, a fine-grained egocentric benchmark for grounded and verifiable operation-centric reasoning with explicit step-by-step rationale annotations. Overall, EgoCoT-Bench comprises 3,172 verifiable QA pairs over 351 egocentric videos separated into four task groups for a total of 12 sub-task groups, encompassing perception and retrospection, anticipation, and high-level reasoning. The benchmark is constructed through a spatio-temporal scene graphs (STSG) guided generation framework and is further refined by human annotators to ensure correctness, egocentric relevance and fine-grained quality. Experimental results show continuing difficulties with egocentric fine-grained reasoning and further reveal that many multimodal models produce explanations that are answer-correct, but have evidence that is inconsistent with the answer. We hope EgoCoT-Bench can serve as a useful testbed for grounded and verifiable reasoning in egocentric video understanding. Project page and supplementary materials are available at: https://dstardust.github.io/EgoCoT/.

</details>


### [62] [Lens Privacy Sealing: A New Benchmark and Method for Physical Privacy-Preserving Action Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19578)
*Mengyuan Liu,Ziyi Wang,Peiming Li,Junsong Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: We analyze a pre-sensor privacy framework for RGB surveillance combining a hardware privacy filter (Lens Privacy Sealing, LPS), a new dataset (P^3AR) for privacy-preserving action recognition, and a single-stage MSPNet with Inter-Frame Noise Suppressor (IFNS) and Cross-Frame Semantic Aggregator (CFSA) to maintain high action recognition accuracy while limiting identity leakage and resisting reconstruction attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address privacy concerns in RGB surveillance by protecting data at the acquisition stage, rather than relying on post-processing obfuscation or costly specialized optics.

Method: 1) Hardware-based LPS using adjustable laminating film to induce stochastic multi-layer scattering at the lens for irreversible privacy. 2) P^3AR dataset with replay-captured (P^3AR-NTU) and real-world (P^3AR-PKU) subsets with privacy annotations. 3) MSPNet: a single-stage network incorporating Inter-Frame Noise Suppressor (IFNS) and Cross-Frame Semantic Aggregator (CFSA), trained with contrastive language-image pre-training to improve semantic extraction under degraded visuals.

Result:  MSPNet with IFNS+CFSA nearly doubles action recognition accuracy compared with baselines on privacy-degraded data, while suppressing identity recognition to low levels. LPS provides superior privacy-utility trade-offs compared to state-of-the-art hardware methods, resists PSF inversion and data-driven reconstruction, and generalizes across configurations and environments. Code is released.

Conclusion: Pre-sensor privacy via LPS offers an inexpensive, effective privacy shield for RGB surveillance without significantly sacrificing action recognition performance, supported by a diverse privacy-aware dataset and a robust single-stage recognition model; however, real-world deployment considerations and potential attack vectors warrant further study.

Abstract: RGB camera-based surveillance systems enable human action recognition for public safety and healthcare, yet raise serious privacy concerns. Existing methods rely on post-capture algorithms, which fail to protect privacy during data acquisition. We propose Lens Privacy Sealing (LPS), a simple hardware solution that physically obscures camera lenses with adjustable laminating film, providing pre-sensor privacy protection at minimal cost. Unlike software methods or expensive engineered optics, LPS achieves strong privacy through stochastic multi-layer scattering that is physically irreversible. We introduce the P$^3$AR dataset for privacy-preserving action recognition, featuring both large-scale replay-captured (P$^3$AR-NTU, 114K videos) and real-world collected (P$^3$AR-PKU) subsets with privacy attribute annotations. To handle video degradation from LPS, we propose MSPNet, a single-stage framework incorporating Inter-Frame Noise Suppressor (IFNS) and Cross-Frame Semantic Aggregator (CFSA), enhanced by contrastive language-image pre-training for robust semantic extraction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MSPNet with IFNS and CFSA nearly doubles action recognition accuracy compared to baseline methods while suppressing identity recognition to low levels. Comprehensive validation shows LPS achieves a superior privacy-utility trade-off compared to state-of-the-art hardware methods, resists reconstruction attacks including PSF inversion and data-driven recovery, and generalizes robustly across optical configurations and challenging environments. Code is available at https://github.com/wangzy01/MSPNet.

</details>


### [63] [A novel YOLO26-MoE optimized by an LLM agent for insulator fault detection considering UAV images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19595)
*João Pedro Matos-Carvalho,Laio Oriel Seman,Stefano Frizzo Stefenon,Mohammad Khalaf Mohammad Khreasat,Gabriel Villarrubia González*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A one-stage detector, YOLO26-MoE, adds a sparse Mixture-of-Experts module into the high-resolution branch to refine features for insulator fault detection in UAV imagery, with LLM-assisted hyperparameter optimization; achieves state-of-the-art mAP and demonstrates strong practical viability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Insulator health assessment for power grids is challenged by small defects, heterogeneous fault patterns, cluttered backgrounds, and varying imaging conditions. UAVs with deep learning offer automated, scalable inspection but require higher accuracy and robustness.

Method: Integrates a sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) module into the high-resolution branch of YOLO26 (forming YOLO26-MoE). This enables adaptive feature refinement for subtle, diverse fault patterns while preserving one-stage efficiency. Hyperparameter optimization and training/evaluation were guided by a tool-augmented Large Language Model (LLM) agent.

Result: The model achieved 0.9900 mAP@0.5 and 0.9515 mAP@0.5:0.95, outperforming the latest YOLO versions on insulator fault detection tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed YOLO26-MoE architecture provides an effective and efficient solution for UAV-based insulator fault detection, demonstrating strong accuracy gains without sacrificing real-time applicability; further validation on broader datasets and real-world deployments would strengthen the findings.

Abstract: The inspection of electrical power line insulators is essential for ensuring grid reliability and preventing failures caused by damaged or degraded insulation components. In recent years, Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) combined with deep learning-based vision systems have emerged as an effective solution for automating this process. However, insulator fault detection remains challenging due to small defect regions, heterogeneous fault patterns, complex backgrounds, and varying imaging conditions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an optimized YOLO26-MoE, a novel object detection architecture that integrates a sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) module into the high-resolution branch of the YOLO26 detector. The proposed modification enables adaptive feature refinement for subtle and diverse fault patterns while preserving the efficiency of a one-stage detection framework. Hyperparameter optimization, final training, and evaluation were coordinated through a tool-augmented Large Language Model (LLM) agent. The proposed model achieved 0.9900 mAP@0.5 and 0.9515 mAP@0.5:0.95, outperforming the latest YOLO versions. These results demonstrate that the proposed model provides an effective and reliable solution for UAV-based insulator fault detection.

</details>


### [64] [deadtrees.earth-aerial: A Multi-Resolution Aerial Image Dataset for Tree Cover and Mortality Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19605)
*Ayushi Sharma,Clemens Mosig,Lukas Drees,Salim Soltani,Janusch Vajna-Jehle,Aaron Sheppard,Belqis Ahmadi,Jonathan Schmid,Paul Neumeier,Nathan Jacobs,Jan Dirk Wegner,Teja Kattenborn*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Open, ML-ready datasets for joint segmentation of tree cover and mortality from centimeter-scale aerial imagery at a global scale, including a large training set and a globally distributed benchmark; results show improved mortality segmentation, especially in boreal forests; data and code to be released openly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of globally representative, harmonized data for joint segmentation of tree cover and tree mortality, enabling scalable monitoring under climate change and disturbances.

Method: Two datasets: DTE-aerial-train (385k image patches, 1024x1024, 2.5–20 cm resolution) with multi-class expert-annotated and audited pseudo-labels for tree cover and mortality; DTE-aerial-bench (25 orthoimages, 525 patches) with high-quality expert annotations for both tasks; spanning tropical to dryland biomes; evaluated using strong baselines.

Result: Benchmarks show robust mortality segmentation improvements across biomes; boreal forests see F1 rise from 0.40 to 0.58 (~45% relative improvement); all data, models, and code will be released openly; interactive visualization available.

Conclusion: This work provides globally representative, ML-ready datasets for joint tree cover and mortality segmentation at centimeter scale, enabling scalable monitoring and advancing remote sensing analytics in forestry; open resources will facilitate benchmarking and model development.

Abstract: Forests worldwide are increasingly threatened by climate change and disturbances such as fire, pests, and pathogens, creating an urgent need for scalable monitoring of tree cover and tree mortality. Aerial imagery from drones and aircraft is a key data source for detailed and large-scale mapping of tree crowns and mortality. However, related progress is limited by the lack of globally representative, harmonized datasets for joint segmentation of tree cover and mortality. We introduce two novel, open, machine-learning-ready datasets to enable joint segmentation of tree cover and tree mortality from centimeter-scale aerial imagery for the first time at global scales. With DTE-aerial-train, we provide a training dataset comprising 385K image patches of size 1024x1024 pixels, with resolutions ranging from 2.5 to 20 cm. It includes multi-class expert-annotated and -audited pseudo-labels for tree cover and mortality. With DTE-aerial-bench, we provide a geographically balanced benchmark test set of 25 globally distributed orthoimages totaling 525 patches with high-quality expert annotations for both tree cover and mortality. Both the training and benchmark datasets span tropical, temperate, boreal, and dryland biomes and cover a wide range of forest structures and mortality patterns. Using the benchmark test set for evaluation, we establish strong reference baselines that improve mortality segmentation across all biomes and scales with significant gains in challenging regions, such as boreal forests, where the F1 score increases from 0.40 to 0.58 with around 45% relative improvement. All data, models, and code will be publicly released under permissive open-source licenses. An interactive visualization of the benchmark dataset is available at deadtrees.earth/releases/dte-aerial-bench.

</details>


### [65] [Spectral Integrated Gradients for Coarse-to-Fine Feature Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19607)
*Soyeon Kim,Seongwoo Lim,Kyowoon Lee,Jaesik Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SIG replaces the straight-line integration path in IG with an SVD-based path that activates principal components of the baseline-to-input difference in a coarse-to-fine order, yielding cleaner attributions and better quantitative performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IG's path choice affects attribution quality; straight-line paths mix noisy gradients. A path that reveals global structure before details could improve both qualitative and quantitative attribution.

Method: Compute SVD of the baseline-to-input difference to obtain singular components; construct the IG path by progressively activating components from largest to smallest, creating a coarse-to-fine progression; evaluate against existing path-based attribution methods; provide code.

Result: Empirically yields cleaner attribution maps with reduced noise and improved quantitative metrics across image classification datasets; code available at GitHub.

Conclusion: SIG provides a principled coarse-to-fine path for IG that improves attribution quality. Potential limitations include the computational cost of SVD on high-dimensional inputs and the need for clarity on whether SVD is applied to a vector or a matrix representation of the input difference; broader generalization to non-image modalities merits investigation.

Abstract: Integrated Gradients (IG) is a widely adopted feature attribution method that satisfies desirable axiomatic properties. However, the choice of integration path significantly affects the quality of attributions, and the standard straight-line path introduces all input features simultaneously, often accumulating noisy gradients along the way. To address this limitation, we propose Spectral Integrated Gradients, which constructs integration paths based on singular value decomposition (SVD) of the baseline-to-input difference. By progressively activating singular components from largest to smallest, SIG introduces global structure before fine-grained details, naturally following a coarse-to-fine progression. Through extensive evaluation across diverse image classification datasets, we demonstrate that SIG produces cleaner attribution maps with reduced noise and achieves improved quantitative performance compared to existing path-based attribution methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/leekwoon/sig/.

</details>


### [66] [Inverse Design of Metasurface based Absorbers using Physics Guided Conditional Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19611)
*Vineetha Joy,Jamshed Palai,Satwik Sahoo,Anshuman Kumar,Amit Sethi,Hema Singh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A physics-guided conditional diffusion model with FiLM conditioning and a surrogate EM simulator enables fast, accurate, and diverse inverse design of metasurface absorbers under spectral constraints, achieving ~30s per design with high fidelity and experimental validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce design time and improve conditional fidelity for inverse metasurface design under stringent spectral constraints, overcoming slow traditional optimization and fidelity issues of generative methods.

Method: A diffusion-based generative framework conditioned on target reflection spectra via FiLM. It embeds a pre-trained surrogate EM simulator to provide physics-aware spectrum loss, enforcing spectral fidelity. The model outputs multiple geometries per condition for manufacturability and is validated in 2–18 GHz with experimental measurements.

Result: Average spectral MSE of 0.0006 and band alignment accuracy of 0.958 between target and generated spectra; multiple geometries per condition; ~30 seconds per design vs months for conventional methods; experimental validation corroborating efficiency.

Conclusion: The framework delivers fast, accurate, and manufacturable metasurface designs under spectral constraints, with strong conditional fidelity and design diversity, enabled by physics-guided diffusion, FiLM conditioning, and physics-aware losses.

Abstract: Inverse design of metasurfaces for specific electromagnetic responses requires generating geometries that satisfy stringent spectral constraints while maintaining manufacturability. Conventional design methodologies rely on iterative optimization routines using full wave simulations, which become extremely time consuming and computationally intensive for large design spaces. In addition, commonly employed generative approaches often exhibit limited conditional fidelity and the generated designs often contain fine or irregular features that are impractical to fabricate. In this regard, we propose a physics guided condition quality enhanced diffusion framework for the inverse design of metasurface based absorbers. Here, the conditioning information consisting of target reflection characteristics is integrated into the model using feature wise linear modulation (FiLM). Furthermore, to enforce adherence to target spectra, a pre trained surrogate EM simulator is embedded into the framework introducing physics aware regularization through spectrum level loss functions. The efficiency of the proposed model is demonstrated by generating practically realizable metasurfaces for different types of reflection characteristics in the frequency range of 2 to 18 GHz. The proposed framework achieves an average spectral mean squared error of 0.0006 and band alignment accuracy of 0.958 between the target spectra and the spectra produced by the generated designs, demonstrating high conditional accuracy. In addition, the model generates multiple geometries for the same condition, thereby providing diverse design alternatives to the engineer. The proposed model produces the suitable design in approximately 30 seconds, whereas the conventional approach can take several months under comparable computational resources. The efficiency of the model is also established via experimental measurements.

</details>


### [67] [White-Balance First, Adjust Later: Cross-Camera Color Constancy via Vision-Language Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19613)
*Shuwei Li,Lei Tan,Robby T. Tan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Presents VLM-CC, a feedback-guided, iterative color-constancy framework using vision-language model feedback to refine illuminant estimates, improving cross-camera generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard data-driven color constancy methods often overfit to the training camera's color response, causing poor generalization to unseen cameras; a perceptual, camera-agnostic evaluation signal could improve cross-camera robustness.

Method: An iterative refinement: initialize the illuminant, white-balance the image, convert to pseudo-sRGB; a lightweight LoRA-tuned vision-language model evaluates the corrected image to identify the dominant residual color cast; map this feedback to a residual illumination direction (R/G/B) and update the illuminant estimate; repeat until convergence.

Result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art robustness in cross-camera color constancy across multiple datasets."

Conclusion: Reframes color constancy as an iterative perceptual feedback problem guided by VLM evaluation instead of direct RGB regression, yielding improved cross-camera generalization; code will be released at the provided GitHub URL.

Abstract: Color constancy aims to keep object colors consistent under varying illumination. Cross-camera generalization in color constancy remains challenging because learning-based models often overfit to the color response characteristics of the training camera, resulting in degraded performance on images captured by other cameras. We propose VLM-CC, a feedback-guided framework that formulates color constancy as an iterative refinement process. Instead of directly estimating the illuminant from raw input, VLM-CC performs iterative correction driven by vision-language model (VLM)-based evaluation. At each iteration, the image is white-balanced using the current estimate and converted to pseudo-sRGB. A lightweight LoRA-tuned VLM then assesses the corrected image, identifying the dominant residual color cast and providing qualitative feedback. This feedback is mapped to a residual illumination direction (red, green, or blue) and used to update the illuminant estimate until convergence. Our key idea is to reframe color constancy as an iterative perceptual feedback problem, leveraging VLM evaluation instead of direct RGB regression. By replacing direct RGB estimation with VLM-guided perceptual feedback, VLM-CC achieves state-of-the-art robustness in cross-camera color constancy across multiple datasets. Code will be available at https://github.com/NothingIknow/VLM-CC.

</details>


### [68] [Bézier Degradation Modeling for LiDAR-based Human Motion Capture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19620)
*Xiaoqi An,Lin Zhao,Jun Li,Chen Gong,Jian Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A LiDAR-based 3D human motion capture system (BMLiCap) uses a coarse-to-fine, Bézier-curve–based representation with a Time-scale Motion Transformer and Multi-level Motion Aggregator to reconstruct temporally coherent actions under occlusions, achieving state-of-the-art results on four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address instability and occlusion-induced jitter in LiDAR-based motion capture; provide a coherent, temporally continuous motion representation suitable for autonomous driving and robotics.

Method: Coarse-to-fine representation by reducing Bézier curve control points via a trajectory-preserving strategy; progressive motion reconstruction with a Time-scale Motion Transformer (TMT) to predict motion curves at multiple temporal scales; a Multi-level Motion Aggregator (MMA) to adaptively fuse multi-scale curves for detailed, temporally coherent poses and to bridge observation gaps caused by occlusions and noise.

Result: State-of-the-art accuracy and temporal continuity across four benchmarks (LiDARHuman26M, FreeMotion, NoiseMotion, SLOPER4D); robust to occlusions and noise and reduces prediction jitter.

Conclusion: BMLiCap provides a coherent motion representation and effective reconstruction in challenging LiDAR scenes, demonstrating strong robustness to occlusions and improved temporal coherence.

Abstract: LiDAR-based 3D human motion capture has broad applications in fields such as autonomous driving and robotics, where accurate motion reconstruction is crucial. However, existing methods often struggle with unstable inputs and severe occlusions, leading to jittery or even failed pose predictions. To address these challenges, we propose BMLiCap, a coarse-to-fine framework that models motion using temporally compressible Bézier curves. By reducing control points through a trajectory-preserving strategy, we obtain a coherent and learning-friendly motion representation. To reconstruct human actions from LiDAR point-cloud cues, we design a progressive motion-reconstruction module. Specifically, a Time-scale Motion Transformer (TMT) is introduced to predict motion curves at multiple temporal scales, and a Multi-level Motion Aggregator (MMA) is utilized to adaptively fuse the multi-scale curves to recover detailed, temporally coherent poses, effectively bridging observation gaps caused by occlusions and noise. Across four mainstream benchmarks LiDARHuman26M, FreeMotion, NoiseMotion, and SLOPER4D, BMLiCap achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and temporal continuity in complex scenes, demonstrating its ability to compensate for severe occlusions and reduce prediction jitter.

</details>


### [69] [UniRefiner: Teaching Pre-trained ViTs to Self-Dispose Dross via Contrastive Register](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19622)
*Congpei Qiu,Zhaoyu Hu,Wei Ke,Zhuotao Tian,Yanhao Wu,Tong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A universal refinement method (UniRefiner) for Vision Transformers that identifies and re-distributes spurious spatial tokens to improve dense prediction and zero-shot segmentation, achieving strong gains on ADE20K after light, data-efficient fine-tuning on ~5k images across large ViTs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spurious tokens corrupt spatial representations in ViTs for dense prediction. Existing approaches focus on narrow artifacts (e.g., high-norm outliers), but a broader taxonomy is needed. The authors argue for three fundamental spurious token types and aim to unlock the spatial potential of large foundation models by teaching ViTs to self-dispose of artifacts.

Method: UniRefiner employs contrastive registers to separate spurious tokens from meaningful tokens. It uses a dual objective: (i) align image tokens with filtered regular tokens to preserve semantics; (ii) align register tokens with detected spurious tokens to capture spurious signals. The approach requires only a few epochs of fine-tuning on ~5k images and is compatible with diverse ViTs, including EVA-CLIP-8B and InternViT-6B.

Result: Refined EVA-CLIP-8B achieves 51.9% mIoU on ADE20K (↑9.4 points) surpassing DINOv2’s 49.1%. Zero-shot segmentation accuracy improves by up to 22%. Demonstrates consistent improvements across multiple ViTs, indicating broad applicability of UniRefiner.

Conclusion: UniRefiner provides a data-efficient, universal framework to diagnose and mitigate spurious tokens in ViTs, enabling dense spatial tasks with large-scale foundation models and enabling their broader deployment.

Abstract: Representation learning with Vision Transformers (ViTs) has advanced rapidly, yet the utility of large-scale models in spatially sensitive tasks is hindered by spurious tokens. Prior efforts to mitigate this have been limited, often defining these artifacts narrowly, for example, as simple high-norm outliers. We argue that this scope is insufficient. For dense prediction tasks, we posit that any token failing to encode location-aligned semantics should be treated as a spurious artifact. This broader definition reveals a more complex problem, leading us to systematically categorize and characterize three fundamental types of spurious tokens that corrupt spatial representations. Based on this comprehensive diagnosis, we propose UniRefiner, a universal refinement framework that teaches pre-trained ViTs to self-dispose of these artifacts. UniRefiner uses contrastive registers to explicitly isolate and redistribute spurious tokens via a dual objective: (i) it aligns image tokens with filtered regular tokens to preserve semantics, and (ii) it aligns register tokens with detected spurious tokens to capture the spurious signals. Our method requires only a few epochs of fine-tuning on ~5k images to refine diverse ViTs, including massive models like EVA-CLIP-8B and InternViT-6B. Experiments demonstrate consistent and significant improvements: notably, the refined EVA-CLIP-8B achieves 51.9\% mIoU on ADE20K (+9.4\%), surpassing specialized vision models like DINOv2 (49.1\%), while zero-shot segmentation accuracy improves by up to 22\%. UniRefiner unlocks the latent spatial potential of existing large-scale foundation models, paving the way for their broader application.

</details>


### [70] [PrAda: Few-Shot Visual Adaptation for Text-Prompted Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19623)
*Gabriele Rosi,Fabio Cermelli,Carlo Masone,Barbara Caputo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces PrAda, a few-shot visual adaptation method for text-prompted segmentation that learns class prototypes to adapt a frozen model with minimal trainable parameters, improving cross-domain performance across semantic, instance, and panoptic tasks on five benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundational text-prompted segmentation models struggle under domain shift due to higher misclassification rates; traditional mask generation is less of a bottleneck; there is a need for efficient adaptation to target domains without full retraining.

Method: Prototype Adaptation (PrAda) learns class-specific prototypes by fusing fine-grained pixel features with high-level transformer features. These prototypes are merged with the model's text-based predictions using a learned importance factor, preserving zero-shot capabilities while enabling domain adaptation. The approach is parameter-efficient and operates on a frozen text-prompted segmentation model.

Result: PrAda yields significant improvements over state-of-the-art and baselines on five benchmarks spanning semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation, demonstrating robust few-shot adaptation under domain shift.

Conclusion: Prototype-based adaptation can effectively bridge domain gaps for text-prompted segmentation, maintaining zero-shot potential while achieving strong, data-efficient performance gains.

Abstract: Segmenting images is critical for visual understanding but demands extensive pixel-level annotations. Foundational models have enabled new paradigms for predicting new classes guided by textual prompts, without annotations from the target domain. Yet, on specialized target domains, far from the original pre-training, their performance degrades. We study the errors of existing methods under such domain-shift, finding that misclassification rather than mask generation is the main culprit. To address this, we introduce the novel problem of Few-Shot Visual Adaptation for text-prompted Segmentation. This kind of adaptation has been largely studied for image classification, but it remains unexplored for segmentation. We tackle this task with Prototype Adaptation (PrAda), a novel, parameter-efficient method that adapts a frozen text-prompted segmentation model. Our approach learns class-specific prototypes by combining fine-grained pixel features and high-level transformer representations, which are then fused with the original text-based predictions through a learned importance factor. This preserves the model's zero-shot potential while enabling strong adaptation to new domains. Experiments across semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation on five benchmarks demonstrate that PrAda yields significant improvements over state-of-the-art and proposed baselines.

</details>


### [71] [Component-Aware Structure-Preserving Style Transfer for Satellite Sim2Real 6D Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19624)
*Yonglong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Component-aware style transfer for synthetic-to-real satellite data to improve monocular 6D pose estimation; uses weakly paired data and mask-aligned region-wise style injection; yields lower distribution discrepancy and better pose metrics when training on translated data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the synthetic-to-real gap and scarcity of real, labeled satellite data while preserving geometric supervision from simulation.

Method: Construct weakly paired real–synthetic samples from calibrated acquisitions, ArUco pose measurements, CAD renders, and component masks. Extract part-wise real-domain style codes from unlabeled real images and inject them into corresponding synthetic regions with mask-aligned modulation. Train with adversarial objectives plus local contrastive consistency, self-regularization, and edge-preserving constraints to keep images useful for supervision.

Result: Quantitative gains: FID 54.32 and KID 0.048 (lower is better) in distribution alignment. When used to train GDRNet, ADD pass rate improves to 0.260 and AUC to 0.611, indicating improved pose estimation in the target domain.

Conclusion: Component-level appearance transfer can enhance satellite Sim2Real pose estimation in calibrated setups while retaining simulation-derived geometric annotations, outperforming representative image-translation baselines.

Abstract: Monocular 6D pose estimation for non-cooperative satellites depends heavily on annotated training data, yet real satellite images with reliable pose labels and component-level masks are difficult to acquire at scale. Synthetic rendering can provide exact geometric annotations, but the appearance gap between rendered and real observations limits direct transfer to the real domain. This paper presents a component-aware structure-preserving style transfer framework for satellite synthetic-to-real data construction. The method builds weakly paired real--synthetic samples from calibrated real acquisition, ArUco-based camera-pose measurement, CAD rendering, and component masks. It then extracts part-wise real-domain style codes from unlabeled real images and injects them into corresponding synthetic satellite regions through mask-aligned modulation. To keep the generated images usable for downstream supervision, adversarial training is combined with local contrastive consistency, self-regularization, and edge-preserving constraints. Experiments are conducted on 5,000 rendered satellite images and 100 real images captured in a calibrated setup. The real images provide target-domain appearance references and final evaluation images, while the downstream GDRNet pose estimator is trained only on synthetic or translated synthetic images. Compared with representative image-translation baselines, the proposed method achieves the lowest image distribution discrepancy, with an FID of 54.32 and a KID of 0.048. When the translated data are used to train GDRNet in this target-domain adaptation setting, the ADD pass rate improves to 0.260 and the AUC improves to 0.611. These results indicate that component-level appearance transfer can improve satellite Sim2Real pose estimation in the considered calibrated setup while retaining simulation-derived geometric annotations.

</details>


### [72] [P2DNav: Panorama-to-Downview Reasoning for Zero-shot Vision-and-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19634)
*Kai Sheng,Liuyi Wang,Haojie Dai,Jinlong Li,Yongrui Qin,Zongtao He,Chengju Liu,Qijun Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: P2DNav introduces a hierarchical, zero-shot VLN framework that decouples panoramic direction reasoning from local grounding via three components (P2D, SDM, RRM), uses a sliding-window dialogue memory for long-horizon context, and reorients when local grounding is unreliable, achieving strong SR gains on R2R-CE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing zero-shot VLN methods often couple high-level waypoint reasoning with fine-grained grounding, leading to unstable decisions in unseen environments. There is a need for a modular approach that separately handles panoramic direction, durable context, and robust reorientation.

Method: A three-component hierarchical framework: (1) Panorama-to-Downview (P2D) for two-stage decision—panoramic direction selection from a 360° panorama and pixel-level target prediction from the downview in that direction; (2) Sliding-Window Dialogue Memory (SDM) to maintain multi-turn context and recent observations; (3) Reflective Reorientation Mechanism (RRM) to assess grounding reliability and revert to panoramic selection if needed.

Result: On the R2R-CE benchmark, P2DNav achieves strong zero-shot performance, with SR gains of 146.6% over state-of-the-art zero-shot waypoint-based methods and 58.9% over zero-shot waypoint-free methods.

Conclusion: The three components collectively improve robustness and performance in zero-shot VLN by separating global directional reasoning from local grounding, maintaining long-horizon context, and enabling reflective reorientation, representing a substantive advance over prior zero-shot approaches.

Abstract: Vision-and-language navigation (VLN) requires an embodied agent to ground natural-language instructions into executable navigation actions in unseen environments. Existing zero-shot methods typically rely on additional waypoint prediction modules, which often entangle high-level directional reasoning with fine-grained local grounding, leading to error-prone and unstable decisions. In this paper, we propose P2DNav, a hierarchical framework for zero-shot vision-and-language navigation. P2DNav consists of three core components: Panorama-to-Downview (P2D), Sliding-Window Dialogue Memory (SDM), and Reflective Reorientation Mechanism (RRM). P2D explicitly decomposes navigation decision-making into two stages: panoramic direction selection and downview local grounding. It first selects the instruction-relevant direction from a 360° panorama, and then predicts a pixel-level target point from the downview RGB observation in that direction. In addition, SDM organizes navigation history as a multi-turn dialogue context and maintains recent visual observations within a sliding window to support long-horizon navigation. RRM further enables reflective reorientation by assessing the reliability of local grounding based on the downview observation and returning to panoramic direction selection when necessary. Experiments on the R2R-CE benchmark show that P2DNav achieves strong performance among zero-shot methods. In particular, compared with the state-of-the-art (SOTA) zero-shot waypoint-based and waypoint-free methods, P2DNav achieves SR gains of 146.6% and 58.9%, respectively, demonstrating the effectiveness of P2D, SDM, and RRM for zero-shot VLN. Code will be released for public use.

</details>


### [73] [Benchmarking and Evolving Reason-Reflect-Rectify for Reflective Visual Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19639)
*Junjie Wang,Xinghua Lou,Jason Li,Ye Tian,Keyu Chen,Yulin Li,Bin Kang,Jacky Mai,Yanwei Li,Zhuotao Tian,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces the R^3 loop (Reason-Reflect-Rectify) and R^3-Bench to address iterative refinement in T2I/UMMs; proposes R^3-Refiner with GRPO and HRM; demonstrates improved reflective and rectification performance and compatibility with MLLMs, with code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-pass generation limits handling of complex prompts and iterative refinement. There is a gap between recognizing generation errors and producing actionable rectification instructions in current models; a formal framework and benchmark are needed to evaluate and improve iterative reasoning and rectification.

Method: Define the Reason-Reflect-Rectify (R^3) loop for multi-round generation. Create R^3-Bench with >600 expert-annotated instances to quantify iterative reasoning and rectification. Develop R^3-Refiner, a dual-stage framework using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and a Hierarchical Reward Mechanism (HRM) to align rectification with reflective reasoning. Demonstrate integration with various Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) on GenEval++ and T2I-CompBench.

Result: R^3-Refiner achieves significant gains on R^3-Bench: +12.0% Reflective Verdict Score and +9.0% Rectification Score. It can be integrated with different MLLMs to improve generation quality on GenEval++ and T2I-CompBench.

Conclusion: The work provides a practical, plug-and-play framework and benchmark for iterative, reflective generation in T2I/UMMs, addressing the gap between error detection and actionable rectification; code is released at the provided repository.

Abstract: Text-to-Image (T2I) models and Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) have achieved remarkable progress in visual generation. However, their reliance on a single-pass generation paradigm limits their ability to handle complex prompts requiring iterative refinement. To enable multi-round Reflective Visual Generation (RVG), we formalize the Reason-Reflect-Rectify (R^3) loop as a core framework and introduce R^3-Bench, a benchmark of over 600 expert-annotated instances that quantifies iterative reasoning and rectification capabilities. Evaluation on R^3-Bench reveals a critical gap: while state-of-the-art models can identify generation errors, they fail to generate actionable rectification instructions. To bridge this gap, we propose R^3-Refiner, a dual-stage framework leveraging Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and a Hierarchical Reward Mechanism (HRM) to better align rectification with reflective reasoning. Experiments show that R^3-Refiner achieves significant improvements on R^3-Bench (+12.0% in Reflective Verdict Score, +9.0% in Rectification Score), and can be seamlessly integrated with various MLLMs to enhance the generation quality of different T2I models on GenEval++ and T2I-CompBench. Code is available at https://github.com/xiaomoguhz/R3-Bench.

</details>


### [74] [CAD-Free Learning of Spacecraft Pose Estimators via NeRF-Based Augmentations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19649)
*Antoine Legrand,Renaud Detry,Christophe De Vleeschouwer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NeRF-based augmentation enables accurate spacecraft pose estimation from few real images by generating a large, diverse, geometry-consistent augmented dataset, eliminating CAD dependency and improving generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Relying on synthetic CAD data limits applicability to targets with reliable geometry priors and causes poor generalization to real on-orbit conditions due to unrealistic illumination and materials.

Method: Learn a Neural Radiance Field (NeRF) of the target from a small set of real images (tens to hundreds); render a large, diverse, geometrically consistent dataset with viewpoint and appearance variations; train target-specific pose estimators on this augmented data without requiring a CAD model.

Result: Accurate pose estimators achieved from only 25–400 realistic images, robust under severe illumination variations; when used with large CAD-based synthetic datasets, the NeRF augmentation improves out-of-domain generalization to better match real on-orbit conditions.

Conclusion: NeRF-based image augmentation provides a data-efficient, CAD-free pathway for training robust spacecraft pose estimators, broadening applicability to poorly documented or uncooperative targets.

Abstract: Spacecraft pose estimation networks require tens of thousands of CAD-rendered images to be trained. This reliance on synthetic CAD data (i) limits applicability to targets with reliable geometry prior, excluding uncooperative or poorly documented spacecraft, and (ii) causes poor generalization to real on-orbit conditions due to unrealistic illumination and material appearance. This paper introduces a NeRF-based image augmentation method that enables the learning of spacecraft pose estimators from only a few tens to a few hundreds of images. The method learns a Neural Radiance Field of the target and generates a large, diverse dataset through geometrically-consistent viewpoint and appearance augmentation. This augmented dataset enables the training of accurate target-specific pose estimators without requiring a CAD model or large synthetic datasets. Experiments show that our approach supports the training of accurate pose estimators from only 25 to 400 realistic images, even under severe illumination variations. When applied on large CAD-based synthetic datasets, the NeRF-based augmentation also enhances out-of-domain generalization, yielding improved robustness to real on-orbit conditions.

</details>


### [75] [Cross-View Splatter: Feed-Forward View Synthesis with Georeferenced Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19656)
*Matias Turkulainen,Akshay Krishnan,Filippo Aleotti,Mohamed Sayed,Guillermo Garcia-Hernando,Juho Kannala,Arno Solin,Gabriel Brostow,Daniyar Turmukhambetov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Cross-View Splatter: a feed-forward method that fuses ground-level photos with satellite imagery to predict pixel-aligned Gaussian splats for outdoor scenes, enabling better coverage and novel-view synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ground imagery is limited in coverage and costly to capture; satellite imagery provides global geometric priors accessible via public APIs; combining both can yield scalable 3D representations.

Method: Train a feed-forward model that predicts Gaussian splats in a unified 3D frame by aligning orthorectified satellite views with GPS-tagged ground photos; fuse ground and bird's-eye features; train on georeferenced ground-satellite datasets mined from open mapping services; evaluate on a new georeferenced NVS benchmark.

Result: Improved scene coverage and novel-view synthesis compared to ground imagery alone; quantitative improvements on the new benchmark; code and data will be released.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the effectiveness of cross-view geometric priors for scalable outdoor 3D modeling; leverages publicly accessible satellite data to augment ground-based capture; supports future large-scale NVS and 3D reconstruction.

Abstract: We present Cross-View Splatter, a feed-forward method that predicts pixel-aligned Gaussian splats for outdoor scenes captured at ground level AND by satellite. Faithful reconstructions require good camera coverage, but ground imagery is time-consuming and hard to capture at scale for large outdoor scenes. Fortunately, satellite imagery can provide a global geometric prior that is easy to access via public APIs. Cross-View Splatter fuses orthorectified satellite views with GPS-tagged ground photos to predict Gaussian splats in a unified 3D coordinate frame. By aligning ground and bird's-eye feature representations, our model improves scene coverage and novel-view synthesis, compared to ground imagery alone. We train on curated georeferenced datasets and paired satellite-terrain data, mined from open mapping services. We evaluate our method on a new benchmark for novel-view synthesis with georeferenced imagery allowing comparison to prior state-of-the-art methods. Our code and data preparation will be available at https://nianticspatial.github.io/cross-view-splatter/.

</details>


### [76] [DocQT: Improving Document Forgery Localization Robustness via Diverse JPEG Quantization Tables](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19688)
*Kylian Ronfleux-Corail,Guillaume Bernard,Mickaël Coustaty,Nicolas Sidère*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: JPEG quantization-table diversity drives robustness in document forgery localization; training with diverse QT distributions and explicit QT conditioning improves real-world performance, outperforming standard QT augmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain and address the generalization gap of document manipulation localization models when moving from benchmark JPEG compression to real-world production pipelines, where quantization tables vary.

Method: A controlled factorial study comparing two QT-aware architectures (FFDN [2] and Mesorch [20]), trained under three QT regimes: Standard-QT, Real-QT (DocQT from MAIF corpus), and evaluated under three recompression conditions; assesses localization performance and pixel-level FP.

Result: Training with Real-QT yields substantial gains on DocTamper and lowers FP on authentic documents, but only for architectures that ingest the quantization table explicitly; standard augmentation fails to proxy compression diversity; DocQT dataset and materials released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: QT-aware architectural design and training with diverse JPEG quantization tables substantially improve robustness of document forgery localization in real-world pipelines; standard augmentation is insufficient.

Abstract: Document manipulation localization models achieve strong performance on public benchmarks yet fail to generalize to operational document workflows. We identify a critical and overlooked source of this gap: the mismatch between the narrow distribution of JPEG quantization tables used during training -restricted to standard libjpeg quality factors -and the heterogeneous compression profiles encountered in real-world insurance document pipelines. To isolate this factor, we conduct a controlled factorial study comparing two architectures with contrasting levels of quantization table awareness -FFDN [2] and Mesorch [20] -each trained under either standard quality factor augmentation (Standard-QT ) or operationally calibrated quantization tables sampled from DocQT, a quantization-table bank derived from a MAIF operational image corpus (Real-QT ), and evaluated under three recompression conditions. Training under Real-QT yields substantial localization gains on DocTamper [15] and significantly reduces the pixel-level false positive rate on authentic operational documents, but only for architectures that explicitly ingest the quantization table as input. The released DocQT quantization-table dataset and compression-reproduction material are directly available at https://github.com/Kyliroco/Improving-Document-Forgery-Localization-Robustness-via-Diverse-JPEG-Quantization-Tables. These results demonstrate that standard quality factor augmentation does not adequately proxy operational compression diversity, and that architectural choices explicitly conditioning on the quantization table provide a meaningful robustness advantage for real-world deployment.

</details>


### [77] [WBCAtt+: Fine-Grained Pixel-Level Morphological Annotations for White Blood Cell Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19692)
*Satoshi Tsutsui,Winnie Pang,Shuting He,Bihan Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A densely annotated WBC image dataset (WBCAtt+) with 11 morphological attributes and 5 pixel-level components, 113k image-level labels and 10k segmentation maps, enabling attribute recognition, semantic segmentation, and explainable AI; baseline models exist and a compositional-structure-aware recognizer improves performance; dataset and code are released.


<details>
