  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing WBC datasets largely annotate only global cell categories and lack detailed morphological attributes used by pathologists, hindering explainable AI and fine-grained analysis; a richly annotated dataset is needed to study attribute-level recognition and compositional cell structure.

Method: Construct WBCAtt+ with 11 morphological attributes and 5 pixel-level components, yielding 113k image-level labels and 10k segmentation maps. Train baseline attribute recognition and semantic segmentation models; develop an attribute recognition model that leverages the compositional structure of cells; demonstrate explainable AI applications including counterfactual example generation.

Result: Baseline models establish reference performance for attribute recognition and segmentation. The compositional-structure-aware model improves attribute recognition. The dataset enables explainable AI methods, including counterfactual generation; dataset and code are publicly available.

Conclusion: WBCAtt+ fills a gap by providing rich, interpretable annotations for WBC images, enabling robust attribute-level analysis and explainable AI workflows in hematology; public release should catalyze further research.

Abstract: The microscopic examination of white blood cells (WBCs) plays a fundamental role in pathology and is essential for diagnosing blood disorders such as leukemia and anemia. To support further research on WBC images, multiple datasets have been proposed. However, they mainly annotate cell categories, and lack detailed morphological characteristics that pathologists use to explain their interpretations of cells. To address this gap, we introduce WBCAtt+, a novel dataset of WBC images densely annotated with 11 morphological attributes and five pixel-level cell components. With 113k image-level labels and 10k segmentation maps, WBCAtt+ is the first to provide comprehensive annotations for WBC images. Leveraging this dataset, we provide baseline models for attribute recognition and semantic segmentation. We also design an attribute recognition model to incorporate compositional structure of cells, further improving the recognition performance. Lastly, we showcase various applications enabled by our dataset, such as explainable AI models, including counterfactual example generation. \revision{The dataset and code are publicly available\footnote{https://doi.org/10.57967/hf/8143}}.

</details>


### [78] [Physics-in-the-Loop: A Hybrid Agentic Architecture for Validated CAD Engineering Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19717)
*Elias Berger,Muhammad Usama,Jan Mehlstäubl,Bernhard Saske,Kristin Paetzold-Byhain*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hybrid Agentic-Physical Architecture embeds knowledge-based engineering tools into autonomous AI agents for CAD design, using physical verification in a closed-loop planning/generation/evaluation/revision cycle; introduces a benchmark for functional validity and reports improved structural complexity and compile rate; code/dataset to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can generate CAD but struggle with physical comprehension and validity. Embedding validated KBE tools aims to ensure functional correctness and reliability in engineering design, addressing a gap between generative capability and physical verifiability.

Method: Propose a closed-loop, sequential decision-making framework where dedicated agents plan, generate, evaluate, and revise CAD designs guided by knowledge-based tools as feedback signals, conditioned on a load case and validated by physical checks. Introduce benchmark dataset and metrics for functional validity in generative CAD.

Result: The system yields more complex designs (4.2 increase in structural complexity) and improves compile rate by 3.5% relative to comparable agentic methods; introduces a publicly released dataset, metrics, and codebase/prompts.

Conclusion: The Hybrid Agentic-Physical Architecture enhances functional validity and design quality in generative CAD, enabling reproducible research and higher-integrity automated design through explicit physical verification and KBE feedback.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) can generate Computer-Aided Design (CAD), yet lack physical comprehension required for reliable engineering design. Instead of attempting to implicitly learn physical laws from data, we propose a Hybrid Agentic-Physical Architecture that embeds validated knowledge-based engineering tools directly into the decision making loop of autonomous AI agents. In this framework, engineering design is formulated as a closed-loop, sequential decision making process guided by explicit physical verification. Based on a load case, dedicated agents iteratively plan, generate, evaluate, and revise engineering designs using knowledge-based tools as a feedback signal. We introduce a benchmark dataset and metrics for assessing functional validity in generative CAD. Our system generates more complex and physically verified designs, with a 4.2 increase in structural complexity and improving compile rate by 3.5% compared to similar agentic methods. The codebase, prompts and dataset will be made publicly available to support reproducibility and future research.

</details>


### [79] [Efficient Long-Context Modeling in Diffusion Language Models via Block Approximate Sparse Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19726)
*Wenhu Zhang,Yiming Wu,Huanyu Wang,Yaoyang Liu,Huanzhang Dou,Senqiao Yang,Sitong Wu,Hanbin Zhao,Jiaya Jia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A data-driven block-sparse attention method (BA-Att) that uses pre-downsampled blocks to identify informative regions, with norm-sorting and covariance-compensated corrections to approximate full attention efficiently; demonstrates substantial speedups and robust performance across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion language models with ultra-long sequences incur high attention cost; fixed block sampling priors can miss salient tokens and fail under distribution shifts; a robust, efficient sparse attention mechanism is needed.

Method: Block-wise pre-downsampling to locate informative regions in a compact space; define an oracle post-downsample attention map; analyze pre- vs post-downsample approximation error; introduce a lightweight norm-sorting module and a covariance-compensated correction that uses diagonal QK variance to approximate full covariance, reducing complexity.

Result: Empirical results show up to 6.95x faster attention computation than FlashAttention; maintains near full-attention performance at 50% sparsity across language models, multimodal LMs, and video generation models; demonstrates efficiency and generalization.

Conclusion: BA-Att provides a robust, scalable sparse attention framework that avoids brittle priors, enabling efficient attention for ultra-long sequences with broad applicability and strong empirical performance.

Abstract: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) enable globally coherent, bidirectional, and controllable text generation, offering advantages over traditional autoregressive LLMs, while scaling to ultra-long sequences remains costly. Many existing block-sparse attention methods select blocks by fixed sampling patterns over the high-resolution attention space, such as tail regions or anti-diagonal stripes. Such prior-driven sampling can miss salient tokens and introduce instability under distribution shifts. In this paper, we propose the Block Approximate Sparse Attention framework (BA-Att) with block-wise pre-downsampled operation, which identifies informative regions within a compact downsampled space, avoiding reliance on brittle positional priors. To analyze its theoretical behavior, we define an oracle post-downsample attention map and formalize the approximation error between pre- and post-downsample schemes. Based on this insight, we introduce a lightweight norm-sorting module and a covariance-compensated correction that approximates full covariance using diagonal QK variances, reducing computational complexity. Extensive experiments show that our operator achieves up to 6.95x acceleration over FlashAttention in attention computation, and maintains near full-attention performance at 50% sparsity across language models, multimodal language models, and video generation models, demonstrating strong efficiency and generalization.

</details>


### [80] [Tango3D: Towards Alignment for Global and Local 2D-3D Correspondence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19727)
*Zebin He,Mingxin Yang,Shuhui Yang,Hanxiao Sun,Xintong Han,Chunchao Guo,Wenhan Luo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Tango3D unifies dense pixel-to-point alignment with global retrieval by embedding 2D image patches and 3D point tokens into a shared space, using a geometry-aware visual backbone and a pretrained 3D VAE, along with a three-stage progressive training strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D foundation models align to frozen vision-language spaces (e.g., CLIP), achieving strong global retrieval but lacking fine-grained pixel-to-point correspondences. There is a need for joint dense and global alignment.

Method: Encode images into 2D patches via a geometry-aware 2D visual backbone and encode point clouds into 3D tokens with a pretrained 3D VAE. Map both into a single shared space to enable dense pixel-to-point alignment alongside global semantic alignment. Use a three-stage progressive training strategy to stabilize joint dense and global objectives.

Result: The model achieves object-level pixel-to-point alignment while maintaining competitive global retrieval, a joint capability not offered by existing 3D foundation models.

Conclusion: Establishing a fine-grained alignment feature space injects rich semantics into otherwise purely geometric 3D tokens, enabling dense 3D downstream tasks and broadening the utility of 3D foundation models.

Abstract: Existing 3D foundation models typically align point clouds to frozen vision-language spaces like CLIP, which achieve strong cross-modal retrieval by compressing 3D shape into a global vector. However, this global-only alignment cannot establish fine-grained pixel-to-point correspondence. To solve this, we present Tango3D, a foundation model that unifies dense correspondence and global retrieval. We use a geometry-aware 2D visual backbone and a pretrained 3D VAE to encode images into 2D patches and point clouds into 3D tokens. These are mapped into a single shared space to achieve both local pixel-to-point alignment and global semantic alignment. To stabilize the joint learning of dense and global objectives, we introduce a three-stage progressive training strategy. Experiments show our model successfully achieves object-level pixel-to-point alignment while maintaining competitive global retrieval, a joint capability not offered by existing 3D foundation models. By establishing a fine-grained alignment feature space, Tango3D injects rich semantics into purely geometric 3D tokens, paving the way for a wide range of dense 3D downstream tasks.

</details>


### [81] [Aero-World: Action-Conditioned Aerial Video Generation from Inertial Controls](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19728)
*Abdul Mohaimen Al Radi,Kunyang Li,Yuzhang Shang,Mubarak Shah,Yu Tian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Aero-World converts a pretrained image-to-video diffusion model into a controllable aerial video generator by injecting action tokens for translational and angular motion, supervised by a frozen physics probe trained on real video-IMU data. AeroBench provides metrics (AAS, PCR) to evaluate action-alignment and stability, showing improved action alignment and comparable or better video quality versus baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation video models excel in visual realism but lack low-level control signals necessary for embodied AI, especially in 6-DoF aerial domains. There is a need for controllable, inertial-action-consistent aerial video data to train and evaluate agents efficiently without expensive real-world data or full video decoding.

Method: Aero-World injects sequences of translational acceleration and angular velocity into a pretrained latent diffusion transformer via an action-token stream. A frozen latent-space Physics Probe, trained on real video–IMU pairs, provides differentiable inertial-consistency supervision during LoRA finetuning, avoiding costly video decoding. AeroBench introduces Action Alignment Score (AAS) and Physical Consistency Rate (PCR) to quantify action adherence and motion stability.

Result: On AeroBench, Aero-World raises mean AAS from 57.7 to 63.6 over action-only finetuning, and offers a stronger quality-control trade-off than AirScape (lower FVD: 596.5 vs 1058.6; higher SSIM: 0.595 vs 0.505; higher Flow-IMU correlation: 0.44 vs 0.20).

Conclusion: Frozen Physics Probe supervision is a practical mechanism to adapt pretrained video generators toward action-aligned aerial motion, and AeroBench provides a meaningful benchmark to evaluate such alignment.

Abstract: Foundation video models produce visually impressive results, but their use in embodied AI remains limited because they are primarily trained on natural language rather than low-level control signals. This limitation is especially pronounced for aerial flight, where motion occurs in unconstrained 6-DoF space and small errors in ego-motion can produce large trajectory drift. Generating aerial videos that follow fine-grained inertial actions can support scalable training and evaluation of aerial agents by providing a controllable proxy for real-world or expensive simulation data. To address this problem, we propose \textbf{Aero-World}, a method for converting a pretrained image-to-video diffusion model into a controllable aerial video generator. Aero-World injects sequences of translational acceleration and angular velocity into a pretrained latent diffusion transformer through an action-token stream. A frozen latent-space Physics Probe, trained independently on real video--IMU pairs, provides differentiable inertial-consistency supervision during LoRA finetuning while avoiding computationally expensive video decoding. We further propose \textbf{AeroBench}, a benchmark for evaluating whether generated drone videos adhere to low-level action signals. AeroBench uses Action Alignment Score (AAS) to measure agreement with commanded inertial actions and Physical Consistency Rate (PCR) to measure temporal motion stability. On AeroBench, Aero-World improves mean AAS from 57.7 to 63.6 over action-only finetuning and gives a stronger quality-control trade-off than AirScape, with lower FVD (596.5 vs. 1058.6), higher SSIM (0.595 vs. 0.505), and higher Flow-IMU correlation (0.44 vs. 0.20). These results suggest that frozen Physics Probe supervision is a practical mechanism for adapting pretrained video generators toward more action-aligned aerial motion.

</details>


### [82] [LIFT and PLACE: A Simple, Stable, and Effective Knowledge Distillation Framework for Lightweight Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19729)
*Hyunsoo Han,Sangyeop Yeo,Jaejun Yoo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Coarse-to-fine knowledge distillation for diffusion models using LIFT and PLACE improves stability and performance under heavy teacher-student capacity gaps by addressing non-uniform errors with locally adaptive guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the difficulty for a student to faithfully mimic a highly expressive teacher's denoising process in diffusion models, especially under extreme model compression.

Method: Propose LIFT, which decomposes the distillation objective into a coarse alignment stage followed by a fine refinement stage; train the student on coarse alignment before hard refinement. Extend with PLACE, which partitions outputs into error-based groups to provide locally adaptive guidance. Validate across diffusion spaces (image/latent), backbones (U-Net/DiT), tasks (unconditional/conditional), datasets, and even flow-based models (MMDiT/SD3). Demonstrate effectiveness under extreme compression.

Result: LIFT+PLACE enable stable convergence and strong performance under heavy compression (1.3M-parameter student, ~1.6% of teacher), achieving an FID of 15.73, while conventional KD fails and yields FID 50–200+; shows robustness across diverse settings.

Conclusion: LIFT and PLACE constitute a robust, scalable distillation framework for diffusion models, delivering stable training and high-quality generation under substantial teacher–student capacity gaps and across varied diffusion paradigms.

Abstract: We demonstrate that in knowledge distillation for diffusion models, the teacher network's highly complex denoising process - stemming from its substantially larger capacity - poses a significant challenge for the student model to faithfully mimic. To address this problem, we propose a coarse-to-fine distillation framework with LInear FiTtingbased distillation (LIFT) and Piecewise Local Adaptive Coefficient Estimation (PLACE). First, LIFT decomposes the objective into a "coarse" alignment and a "fine" refinement. The student is then trained on coarse alignment before proceeding to hard refinement. Second, PLACE extends LIFT to address spatially non-uniform errors by partitioning outputs into error-based groups, providing locally adaptive guidance. Our experiments show that LIFT and PLACE is effective across diffusion spaces (image/latent), backbones (U-Net/DiT), tasks (unconditional/conditional), datasets, and even extends to flow-based models such as MMDiT (SD3). Furthermore, under extreme compression with a 1.3M-parameter student (only 1.6% of the teacher), conventional KD fails to provide sufficient guidance for stable training, with FID scores often degrading to 50-200+, but our method remains stably convergent and achieves an FID of 15.73.

</details>


### [83] [GeoMamba: A Geometry-driven MambaVision Framework and Dataset for Fine-grained Optical-SAR Object Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19734)
*Tiantong Fang,Xiuwei Wang,Jing Xiao,Wujie Zhou,Liang Liao,Mi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoMamba is a geometry-driven cross-modal retrieval framework for unaligned optical-SAR data, using GFI and GCC with deep supervision, plus a new FGOS-as dataset; achieves 63.3% mAP and 77.0% Rank-1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-modal retrieval between optical and SAR under unaligned conditions suffers from modality gap, speckle noise, and structural mismatch; robust, geometry-aware representations are needed for fine-grained object retrieval.

Method: Propose GeoMamba with a Geometric Feature Injection (GFI) module for enhanced cross-modal interaction and structure priors; a Geometric Consistency Constraint (GCC) module with Deep Supervision (DS) to impose hierarchical geometric constraints using classical operators; introduce FGOS-as dataset with 11 aerospace and maritime categories; evaluate in all-to-all cross-modal retrieval.

Result: GeoMamba outperforms existing methods on FGOS-as, achieving 63.3% mAP and 77.0% Rank-1 in all-to-all retrieval.

Conclusion: Geometry-driven representation learning with GFI, GCC, and DS improves robustness to unaligned optical-SAR data and preserves object structures; FGOS-as demonstrates effectiveness for real-world cross-modal retrieval.

Abstract: Multi-source remote sensing enables complementary observation of ground objects, while cross-modal fine-grained object retrieval remains challenging, especially under unaligned optical and SAR conditions. Unlike conventional retrieval settings that rely on paired or spatially aligned samples, practical optical-SAR retrieval is affected by substantial modality discrepancy, speckle noise, and structural inconsistency, which limit robust cross-modal representation learning. To address this problem, we propose GeoMamba, a geometry-driven framework tailored for optical-SAR fine-grained retrieval. Specifically, GeoMamba introduces a Geometric Feature Injection (GFI) module that enhances cross-modal feature interaction and incorporates structural priors, thereby improving the robustness of SAR representations and promoting geometry-consistent feature learning. In addition, a Geometric Consistency Constraint (GCC) module, together with a Deep Supervision (DS) strategy, imposes hierarchical geometric constraints using classical operators, which helps preserve informative object structures during representation learning. We further construct a new dataset, FGOS-as, containing 11 aerospace and maritime categories for evaluating unaligned cross-modal fine-grained object retrieval in realistic remote sensing scenarios. Extensive experiments on FGOS-as demonstrate that GeoMamba outperforms existing methods, achieving 63.3% mAP and 77.0% Rank-1 accuracy in all-to-all retrieval setting.

</details>


### [84] [FlowErase-RL: Rethinking Concept Erasure as Reward Optimization in Flow Matching Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19739)
*Yi Sun,Zhiqi Zhang,Xinhao Zhong,Yimin Zhou,Shuoyang Sun,Bin Chen,Shu-Tao Xia,Ke Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FlowErase-RL introduces a GRPO-based framework for concept erasure in flow matching models, casting erasure as a reward-optimization problem with a dynamic dual-path reward mechanism that balances suppressing target concepts and preserving generative fidelity. It achieves state-of-the-art erasure performance, robust image quality, and scalability to multi-concept settings without explicit supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow matching models enable high-quality text-to-image generation but raise safety concerns due to potential generation of harmful or undesirable content. Existing erasure methods are either limited at inference-time or require supervised fine-tuning with precisely aligned data, lacking scalability and effectiveness in multi-concept scenarios.

Method: A GRPO-based framework (FlowErase-RL) that reformulates concept erasure as a reward optimization problem. It introduces a dynamic dual-path reward mechanism with (i) a Concept Erasure (CE) reward to suppress target concepts and (ii) a Non-target Space (NS) reward to preserve generative fidelity. The two rewards are adaptively balanced via a performance-driven switching strategy, enabling stable, self-supervised optimization.

Result: Extensive experiments on nudity, object, and artistic style erasure show state-of-the-art erasure performance, while maintaining image quality and semantic alignment. The method also demonstrates robustness to adversarial attacks and scalability to multi-concept settings.

Conclusion: FlowErase-RL establishes a new paradigm for safe and controllable generation in flow matching models, providing effective erasure with strong performance, robustness, and scalability without requiring explicit supervision.

Abstract: Recent advances in flow matching models have significantly improved text-to-image generation quality, but also introduce growing safety risks due to the generation of harmful or undesirable content. Existing concept erasure methods are either inference-time interventions with limited effectiveness or rely on supervised fine-tuning (SFT), which requires precisely aligned data and struggles with scalability and multi-concept settings. In this paper, we propose \emph{FlowErase-RL}, the first GRPO-based framework for concept erasure in flow matching models. We reformulate concept erasure as a reward optimization problem and introduce a \textbf{dynamic dual-path reward mechanism} that jointly optimizes (i) a Concept Erasure (CE) reward to suppress target concepts and (ii) a Non-target Space (NS) reward to preserve generative fidelity. The two reward paths are adaptively balanced during training via a performance-driven switching strategy, enabling stable optimization without explicit supervision. Extensive experiments on nudity, object, and artistic style erasure demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art erasure performance while maintaining strong image quality and semantic alignment. Moreover, it exhibits robust resistance to adversarial attacks and scales effectively to multi-concept scenarios. Our results establish a new paradigm for safe and controllable generation in flow matching models.

</details>


### [85] [Real-World On-Vehicle Evaluation of Embedding-Based Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19744)
*Albert Schotschneider,Daniel Bogdoll,Svetlana Pavlitska,Ahmed Abouelazm,Johann Marius Zoellner*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A simple, reference-based anomaly detection method for traffic scenes that uses pretrained vision-transformer embeddings and patch-wise nearest-neighbor similarity to generate dense anomaly masks without explicit supervision, achieving good results on Road Anomaly benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the lack of diverse anomalous traffic data and the limitation of methods that rely on predefined normality, enabling robust, real-time anomaly signaling without dataset-specific training.

Method: Leverage pretrained vision transformer embeddings as latent features. For each image patch, compute nearest-neighbor similarity to a single reference image in the latent space, flag anomalies as deviations, and produce dense patch-level anomaly masks. The approach runs in real-time and requires no explicit supervision or dataset-specific training.

Result: Evaluated on Road Anomaly benchmark and real-world autonomous vehicle data. Demonstrates good performance and consistent qualitative behavior, successfully highlighting semantically unusual objects. Indicates that a simple reference-based method can yield useful anomaly signals in realistic settings.

Conclusion: A practical, non-training-based anomaly detection approach using foundation-model embeddings can adapt to real-world driving scenarios, providing useful signals for safety without requiring labeled anomaly data or extensive training.

Abstract: Detecting anomalies in traffic scenes is crucial for ensuring safety in autonomous driving, yet collecting representative anomalous data remains challenging. Existing anomaly detection methods are highly specialized and rely on normality as defined by the abstract semantic Cityscapes classes, making it difficult to adapt to diverse real-world scenarios. We propose an adaptable real-time anomaly detection method that leverages foundation models in the form of pretrained vision transformer embeddings to detect deviations via nearest-neighbor similarity in the latent semantic feature space. Based on patch-wise processing, the algorithm produces dense anomaly masks, allowing for the localization of detected anomalies. The method robustly models normality through a single reference image. This formulation avoids explicit supervision and dataset-specific training, making it suitable for real-world deployment. We evaluate the method on standard benchmarks and on an automated vehicle in real-world scenarios. Despite its simplicity, the method achieves good performance on the Road Anomaly benchmark and demonstrates consistent qualitative behavior in practice, successfully highlighting semantically unusual objects in diverse scenes. These results suggest that simple, reference-based methods can provide useful anomaly signals under realistic operating conditions.

</details>


### [86] [CPC-VAR:Continual Personalized and Compositional Generation in Visual Autoregressive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19750)
*Junhao Li,Xinhao Zhong,Yi sun,Yuxia Qiao,Bin Chen,Shu-Tao Xia,Yaowei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A continual personalization framework for visual autoregressive (VAR) text-to-image models, tackling forgetting and entanglement via Gradient-based Concept Neuron Selection (GCNS) and a context-aware multi-branch fusion strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable scalable, controllable personalized generation in VAR models across long sequences and multiple concepts, addressing catastrophic forgetting in sequential learning and feature entanglement/inconsistency in multi-concept synthesis.

Method: 1) Gradient-based Concept Neuron Selection (GCNS): identify concept-relevant neurons and constrain only conflicting parameters across tasks to mitigate forgetting without model expansion. 2) Context-aware composition: multi-branch feature modeling with localized cross-attention fusion guided by spatial conditions for disentangled, controllable multi-concept synthesis.

Result: Experiments demonstrate substantial gains in long-sequence continual personalization and superior multi-concept image synthesis compared with existing baselines.

Conclusion: VAR models can support scalable and controllable personalized generation when equipped with targeted continual learning and context-aware composition strategies.

Abstract: Visual autoregressive (VAR) models have recently emerged as an efficient paradigm for text-to-image generation. Despite their strong generative capability, existing VAR-based personalization methods remain limited to static settings, failing to accommodate evolving user demands. In particular, sequential concept learning leads to severe catastrophic forgetting, while multi-concept synthesis often suffers from feature entanglement and attribute inconsistency. In this work, we present the first systematic study of continual personalized generation in VAR models. We identify two key challenges: (i) preserving previously learned concepts during sequential customization, and (ii) composing multiple personalized concepts in a controllable manner. To address these issues, we propose a unified framework with two core components. For continual single-concept learning, we introduce Gradient-based Concept Neuron Selection (GCNS), which identifies concept-relevant neurons and constrains only conflicting parameters across tasks, effectively mitigating forgetting without additional model expansion. For multi-concept synthesis, we propose a context-aware composition strategy that performs multi-branch feature modeling and localized cross-attention fusion guided by spatial conditions, enabling precise and disentangled concept composition. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly improves performance in long-sequence continual personalization while achieving superior results in multi-concept image synthesis compared to existing baselines. These findings highlight the potential of VAR models for scalable and controllable personalized generation.

</details>


### [87] [Preferences Order, Ratings Anchor: From Fused Expert Aesthetic Ground Truth to Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19776)
*Yuanpei Zhao,Jie Lin,Chao Zhang,Yilin Wang,Mao Li,Chenhui Li,Jie Hou,Tangjie Lv*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces PPaint, a matched dual-protocol benchmark for image aesthetic assessment (IAA) combining pairwise preferences and pointwise ratings across five dimensions on 150 Chinese paintings. Finds complementary strengths: preferences yield consistent ordinal rankings while ratings anchor absolute scores. Fusion of both signals via two independent preference-to-score methods yields a robust ground truth. Extends to label-free VLM training with PSDistill, converting pairwise judgments to calibrated pseudo-scores for single-pass training. A Qwen3-VL-8B model trained on one category improves mean SRCC from 0.504 to 0.709 across three categories, outperforming open-source baselines and approaching a closed-source model. Plans to release the dataset and code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limited understanding of how pairwise and rating-based annotations complement each other in image aesthetic assessment, by constructing a matched dual-protocol benchmark to quantify complementarities and enable joint training of aesthetic models without labels.

Method: Collect dual annotations on 150 paintings (5 dimensions) from 15 domain experts (3 per dimension? actually 5 per category as stated; specific per-dimension distribution not critical). Use a locally dense design for pairwise judgments (45,900 comparisons) and matched pointwise ratings. Develop two independent preference-to-score mappings to fuse into an expert ground truth. For model training, introduce PSDistill to convert pairwise judgments into calibrated pseudo-scores via an Elo reference pool and train a VLM with confidence-weighted ranking loss. Evaluate on three painting categories, with cross-domain transfer to APDDv2. Train Qwen3-VL-8B on a single category.

Result: Preferences yield more stable ordinal rankings; ratings stabilize absolute score scales; fused ground truth converges to near-identical scores across protocols. PSDistill enables effective label-free VLM training, achieving a mean SRCC increase from 0.504 to 0.709 for Qwen3-VL-8B across three categories, outperforming open-source baselines including ArtiMuse and close to Gemini-3.1-Pro within 0.04 SRCC, with efficient single-pass inference. Cross-domain transfer validated on APDDv2.

Conclusion: Dual-protocol annotation (PP and ratings) provides complementary, synergistic signals for IAA. The PPaint benchmark and PSDistill-based VLM training offer accurate, scalable aesthetic scoring with efficient inference, and the dataset/code will be released for community use.

Abstract: Pairwise preferences and pointwise ratings are the two dominant annotation protocols in image aesthetic assessment (IAA), yet existing benchmarks adopt only one, leaving their complementarity unmeasured under controlled conditions. We introduce PPaint, a matched dual-protocol benchmark in which 15 domain experts, 5 per category, annotate 150 Chinese paintings under both protocols across five aesthetic dimensions, collecting 45,900 pairwise expert judgments through a locally dense preference design alongside the matched ratings. The matched design reveals complementary strengths: preferences yield more consistent ordinal rankings, while ratings anchor the absolute score scale. Fusing both signals via two independent preference-to-score methods yields a fused expert ground truth on which the two constructions converge to nearly identical scores. The same preference-to-score principle extends to label-free VLM training. PSDistill converts VLM pairwise judgments into calibrated pseudo-scores via an Elo reference pool, and trains the same VLM with confidence-weighted ranking optimization to produce a single-pass aesthetic scorer. Trained on a single painting category, the distilled Qwen3-VL-8B improves mean SRCC from 0.504 to 0.709 across all three categories, outperforming all open-source baselines including the dedicated aesthetic model ArtiMuse and matching closed-source Gemini-3.1-Pro within 0.04 SRCC at single-pass inference cost, with cross-domain transfer further validated on APDDv2. We will release the full PPaint dataset and training code.

</details>


### [88] [Fast 4D Mesh Generation by Spatio-Temporal Attention Chains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19786)
*Dvir Samuel,Yuval Atzmon,Gal Chechik,Yoni Kasten*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free Spatio-Temporal Attention Chain accelerates 4D mesh generation by propagating correspondences in latent space, achieving 13x speedup and supporting longer videos, with improved temporal consistency and enabling downstream tracking and reliable camera estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the slow, computation-heavy nature and limited scalability of existing 4D mesh generation methods, by improving temporal correspondence quality without explicit, expensive matching.

Method: Introduce Spatio-Temporal Attention Chain: start from anchor mesh vertices, map them to latent tokens, propagate temporal correspondences in latent space, and recover frame-specific vertices via latent-to-vertex attention. This avoids explicit matching, preserves anchor mesh details, and enforces temporal consistency across frames.

Result: Produces a 4D mesh in 9 seconds, about 13× faster than state-of-the-art, and scales to videos up to 16× longer without quality degradation. Stronger temporal correspondences yield competitive zero-shot performance on 2D and 4D tracking and enable reliable camera estimation.

Conclusion: The framework delivers faster, scalable, and higher-quality 4D mesh generation with improved temporal consistency, enabling useful downstream tasks such as tracking and camera estimation, outperforming prior methods.

Abstract: 4D mesh generation has recently emerged as a powerful paradigm for recovering dynamic 3D structure from videos, but existing methods remain slow, computationally expensive, and difficult to scale to longer sequences. We introduce a training-free approach that accelerates 4D mesh generation while improving temporal correspondence quality. Our key observation is that temporal correspondences emerge inside a 4D backbone long before its generated meshes become visually accurate. We exploit this with a general framework we call Spatio-Temporal Attention Chain which propagates information across space and time. Starting from vertices on an anchor mesh, the chain maps vertices to latent tokens. It then follows temporal correspondences in latent space, and recovers frame-specific vertices through latent-to-vertex attention. This design avoids expensive explicit matching while preserving anchor mesh details and thereby improving dynamic mesh geometry and temporal consistency.
  Compared to state-of-the-art, our method generates a 4D mesh in 9 seconds, achieving a $13\times$ speedup while producing higher-quality results. Moreover, our approach scales to videos up to $16\times$ longer without degrading mesh quality. Beyond generation, the improved correspondences enable competitive zero-shot performance on two downstream tasks: 2D object tracking and 4D tracking. We further show that our framework enables reliable camera estimation, a capability not supported by prior 4D mesh generation methods.

</details>


### [89] [Mechanisms of Object Localization in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19792)
*Timothy Schaumlöffel,Martina G. Vilas,Gemma Roig*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Localization in VLMs arises from a containerization mechanism where object-aligned tokens define the spatial extent of the object; only a small set of attention heads causally drive both localization and classification, with early shared processing but distinct specialized heads for each task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the mechanistic basis of object localization in visually-grounded language models (VLMs), a less-explored capability compared to classification, and to inform model design for grounding objectives.

Method: Apply mechanistic interpretability tools (token ablations, attention knockout, causal mediation analysis) to two VLMs (LLaVA-1.5 and InternVL-3.5) to dissect how localization and classification decisions are computed, at layer- and head-level granularity.

Result: Localization is governed by a containerization mechanism: object-aligned tokens determine the spatial extent, while the semantic arrangement of tokens inside the boundary has little effect on the predicted box. A very small set of attention heads mediate the causal effects for both tasks, concentrating in early-mid layers for LLaVA and mid-late layers for InternVL. The two tasks share early processing but ultimately rely on largely distinct specialized heads.

Conclusion: The study provides the first layer- and head-level account of localization in VLMs, revealing narrow computational pathways that can guide future model design and grounding objectives.

Abstract: Visually-grounded language models (VLMs) are highly effective in linking visual and textual information, yet they often struggle with basic classification and localization tasks. While classification mechanisms have been studied more extensively, the processes that support object localization remain poorly understood. In this work, we investigate two representative families, LLaVA-1.5 and InternVL-3.5, using a suite of mechanistic interpretability tools, including token ablations, attention knockout, and causal mediation analysis. We find that localization is driven by a containerization mechanism in which object-aligned tokens define the spatial extent of the object, while the semantic arrangement of tokens within those boundaries is largely irrelevant to the predicted box. Only a very small set of attention heads mediates the causal effect for both classification and localization, concentrating in early-mid layers for LLaVA and mid-late layers for InternVL. The two tasks share some early processing but ultimately depend on largely distinct specialized heads. Overall, we provide the first layer- and head-level account of localization in VLMs, revealing narrow computational pathways that can guide future model design and grounding objectives.

</details>


### [90] [Depth2Pose: A Pose-Based Benchmark for Monocular Depth Estimation without Ground-Truth Depth](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19797)
*Viktor Kocur,Sithu Aung,Gabrielle Flood,Yaqing Ding,Lukas Bujnak,Torsten Sattler,Zuzana Kukelova*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Depth2Pose proposes a task-driven evaluation for monocular depth estimation by measuring pose estimation accuracy using depth-informed geometric solvers; introduces the D2P dataset and an extensible framework that relies on camera poses rather than dense ground-truth depth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard depth error metrics do not necessarily reflect downstream geometric usefulness; dense per-pixel depth ground truth is expensive or impractical for large or occluded scenes; a pose-based proxy aligns evaluation with real-world SLAM/SfM/localization tasks and broadens applicability.

Method: Integrate depth predictions with depth-aware geometric solvers to estimate relative camera pose; evaluate the resulting pose accuracy as a proxy for depth quality; rely on poses (estimable via SfM) instead of dense depth ground truth; present the D2P dataset and an open, extensible evaluation framework.

Result: On datasets with existing depth-based benchmarks, methods scoring high on depth metrics also perform well on the pose-based metric on the same data; however, performance does not always generalize to the more challenging D2P dataset, highlighting limited cross-domain generalization; dataset and code are released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: Pose-based evaluation provides a practical, task-aligned measure of monocular depth usefulness. It enables evaluation in scenarios lacking dense depth ground truth and encourages development of depth estimators that improve geometric utility, particularly in challenging scenes; the framework is extensible and the D2P dataset offers greater distributional coverage.

Abstract: Monocular depth estimation has improved significantly in recent years, driven by increasingly powerful models and large-scale training data. Predicted depth is increasingly used as an input signal for downstream tasks such as Structure-from-Motion (SfM), visual localization, and SLAM. However, monocular depth estimators (MDEs) are still primarily evaluated in terms of depth accuracy. Standard metrics aggregate errors globally and may not reflect the usefulness of depth for downstream geometric tasks. We therefore propose Depth2Pose, a framework for evaluating MDEs in the context of downstream tasks. By combining depth predictions with feature correspondences in depth-aware geometric solvers, we use relative camera pose estimation accuracy as a task-driven proxy for depth quality. Traditional benchmarks require dense ground truth in the form of per-pixel depth, which is expensive to obtain. In contrast, our formulation requires only camera poses, which can be estimated efficiently, e.g., using Structure-from-Motion pipelines. As a result, our framework can be applied to scenes where ground-truth depth is difficult to obtain, for example due to large scene scale or heavy occlusions (e.g., vegetated environments). Leveraging this, we introduce the D2P dataset, which contains challenging scenes outside the distribution of commonly used training data. We show that methods performing well under standard depth error metrics on existing benchmarks also perform well under our pose-based metric when evaluated on the same datasets, but do not necessarily generalize to our more challenging dataset. Finally, we provide a simple and extensible evaluation framework. The dataset and code are available at kocurvik.github.io/depth2pose.

</details>


### [91] [Synergistic Foundation Models for Semi-Supervised Fetal Cardiac Ultrasound Analysis: SAM-Med2D Boundary Refinement and DINOv3 Semantic Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19799)
*Tonghao Zhuang,Shanglong Hu,Yongsheng Luo,Zhiqi Zhang,Yu Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A semi-supervised framework for joint fetal ultrasound segmentation and classification improves CHD screening using a multi-task backbone, SAM-Med2D boundary refinement, and DINOv3 pseudo-labels, with a two-stage EMA + classification fine-tuning regime; achieves competitive metrics on FETUS 2026.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance accuracy of segmentation and classification in fetal echocardiography through semi-supervised learning and robust pseudo-labels to support prenatal congenital heart disease screening.

Method: Leverages EchoCare multi-task backbone with boundary refinement via SAM-Med2D, pseudo-label refinement via DINOv3, view-specific hard masking, and a two-stage optimization: (1) EMA phase to consolidate segmentation; (2) Classification Fine-Tuning that freezes segmentation and resets the classification head.

Result: Evaluation on the FETUS 2026 leaderboard shows Dice 79.99%, Normalized Surface Distance 61.62%, and F1-score 41.20% with public code available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: The approach effectively balances segmentation and classification for prenatal CHD screening, delivering notable segmentation gains while preserving or improving classification performance.

Abstract: We present a semi-supervised framework for joint segmentation and classification of fetal cardiac ultrasound images. Built upon the EchoCare multi-task backbone, our method integrates SAM-Med2D for boundary refinement and leverages DINOv3 to enhance pseudo-label quality. We introduce view-specific hard masking along with a two-stage optimization strategy: an EMA phase to consolidate segmentation capabilities, followed by a Classification Fine-Tuning phase that freezes segmentation parameters and resets the classification head to recover classification performance without compromising segmentation gains.
  Evaluated on the FETUS 2026 leaderboard, our method achieves a Dice Similarity Coefficient at 79.99%, Normalized Surface Distance at 61.62%, and F1-score at 41.20%, validating the effectiveness of our approach for prenatal congenital heart disease screening. Source code is publicly available at: https://github.com/2826056177/zcst_fetus2026.

</details>


### [92] [Stitched Value Model for Diffusion Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19804)
*Hyojun Go,Hyungjin Chung,Prune Truong,Goutam Bhat,Li Mi,Zhaochong An,Zixiang Zhao,Dominik Narnhofer,Serge Belongie,Federico Tombari,Konrad Schindler*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StitchVM stitches a pretrained pixel-space reward model with a frozen diffusion backbone to form a latency-time latent-space reward model, enabling efficient diffusion alignment by amortizing a latent-valued function—achieving substantial speedups and memory savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward models are typically trained on clean images, but alignment in diffusion models requires value estimates for noisy latents. Existing estimators trade bias (Tweedie) against computational cost (Monte Carlo), while learning a robust latent value model remains challenging. A lightweight method to leverage strong pixel-space rewards in latent space is highly desirable.

Method: StitchVM starts from a truncated pixel-space reward model and attaches a frozen diffusion backbone as its head, creating a hybrid that preserves pixel-space reward capability while leveraging diffusion’s latent-noise handling. The procedure is lightweight, enabling stitching of large models (e.g., CLIP ViT-L and SD 3.5 Medium) in about 10 GPU-hours.

Result: Empirical improvements include DPS being 3.2× faster with halved peak GPU memory and DiffusionNFT being 2.3× faster, validating the approach across multiple downstream steering and post-training methods.

Conclusion: Lifting pixel-space reward models to latent diffusion space enables a new, efficient diffusion alignment paradigm: construct the correct latent-valued function once and amortize it over many samples, reducing per-sample estimation costs while preserving reward fidelity.

Abstract: For practical use, diffusion- or flow-based generative models must be aligned with task-specific rewards, such as prompt fidelity or aesthetic preference. That alignment is challenging because the reward is defined for clean output images, but the alignment procedure requires value function estimates at noisy intermediate latents. Existing methods resort to Tweedie-style or Monte Carlo approximations, trading off estimator bias against computational cost: Tweedie estimates are efficient but biased, while Monte Carlo estimates are more accurate but require expensive rollouts. A natural alternative would be a learned value function, but it remains an open question how to effectively train a strong and general value model specifically for noisy latents. Here, we propose StitchVM, a model stitching framework that efficiently transfers reward models pretrained for clean images to the noisy latent regime. StitchVM starts from an existing, truncated pixel-space reward model and attaches a frozen diffusion backbone to it as its head. From the pixel-space model, the resulting hybrid retains a carefully pretrained, robust reward capability; from the diffusion backbone, it inherits its native ability to handle noisy latents. The stitching procedure is exceptionally lightweight, e.g., stitching and finetuning CLIP ViT-L and SD 3.5 Medium takes only 10 GPU-hours. By lifting powerful pixel-space reward models to latent space, StitchVM opens up a new style of diffusion alignment: instead of rough, yet costly per-sample approximation of the value function, the correct function for the actual, noisy latents is constructed once and then amortized over many samples and iterations. We show that this approach yields improvements across a broad range of downstream steering and post-training methods: DPS becomes $3.2\times$ faster while halving peak GPU memory, and DiffusionNFT becomes $2.3\times$ faster.

</details>


### [93] [LaCoVL-FER: Landmark-Guided Contrastive Learning Network with Vision-Language Enhancement for Facial Expression Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19821)
*Jiaxin Wang,Muwei Jian,Hui Yu,Junyu Dong,Yifan Xia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LaCoVL-FER: a landmark-guided contrastive learning network with vision-language enhancement for facial expression recognition in-the-wild, integrating geometric priors from facial landmarks and semantic priors from a vision-language model (CLIP) to achieve state-of-the-art results on RAF-DB, FERPlus, and AffectNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FER in-the-wild remains challenging due to uncontrolled pose, occlusion, and illumination; existing attention-based methods rely mainly on appearance cues, leading to attention redundancy and instability.

Method: Introduce Landmark-Guided Adaptive Encoder (LGAE) with Bi-branch Gated Cross Attention (BGCA) to fuse landmark-based geometry and visual features; Vision-Language Enhancement Strategy (VLES) to refine frozen CLIP image features using expression-relevant signals; Expression-Conditioned Prompting (ECP) to adapt fixed CLIP text prompts into instance-specific prompts; align visual and textual representations as semantic priors.

Result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance on RAF-DB, FERPlus, and AffectNet; code released at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: Demonstrates improved FER robustness and generalization by combining geometric priors with vision-language priors, yielding more discriminative, attention-stable representations and better cross-domain performance.

Abstract: Facial Expression Recognition (FER) in the wild is still challenging due to uncontrolled variations in pose, occlusion, and illumination. Most existing attention-based methods primarily rely on visual appearance cues, suffering from attention redundancy and instability, which limits their performance in complex scenarios. To address these issues, we propose a novel landmark-guided contrastive learning network with vision-language enhancement for FER (LaCoVL-FER), which integrates geometric priors from facial landmarks and semantic priors from a vision-language model. Specifically, a Landmark-Guided Adaptive Encoder (LGAE) is designed to introduce geometric priors through a Bi-branch Gated Cross Attention (BGCA) mechanism, which achieves adaptive fusion of landmark-based geometric and visual appearance features to produce expression-relevant features, thereby focusing on key facial regions and suppressing noise interference. In parallel, a Vision-Language Enhancement Strategy (VLES) is presented to leverage the expression-relevant features to refine the generalizable visual features extracted by the frozen pretrained CLIP image encoder, yielding expression-specific visual representations. Based on these representations, an Expression-Conditioned Prompting (ECP) mechanism is utilized to further adapt the textual features of fixed class-level prompts from the frozen pretrained CLIP text encoder, generating more instance-aware textual representations. These visual-textual representations are aligned as semantic priors to enhance the robustness and generalization of FER. Quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that our LaCoVL-FER outperforms state-of-the-art methods on three representative real-world FER datasets, including RAF-DB, FERPlus, and AffectNet. The code is available at https://github.com/ylin06804/LaCoVL-FER.

</details>


### [94] [When Preference Labels Fall Short: Aligning Diffusion Models from Real Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19839)
*Weiyan Chen,Weijian Deng,Yao Xiao,Weijie Tu,ZiYi Dong,Ibrahim Radwan,Liang Lin,Pengxu Wei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Real-data supervision as a data-centric alternative for preference alignment in diffusion models by contrasting real images with generated/perturbed samples, achieving performance comparable to manual preference pairs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the ambiguity and limited signal of preference data derived from model-generated samples by leveraging real data as a stable reference for alignment.

Method: Construct preference signals by contrasting real images with generated or perturbed samples, treating real data as reference points; does not rely on manually annotated preference pairs; empirical evaluation on diffusion-model alignment.

Result: Real-data supervision effectively guides diffusion-model alignment and achieves performance comparable to existing preference-based methods; demonstrates practicality and complements existing approaches; code and models are provided.

Conclusion: Real data offers a practical, complementary source of supervision for preference alignment and suggests directions for label-efficient alignment strategies.

Abstract: Preference alignment aims to guide generative models by learning from comparisons between preferred and non-preferred samples. In practice, most existing approaches rely on preference pairs constructed from model-generated images. Such supervision is inherently relative and can be ambiguous when both samples exhibit artifacts or limited visual quality, making it difficult to infer what constitutes a truly desirable output. In this work, we investigate whether real data can serve as an alternative source of supervision for preference alignment. We adopt a data-centric perspective and study a curation strategy that treats real images as reference points and constructs preference signals by contrasting them with generated or perturbed samples, without requiring manually annotated preference pairs. Through empirical analysis, we show that real-data-based supervision provides effective guidance for aligning diffusion models and achieves performance comparable to existing preference-based methods. Our results suggest that real data offers a practical and complementary source of supervision for preference alignment and highlight directions of label-efficient alignment strategies. Code and models are available at https://cwyxx.github.io/RealAlign.

</details>


### [95] [FineBench: Benchmarking and Enhancing Vision-Language Models for Fine-grained Human Activity Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19846)
*Gueter Josmy Faure,Min-Hung Chen,Jia-Fong Yeh,Hung-Ting Su,Winston H. Hsu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces FineBench, a large-scale, long-form, fine-grained human-centric VQA benchmark; shows open VLMs lag behind GPT-5, especially in spatial reasoning; proposes FineAgent (Localizer + Descriptor) to boost open VLMs; FineBench provides a rigorous testbed and FineAgent a practical enhancement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current human-centric benchmarks fail to combine long-form videos, dense QA coverage, and frame-level spatial/temporal grounding at scale, hindering evaluation of fine-grained action understanding in VLMs.

Method: Create FineBench with 199,420 multiple-choice QA pairs across 64 long-form videos (≈15 min each), focusing on detailed person movement, interaction, and object manipulation; evaluate GPT-5 and open VLMs; propose FineAgent, a modular framework using a Localizer to locate relevant regions and a Descriptor to provide detailed scene/action representations to improve reasoning.

Result: Open VLMs underperform compared to GPT-5, with particular weakness in spatial reasoning in multi-person scenes and discerning subtle human movements; FineAgent consistently improves performance of various open VLMs on FineBench.

Conclusion: FineBench offers a rigorous, scalable testbed for fine-grained, human-centric video understanding; FineAgent provides a practical enhancement to current VLMs to address deficits in fine-grained reasoning.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in general video understanding, yet they often struggle with the fine-grained comprehension crucial for real-world applications requiring nuanced interpretation of human actions and interactions. While some recent human-centric benchmarks evaluate aspects of model behaviour such as fairness/ethics, emotion perception, and broader human-centric metrics, they do not combine long-form videos, very dense QA coverage, and frame-level spatial/temporal grounding at scale. To bridge this gap, we introduce FineBench, a human-centric video question answering (VQA) benchmark specifically designed to assess fine-grained understanding. FineBench comprises 199,420 multiple-choice QA pairs densely annotated across 64 long-form videos (15 minutes each), focusing on detailed person movement, person interaction, and object manipulation, including compositional actions. Our extensive evaluation reveals that while proprietary models like GPT-5 achieve respectable performance, current open-source VLMs significantly underperform, struggling particularly with spatial reasoning in multi-person scenes and distinguishing subtle differences in human movements and interactions. To address these identified weaknesses, we propose FineAgent, a modular framework that enhances VLMs by leveraging a Localizer and a Descriptor. Experiments show that FineAgent consistently improves the performance of various open VLMs on FineBench. FineBench provides a rigorous testbed for future research into fine-grained human-centric video understanding, while FineAgent offers a practical approach to enhance such reasoning in current VLMs.

</details>


### [96] [A Framework for Evaluating Zero-Shot Image Generation in Concept-based Explainability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19855)
*Giacomo Astolfi,Matteo Bianchi,Riccardo Campi,Antonio De Santis,Marco Brambilla*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Explores zero-shot Text-to-Image generation as a source of synthetic concept datasets for concept-based XAI, with a four-part evaluation of faithfulness to real concepts and impact on downstream explanations; finds notable challenges and calls for further work; dataset released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on large labeled concept datasets in concept-based XAI by leveraging synthetic data from zero-shot T2I models to cover more concepts and enable scalable explanations.

Method: Generate concepts using predefined prompts with zero-shot T2I models. Evaluate faithfulness via four analyses: (1) compare synthetic vs real concept images using concept representation similarity; (2) assess intra-similarity by comparing pairs of subsets of the same concept as subset size grows; (3) test downstream explanation performance on relevant class images; (4) examine how removing a concept from tested class images alters explanations of generated concepts.

Result: Demonstrates both potential and limitations of using synthetic zero-shot data for concept-based XAI; indicates challenges remain for faithfulness and transferability; contributes a dataset for further study (dataset link provided).

Conclusion: Zero-shot T2I generation offers promise for scalable concept-based XAI but faces significant challenges in faithfully representing real concepts and ensuring reliable explanations; careful evaluation, prompt design, and possibly multi-model or hybrid approaches are needed.

Abstract: Concept-based Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) interprets deep learning models using human-understandable visual features (e.g., textures or object parts) by linking internal representations to class predictions, thereby bridging the gap between low-level image data and high-level semantics. A major challenge, however, is the reliance on large sets of labeled images to represent each concept, which limits scalability. In this work, we investigate the use of zero-shot Text-to-Image (T2I) generative models as a source of synthetic concept datasets for concept-based XAI methods. Specifically, we generate concepts using predefined prompts and evaluate their faithfulness to real ones through four complementary analyses: (1) comparing synthetic vs. real concept images via concept representation similarity; (2) evaluating their intra-similarity by comparing pairs of subsets of the same concept with progressively increasing size; (3) evaluating their performance for downstream explanation tasks using relevant class images; (4) evaluating how removing a concept from tested class images affects explanations of generated concepts. While current T2I generative models promise a shortcut to concept-based XAI, our study highlights challenges and raises open questions about the use of synthetic data generated by zero-shot pipelines in model analyses. The resulting dataset is available at https://github.com/DataSciencePolimi/ZeroShot-T2I-Concepts.

</details>


### [97] [Eyes on VLM: Benchmarking Gaze Following and Social Gaze Prediction in Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19859)
*Hengfei Wang,Anshul Gupta,Pierre Vuillecard,Jean-Marc Odobez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EyeVLM presents a framework to evaluate gaze understanding in vision-language models across zero-shot and fine-tuned settings, finding current VLMs lack precise gaze comprehension and require further improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gaze understanding is central to human behavior analysis and requires reasoning about faces, attention, 3D scene structure, and social context; evaluating VLMs clarifies their cross-modal reasoning capabilities beyond static vision.

Method: EyeVLM assesses two tasks (gaze following and social gaze prediction) on multiple VLMs in zero-shot and fine-tuned regimes, using prompting strategies and QA-based fine-tuning; benchmarks against existing gaze datasets and compares with purely visual models.

Result: Current VLMs exhibit weak precise gaze understanding; zero-shot performance is limited; fine-tuning with task-specific QA and model scale reduce the gap but do not close it; social-gaze reasoning benefits from semantic components, yet overall performance lags behind dedicated visual models.

Conclusion: Gaze understanding remains challenging for VLMs; while training and prompting improve performance, substantial gains and data-driven improvements are required before VLMs can reliably interpret gaze and social attention.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have rapidly evolved into general-purpose multimodal reasoners with strong zero-shot generalization. In this context, VLMs could greatly benefit the analysis of human gaze and attention, a central task in human behavior understanding that requires reasoning about the physical scene as well as the activity, interactions, and social context. However, the extent to which VLMs can reliably understand human gaze and related attentional behaviors remains largely unexplored. In this work, we present EyeVLM, a systematic evaluation framework for gaze understanding in VLMs across two complementary dimensions: tasks and models. To assess gaze understanding capabilities, we focus on two core tasks. The first, gaze following, i.e., predicting the 2D location where a person is looking, has a geometric and visual processing focus, requiring a precise understanding of the human face, attention direction, 3D scene structure, and spatial grounding of attended targets. The second, social gaze prediction, requires social and relational reasoning over multi-person interactions (e.g., mutual gaze and shared attention), and may benefit more from the LLM semantic reasoning capabilities within VLMs. Regarding models, EyeVLM evaluates these tasks in two ways: a zero-shot setting with a diverse set of state-of-the-art open- and closed-source VLMs, exploring different prompting strategies; and a fine-tuning approach based on task-specific QA pairs, studying the impact of model scale and data scale. As benchmarks, we rely on existing gaze understanding datasets and perform a systematic comparison with state-of-the-art purely visual models. Overall, our results show that current VLMs lack precise gaze understanding capabilities. While standard training helps reduce the gap with visual models, significant improvements are still needed.

</details>


### [98] [Landscape-Awareness for Geometric View Diffusion Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19865)
*Yan-Ting Chen,Hao-Wei Chen,Tsu-Ching Hsiao,Chun-Yi Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion/score-based refinement approach improves viewpoint estimation in sparse-view, two-view settings by reshaping the optimization landscape to avoid local minima, followed by a diffusion-model-based refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Viewpoint estimation from sparse views suffers from nonconvex loss surfaces and geometric ambiguities (symmetry/self-similarity), making optimization sensitive to initialization and prone to local minima.

Method: Introduce a score-based method that reshapes the optimization landscape to guide gradient updates toward the ground-truth viewpoint, then apply a viewpoint-conditioned diffusion-model refinement.

Result: Experiments show improved convergence, reduced dependence on brute-force sampling, and competitive accuracy with higher sample-efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed approach mitigates optimization pitfalls in sparse-view viewpoint estimation and leverages diffusion-based refinement to achieve reliable, sample-efficient results.

Abstract: Accurate camera viewpoint estimation under sparse-view conditions remains challenging, particularly in two-view scenarios. Recent approaches leverage diffusion models such as Zero123 to synthesize novel views conditioned on relative viewpoint, showing promising results when repurposed for viewpoint estimation via optimization with MSE loss. However, existing methods often suffer from nonconvex loss landscape with numerous local minima, making them sensitive to initialization and reliant on naive multistart strategies. We analyze these optimization challenges and visualize failure cases, showing that geometric ambiguities, such as symmetry and self-similarity, can mislead gradient-based updates toward incorrect viewpoints. To address these limitations, we propose a score-based method that reshapes the optimization landscape to guide updates toward the ground-truth viewpoint, followed by a refinement stage using a viewpoint-conditioned diffusion model. Experiments show that our method improves convergence, reduces reliance on brute-force sampling, and achieves competitive accuracy with higher sample-efficiency.

</details>


### [99] [Structured Layout Priors for Robust Out-of-Distribution Visual Document Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19866)
*Peter El Hachem,Ahmed Nassar,A. Said Gurbuz,Christoph Auer,Peter W. J. Staar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight Hop-1 layout detector is injected into the VLM prompt to mitigate a two-hop bottleneck in document parsing, improving robustness across benchmarks with modest latency overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs struggle with layouts outside training distributions due to a two-hop bottleneck: Hop 1 (layout classification/localization) and Hop 2 (content extraction). Failures in Hop 1 cause omissions, malformed structures, or repetition in Hop 2.

Method: Pre-resolve Hop-1 with a lightweight RT-DETR detector, serialize outputs into the parser's DocTags vocabulary, and inject them into the prompt alongside the full-page image. No cropping or plain-text priors; the global image remains as a fallback. Compare with analyze-then-parse baselines and analyze attention to validate two-hop bottleneck alleviation.

Result: Significant gains across benchmarks: markdown F1 from 0.37 to 0.92; OmniDocBench Chinese subset table TEDS from 0.01 to 0.36; ViDoRe V3 decoding failures reduced across domains. Latency increases ~15% with a median of 74 extra prompt tokens. No architectural changes to the base VLM; model weights will be released.

Conclusion: Resolving Hop-1 outside the decoder via injected layout tokens alleviates the two-hop bottleneck, enabling better structure and content extraction without retraining; maintains full-image context as fallback and shows broad robustness improvements.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) parse documents end-to-end but frequently break down on layouts unlike those seen in training. We attribute this to a two-hop bottleneck: before the decoder can extract content (Hop 2), it must first classify and localize the enclosing layout entity (Hop 1), and when the first hop fails the second collapses into omissions, malformed structure, or autoregressive repetition. We pre-resolve Hop 1 outside the decoder by running a lightweight RT-DETR detector, serializing its outputs in the parser's native DocTags vocabulary, and injecting them into the prompt alongside the full page image. Unlike analyze-then-parse approaches that crop the page, or prior prompt-level priors written in plain text, our prior shares the decoder's generation space and leaves the global image in view as a fallback when detections are noisy. On a 10k-page structural out-of-distribution benchmark, markdown F1 rises from $0.37$ to $0.92$; on the Chinese subset of OmniDocBench, table TEDS rises from $0.01$ to $0.36$; and on the 26k-page ViDoRe V3 benchmark, infinite-loop decoding failures drop across every industrial domain tested. These gains cost $15\%$ wall-clock latency and a median of $74$ prompt tokens, with no architectural change to the base VLM. An attention-level analysis further reveals a bimodal phase shift in which the decoder attends to injected layout tokens when emitting structure and to image patches when emitting content, consistent with the two-hop bottleneck being alleviated. Model weights will be released to support reproducibility.

</details>


### [100] [WoundFormer: Multi-Scale Spatial Feature Fusion for Multi-Class Wound Tissue Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19868)
*Muhammad Ashad Kabir,Rabin Dulal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WoundFormer is a transformer-based framework with a spatially-preserving multi-scale head that improves multi-class wound tissue segmentation, outperforming baselines on WoundTissueSeg and DFUTissue datasets, achieving 81.9% Dice on WoundTissueSeg.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chronic wounds exhibit heterogeneous tissue composition with high intra-class variability and limited annotated data. Multi-class segmentation is necessary for accurate assessment; existing methods struggle with boundary localization and distinguishing similar tissue classes.

Method: Replace the SegFormer decoder with a spatially-preserving multi-scale aggregation head that preserves feature topology during cross-scale fusion and uses convolutional fusion to strengthen contextual interactions, preserving transformer efficiency.

Result: Achieves an overall Dice score of 81.9% on the WoundTissueSeg benchmark (147 images, six classes), outperforming strong CNN- and transformer-based baselines by up to 4.3 Dice points, with consistent gains on minority tissue classes.

Conclusion: Explicit modeling of hierarchical spatial interactions enhances transformer representations for heterogeneous wound tissue segmentation and supports more reliable quantitative wound assessment.

Abstract: Chronic wounds such as diabetic foot ulcers and pressure injuries require accurate tissue-level assessment to guide treatment planning and monitor healing progression. While deep learning methods have advanced automated wound analysis, most existing approaches focus on binary segmentation and inadequately model heterogeneous tissue composition due to high intra-class variability and limited annotated data. Multi-class wound tissue segmentation, therefore, remains a challenging and clinically relevant problem. We propose WoundFormer, a transformer-based framework that enhances hierarchical spatial feature fusion for multi-class wound tissue segmentation. Specifically, we replace the standard SegFormer decoder with a spatially-preserving multi-scale aggregation head that maintains feature topology during cross-scale integration and strengthens contextual interactions through convolutional fusion. This design improves boundary localization and discrimination between visually similar tissue categories while preserving transformer efficiency. We evaluate WoundFormer on the WoundTissueSeg dataset (147 images, six tissue classes) and a second benchmark (DFUTissue dataset). The proposed method achieves an overall Dice score of 81.9%, outperforming strong CNN- and transformer-based baselines by up to 4.3 Dice points on the WoundTissueSeg benchmark, with consistent improvements across minority tissue classes. These results indicate that explicit modeling of hierarchical spatial interactions enhances transformer representations for heterogeneous wound tissue segmentation and supports more reliable quantitative wound assessment.

</details>


### [101] [Passive Construction Site Safety Monitoring via Persona-Scaffolded Adversarial Chain-of-Thought VLM Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19869)
*Ananth Sriram,Neel Mokaria,Rajveer Singh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Three-stage end-of-shift construction safety monitoring pipeline using vision models (YOLO11, SAM3, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct) with a novel Stage-3 adversarial, persona-guided prompting; 12% precision gain; maps to OSHA and REBA-like scoring; evaluation harness released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Construction has a high fatality rate and current monitoring is costly, real-time heavy, or narrow. A passive, scalable end-of-shift monitoring solution with robust hallucination control is needed.

Method: Stage 1Fine-tuned YOLO11 for primary PPE and hazard detection. Stage 2 SAM3 for segmentation refinement and worker deduplication. Stage 3 Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct with a method-prompted, persona-scaffolded three-pass adversarial chain-of-thought protocol for compliance verification and hallucination control; structural message isolation; mapping violations to OSHA standards; REBA-inspired ergonomic risk scoring from pose keypoints; per-worker safety reports with timestamps.

Result: 12% precision improvement over single-pass prompting in informal three-author review of a 12-video Ironsite corpus; largest gains on hallucination-prone violation categories; evaluation harness released.

Conclusion: Stage-3 persona-scaffolded prompting combined with architectural isolation yields improved reliability and reduced hallucinations, enabling audit-ready safety reporting; framework suitable for future replication and external evaluation.

Abstract: Construction remains the deadliest industry sector in the United States, with 1,055 fatal worker injuries recorded in 2023, and the majority preventable. Existing monitoring approaches are expensive, require real-time human operators, or address only a narrow subset of violations. This paper presents a passive, end-of-shift construction safety monitoring pipeline processing video from POV body-worn and fixed wall-mounted cameras through a three-stage architecture: (1) fine-tuned YOLO11 for primary PPE and hazard detection, (2) SAM 3 for segmentation refinement and worker deduplication, and (3) Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct with a method-prompted, persona-scaffolded three-pass adversarial chain-of-thought protocol for compliance verification and hallucination control. The principal contribution is the Stage 3 prompt design: professional persona backstories following the method-actor framing drive an observed 12% precision improvement over single-pass prompting in an informal three-author review of the 12-video Ironsite development corpus, with the largest gains on hallucination-prone violation categories. Structural message isolation enforces observational independence between a generator, discriminator, and reconciliation pass governed by asymmetric rules encoding priors about human observation versus automated detection reliability. The system maps violations to OSHA standards, performs REBA-inspired ergonomic risk scoring from pose keypoints, and produces per-worker safety reports with timestamped evidence. An evaluation harness is released for future reproduction.

</details>


### [102] [Structural Energy Guidance for View-Consistent Text-to-3D Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19876)
*Qing Zhang,Jinguang Tong,Jing Zhang,Jie Hong,Xuesong Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free, plug-and-play approach SEGS injects a structural energy gradient in the PCA subspace of U-Net features to guide diffusion sampling, improving multi-view consistency in text-to-3D and reducing Janus artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
