Motivation: Viewpoint bias in 2D diffusion priors causes inconsistent geometry (Janus problem) across views; a method to enforce 3D consistency without retraining is needed.

Method: Construct a structural energy in the PCA subspace of U-Net features during denoising, and inject its gradient into the sampling process of SDS/VSD pipelines.

Result: ≈10% reduction in Janus rate on average; improved View-CS scores across multiple baselines (DreamFusion, Magic3D, LucidDreamer); preserves appearance fidelity; plug-and-play without retraining.

Conclusion: SEGS provides a flexible, training-free solution to mitigate viewpoint artifacts in text-to-3D diffusion generation while retaining quality, and is readily integrable into existing pipelines.

Abstract: Text-to-3D generation based on diffusion models often suffers from the Janus problem, leading to inconsistent geometry across viewpoints. This work identifies viewpoint bias in 2D diffusion priors as the main cause and proposes Structural Energy-Guided Sampling (SEGS), a training-free and plug-and-play framework to improve multi-view consistency. SEGS constructs a structural energy in the PCA subspace of U-Net features and injects its gradient into the denoising process. It can be easily integrated into SDS/VSD pipelines without retraining. Experiments show that SEGS reduces the Janus Rate by about 10% on average and improves View-CS scores across multiple baselines, including DreamFusion, Magic3D, and LucidDreamer. This method effectively alleviates viewpoint artifacts while preserving appearance fidelity, providing a flexible solution for high-quality text-to-3D content generation.

</details>


### [103] [GoTTA be Diverse: Rethinking Memory Policies for Test-Time Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19890)
*Shyma Alhuwaider,Yasmeen Alsaedy,Merey Ramazanova,Silvio Giancola,Bernard Ghanem*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Decouples memory design from TTA algorithms in a systematic benchmark, showing intra-class diversity is key; introduces GOTTA, diversity-aware memory policies; memory management yields benefits under limited budgets and non-i.i.d. streams and remains competitive with larger buffers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memory handling in test-time adaptation is usually bundled with a specific algorithm, making it hard to identify which memory design choices matter. A unified benchmark is needed to isolate the impact of memory across i.i.d., non-i.i.d., continual, and practical streams.

Method: Create a unified benchmark that evaluates memory policies independently of adaptation objectives across diverse test streams (i.i.d., non-i.i.d., continual, practical). Propose GOTTA, a family of diversity-aware memory policies combining class-balanced allocation with feature-space diversity, usable as drop-in buffers with various TTA objectives. Evaluate on corruption benchmarks and video streams.

Result: Diversity-aware memory policies improve adaptation under constrained memory and non-i.i.d. streams, more clearly than when memory size increases; GOTTA can match or exceed baseline memory performance while using the same budgets. Diversity is identified as a central principle for robust TTA in practical settings.

Conclusion: Memory management should be treated as a first-class component of robust TTA. Incorporating diversity (via GOTTA) yields more representative adaptation signals and reduces redundancy, especially under budget limits and temporally correlated, skewed data.

Abstract: Test-time adaptation (TTA) enables a pre-trained model to adapt online to an unlabeled test stream under distribution shift. While most TTA research focuses on the adaptation objective, practical streams also depend critically on the memory used to select which test samples drive adaptation. Existing memory mechanisms are usually evaluated as components of specific TTA algorithms, making it difficult to isolate which memory design choices matter and when they matter. In this work, we provide a systematic benchmark that decouples memory from the adaptation algorithm and evaluates memory policies under unified conditions across i.i.d., non-i.i.d., continual, and practical test streams. Our study shows that effective memory management requires more than retaining recent or class-balanced samples. In particular, intra-class diversity is a key factor for avoiding redundant buffers and maintaining representative adaptation signals under temporally correlated and label-skewed streams. Motivated by this finding, we introduce Guided Observational Test-Time Adaptation (GOTTA), a family of diversity-aware memory policies that combine class-balanced allocation with feature-space diversity. GOTTA memories act as drop-in replacements for existing buffers and can be paired with different TTA objectives. Across corruption benchmarks and video-stream settings, diversity-aware memory improves adaptation most clearly under constrained memory budgets and challenging non-i.i.d. streams, while remaining competitive as memory capacity increases. These results highlight memory management as a first-class component of robust test-time adaptation and identify diversity as a central principle for practical TTA.

</details>


### [104] [Breaking Modality Heterogeneity in Low-Bit Quantization for Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19929)
*Yi Zhong,Haotong Qin,Xindong Zhang,Lei Zhang,Guolei Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SplitQ is a channel-splitting post-training quantization framework for Vision-Language Models that decouples modality-specific outlier channels and adaptively calibrates cross-modal distributions, achieving strong low-bit quantization performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-modal activation distribution heterogeneity during quantization degrades VLM accuracy. Outliers are concentrated in a small subset of channels and differ across modalities, making uniform quantization ineffective.

Method: MOCD isolates salient modality-specific outlier channels with minimal overhead. ACC uses dual lightweight learnable branches to adaptively mitigate cross-modal quantization errors. The approach is channel-splitting-driven to address cross-modal distribution discrepancies.

Result: Extensive experiments on popular VLMs across 6 multi-modal datasets show SplitQ consistently outperforms existing methods under W4A8, W4A4, W3A3, and W3A2. It preserves 93.5% of FP16 performance under W3A3 (69.5 vs. 74.3).

Conclusion: SplitQ advances the efficiency frontier for deploying advanced VLMs with low-bit PTQ. Code is available at the authors’ GitHub repository.

Abstract: Low-bit post-training quantization (PTQ) is a pivotal technique for deploying Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on resource-constrained devices. However, existing PTQ methods often degrade VLMs' accuracy due to the heterogeneous activation distributions of text and vision modalities during quantization. We find that this cross-modal heterogeneity is distributed unevenly across channels: a small subset of channels contains most modality-specific outliers, and these outliers typically reside in different channels for each modality. Motivated by this, we propose SplitQ, a channel-Splitting-driven post-training Quantization framework. At its core, SplitQ introduces a novel Modality-specific Outlier Channel Decoupling (MOCD) module that effectively isolates salient modality-specific outlier channels with minimal overhead. To further address the remaining cross-modal distribution discrepancies, we design an Adaptive Cross-Modal Calibration (ACC) module that employs dual lightweight learnable branches to dynamically mitigate modality-induced quantization errors. Extensive experiments on popular VLMs demonstrate that SplitQ significantly outperforms existing approaches across 6 popular multi-modal datasets under all evaluated quantization settings, including W4A8, W4A4, W3A3, and W3A2. Notably, SplitQ preserves 93.5% of FP16 performance under the challenging W3A3 setting (69.5 vs. 74.3), pushing the efficiency frontier for deploying advanced VLMs. Our code is available at https://github.com/EMVision-NK/SplitQ

</details>


### [105] [StruMPL: Multi-task Dense Regression under Disjoint Partial Supervision and MNAR Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19931)
*Reza M. Asiyabi,Juan Alberto Molina-Valero,The SEOSAW Partnership,Steven Hancock,Casey M. Ryan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: StruMPL is a multi-task, MNAR-aware deep learning framework that fuses spaceborne lidar and ground biomass plots to estimate forest AGB, using a shared encoder, MNAR correction via propensity/imputation heads, a physics module enforcing inter-task constraints, and an AIPW-based loss; it achieves lower RMSE and bias across biomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robust forest aboveground biomass (AGB) estimation from heterogeneous, structurally incompatible label sources (lidar-derived structure with no biomass labels and biased, MNAR field plots) by jointly modeling structure, biomass, and their physical relationship.

Method: A StruMPL architecture with a shared encoder feeding per-variable regression, imputation, and propensity heads to correct MNAR. Includes a learnable physics module enforcing biome-specific allometric constraints between structure and biomass. The supervised loss uses an Augmented IPW (AIPW) pseudo-outcome with stop-gradients on propensity and on the imputation baseline to ensure IPW-weighted stationary points and bounded loss.

Result: In two ecologically distinct biomes, StruMPL outperforms ablations and the closest published method on AGB RMSE and bias; stratified analysis shows AIPW reduces high-AGB bias by ~54%.

Conclusion: StruMPL provides a principled framework for integrating heterogeneous labels under MNAR and physics-based constraints, delivering robust AGB estimates with biased reduction via AIPW-based training.

Abstract: Estimating forest aboveground biomass (AGB) from Earth observation combines two structurally incompatible label sources: spaceborne lidar provides canopy structure at millions of locations but no biomass estimate, and ground-based plots provide biomass at thousands of biased locations but no metrics of structure. No single training sample carries labels for all target variables, plot labels are missing not at random (MNAR), and biomass is linked to the structural variables by known but biome-specific allometric laws. We formalise this as multi-task dense regression under heterogeneous disjoint partial supervision with MNAR labels and inter-task physical constraints, and propose StruMPL to address it jointly. A shared encoder feeds per-variable regression, imputation, and propensity heads for spatial MNAR correction, and a learnable physics module that evaluates the inter-task constraint on the model's own predictions at every pixel. The supervised loss uses an Augmented IPW (AIPW) pseudo-outcome with stop-gradients on the propensity and on the imputation baseline; we show analytically and empirically that both are necessary for joint optimisation to recover IPW-weighted stationary points while keeping the loss bounded. On two ecologically distinct biomes, StruMPL outperforms ablation variants and the closest published method on AGB RMSE and bias, with a stratified analysis showing AIPW reduces high-AGB bias by ~54%.

</details>


### [106] [Feed-Forward Gaussian Splatting from Sparse Aerial Views](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19949)
*Dongli Wu,Zhuoxiao Li,Tongyan Hua,Yinrui Ren,Xiaobao Wei,Rongjun Qin,Wufan Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AnyCity is an observation-grounded generative reconstruction framework that anchors geometry to observed data and uses scaffold-conditioned tokens with a diffusion prior to produce coherent 3D Gaussian reconstructions of sparse aerial urban scenes in a single feed-forward pass.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse aerial captures suffer from evidence imbalance: repeatedly observed roofs/open regions vs. under-constrained facades/occluded structures. Existing feed-forward or priors either mis-handle geometry or produce inconsistent content.

Method: A two-stage approach: (1) predict an observation-supported geometry latent to anchor reliable structures; (2) use scaffold-conditioned aerial completion tokens to predict a gated residual update for weakly constrained content, followed by Gaussian decoding. Training includes dense-to-sparse distillation from dense-view reconstructions and an aerial-adapted video diffusion prior for fine-grained appearance. Observation-preserving objectives enforce consistency with input-supported geometry. Inference is a single feed-forward pass.

Result: Experiments on synthetic, aerial-domain, UAV-textured, and real-world scenes show consistent improvements over feed-forward baselines, achieving coherent novel-view synthesis with second-level inference.

Conclusion: Observation-grounded geometry with diffusion-prior appearance cues enables coherent large-scale urban reconstructions from sparse aerial views, overcoming typical ghosting and texture distortions in prior methods.

Abstract: Reconstructing large-scale urban scenes from sparse aerial views is a crucial yet challenging task. Due to biased top-down and shallow-oblique camera poses, sparse aerial captures exhibit strong evidence imbalance: roofs and open regions are repeatedly observed, while facades, distant buildings, and occluded structures receive little multi-view support. Existing feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting methods directly regress a deterministic representation from sparse inputs, but this often leads to ghosting, melted facades, and stretched textures. Recent pseudo-view and video-based generative reconstruction methods use additional supervision or generative priors. However, they often lack a clear separation between observed geometry and prior-driven content, which can lead to plausible but inconsistent structures. We propose AnyCity, an observation-grounded generative reconstruction framework for sparse aerial urban scenes. AnyCity first predicts an observation-supported geometry latent to anchor reliable structures, and then uses scaffold-conditioned aerial completion tokens to predict a gated residual update for weakly constrained content before Gaussian decoding. During training, dense-to-sparse distillation transfers structural cues from dense-view reconstruction, while an aerial-adapted video diffusion prior provides fine-grained urban appearance cues through gated token conditioning. Observation-preserving objectives keep the refined representation consistent with input-supported geometry. At inference time, AnyCity reconstructs the final 3D Gaussian scene from sparse aerial views in a single feed-forward pass, achieving coherent urban novel-view synthesis with second-level inference. Experiments on synthetic, aerial-domain, UAV-textured, and real-world scenes show consistent improvements over feed-forward baselines.

</details>


### [107] [AffectVerse: Emotional World Models for Multimodal Affective Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19950)
*Bo Zhao,Fanghua Ye,Yixin Ji,Sicheng Zhao,Xiaojiang Peng,Zitong YU*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AffectVerse introduces an Emotion World Module (EWM) that uses temporal imagination and belief aggregation to predict short-horizon affective dynamics and injects these predictive beliefs into a language model, yielding consistent gains across nine benchmarks (~2.6%+).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of existing MLLMs that treat emotion recognition as static fusion over complete inputs, by explicitly modeling affective dynamics and future state expectations.

Method: AffectVerse uses a Qwen2.5-Omni backbone with an EWM consisting of (1) Cross-Modal Temporal Imagination to forecast future video/audio representations via multi-step rollouts from past tokens; (2) MAMA Belief Aggregation to compress imagined tokens into modality-aware belief tokens; (3) Belief Injection to introduce these tokens into the LLM for affective reasoning. Future prediction serves as a self-supervised signal conditioned on history, guiding current belief states without requiring unseen signals at inference.

Result: On nine benchmarks, AffectVerse achieves at least 2.57% improvement over comparable models. Ablations indicate additive gains from temporal imagination, cross-modal rollout, and belief aggregation.

Conclusion: Predictive belief-state modeling is a practical and effective approach for affective computing, enabling dynamic emotion reasoning by forecasting and leveraging future-conditioned beliefs without altering inference-time inputs.

Abstract: Humans infer emotions by integrating observed multimodal cues with expectations about how affective states may unfold. Existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs), however, often treat emotion recognition as static fusion over complete audiovisual-text inputs, leaving affective dynamics implicit. We propose AffectVerse, a Qwen2.5-Omni-based model equipped with an Emotion World Module (EWM), an action-free representation-level module for short-horizon latent affective prediction. \rev{EWM contains three modules: 1) Cross-Modal Temporal Imagination predicts future video/audio representations from past tokens with multi-step rollout. 2) MAMA(Modality-Aware Multi-step Attention) Belief Aggregation compresses imagined tokens into modality-aware belief tokens. 3) Belief Injection inserts these belief tokens into the LLM for affective reasoning.} AffectVerse uses future prediction as a past-conditioned self-supervised signal: it does not replace modeling observed history or require unseen signals at inference, but forces the current belief state to encode transition cues that are predictive of subsequent affective change. Across nine benchmarks, AffectVerse improves at least 2.57\% over other models, while controlled ablations show additive gains from temporal imagination, cross-modal rollout, and belief aggregation. These results suggest predictive belief-state modeling is a practical alternative for affective computing.

</details>


### [108] [Towards Fine-Grained Robustness: Attention-Guided Test-Time Prompt Tuning for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19956)
*Jia-Wei Hai,Yijun Wang,Xiu-Shen Wei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A-TPT is a semantics-preserving test-time prompt tuning method for Vision-Language Models that uses refined gradient attention rollout to locate semantically meaningful regions surviving adversarial attacks, then guides spatially varying augmentations and a multi-view ensemble for prompt tuning and inference, improving performance on both adversarial and clean data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current test-time adaptation (TTA) for VLMs relies heavily on multi-view augmentation, which can miss semantic information and can disrupt discriminative regions in fine-grained tasks, especially under adversarial attacks. A method that preserves semantics during TTA is needed to improve robustness and reliability.

Method: Refine gradient attention rollout to identify semantically meaningful regions that survive adversarial perturbations, then use these regions to guide spatially varying augmentation intensities and employ a multi-view ensemble for prompt tuning and inference.

Result: Extensive experiments show that A-TPT outperforms existing test-time adaptation methods on both adversarial and clean data.

Conclusion: A-TPT provides a semantics-preserving approach for test-time adaptation in VLMs by leveraging attention-guided region identification to steer augmentation and ensemble-based prompt tuning, enhancing robustness without sacrificing performance on clean data.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs), such as CLIP, have achieved significant zero-shot performance on downstream tasks with various fine-tuning adaptation methods. However, recent studies have proven that adversarial attacks can significantly degrade the inference ability of VLMs, posing substantial risks to their practical applications. Prevalent test-time adaptation methods typically rely on multi-view augmentation to implement various fine-tuning strategies, which struggle to identify semantic information and are prone to destroying discriminative regions in fine-grained scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose Attention-Guided Test-Time Prompt Tuning (A-TPT), a semantics-preserving method designed for test-time adaptation. We first refine the gradient attention rollout mechanism to identify semantically meaningful regions surviving under adversarial attacks. Furthermore, we leverage them to guide the spatially varying augmentation intensities and multi-view ensemble for prompt tuning and inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that A-TPT outperforms existing test-time adaptation methods on both adversarial and clean data. Codes are available at https://github.com/SEU-VIPGroup/A-TPT .

</details>


### [109] [SphericalDreamer: Generating Navigable Immersive 3D Worlds with Panorama Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19974)
*Antoine Schnepf,Karim Kassab,Flavian Vasile,Andrew Comport*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SphericalDreamer generates long-range, immersive 3D outdoor environments from text prompts by creating multiple panoramas, lifting them to 3D, and fusing them into a coherent whole.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods struggle to produce 3D worlds that are both navigable over large spatial extents and cover the full 360°×180° field of view, limiting immersion and scalability.

Method: Generate several panoramic images from a textual prompt, lift each panorama into a 3D representation, and fuse the views while preserving visual and geometric consistency to create a seamless, navigable 3D environment.

Result: The approach yields highly detailed, fully immersive 3D environments with improved scale and navigability compared to prior methods.

Conclusion: SphericalDreamer offers a practical pathway to fully immersive, long-range 3D outdoor worlds from text, enabling scalable navigation and 360°-We view consistency.

Abstract: The generation of immersive and navigable 3D environments is increasingly prevalent with the growing adoption of virtual reality and 3D content. However, recent methods face a fundamental limitation: they cannot produce 3D worlds that simultaneously (i) are navigable over long-range spatial extents and (ii) cover the complete omnidirectional field of view ($360^\circ$ horizontally and $180^\circ$ vertically). To address this challenge, we introduce SphericalDreamer, a method for generating fully immersive and long-range 3D outdoor environments from textual prompts. Our approach is built on the generation of multiple panoramic images, which are subsequently lifted into 3D and fused together while maintaining visual and geometric consistency. SphericalDreamer produces highly detailed, fully immersive 3D environments, while substantially improving scale and navigability compared to prior approaches.

</details>


### [110] [RECIPE: Procedural Planning via Grounding in Instructional Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19976)
*Luigi Seminara,Antonino Furnari,Lorenzo Torresani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RECIPE uses a verifier-based RL framework to convert noisy, large-scale instructional video corpora into scalable, weak supervision for visual planning, yielding robust gains across models and benchmarks without relying on clean manual labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Annotation bottlenecks in visual planning: small, single-trajectory, domain-limited datasets vs. abundant noisy ASR from large video corpora. Verifying generated step sequences is cheaper and scalable via text embeddings, enabling scale without requiring clean step labels.

Method: RECIPE trains a planner using a grounding quality reward (GRPO) derived from how well a generated step sequence is grounded in ASR transcripts. It treats the ASR-grounded verification as a verifier rather than a label source, and supports two planner inputs (Socratic with textual history from a frozen VLM, and Video consuming video tokens) in annotated or weakly supervised regimes. Evaluation uses a reference-based LLM-as-judge protocol across 7 benchmarks and 6 procedural criteria; tested at scales 0.5B, 3B, 7B.

Result: RECIPE-RL improves over base checkpoints across all scales and benchmarks, with macro-accuracy gains of around +7 to +8 in-domain and up to +16 zero-shot. It outperforms supervised fine-tuning on both annotated and pseudo-labeled plans and remains robust without human annotations. It also enhances a prior propose–assess–search planner, preserving diversity on COIN where SFT collapses.

Conclusion: Turning noisy video data into a scalable verifier enables robust, scalable visual planning without clean labels. RECIPE demonstrates strong cross-scale, cross-benchmark gains and suggests broad applicability of verifier-based RL for procedural tasks, while maintaining diversity and compatibility with multiple planner architectures.

Abstract: Visual planning asks a model to generate the remaining steps of a procedure in natural language given a partial video context and a goal. Progress on this task is bottlenecked by annotation: clean labeled datasets are small, domain-narrow, and encode a single execution trajectory per example, even though many valid orderings exist. Large-scale instructional video corpora offer orders of magnitude more procedural content, but supervised fine-tuning on pseudo-labels from their noisy ASR narrations propagates segmentation and alignment errors and stays single-trajectory. We identify a key asymmetry: extracting clean step labels from noisy video is hard, but verifying whether a generated step sequence is temporally grounded in ASR transcripts is cheap and scales to millions of videos via precomputed text embeddings. We exploit this asymmetry in RECIPE, which uses grounding quality as a reward for GRPO, turning the noisy corpus into a verifier rather than a label source. The framework applies uniformly to two planner input configurations (Socratic, with a textual history extracted by a frozen VLM, and Video, consuming video tokens directly) and to annotated and weakly supervised regimes. We evaluate on 7 procedural benchmarks using a reference-based LLM-as-judge protocol scoring plans across 6 procedural criteria. RECIPE-RL improves over the base checkpoint at all scales (0.5B, 3B, 7B) and every benchmark, with macro-accuracy gains of +7 to +8 points in-domain and up to +16 points zero-shot. It outperforms supervised fine-tuning on both annotated and pseudo-labeled plans (the latter degrades the base) and remains robust without human annotations. Used as the proposal stage of a prior propose-assess-search planner, it improves over the strongest zero-shot baseline at every horizon on Visual Planning for Assistance, and on COIN it preserves the generation diversity that SFT collapses.

</details>


### [111] [InterLight: Leveraging Intrinsic Illumination Priors for Low-Light Image Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19982)
*Ziqi Wang,Xu Zhang,Laibin Chang,Shi Chen,Jiaqi Ma,Huan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InterLight is a new LLIE framework that leverages intrinsic illumination priors via physics-guided augmentation and adaptive, illumination-conditioned prompts, coupled with a luminance-gated intrinsic memory and self-supervised consistency, to achieve clearer textures and coherent enhancement across benchmarks; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-Light Image Enhancement often suffers from over-enhancement, color distortions, and reliance on uniform noise assumptions. A robust solution requires an illumination-aware pipeline that leverages intrinsic priors to recover details in dark regions while preserving fidelity in bright areas.

Method: 1) Inject sensor-level illumination-response priors through physics-guided augmentation. 2) Represent degradation with adaptive prompts conditioned on the scene's latent illumination state. 3) Use a luminance-gated intrinsic memory mechanism to selectively compensate information loss, prioritizing dark region reconstruction and preserving bright-area fidelity. 4) Regularize with a self-supervised consistency objective to distill illumination-invariant features.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach. Code is available at the provided GitHub repository.

Conclusion: By deeply exploiting intrinsic illumination priors, InterLight achieves clearer textures and more visually coherent enhancement, showing robust performance across varied lighting conditions.

Abstract: Low-Light Image Enhancement (LLIE) has long been a challenging problem in low-level vision, as insufficient illumination often leads to low contrast, detail loss, and noise. Recent studies show that deep learning-based Retinex theory can effectively decouple illumination and reflectance. However, existing methods frequently suffer from over-enhancement or color distortion, and often assume uniform noise or ideal lighting. To address these limitations, we propose InterLight, a novel framework that systematically excavates and operationalizes intrinsic illumination priors for LLIE.Our core insight is that robust enhancement requires not just estimating illumination, but constructing an illumination-aware pipeline. We first inject sensor-level illumination-response priors via physics-guided augmentation, then represent the degradation through adaptive prompts conditioned on the scene's latent illumination state. This explicit representation directly guides a luminance-gated intrinsic memory mechanism to selectively compensate for information loss, prioritizing reconstruction in dark regions while preserving fidelity in bright ones. Finally, the entire process is regularized by a self-supervised consistency objective that distills illumination-invariant features. By deeply exploiting intrinsic illumination priors, our method achieves clearer textures and more visually coherent enhancement results. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Code is available at: https://github.com/House-yuyu/InterLight.

</details>


### [112] [CogOmniControl: Reasoning-Driven Controllable Video Generation via Creative Intent Cognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19995)
*Hongji Yang,Songlian Li,Yucheng Zhou,Xiaotong Zhao,Alan Zhao,Chengzhong Xu,Jianbing Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A reasoning-driven, domain-specific, closed-loop framework for controllable video generation that trains CogVLM on anime production data, unifies controls with CogOmniDiT, uses RL alignment, and supports Best-of-N selection via CogReasonBench and CogControlBench to outperform open-source models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address fragility of diffusion-based video generation under abstract, sparse, or complex prompts and the gap between user creative intent and existing conditioning methods (adapters or generic VLMs).

Method: Develop CogVLM trained on authentic anime production data; unify multiple conditional inputs with CogOmniDiT; align outputs via reinforcement learning; design evaluators and Best-of-N selection; construct a closed-loop harness-like architecture; create CogReasonBench and CogControlBench from professional workflows data.

Result: Achieves superior performance to existing open-source models on two benchmarks, producing more professional, clear outputs that better cognize user intent and integrate diverse controls; demonstrates the efficacy of the closed-loop, domain-specific approach.

Conclusion: CogOmniControl closes the gap between creative intent and controllable video generation by leveraging domain-specific reasoning, unified conditioning, and professional benchmarks; shows promise for integration into professional production workflows.

Abstract: Recent diffusion models achieve strong photorealism and fluency in video generation, yet remain fragile under abstract, sparse or complex conditions, leading to poor performance in professional production workflows such as storyboard sketches and clay render conditions. Existing video generation models, either inject conditions through adapters or couple a generic vision-language model (VLM) within a diffusion backbone, leaving a capability gap and failing to produce the videos that align with the user's creative intent. We present CogOmniControl, a reasoning-driven framework that factorizes controllable video generation into creative intent cognition and generation. Specifically, we train a specialized CogVLM using authentic anime production data. Compared to generic VLMs, it generates more professional and clear outputs, accurately cognizing user creative intent from sparse and abstract conditions and tuning these cues into dense reasoning output. Besides, CogOmniDiT unifies the controls from various conditions through in-context generation and is aligned to the CogVLM reasoning outputs via reinforcement learning. Furthermore, leveraging CogVLM's robust capability in guiding video generation, we release its potential in planning specific evaluators and enable a Best-of-N selection for the generated videos. This integration transforms the entire framework into a closed-loop "harness-like" architecture. We further introduce CogReasonBench and CogControlBench, built from professional workflows data that carry genuine creative intent rather than simulated ones. Experiments on two benchmarks show that CogOmniControl surpassed the existing open-source models. The project website: https://um-lab.github.io/CogOmniControl/

</details>


### [113] [A Nash Equilibrium Framework For Training-Free Multimodal Step Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20033)
*Rohit Sinha,Kunal Tilaganji,Tanuja Ganu,Nagarajan Natarajan,Amit Sharma,Vineeth N. Balasubramanian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a training-free, disagreement-aware verification framework for stepwise reasoning in multimodal LLMs, modeling cross-model judgments as a Nash equilibrium among specialized judges and using a closed-form solution for equilibrium scores to filter and rank steps; achieves gains over baselines and competitive results with learned critics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current verification methods fall into two camps: data-hungry learned critics with inconsistent cross-task performance, and training-free averaging of scores that ignore informative disagreement signals. There is a need for a task-agnostic, training-free verifier that leverages cross-modal disagreement as a signal of validity.

Method: Model step-wise verification as a coordination game among specialized judges. Formalize their interactions as a Nash equilibrium and derive a closed-form solution to compute equilibrium scores. Use these scores for disagreement-aware filtering and stability-conscious ranking of reasoning steps, leveraging cross-modal sources without task-specific adaptation.

Result: Empirical evaluation on six benchmarks shows consistent improvements of 2.4% to 5.2% over baseline models. The method is competitive with learned critics, demonstrating that cross-modal agreement signals provide robust verification without task-specific adaptation.

Conclusion: Cross-modal agreement, captured through a Nash-equilibrium-based, training-free verifier, provides robust verification signals for stepwise reasoning, enabling effective filtering and ranking without labeled-task adaptation and with competitive performance relative to learned critics.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models often generate reasoning chains containing subtle errors that lead to incorrect answers. Current verification approaches have notable limitations. Learned critics need extensive labeled data and show inconsistent performance across different tasks. Meanwhile, existing training-free methods simply average scores from different sources, missing a key insight: when these scores disagree, that disagreement itself carries important information about whether a reasoning step is truly valid or not. We propose a training-free verification approach that treats step-wise verification as a coordination problem among specialized judges. We formalize these judges' interaction as a Nash equilibrium game where agreement signals valid steps while disagreement reveals instability. Our method computes equilibrium scores through a closed-form solution, enabling both disagreement-aware filtering and stability-conscious ranking of reasoning steps. Evaluated across six benchmarks, our approach achieves consistent improvements of 2.4% to 5.2% over baseline models and shows competitive performance against learned critics, demonstrating that cross-modal agreement (not just average confidence) provides robust verification signals without task-specific adaptation.

</details>


### [114] [Stage-adaptive Token Selection for Efficient Omni-modal LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20035)
*Zijie Xin,Jie Yang,Ruixiang Zhao,Tianyi Wang,Fengyun Rao,Jing Lyu,Xirong Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SEATS is a training-free, stage-adaptive token pruning framework for Omni-modal LLMs that reduces tokens across layers and modalities, yielding substantial FLOPs reduction with minimal performance loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Omni-modal LLMs encode dense non-textual tokens for audio-visual inputs, causing high computational cost. Existing training-free token selection methods are limited to visual inputs or fixed per-modality pruning and do not account for how cross-modal token importance evolves across layers. There is a need to capture layer-wise token dependency and redundancy in om-LLMs to improve efficiency without sacrificing performance.

Method: The authors first analyze layer-wise token dependencies in om-LLMs and observe block-wise dependencies that weaken with depth. SEATS performs: (1) before the LLM, attention-weighted diversity-based pruning to remove spatiotemporal redundancy; (2) inside the LLM, progressive pruning across blocks and dynamic budget allocation between temporal windows and modalities using query relevance scores; (3) in late layers, removal of remaining non-textual tokens after cross-modal fusion.

Result: Experiments on Qwen2.5-Omni and Qwen3-Omni show that retaining 10% of visual and audio tokens yields 9.3x FLOPs reduction and 4.8x prefill speedup, while preserving 96.3% of the original performance.

Conclusion: SEATS demonstrates that training-free, stage-adaptive token pruning can substantially improve om-LLM inference efficiency by exploiting layer-wise cross-modal token dependencies and allocating retention budgets across modalities and temporal windows, with minimal performance loss.

Abstract: Omni-modal large language models (om-LLMs) achieve unified audio-visual understanding by encoding video and audio into temporally aligned token sequences interleaved at the window level. However, processing these dense non-textual tokens throughout the LLM incurs substantial computational overhead. Although training-free token selection can reduce this cost, existing methods either focus on visual-only inputs or prune om-LLM tokens only before the LLM with fixed per-modality ratios, failing to capture how cross-modal token importance evolves across layers. To address this limitation, we first analyze the layer-wise token dependency of om-LLMs. We find that visual and audio dependencies follow a block-wise pattern and gradually weaken with depth, indicating that many late-layer non-textual tokens become redundant after cross-modal fusion. Motivated by this observation, we propose SEATS, a training-free, stage-adaptive token selection method for efficient om-LLM inference. Before the LLM, SEATS removes spatiotemporal redundancy via attention-weighted diversity selection. Inside the LLM, it progressively prunes tokens across blocks and dynamically allocates the retention budget from temporal windows to modalities using query relevance scores. In late layers, it removes all remaining non-textual tokens once cross-modal fusion is complete. Experiments on Qwen2.5-Omni and Qwen3-Omni demonstrate that SEATS effectively improves inference efficiency. Retaining only 10% of visual and audio tokens, it achieves a 9.3x FLOPs reduction and a 4.8x prefill speedup while preserving 96.3% of the original performance.

</details>


### [115] [OP2GS: Object-Aware 3D Gaussian Splatting with Dual-Opacity Primitives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20044)
*Guiyu Liu,Niklas Vaara,Janne Mustaniemi,Juho Kannala,Janne Heikkilä*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OP2GS introduces an object-aware Gaussian representation for 3D Gaussian Splatting by adding per-Gaussian instance identity and a dedicated instance opacity σ*, while retaining the original opacity σ for rendering. A random object loss learns a 1D occupancy field for object masks, enabling semantic descriptors via multi-view aggregation without per-Gaussian features, yielding competitive open-vocabulary performance with lower overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) provides efficient scene representation but lacks inherent object-level identity, hindering open-vocabulary scene understanding. Feature-based methods incur high storage/decoding costs; lift-based mask propagation suffers from label contamination during 2D-3D projection. A compact, object-aware representation is needed.

Method: Augment each Gaussian with an explicit instance identity and an instance opacity σ* for object-mask rendering, while keeping the original opacity σ for visual rendering. Learn σ* via a random object loss that optimizes a 1D instance occupancy field using 3DGS transmittance-based visibility. Attach semantic descriptors at the object level through multi-view aggregation, avoiding per-Gaussian feature storage.

Result: OP2GS achieves competitive open-vocabulary performance compared to feature-training approaches while substantially reducing computational overhead. It also utilizes occupancy learning to resolve visibility ambiguities, and outperforms training-free pipelines by providing physically consistent instance occupancy and semantics.

Conclusion: OP2GS decouples visual existence from object occupancy, enabling robust, scalable object-aware 3D scene understanding with lower storage and computation costs.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) provides an explicit and efficient scene representation, but its primitives lack inherent object-level identity, hindering downstream tasks such as open-vocabulary scene understanding. Existing methods typically address this by either distilling high-dimensional feature embeddings into Gaussians or by lifting 2D mask labels into 3D via heuristic refinement. However, feature-based approaches incur heavy storage and decoding overhead, while lifting-based pipelines remain vulnerable to label contamination: Gaussians necessary for appearance reconstruction often receive incorrect object labels during 2D-to-3D projection. We propose OP2GS, an object-aware Gaussian representation that augments each primitive with an explicit instance identity and a dedicated instance opacity $σ^{*}$ for object-mask rendering. The original opacity $σ$ remains responsible for visual reconstruction, while $σ^{*}$ models whether a Gaussian should contribute to a particular object mask. This dual-opacity formulation decouples visual existence from instance occupancy: mislabeled Gaussians can remain available for image rendering while becoming transparent in the object-mask branch. To learn this representation, we introduce a random object loss that optimizes the 1D instance occupancy field using the standard transmittance-based visibility of 3DGS. Semantic descriptors are then attached at the object level through multi-view aggregation, eliminating per-Gaussian feature storage. Compared with feature-training approaches, OP2GS achieves competitive open-vocabulary performance while significantly reducing computational overhead. Compared with training-free pipelines, it leverages physically consistent occupancy learning to resolve visibility ambiguities.

</details>


### [116] [Cardiac fat segmentation using computed tomography and an image-to-image conditional generative adversarial neural network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20064)
*Guilherme Santos da Silva,Dalcimar Casanova,Jefferson Tales Oliva,Erick Oliveira Rodrigues*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A pix2pix-based deep learning method for autonomous segmentation and quantification of epicardial and mediastinal fats, achieving near-100% accuracy and high F1-scores with real-time performance; claims superiority over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual segmentation of cardiac fat is labor-intensive and costly; precise, efficient quantitative analysis is needed for cardiovascular risk assessment.

Method: Utilizes the pix2pix conditional GAN framework for image-to-image translation to segment two cardiac fat deposits (epicardial and mediastinal) separated by the pericardium; reports accuracy and F1-score as evaluation metrics; real-time segmentation capability claimed.

Result: Epicardial fat: accuracy 99.08%, F1 98.73. Mediastinal fat: accuracy 97.90%, F1 98.40. Reported real-time performance and superior F1-score and run-time compared to existing studies.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that pix2pix-based segmentation can achieve high precision and speed for cardiac fat quantification, suggesting clinical utility while noting need for broader validation across datasets and modalities.

Abstract: In recent years, research has highlighted the association between increased adipose tissue surrounding the human heart and elevated susceptibility to cardiovascular diseases such as atrial fibrillation and coronary heart disease. However, the manual segmentation of these fat deposits has not been widely implemented in clinical practice due to the substantial workload it entails for medical professionals and the associated costs. Consequently, the demand for more precise and time-efficient quantitative analysis has driven the emergence of novel computational methods for fat segmentation. This study presents a novel deep learning-based methodology that offers autonomous segmentation and quantification of two distinct types of cardiac fat deposits. The proposed approach leverages the pix2pix network, a generative conditional adversarial network primarily designed for image-to-image translation tasks. By applying this network architecture, we aim to investigate its efficacy in tackling the specific challenge of cardiac fat segmentation, despite not being originally tailored for this purpose. The two types of fat deposits of interest in this study are referred to as epicardial and mediastinal fats, which are spatially separated by the pericardium. The experimental results demonstrated an average accuracy of 99.08% and f1-score 98.73 for the segmentation of the epicardial fat and 97.90% of accuracy and f1-score of 98.40 for the mediastinal fat. These findings represent the high precision and overlap agreement achieved by the proposed methodology. In comparison to existing studies, our approach exhibited superior performance in terms of f1-score and run time, enabling the images to be segmented in real time.

</details>


### [117] [X-Ray cardiac angiographic vessel segmentation based on pixel classification using machine learning and region growing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20073)
*E O Rodrigues,L O Rodrigues,J J Lima,D Casanova,F Favarim,E R Dosciatti,V Pegorini,L S N Oliveira,F F C Morais*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Pixel-classification with texture-based features and region-growing (ELEMENT) using Random Forests; achieves high vessel-segmentation accuracy in x-ray angiograms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve automatic vessel segmentation in x-ray angiograms by leveraging per-pixel texture features and a region-growing guided classification to enhance discrimination of vascular structures.

Method: Extract texture-based features per pixel neighborhood (anisotropic diffusion, Hessian-based features, mathematical morphology, and statistics); apply the ELEMENT methodology, enabling a region-growing process where the classification outcome informs subsequent pixel classifications; employ a Random Forest classifier to predict vessel vs. non-vessel pixels.

Result: Reported accuracy of 95.48%, claimed as best in the literature and superior to unsupervised state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The approach integrates texture-based features with a region-growing, supervised pixel-classification framework to achieve high vessel-segmentation performance; practical deployment would require thorough baseline comparisons and robustness validation.

Abstract: This work proposes a pixel-classification approach for vessel segmentation in x-ray angiograms. The proposal uses textural features such as anisotropic diffusion, features based on the Hessian matrix, mathematical morphology and statistics. These features are extracted from the neighborhood of each pixel. The approach also uses the ELEMENT methodology, which consists of creating a pixel-classification controlled by region-growing where the result of the classification affects further classifications of pixels. The Random Forests classifier is used to predict whether the pixel belongs to the vessel structure. The approach achieved the best accuracy in the literature (95.48%) outperforming unsupervised state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [118] [Probability-Conserving Flow Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20079)
*Parsa Esmati,Junha Hyung,Amirhossein Dadashzadeh,Jaegul Choo,Majid Mirmehdi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AdaMaG: a principled, plug-and-play guidance method for diffusion/flow models that decomposes guidance into divergence and score-parallel terms, addresses manifold geometry to preserve probability mass, and improves realism with controlled desaturation in high-guidance regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current guidance methods (CFG and extrapolation) are heuristic linear combinations of velocities/scores that ignore the data manifold geometry, violating probability conservation and causing samples to drift off the learned manifold under strong guidance.

Method: An analytical study of guidance via the continuity equation, revealing a decomposition into a divergence term and a score-parallel term invariant to parameterization. The divergence term diverges near the data manifold as sampling approaches it. The authors introduce Adaptive Manifold Guidance (AdaMaG), a plug-and-play rule combining a time-dependent schedule with score-parallel attenuation to bound both terms without extra inference cost.

Result: AdaMaG improves realism and reduces hallucinations across image generation benchmarks, and induces controlled desaturation in high-guidance regimes. It also aligns with many empirical heuristics for mitigating saturation or improving generation quality by corresponding to the two decomposition terms.

Conclusion: AdaMaG provides a principled framework for guidance that respects manifold geometry and probability conservation, offering a simple, plug-and-play improvement over CFG-like methods and explaining why existing heuristics work.

Abstract: Diffusion and flow-based generative models dominate visual synthesis, with guidance aligning samples to user input and improving perceptual quality. However, Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and extrapolation-based methods are heuristic linear combinations of velocities/scores that ignore the generative manifold geometry, breaking probability conservation and driving samples off the learned manifold under strong guidance. We analyse guidance through the continuity equation and show its effect decomposes into a divergence term and a score-parallel term defined invariantly across parameterisations. We prove the divergence term blows up structurally as sampling approaches the data manifold, motivating a time-dependent schedule alongside score-parallel attenuation. The resulting plug-and-play rule, Adaptive Manifold Guidance (AdaMaG), bounds both terms at no additional inference cost. Finally, we show that most empirical heuristics for reducing saturation or improving generation quality correspond directly to the two terms in our decomposition. Across image generation benchmarks, AdaMaG improves realism, reduces hallucinations, and induces controlled desaturation in high-guidance regimes.

</details>


### [119] [VL-DPO: Vision-Language-Guided Finetuning for Preference-Aligned Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20082)
*Zhefan Xu,Ghassen Jerfel,Marina Haliem,Qi Zhao,Jeonhyung Kang,Khaled S. Refaat*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VL-DPO uses a vision-language model to automatically generate human-preference pairs from model rollouts and trains motion forecasting with Direct Preference Optimization, achieving improvements on RFS and ADE on Waymo Open End-to-End Driving data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation objectives in motion forecasting may fail to capture nuanced human driving preferences; leveraging vision-language reasoning can align models with human preferences.

Method: Use a pretrained vision-language model as a zero-shot reasoner to generate preference pairs from ego-vehicle trajectory rollouts. Finetune the forecasting model with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on these pairs, evaluated on the Waymo Open End-to-End Driving dataset (WOD-E2E) against held-out human preference annotations.

Result: VL-DPO yields an 11.94% increase in RFS and a 10.01% reduction in ADE over the pretrained model.

Conclusion: Vision-language-guided preference alignment via DPO improves alignment with human preferences for ego-vehicle motion forecasting; VLM-derived preferences serve as a high-quality proxy for human judgments.

Abstract: The rapid growth of autonomous driving datasets has enabled the scaling of powerful motion forecasting models. While large-scale pretraining provides strong performance, the standard imitation objective may not fully capture the complex nuances of human driving preferences. Meanwhile, recent advances in vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated impressive reasoning and commonsense understanding. Building on these capabilities, this paper presents VL-DPO, a vision-language-guided framework that aligns ego-vehicle motion forecasting models with human preferences. Our approach leverages a VLM as a zero-shot reasoner to automatically generate preference pairs from a pretrained model's rollouts, which are then used to finetune the model via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). We finetune our models on the Waymo Open End-to-End Driving Dataset (WOD-E2E) and evaluate performance against held-out human preference annotations using rater feedback score (RFS) and average displacement error (ADE). Our experiments confirm that the VLM's trajectory selection is a high-quality proxy for human preference. Our final model, VL-DPO, yields an 11.94% increase in RFS and a 10.01% reduction in ADE over the pretrained model.

</details>


### [120] [Spatially Prompted Visual Trajectory Prediction for Egocentric Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20085)
*Yifan Li,Xinyu Zhou,Yunhao Ge,Yu Kong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces SP-VTP, a vision-driven framework for predicting egocentric manipulation trajectories from spatial prompts that specify object and goal locations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses cluttered environments with similar objects and static task specifications by leveraging explicit spatial prompts, enabling trajectory forecasting from egocentric streams.

Method: SPOT (Spatially Prompted Object-Target Policy) uses a three-branch architecture: a task encoder for first-frame spatial prompts and visuals, an observation encoder for current context and history, and a trajectory generator for future end-effector motion. The EgoSPT dataset provides first-frame grounding and recovered 3D motion for evaluation.

Result: Under strict scene-level splits, SPOT improves cross-scene trajectory prediction compared to non-prompted baselines or baselines using a single prompting source.

Conclusion: SP-VTP offers a simple, scalable way to condition egocentric manipulation tasks via spatial prompts and establishes EgoSPT/SPOT as a new research direction in spatially prompted visuomotor prediction.

Abstract: Robotic manipulation is often specified through language instructions or task identifiers, yet cluttered environments with similar objects are better handled by spatially indicating what to move and where to place it. Addressing the vision-centric challenge of object and goal specification, we present, to the best of our knowledge, the first formalization of Spatially Prompted Visual Trajectory Prediction (SP-VTP). This novel setting utilizes initial spatial prompts (like bounding boxes or points) to define task objectives, tasking the model with forecasting future end-effector trajectories from egocentric streams. To study this problem, we collect and annotate EgoSPT, a dataset of egocentric spatially prompted manipulation trajectories with first-frame object and target grounding annotations and recovered 3D end-effector motion. SP-VTP is challenging because the task specification is static, while the scene configuration evolves over time. To solve this problem, we propose SPOT(Spatially Prompted Object-Target Policy), which combines a task encoder for first-frame visual and coordinate spatial prompts, an observation encoder for current visual and history context, and a trajectory generator for future end-effector motion. Experiments under strict scene-level splits show that SPOT improves cross-scene trajectory prediction over non-prompted or single-source prompted baselines. Together, EgoSPT and SPOT establish a new spatial prompting problem SP-VTP, as a simple and scalable task condition for egocentric manipulation.

</details>


### [121] [MetaEarth-MM: Unified Multimodal Remote Sensing Image Generation with Scene-centered Joint Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20090)
*Zhiping Yu,Chenyang Liu,Jinqi Cao,Qinzhe Yang,Siwei Yu,Zhengxia Zou,Zhenwei Shi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes MetaEarth-MM, a unified five-modality generative foundation model for remote sensing enabling paired joint generation and any-to-any translation via a scene-centered, decoupled latent representation approach; accompanied by EarthMM, a large-scale paired dataset; demonstrates strong generative performance and downstream utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scarce complete paired multi-modal remote sensing data; existing methods are pairwise and do not scale well with modality count; need scalable, versatile generative model across multiple modalities and tasks.

Method: Scene-centered joint modeling; decoupled architecture that first infers a latent scene representation from available modalities and then generates target modalities conditioned on that latent state; supports five modalities and any-to-any translation within a unified model; trained on EarthMM dataset of 2.8M global images with 2.2M aligned pairs.

Result: Experimental results show strong generative capability and robust generalization across tasks; effectiveness for downstream tasks at data and representation levels.

Conclusion: MetaEarth-MM has potential as a general foundation model for cross-modal Earth observation, enabling scalable multi-modal generation and downstream utility; code and EarthMM dataset will be released.

Abstract: Multi-modal remote sensing images are vital for Earth observation, yet complete paired observations are often scarce in practice. Existing generative methods commonly address this problem through isolated pairwise modality translation, but their versatility and scalability remain limited as the number of modalities and generation tasks increases. Here, we develop a generative foundation model MetaEarth-MM for multi-modal remote sensing imagery, enabling paired joint generation and any-to-any translation across five modalities within a unified model. Recognizing the intrinsic scene consistency underlying multi-modal observations, we introduce a scene-centered joint modeling paradigm in MetaEarth-MM. Unlike previous methods that rely on direct appearance-level cross-modal mapping, our model organizes the generation around the underlying scene content. Specifically, MetaEarth-MM adopts a decoupled architecture that first infers a latent scene representation from available observations, and then generates target modalities conditioned on this intermediate state. To support training, we further construct EarthMM, a large-scale dataset comprising 2.8 million multi-resolution global images with 2.2 million aligned pairs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MetaEarth-MM not only exhibits strong generative capability and robust generalization across diverse generation tasks, but also supports downstream tasks at both data and representation levels, highlighting its potential as a general foundation model for cross-modal Earth observation. The code and dataset will be available at https://github.com/YZPioneer/MetaEarth-MM.

</details>


### [122] [SetCon: Towards Open-Ended Referring Segmentation via Set-Level Concept Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20110)
*Zhixiong Zhang,Yizhuo Li,Shuangrui Ding,Yuhang Zang,Shengyuan Ding,Long Xing,Yibin Wang,Qiaosheng Zhang,Jiaqi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SetCon reframes open-ended referring segmentation as set-level concept prediction using LVLM-derived natural-language concepts for joint mask-set decoding, aided by hierarchical semantic decomposition and augmented semantic supervision. It enables coherent handling of multiple targets and shows state-of-the-art gains on image benchmarks and video benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extending referring segmentation to multiple instances, cross-category groups, or open-ended target sets is difficult. LVLM-based methods treat targets as separate tokens, neglecting set-level properties like completeness and mutual exclusivity.

Method: Introduce SetCon with LVLM-generated natural-language concepts as semantic conditions for joint mask-set decoding. Use a hierarchical semantic decomposition: predict a shared set-level concept defining the target scope, then refine into fine-grained concept groups aligned with target subsets. Augment existing reasoning segmentation datasets with a two-stage annotation pipeline providing hierarchical semantic supervision (236k samples, 784k concept phrases).

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art on image benchmarks: +3.3 gIoU on gRefCOCO, +12.1 gIoU on MUSE; gains grow with more referred targets. Transfers to video under detect-and-track setting, achieving SOTA on seven referring video benchmarks: +10.9 J&F on MeViS, +12.4 J&F on Ref-SeCVOS.

Conclusion: SetCon demonstrates that predicting set-level concepts with LVLM-based natural-language concepts enables coherent, scalable open-ended referring segmentation for images and video, with strong cross-domain transfer and improvements as target counts increase.

Abstract: Referring segmentation grounds natural-language queries to pixel-level masks, but extending it to complex scenarios with multiple instances, cross-category groups, or open-ended target sets remains challenging. Previous Large Vision Language Model (LVLM)-based methods represent referred targets with one or more special tokens sequentially, treating multiple targets as separate outputs rather than a coherent set and offering little incentive to capture set-level properties such as completeness and mutual exclusivity. We reformulate open-ended referring segmentation as explicit set-level concept prediction and propose Set-Concept Segmentation (SetCon), which uses LVLM-generated natural-language concepts, instead of segmentation-specific tokens, as semantic conditions for joint mask-set decoding. A hierarchical semantic decomposition first predicts a shared set-level concept defining the target scope and then refines it into fine-grained concept groups aligned with target subsets. To support this, a two-stage annotation pipeline augments existing reasoning segmentation datasets with hierarchical semantic supervision (236k samples, 784k concept phrases). SetCon achieves state-of-the-art results on image benchmarks (+3.3 gIoU on gRefCOCO, +12.1 gIoU on MUSE), with margins that grow as the number of referred targets increases. The concept interface also transfers to video under a detect-and-track setting, yielding new state-of-the-art results on seven referring video benchmarks, including +10.9 J&F on MeViS and +12.4 J&F on Ref-SeCVOS.

</details>


### [123] [PixVerve: Advancing Native UHR Image Generation to 100MP with a Large-Scale High-Quality Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20147)
*Haojun Chen,Haoyang He,Chengming Xu,Qingdong He,Junwei Zhu,Yabiao Wang,Zhucun Xue,Xianfang Zeng,Zhennan Chen,Xiaobin Hu,Hao Zhao,Yong Liu,Jiangning Zhang,Dacheng Tao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces PixVerve-95K, a 95K-image, 100MP ultra-high-resolution T2I dataset with 7D annotations; proposes three training schemes to achieve native 100MP generation; presents PixVerve-Bench, a comprehensive UHR evaluation suite using traditional metrics and LLM-based semantic assessments; offers extensive experiments and actionable insights for future UHR T2I research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the scarcity of high-resolution content and the lack of standardized evaluation for native 100MP Text-to-Image generation, enabling both data-driven model scaling and robust benchmarking.

Method: Develop a data pipeline to curate PixVerve-95K (≥100M pixels per image) with seven-dimensional annotations; implement three training schemes to extend T2I foundation models to native 100MP generation; construct PixVerve-Bench combining conventional image-text metrics with multimodal LLM-based assessments for UHR quality and semantic alignment.

Result: Extensive experiments on PixVerve-Bench validate the proposed training schemes and demonstrate the practicality of native-100MP T2I; the benchmark provides a comprehensive evaluation protocol that informs future UHR generation research.

Conclusion: The dataset and benchmark resources advance UHR T2I by enabling native 100MP generation and rigorous multi-faceted evaluation, offering guidance and insights to drive future breakthroughs in high-resolution image synthesis.

Abstract: Text-to-Image (T2I) models have recently seen notable progress around 1K and 2K resolution. With the extreme desire for better visual experience and the rapid development of imaging technology, the demand for Ultra-High-Resolution (UHR) image generation has grown significantly. However, UHR image generation poses great challenges due to the scarcity and complexity of high-resolution content. In this paper, we first introduce PixVerve-95K, a high-quality, open-source UHR T2I dataset curated with a carefully designed data pipeline, which contains 95K images across diverse scenarios (each image has a minimum pixel-count of 100M) and seven-dimensional annotations. Based on our large-scale image-text dataset, we take a pioneering step to extend various T2I foundation models to native 100MP generation with three training schemes. Finally, leveraging both conventional metrics and multimodal large language model-based assessments, our proposed PixVerve-Bench benchmark establishes a comprehensive evaluation protocol for UHR images encompassing visual quality and semantic alignment. Extensive experimental results on our benchmark and the constructive exploration of training strategies collaboratively provide valuable insights for future breakthroughs.

</details>


### [124] [TideGS: Scalable Training of Over One Billion 3D Gaussian Splatting Primitives via Out-of-Core Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20150)
*Chonghao Zhong,Linfeng Shi,Hua Chen,Tiecheng Sun,Hao Zhao,Binhang Yuan,Chaojian Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TideGS enables training 3D Gaussian Splatting at >1B Gaussians on a 24 GB GPU by an out-of-core pipeline that uses SSD-CPU-GPU hierarchy, block-virtualized geometry, hierarchical asynchronous I/O-computation overlap, and trajectory-adaptive differential streaming to transfer only incremental working-set deltas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting training is memory-bound due to large per-primitive attributes and parameter growth; existing single-GPU or simple out-of-core approaches cannot scale to billions of Gaussians. The observed sparsity and trajectory-conditioned activation across camera frames suggest that a cache-like, selective loading strategy can dramatically improve scalability.

Method: Introduce TideGS with three techniques: (1) block-virtualized geometry to align spatial data with SSD locality, (2) a hierarchical asynchronous pipeline to overlap I/O with computation across CPU/SSD/GPU, and (3) trajectory-adaptive differential streaming that transfers only incremental deltas of the active working set between iterations. The system manages parameters across SSD, CPU, and GPU during training of 3D Gaussian Splatting.

Result: Empirically demonstrates training with over one billion Gaussians on a single 24 GB GPU; achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality among evaluated single-GPU baselines on large-scale scenes; scales beyond prior out-of-core baselines (~100M Gaussians) and conventional in-memory training (~11M Gaussians).

Conclusion: TideGS provides a practical path to scalable, high-fidelity 3D Gaussian Splatting training on commodity hardware by exploiting sparsity and trajectory-conditioned activation with an efficient SSD-CPU-GPU data path and incremental working-set transfer.

Abstract: Training 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) at billion-primitive scale is fundamentally memory-bound: each Gaussian primitive carries a large attribute vector, and the aggregate parameter table quickly exceeds GPU capacity, limiting prior systems to tens of millions of Gaussians on commodity single-GPU hardware. We observe that 3DGS training is inherently sparse and trajectory-conditioned: each iteration activates only the Gaussians visible from the current camera batch, so GPU memory can serve as a working-set cache rather than a persistent parameter store. Building on this insight, we introduce TideGS, an out-of-core training framework that manages parameters across an SSD-CPU-GPU hierarchy via three synergistic techniques: block-virtualized geometry for SSD-aligned spatial locality, a hierarchical asynchronous pipeline to overlap I/O with computation, and trajectory-adaptive differential streaming that transfers only incremental working-set deltas between iterations. Experiments show that TideGS enables training with over one billion Gaussians on a single 24 GB GPU while achieving the best reconstruction quality among evaluated single-GPU baselines on large-scale scenes, scaling beyond prior out-of-core baselines (e.g., approximately 100M Gaussians) and standard in-memory training (e.g., approximately 11M Gaussians).

</details>


### [125] [Rethinking Visual Attribution for Chest X-ray Reasoning in Large Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20158)
*Guangzhi Xiong,Qiao Jin,Sanchit Sinha,Zhiyong Lu,Aidong Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A framework to evaluate and improve visual explanations for medical LVLMs in chest X-ray reasoning; introduces MedFocus, a concept-based attribution method using unbalanced OT, achieving better faithful explanations than existing attribution methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Trustworthy clinical use of LVLMs requires that visual explanations reflect true evidence; current attribution methods lack ground-truth validation due to unavailable internal reasoning annotations.

Method: Develop a causal evaluation framework that retains only CXR-VQA samples with expert-annotated regions verified via counterfactual editing as causally responsible; assess 11 attribution methods across 6 open-source LVLMs and two output modes; propose MedFocus, a concept-based attribution using unbalanced optimal transport to localize anatomical regions and measure their causal effect through targeted interventions, providing spatial, concept-level, and token-level attributions.

Result: Existing attribution methods often fail to identify the actual evidence used by LVLMs; MedFocus substantially outperforms prior methods on fidelity and interpretability across tested settings.

Conclusion: MedFocus advances trustworthy attribution for medical LVLMs by combining causal evaluation with a robust concept-based attribution framework; code and data are publicly available.

Abstract: Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) show promise in medical applications, but their inability to faithfully ground responses in visual evidence raises serious concerns about clinical trustworthiness. While visual attribution methods are widely used to explain LVLM predictions, whether these explanations actually reflect the visual evidence underlying the model's decision is largely unverified, since ground-truth annotations for internal model reasoning are typically unavailable. We address this question for chest X-ray (CXR) reasoning by developing a causal evaluation framework that retains only CXR-VQA samples for which the expert-annotated region is verified, via counterfactual editing, to be causally responsible for the model's prediction. Using this framework across 11 attribution methods, six open-source LVLMs, and two output modes (direct answer and step-by-step reasoning), we find that existing attribution methods often fail to identify the evidence used by LVLMs. To address this failure, we propose MedFocus, a concept-based attribution method that localizes clinically meaningful anatomical regions via unbalanced optimal transport and measures their causal effect on model outputs through targeted interventions. MedFocus produces spatial, concept-level, and token-level attributions and substantially outperforms prior methods, taking a step toward more trustworthy attribution for medical LVLMs. Our data and code are available at https://github.com/gzxiong/medfocus/.

</details>


### [126] [Interpretable Computer Vision for Defect Detection in X-ray Tomography of Aerospace SiC/SiC Composites](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20159)
*Antonio Peña Corredor,Julien Lesseur,Romain Nunez,Paul Rivalland,Thomas Philippe*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Prototype-augmented ResNet-50 (p-ResNet-50) for aerospace XCT defect detection with interpretable prototypes, achieving near-baseline accuracy and providing traceable, domain-aligned explanations and uncertainty mapping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial XCT inspection requires transparent, auditable decisions. Standard CNNs are black-box; there is a need for explanations aligned with expert semantic categories to support decision-making.

Method: Extend ResNet-50 with a prototype layer consisting of six learned prototypes mapped to expert-defined semantic categories (healthy matrix, matrix–air interfaces, pores, line-like defects, and mixed morphologies). Introduce two regularisers—anchor-based to tie prototypes to expert patches and medoid-based to prevent prototype collapse. Use UMAP for latent-space analysis to identify coherent sub-domains and uncertainty regions. Evaluate on ~12,000 XCT patches from four defect-rich SiC/SiC specimens, comparing to a ResNet-50 baseline.

Result: Prototype-augmented model achieves accuracy 0.957 and ROC-AUC 0.994, versus ResNet-50 baseline accuracy 0.959 and ROC-AUC 0.993; slight drop in sensitivity but gains in precision and specificity. Each decision is supported by representative evidence patches, and the model explicitly flags uncertainty regions. The approach yields traceable, auditable decisions and a reusable methodology for embedding domain knowledge into prototype networks for XCT inspections.

Conclusion: The p-ResNet-50 framework provides a tractable, interpretable solution that maintains near-baseline performance while delivering domain-aligned explanations and uncertainty awareness, with potential applicability to other XCT inspection contexts requiring traceability.

Abstract: Non-destructive testing of aerospace SiC/SiC composites via X-ray computed tomography (XCT) relies on expert visual assessment, with current workflows offering limited traceability for accept/reject decisions. Deep convolutional networks can automate defect detection, yet their black-box nature conflicts with the transparency that industrial inspection practice demands. To close this gap, we introduce p-ResNet-50, a convolutional framework extended with a prototype layer that couples high detection accuracy with case-based explanations. Six learned prototypes are explicitly aligned with expert-defined semantic categories-healthy matrix, matrix--air interfaces, pores, line-like defects, and mixed morphologies-so that every classification is traceable to a physically meaningful reference. Two novel regularisation terms, anchor-based and medoid-based, tether prototypes to expert-selected patches and prevent prototype collapse, addressing a known limitation of prototype networks. Latent-space analysis via UMAP delineates semantically coherent sub-domains and maps zones of uncertainty where misclassifications concentrate, giving inspectors an explicit picture of where the model is-and is not-reliable. The framework is validated on an XCT patch dataset of approximately 12,000 patches extracted from four defect-rich SiC/SiC laboratory specimens. Taking a black-box ResNet-50 as a baseline (ROC-AUC = 0.991), the prototype extension achieves comparable performance (accuracy 0.957 vs. 0.959; ROC-AUC 0.994 vs. 0.993) while trading a slight reduction in sensitivity for higher precision and specificity. Each decision is backed by representative evidence patches, and the model explicitly flags its uncertainty regions. Beyond defect mapping, the framework establishes a reusable methodology for embedding domain-expert knowledge into prototype networks, applicable to other XCT inspection scenarios requiring traceable, auditable decisions.

</details>


### [127] [CaMo: Camera Motion Grounded Evaluation and Training for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20165)
*Hsiang-Wei Huang,Junbin Lu,Kuang-Ming Chen,Jianxu Shangguan,Cheng-Yen Yang,Jenq-Neng Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Spatial Narrative Score (SNS) to test 3D spatial understanding in Vision-Language Models by requiring explicit spatial narratives of scenes and camera motion; reveals QA accuracy can mask poor spatial reasoning; proposes CaMo, a camera-motion grounded VLM, achieving consistency across SNS and direct spatial QA; code and data released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether current spatial VLMs truly possess 3D spatial understanding or merely excel at direct QA, by evaluating their ability to generate explicit, transferable spatial narratives that incorporate camera motion.
