Method: Define SNS that requires models to produce explicit spatial narratives describing scene semantics and camera motion, followed by reasoning with a frozen proxy LLM; evaluate state-of-the-art spatial VLMs; introduce CaMo, a camera-motion grounded VLM, and show improved performance on SNS and direct QA.

Result: Existing state-of-the-art spatial VLMs show significant degradation on SNS despite high direct QA accuracy; CaMo achieves consistent performance on SNS and direct spatial QA metrics.

Conclusion: Explicit externalization of spatial narratives is crucial for reliable evaluation of 3D spatial understanding in VLMs; CaMo demonstrates how grounding camera motion improves transferability; code/data/model available for replication.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) achieve strong performance on spatial question answering benchmarks, yet it remains unclear whether such gains reflect genuine spatial intelligence. We show that existing spatial VLMs lack basic camera motion understanding, a key component of spatial cognition. We propose the Spatial Narrative Score (SNS), an evaluation framework that requires VLMs to generate explicit spatial narratives capturing both scene semantics and camera motion, followed by reasoning with a frozen proxy LLM. Under SNS, state-of-the-art spatial VLMs exhibit significant performance degradation despite high direct question answering accuracy. To address this gap, we introduce CaMo, a camera motion grounded VLM that achieves consistent performance across SNS evaluation and direct spatial question answering accuracy. Our results highlight the importance of explicit spatial narrative externalization for evaluating VLMs with transferable 3D spatial understanding. Our code, data, and model is available at https://github.com/hsiangwei0903/CaMo

</details>


### [128] [Multi-axis Analysis of Image Manipulation Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20174)
*Keanu Nichols,Divya Appapogu,Giscard Biamby,Dina Bashkirova,Anna Rohrbach,Bryan A. Plummer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AUDITS: a large-scale, domain-shift-aware benchmark for image manipulation detection, with 530k images from user/news sources, using diffusion-based inpainting; enables cross-domain robustness evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manipulated images threaten misinformation; current detection methods lack cross-domain robustness; need comprehensive benchmarks to study axes of analysis (domain shift, quality, type, size).

Method: Construct AUDITS with two sources, curate across axes, include diffusion-based inpaintings of varying types/sizes; run experiments under several domain shifts on existing detectors.

Result: Baseline experiments conducted; evidence that domain shifts affect detection performance; AUDITS provides a resource to assess and improve robustness.

Conclusion: AUDITS facilitates research toward more reliable, generalizable manipulation detection across domains.

Abstract: Advanced image editing software enables easy creation of highly convincing image manipulations, which has been made even more accessible in recent years due to advances in generative AI. Manipulated images, while often harmless, could spread misinformation, create false narratives, and influence people's opinions on important issues. Despite this growing threat, there is limited research on detecting advanced manipulations across different visual domains. Thus, we introduce Analysis Under Domain-shifts, qualIty, Type, and Size (AUDITS), a comprehensive benchmark designed for studying axes of analysis in image manipulation detection. AUDITS comprises over 530K images from two distinct sources (user and news photos). We curate our dataset to support analysis across multiple axes using recent diffusion-based inpaintings, spanning a diverse range of manipulation types and sizes. We conduct experiments under different types of domain shift to evaluate robustness of existing image manipulation detection methods. Our goal is to drive further research in this area by offering new insights that would help develop more reliable and generalizable image manipulation detection methods.

</details>


### [129] [MSAVBench: Towards Comprehensive and Reliable Evaluation of Multi-Shot Audio-Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20183)
*Yujie Wei,Yujin Han,Zhekai Chen,Yongming Li,Kaixun Jiang,Zhihang Liu,Quanhao Li,Zhiwu Qing,Xiang Wang,Zhen Xing,Ruihang Chu,Lingyi Hong,Yefei He,Junjie Zhou,Junqiu Yu,Yang Shi,Difan Zou,Kai Zhu,Shiwei Zhang,Yingya Zhang,Yu Liu,Xihui Liu,Hongming Shan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces MSAVBench, a benchmark and adaptive evaluation framework for multi-shot audio-video generation, with four dimensions (video, audio, shot, reference) and up to 15 shots; features adaptive shot segmentation, instance-wise rubrics, and tool-grounded evidence, achieving high alignment with human judgments (Spearman 0.915) and revealing current models' gaps in director-level control and audio-visual synchronization; data/code release planned.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for multi-shot audio-video generation are limited in scope, diversity, and evaluation pipelines, hindering reliable, systematic assessment of modern MSAV models.

Method: A four-dimensional benchmark (video, audio, shot, reference) with variable shot counts up to 15; an adaptive self-correction mechanism for shot segmentation; instance-wise rubrics for subjective metrics; and tool-grounded evidence extraction for complex judgments.

Result: Demonstrates a Spearman rank correlation of 91.5% with human judgments across 19 models; current systems struggle with director-level control and fine-grained audio-visual synchronization; modular/agentic pipelines show promise in narrowing the gap between open- and closed-source models.

Conclusion: MSAVBench provides a comprehensive, adaptive framework and data/code release to enable robust, systematic evaluation of MSAV models, and highlights the need for improved director-level control and synchronization in future research.

Abstract: Video generation is rapidly evolving from single-shot synthesis to complex multi-shot audio-video (MSAV) narratives to meet real-world demands. However, evaluating such frontier models remains a fundamental challenge. Existing benchmarks are limited in scope and data diversity, and rely on rigid evaluation pipelines, preventing systematic and reliable assessment of modern MSAV models. To bridge these gaps, we introduce MSAVBench, the first comprehensive benchmark and adaptive hybrid evaluation framework for multi-shot audio-video generation. Our benchmark spans four key dimensions, video, audio, shot, and reference, covering diverse task settings, varying shot counts of up to 15, and challenging non-realistic scenarios. Our evaluation framework improves robustness through an adaptive self-correction mechanism for shot segmentation, instance-wise rubrics for subjective metrics, and tool-grounded evidence extraction for complex judgments. Furthermore, MSAVBench achieves high alignment with human judgments, reaching a Spearman rank correlation of 91.5%. Our systematic evaluation of 19 state-of-the-art closed- and open-source models shows that current systems still struggle with director-level control and fine-grained audio-visual synchronization, while modular or agentic generation pipelines offer a promising path toward narrowing the gap between open- and closed-source models. We will release the benchmark data and evaluation code to facilitate future research.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [130] [KG-ASG: Collision-Knowledge-Guided Closed-Loop Adversarial Scenario Generation With Primary-Support Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18895)
*Cheng Wang,Chen Xiong,Ziwen Wang,Yuchen Zhou,Qiang Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: KG-ASG introduces a collision-knowledge-guided, closed-loop multi-vehicle adversarial scenario generator for autonomous driving safety validation, using a knowledge base and primary-support reasoning to achieve interpretable, executable samples with improved attack effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety-scenario generators struggle with ambiguous collision causes and uncontrolled multi-vehicle interactions due to perturbation-based or single-adversary approaches. A structured collision knowledge base can enable clearer semantics and better control of multi-vehicle dynamics.

Method: Build a structured collision knowledge base; train a lightweight Collision Expert to infer target collision mode, primary adversary, support vehicles, and their interaction roles. Generate adversarial scenarios via a primary-support process where the primary adversary triggers the main conflict while support vehicles shape risk structure without becoming colliders. Impose hard constraint gates (rule, physical, interaction-safety, single-collider) to ensure executable samples. Use planner-controller feedback for failure diagnosis, re-ranking, and terminal refinement.

Result: On WOMD scenarios reconstructed in MetaDrive, KG-ASG achieves strong adversarial effectiveness, improves Valid Primary Attack, reduces multi-collision, and gains closed-loop recovery under IDM, Cruise, and Expert controllers.

Conclusion: Collision-knowledge guidance and primary-support single-collider reasoning enhance adversarial effectiveness, interpretability, and executability for autonomous driving safety validation.

Abstract: Safety validation of autonomous driving systems requires high-risk scenario coverage, clear collision semantics, executable trajectories, and attributable multi-vehicle interactions. Existing safety-critical scenario generation methods often rely on low-level trajectory perturbations, collision-proxy optimization, or single-adversary search, which may produce adversarial samples with ambiguous collision causes or uncontrolled multi-vehicle collisions. This paper proposes KG-ASG, a collision-knowledge-guided closed-loop adversarial scenario generation framework with primary-support attribution. KG-ASG constructs a structured collision knowledge base and trains a lightweight Collision Expert to infer the target collision mode, the unique primary adversary, support vehicles, and their interaction roles. Guided by this semantic prior, multi-vehicle adversarial generation is formulated as a primary-support process, where the primary adversary induces the main conflict and support vehicles shape the surrounding risk structure without becoming additional colliders. Rule, physical, interaction-safety, and single-collider constraints are imposed as hard gates to filter non-executable samples. To handle reactive ego behaviors, planner-controller feedback is further used for failure diagnosis, candidate re-ranking, and terminal refinement. Experiments on WOMD scenarios reconstructed in MetaDrive show that KG-ASG achieves strong adversarial effectiveness while improving Valid Primary Attack, reducing multi-collision, and obtaining closed-loop recovery gains under IDM, Cruise, and Expert controllers. These results demonstrate that collision-knowledge guidance and primary-support single-collider reasoning improve adversarial effectiveness, interpretability, and executability for autonomous driving safety validation.

</details>


### [131] [Distributionally Robust Control via Stein Variational Inference for Contact-Rich Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19029)
*Hrishikesh Sathyanarayan,Victor Vantilborgh,Harish Ravichandar,Tom Lefebvre,Ian Abraham*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a distributionally robust, Stein variational inference–based model-based controller for contact-rich manipulation, yielding improved robustness (up to 3x) under parametric uncertainty without sacrificing performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the mismatch between data-driven methods (data-hungry) and classical model-based controllers (limited uncertainty representation) in contact-rich manipulation; aim to explicitly model task-sensitive uncertainty while preserving efficiency.

Method: A deterministic formulation using Stein variational inference to model a distribution over uncertain parameters within a distributionally robust control framework, applied to model-based manipulation control.

Result: Experimental results show up to 3× robustness improvement across a range of contact-rich manipulation tasks under broad parametric uncertainty, outperforming existing model-based controllers.

Conclusion: The approach yields controllers that are more aware of task sensitivities to uncertainty and maintain high performance, indicating a reliable, efficient strategy for uncertainty-aware manipulation.

Abstract: Reliable robotic manipulation requires control policies that can accurately represent and adapt to uncertainty arising from contact-rich interactions. Modern data-driven methods mitigate uncertainty through large-scale training and computation, and degrade significantly in performance with limited number of training samples. By contrast, classical model-based controllers are computationally efficient and reliable, but their limited ability to represent task-relevant uncertainty can hinder performance in contact-rich interactions.
  In this work, we propose to expand the capabilities of model-based manipulation control through more flexible uncertainty modeling that retains performance while exactly adapting to uncertainty. Our approach casts the manipulation problem as a distributionally robust control optimization and proposes a novel deterministic formulation based on Stein variational inference that preserves performance while explicitly modeling task-sensitive parameter uncertainty. As a result, the derived controllers are more aware of task sensitivities to uncertainty, yielding high reliability without compromising performance. Experimental results demonstrate up to 3$\times$ improved robustness across a range of contact-rich manipulation tasks under broad parametric uncertainty, outperforming existing model-based control methods.

</details>


### [132] [RLFTSim: Realistic and Controllable Multi-Agent Traffic Simulation via Reinforcement Learning Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19033)
*Ehsan Ahmadi,Hunter Schofield,Behzad Khamidehi,Fazel Arasteh,Jinjun Shan,Lili Mou,Dongfeng Bai,Kasra Rezaee*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RLFTSim is a reinforcement-learning fine-tuning framework that improves realism and controllability of traffic simulators by aligning rollouts with real data, achieving state-of-the-art realism with high sample efficiency on Waymo data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Supervised open-loop training struggles to capture dynamic, multi-agent interactions in driving scenarios, leading to realism gaps and limited controllability. A sample-efficient RL-based fine-tuning approach is needed to align simulator behavior with real-world distributions while enabling goal-conditioned control.

Method: Start from a pre-trained simulator, define a reward balancing fidelity to real data distributions with controllability (via goal conditioning), and optimize with reinforcement learning using a low-variance, dense reward signal. Evaluate on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset, compare to heuristic fine-tuning methods, and demonstrate distillation of controllability.

Result: RLFTSim achieves improved realism and state-of-the-art realism metrics on Waymo Open Motion, with significantly reduced sample requirements due to the low-variance dense reward. It also demonstrates effective distillation of traffic-simulation controllability through goal conditioning.

Conclusion: RLFTSim directly addresses realism alignment in traffic simulation and enables goal-conditioned controllability; the approach offers sample-efficient RL fine-tuning and strong empirical results, suggesting broad applicability to data-driven, multi-agent simulation tasks.

Abstract: Supervised open-loop training has been widely adopted for training traffic simulation models; however, it fails to capture the inherently dynamic, multi-agent interactions common in complex driving scenarios. We introduce RLFTSim, a reinforcement-learning-based fine-tuning framework that enhances scenario realism by aligning simulator rollouts with real-world data distributions and provides a method for distilling goal-conditioned controllability in scenario generation. We instantiate RLFTSim on top of a pre-trained simulation model, design a reward that balances fidelity and controllability, and perform comprehensive experiments on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset. Our results show improvements in realism, achieving state-of-the-art performance. Compared with other heuristic search-based fine-tuning methods, RLFTSim requires significantly fewer samples due to a proposed low-variance and dense reward signal, and it directly addresses the realism alignment issue by design. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach for distilling traffic simulation controllability through goal conditioning. The project page is available at https://ehsan-ami.github.io/rlftsim.

</details>


### [133] [Guiding Neuro-Symbolic Scenario Generation with Spatio-Temporal Logic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19038)
*Lorenzo Bonin,Francesco Giacomarra,Luca Bortolussi,Jyotirmoy V. Deshmukh,Francesca Cairoli*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-based multi-agent trajectory generator guided by Spatio-Temporal Logic (STREL) for targeted, safety-critical driving scenario generation, enabling differentiable satisfaction and gradient-based optimization to stress-test autonomous driving (AD) systems within learned data distribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional brute-force real-world data collection is expensive and inefficient for capturing rare, safety-critical edge cases essential for validating AD robustness; a targeted, interpretable, scalable approach is needed.

Method: Combine a multi-agent trajectory-generation diffusion model with STREL specifications that encode safety and realism. Satisfaction monitoring is differentiable, enabling gradient-based search. At inference, optimize over the DM latent space to maximize STREL satisfaction, generating plausible yet safety-critical scenarios within the learned data distribution.

Result: Enables efficient generation of highly plausible but safety-critical multi-agent driving scenarios that lie within the learned data distribution, improving stress-testing coverage without brute-force data collection.

Conclusion: STRELGen provides a flexible, interpretable, and scalable framework for safety-critical scenario generation to stress-test autonomous driving systems, addressing the limitations of traditional data collection.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of autonomous driving (AD) technologies has outpaced the development of robust safety evaluation methods. Conventional testing relies on exposing AD systems to vast numbers of real-world traffic scenes -- a brute-force approach that is prohibitively expensive and statistically ineffective at capturing the rare, safety-critical edge cases essential for validating real-world robustness. To address this fundamental limitation, we introduce STRELGen, a scalable framework for the targeted generation of safety-critical driving scenarios. STRELGen synergistically combines a multi-agent trajectory-generation diffusion model (DM) with Spatio-Temporal Logic (STREL) specifications that encode complex safety and realism properties through a highly interpretable formalism. Crucially, monitoring satisfaction levels of these specifications is differentiable, enabling gradient-based search. At inference time, we optimize directly over the DM latent space to maximize STREL formula satisfaction. The result is efficient generation of highly plausible yet safety-critical multi-agent scenarios that lie within the learned data distribution. STRELGen thus provides a flexible, interpretable, and powerful tool for stress-testing autonomous driving systems, moving beyond the limitations of brute-force data collection.

</details>


### [134] [Neural Operators for Design-Space Surrogate Modeling of Tendon-Actuated Continuum Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19104)
*Branden Frieden,James M. Ferguson,Alan Kuntz,Varun Shankar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Surrogate modeling for tendon-driven continuum robots via neural operator learning that generalizes across designs. Four architectures (two DeepONet variants and two Fourier Neural Operator variants) are trained on simulation data to map design parameters and tendon actuation to configurations, enabling fast, design-wide generalization for control and design optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuum robots in constrained environments require accurate, real-time models. Physics-based models are computationally heavy and may be inaccurate due to unmodeled effects; standard learning-based methods often do not generalize beyond the trained robot. A single surrogate that generalizes across a broad class of designs is highly desirable for real-time control, planning, and design optimization.

Method: Cast as an operator-learning problem. Inputs: robot design parameters and tendon actuation; Outputs: resulting configurations. Develop four neural-operator architectures—two DeepONet variants and two Fourier Neural Operator variants—and train them on simulation data to predict configurations. Evaluate generalization across designs.

Result: All four architectures achieve good predictive accuracy and enable fast generalization across designs, demonstrating that operator learning can serve as an effective, generalizable surrogate for continuum robot mechanics in the design space. This supports fast modeling for control, planning, and design optimization in surgical and industrial contexts.

Conclusion: Neural operator learning provides a viable, generalizable surrogate for tendon-driven continuum robot mechanics across a design family, enabling rapid design-space exploration and real-time control.

Abstract: Continuum robots enable dexterous manipulation in constrained environments, but require accurate and efficient models for real-time manipulation and control. Traditional physics-based models can be computationally expensive and may suffer from inaccuracies due to unmodeled effects, while current learning-based methods often generalize poorly beyond the specific robot on which they are trained. We present a formulation of surrogate modeling for tendon-driven continuum robots as an operator learning problem that maps robot design parameters and tendon actuation inputs to resulting configurations. This formulation enables a single trained model to generalize across a large class of robot designs. We develop four novel neural operator architectures--two based on Deep Operator Networks (DeepONets) and two based on Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs)--and train them on simulation data to predict robot configurations. All architectures achieve good accuracy while allowing for fast and accurate generalization across designs. Our results demonstrate that operator learning provides an effective and generalizable surrogate for continuum robot mechanics in the design space, enabling fast modeling for control, planning, and design optimization in surgical and industrial applications.

</details>


### [135] [CosFly: Plan in the Matrix, Fly in the World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19120)
*Hanxuan Chen,Xiangyue Wang,Songsheng Cheng,Ruilong Ren,Jie Zheng,Shuai Yuan,Tianle Zeng,Hanzhong Guo,Binbo Li,Kangli Wang,Ji Pei*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CosFly introduces a modular, CARLA-based aerial tracking pipeline with a 7-step world-to-sensor flow and a large CosFly-Track dataset (250 trajectories, ~100k rendered images) for dynamic target tracking across diverse environments; supports fixed-FOV zoom, 6-DOF annotations, and two planning paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a scalable, unified simulation and data foundation for aerial tracking with multimodal perception, bridging 3D maps, planning, and sensor data, and enabling evaluation of planning paradigms and dynamic target tracking in diverse settings.

Method: A 7-step pipeline that converts complex 3D maps into structured obstacle representations for planning, then renders trajectories back into multi-modal sensor data (RGB, depth, semantics) with 6-DOF drone poses and natural-language navigation cues. Features configurable fixed-FOV zoom via camera intrinsics. Includes 3D map export, grid simplification, planning for pedestrians and drones, multi-modal rendering with 6-DOF annotations, quality inspection, and teacher-student caption generation. Evaluates two planning paradigms: a conventional two-stage front-end/backend flow and a direct gradient-based single-objective formulation. The public CosFly-Track release comprises 250 validated trajectories and ~100,000 rendered images with complete 6-DOF pose annotations.

Result: Demonstrates a modular, CARLA-based pipeline that produces synchronized, multi-modal data and high-quality annotations from 3D maps, enabling dynamic target tracking and UAV navigation research. The CosFly-Track dataset provides substantial coverage (250 trajectories, ~100k images) across urban, highway, rural, forest, and coastal environments, with implications for evaluation of planning strategies and perception models.

Conclusion: CosFly and CosFly-Track establish a scalable infrastructure for aerial-ground collaborative research in dynamic tracking and navigation, supporting multi-modal perception and evaluation across diverse environments, and enabling analysis of different planning paradigms (two-stage vs gradient-based) within a unified simulation-to-dataset framework.

Abstract: We present CosFly, a box-structured planning and multimodal simulation pipeline for aerial tracking, together with CosFly-Track, a large-scale UAV dataset for dynamic target tracking across diverse environments including urban centers, highways, rural landscapes, forests, and coastal towns. In our current implementation on CARLA, CosFly provides a modular 7-step construction pipeline that converts complex 3D worlds into structured obstacle representations for planning, then projects the resulting trajectories back into multi-modal sensor data -- including RGB images, high-precision depth maps, and semantic segmentation masks -- paired with natural language navigation instructions. A key feature is the support for configurable fixed-FOV zoom levels (one FOV setting drawn per trajectory and held constant throughout), enabling simulation of various focal lengths through camera-intrinsic adjustments. The pipeline covers the complete workflow from 3D map export through grid simplification, pedestrian and drone trajectory planning, multi-modal rendering with 6-DOF pose annotations, quality inspection, and teacher-student caption generation. We analyze two trajectory-planning paradigms for aerial target tracking: a conventional two-stage pipeline with front-end candidate generation and backend refinement, and a direct gradient-based formulation that optimizes multiple tracking constraints in a single objective. The public CosFly-Track release contains 250 validated trajectories and approximately 100,000 rendered images with complete 6-DOF drone pose annotations (position x, y, z and orientation yaw, pitch, roll). Together, the pipeline and dataset establish a scalable foundation for aerial-ground collaborative research, supporting dynamic target tracking, UAV navigation, and multi-modal perception across diverse environments.

</details>


### [136] [Automatically Improving Simulation Physics for Articulated Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19136)
*Anh-Quan Pham*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces 'interaction-readiness' for articulated objects in simulation, with a quantitative evaluation framework and a simulator-in-the-loop, multi-modal refinement pipeline to derive physically consistent assets from incomplete 3D data, improving simulation stability and downstream learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D asset datasets lack physical properties necessary for stable, realistic interaction; crafting simulation-ready articulated objects is labor-intensive, hindering scalable robot learning.

Method: Proposes a quantitative evaluation framework decomposing interaction-readiness into measurable components; develops a simulator-in-the-loop, multi-modal pipeline that fuses geometric, visual, and semantic information to infer and refine physical properties of objects, iteratively using simulator feedback.

Result: Experiments across diverse articulated objects and tasks show object quality directly affects simulation stability, interaction behavior, and policy performance; refined objects yield more stable and realistic dynamics, improving reliability of downstream learning and evaluation.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the necessity of physical realism for articulated objects in simulation and offers a scalable, multi-modal refinement approach guided by simulator feedback to construct such objects at scale.

Abstract: Simulation is a central tool for scalable robot learning, but its effectiveness depends on the quality of object assets. While modern 3D datasets provide rich geometric and kinematic representations, they typically lack the physical properties required for stable and realistic interaction, requiring significant manual effort to construct simulation-ready articulated objects. In this thesis, we introduce interaction-readiness, which characterizes whether an object can be reliably simulated under manipulation. We propose a quantitative evaluation framework that decomposes interaction-readiness into measurable components, enabling systematic analysis of object quality and revealing failure modes not captured by conventional evaluation. We further present a multi-modal, simulator-in-the-loop approach for generating interaction-ready articulated objects from incomplete 3D assets. The method integrates geometric, visual, and semantic information to infer physical properties and refines them through iterative simulator feedback to improve physical consistency. Experiments across diverse articulated objects and manipulation tasks show that object quality directly impacts simulation stability, interaction behavior, and policy performance. Objects refined by our method exhibit more stable and realistic dynamics, enabling more reliable downstream learning and evaluation. Overall, this thesis demonstrates the importance of physical realism for articulated objects in simulation and introduces a practical multi-modal refinement approach, guided by simulator feedback, for constructing such objects at scale.

</details>


### [137] [COBALT: Crowdsourcing Robot Learning via Cloud-Based Teleoperation with Smartphones](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19138)
*Ayush Agarwal,Ansh Gandhi,Jeremy A. Collins,Omar Rayyan,Aryan Sarswat,Ranjani Koushik,Masoud Moghani,Ajay Mandlekar,Animesh Garg*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: COBALT is a scalable teleoperation platform enabling multi-user, device-agnostic demonstrations for scalable imitation learning, featuring real- and simulated-world support, low latency, real-time quality metrics, and a large crowdsourced dataset (7500+ demonstrations, 50+ hours across nine countries).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale, high-quality demonstration data is a bottleneck for scaling imitation learning in robotic manipulation; existing data collection is costly and limited in scale.

Method: Vectorized environments with a load-balanced infrastructure; in-memory data cache and efficient video streaming for synchronous control and rendering; supports dozens of users at 20 Hz with end-to-end latency under 100 ms per GPU (up to 8 users per GPU); demonstrates 256 simulated clients across 8 GPUs; comprehensive user studies comparing phone-based teleoperation to specialized hardware; real-time metrics for automatic filtering of suboptimal demonstrations; structured training curriculum; crowdsourced data collection via smartphones across nine countries over five days (7500+ demonstrations, 50+ hours).

Result: Demonstrates stable, scalable operation across hardware and nodes; phone-based teleoperation performs comparably to or better than specialized hardware for data collection; large-quality dataset validated by training state-of-the-art imitation learning algorithms.

Conclusion: COBALT enables scalable, accessible data collection for imitation learning in robotics, demonstrating data quality and enabling large-scale evaluation of IL algorithms; crowd-sourced smartphone data collection across diverse settings suggests broad applicability.

Abstract: The scarcity of large-scale, high-quality demonstration data remains a bottleneck in scaling imitation learning for robotic manipulation. We present COBALT, a teleoperation platform designed to democratize robot learning at scale both in simulation and in the real world. By leveraging vectorized environments, our scalable, load-balanced infrastructure supports concurrent teleoperation by multiple users on a single GPU, yielding a significant reduction in teleoperation cost. Operators can connect from nearly anywhere on Earth using commonly available devices, including single or dual smartphones, VR headsets, 3D mice, and keyboards. An inmemory data cache and efficient video streaming keep control and rendering synchronous, sustaining dozens of concurrent users at 20 Hz with sub-100 ms end-to-end latency for up to 8 concurrent users per GPU. We also demonstrate stable operation supporting 256 simulated clients across 8 GPUs, underscoring the system's ability to scale across hardware and within individual servers. We perform a comprehensive user study showing that phone-based teleoperation performs comparably to or better than specialized hardware, enabling faster, more ergonomic data collection. To ensure data quality, COBALT logs a suite of real-time metrics to automatically filter suboptimal demonstrations. We further demonstrate that a structured user training curriculum significantly improves data collection quality. Guided by insights from our user study, we crowdsource the collection of a large-scale, high-quality pilot dataset with 7500+ demonstrations (50+ hours) collected with smartphones across nine countries over five days. We validate the dataset's quality by training state-of-the-art imitation learning algorithms. Please visit \href{https://cobalt-teleop.github.io/}{cobalt-teleop.github.io} for more details.

</details>


### [138] [A Heuristic Approach for Performance Tuning in RL-based Quadrotor Control via Reward Design and Termination Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19166)
*Fausto Mauricio Lagos Suarez,Akshit Saradagi,Vidya Sumathy,George Nikolakopoulos*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RL-based quadrotor control with tunable performance via dual-bandwidth exponential rewards, achieving baseline critically damped tracking and two tunable modes (acrobatic/inspection) with PPO in 6M steps; evaluation shows tunable, accurate tracking across random initial conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In infrastructure inspection and similar tasks, precise, controlled maneuvers are essential. This requires tunable performance beyond fast-navigation RL policies.

Method: Introduce a novel reward structure using dual bandwidth exponentials to enforce a baseline critically damped (CD) response with low steady-state error. Train policies with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and apply episode truncation. Develop three policies (baseline, acrobatic, inspection) and tune via intuitive heuristic rules (reward weights and exponential coefficients).

Result: Policy set achieving tunable performance in position and yaw tracking across 100 trials. Baseline yields CD-like response with ~2% steady-state error; acrobatic and inspection modes adjust settling time (faster or slower) while preserving the baseline behavior.

Conclusion: Reward shaping with dual exponentials and controlled termination enables tunable, accurate quadrotor control via RL. The approach provides intuitive guidelines for trading speed and precision and shows sample-efficient achievement (6M steps) in PPO training.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL)-based quadrotor control policies have achieved impressive performance in tasks such as fast navigation in cluttered environments and drone racing, where the focus is on speed and agility. However, in several applications, such as infrastructure inspection, it is critical to achieve precise, controlled maneuvers with tunable performance. In this article, we present a novel heuristic approach to achieve tunable performance in RL-based Quadrotor control through reward design and termination conditions. We present a novel reward structure containing dual bandwidth exponentials that achieves a baseline critically damped response in setpoint tracking, with low steady-state errors. When trained with a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm, in conjunction with episode truncation conditions, the desired performance is achieved in 6 million time steps in a sample-efficient manner. In order to tune the performance about the baseline behavior, we present intuitive heuristic rules to adjust the reward weights and exponential coefficients to achieve faster (acrobatic-like) and slower (inspection-like) settling time performance, while retaining the baseline critically damped response and approximately 2\% steady-state error. We evaluate the three RL policies (baseline, acrobatic, and inspection) across 100 trials and show accurate and tunable performance in position and yaw tracking from random initial conditions, thereby demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed heuristic approach.

</details>


### [139] [Aerial Inspection Behaviors via RL-based Quadrotor Control for Under-canopy Forest Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19202)
*Fausto Mauricio Lagos Suarez,Akshit Saradagi,Vidya Sumathy,Viswa Narayanan Sankaranarayanan,George Nikolakopoulos*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An end-to-end RL-based quadrotor controller is integrated within a hierarchical navigation stack (TSP planning + RRT* collision avoidance) to enable safe, long-range under-canopy forest inspection with view-pose tracking, demonstrated across five target scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of autonomous aerial inspection under forest canopy, requiring precise low-level motor control (view-pose tracking) and safe high-level path planning, suitable for long-range missions.

Method: Train an end-to-end RL policy mapping state to motor RPMs to achieve view-pose tracking; employ a TSP planner to determine visitation order of inspection regions; between regions, use RRT* to generate collision-free paths that respect the RL policy's tracking limits; evaluate on a known forest map with five inspection regions.

Result: The approach demonstrates that an RL-based motor-level stabilizing controller, backed by a navigation guidance layer, can serve effectively as the low-level execution module for under-canopy forest inspections across five scenarios.

Conclusion: Hierarchical integration of end-to-end RL control with TSP/RRT* planning is viable for safe, long-range autonomous forest inspection; a known-map assumption is a noted limitation and future work could address unknown or dynamic environments.

Abstract: This paper addresses the problem of using a deep Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based low-level Quadrotor controller within an autonomous Quadrotor navigation stack for aerial inspection missions in under-canopy forest environments. Specifically, the article presents an end-to-end (mapping states to RPMs) Quadrotor control policy that achieves inspection view-pose tracking (simultaneous position and yaw reference tracking), which is crucial for various target inspection behaviors and point-to-point navigation in forests. To ensure safe and reliable deployment of the end-to-end RL controller in long-range missions, this article utilizes a higher navigation guidance layer comprising of a Traveling Salesman Problem planner (TSP) and a Rapidly-exploring Random Tree Star (RRT*) planner. Over a known map of a forest and a set of user-specified inspection regions, the TSP planner finds the optimal visitation sequence. Between two target regions, collision-free paths that respect the tracking limitations of the lower end-to-end RL policy are generated by an RRT* planner. Through five target inspection scenarios, this article demonstrates that an RL-based motor-level stabilizing controller, supported by a navigation guidance layer, can be used effectively as the low-level inspection execution module for under-canopy forest inspection missions.

</details>


### [140] [CLUE: Adaptively Prioritized Contextual Cues by Leveraging a Unified Semantic Map for Effective Zero-Shot Object-Goal Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19206)
*Taeyun Kim,Alvin Jinsung Choi,Dasol Hong,Hyun Myung*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CLUE adaptively fuses room-context and object-context using LLM-derived associations to guide exploration in zero-shot object-goal navigation, yielding robust performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contextual cues from rooms and nearby objects provide varying predictive power for different targets; treating them uniformly leads to inefficient exploration. The work aims to exploit commonsense knowledge to weigh cues adaptively.

Method: Estimate target-room type associations with an offline large language model (LLM), compute adaptive weights between room cues and object cues, and build a unified semantic value map that guides exploration. Integrates multi-viewpoint verification and a context-informed exploration strategy.

Result: In simulation and real-world tests, CLUE outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in success rate (SR) and success weighted by path length (SPL), demonstrating improved efficiency and practicality.

Conclusion: Adaptive fusion of contextual room and object information via commonsense knowledge yields robust, efficient zero-shot navigation and broader applicability in real-world settings.

Abstract: Zero-shot object-goal navigation (ZSON) is a challenging problem in robotics that requires a comprehensive understanding of both language and visual observations. Contextual cues from rooms and objects are critical, but their relative importance depends on the target: some objects are strongly tied to specific room types, while others are better predicted by nearby co-located objects. Existing methods overlook this distinction, leading to inefficient and inaccurate exploration. We present CLUE, a novel navigation framework that adaptively balances the use of contextual rooms and objects by leveraging commonsense knowledge extracted from an offline large language model (LLM). By estimating a target's association with room types using LLM, the agent prioritizes room cues for predictable objects and object cues for those with weak room associations. Our framework constructs a unified semantic value map that integrates both types of contextual information, adaptively weighted by the target's ambiguity to guide exploration. Combined with multi-viewpoint verification and an exploration strategy informed by contextual cues, CLUE achieves robust and efficient navigation. Extensive experiments in simulation and real-world deployments show that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both success rate (SR) and success weighted by path length (SPL), demonstrating its effectiveness and practicality for real-world navigation tasks.

</details>


### [141] [Graph Neural Planning and Predictive Control for Multi-Robot Communication-Constrained Unlabeled Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19209)
*Manohari Goarin,Yang Zhou,Giuseppe Loianno*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hierarchical framework combines a Graph Attention Planner (GATP) with a decentralized NMPC to solve unlabeled multi-robot motion planning: GATP provides subgoals while NMPC enforces safety under nonlinear dynamics; validated on simulations and real quadrotors with robustness to delays and on-board decentralized inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph-based planners offer scalable coordination but rely on oversimplified dynamics and simulations, failing to capture dynamic feasibility and communication constraints present in real deployments; there is a need for a framework that integrates cooperative subgoal generation with safe, nonlinear control.

Method: A two-level approach where GATP (graph attention-based planner) generates intermediate subgoals through multi-robot cooperation, and a decentralized Nonlinear Model Predictive Controller (NMPC) enforces safety and feasibility under nonlinear dynamics and actuation limits; the communication is minimized and planning is executed on-board.

Result: The framework is tested in both simulation and real-world quadrotor experiments. It shows improved generalization to larger robot teams, robustness to communication delays up to 200 ms, and practical feasibility via decentralized on-board inference.

Conclusion: A hierarchical planning-and-control architecture that couples attention-based multi-robot planning with decentralized NMPC achieves safer, more scalable, and robust unlabeled motion planning in realistic settings, enabling on-board deployment and tolerance to latency.

Abstract: The multi-robot unlabeled motion planning problem of concurrently assigning robots to goals and generating safe trajectories is central in many collaborative tasks. Recent Graph Neural Network methods offer scalable decentralized solutions but rely on simplified dynamics and simulation environments, overlooking key challenges of real-world deployment such as dynamic feasibility and communication constraints. To address these gaps, we propose a hierarchical framework that combines a Graph ATtention Planner (GATP) with a decentralized Nonlinear Model Predictive Controller (NMPC). GATP provides intermediate subgoals through multi-robot cooperation, and the NMPC enforces safety under nonlinear dynamics and actuation constraints. We evaluate our framework in both simulation and real-world quadrotor experiments. Thanks to attention mechanisms and minimal communication requirements, we demonstrate improved generalization to larger teams, robustness to communication delays up to 200 ms and practical feasibility with decentralized on-board inference.

</details>


### [142] [Bilateral Teleoperation with Compliant 6-DOF Pose-and-Force Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19255)
*Yue Feng,Weicheng Huang,I-Ming Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hardware-agnostic, low-cost Cartesian bilateral teleoperation using Delta6 SEA end-effectors with decoupled time scales; leader uses damping admittance, follower uses stiffness-damping impedance; stable under delays and packet loss on Lite6/FR3 at 150 Hz.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on expensive rigid 6-DOF force/torque sensors and tightly coupled hardware; enable stable, hardware-agnostic bilateral teleoperation across heterogeneous arms with robustness to communication imperfections.

Method: WOS middleware with Delta6 SEA end-effectors on both sides; leader implements damping-only admittance with a 6-D biquad notch; follower implements a stiffness-damping impedance via a position-based outer loop with PID wrench-to-pose mapping; three decoupled time scales (hardware I/O, mid-rate impedance/admittance, low-rate teleoperation messages); tested on Lite6/FR3 at 150 Hz with delays up to 120±40 ms and 1% packet loss; evaluation includes contact stiffness tracking and passivity-style energy signature.

Result: Stable tracking under delays up to 120±40 ms and 1% packet loss; matches prescribed virtual stiffness in contact; favorable cumulative energy in passivity-style tests.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a viable, hardware-agnostic, low-cost bilateral teleoperation framework with decoupled control loops and time scales, capable of handling heterogeneous arms and moderate communication imperfections while maintaining stability and contact performance.

Abstract: Existing bilateral teleoperation platforms still rely on costly rigid six-axis force/torque sensors, tightly coupled leader-follower hardware, and kilohertz control loops. We present a Cartesian bilateral framework built on the hardware-agnostic WinGs Operating Studio (WOS) middleware, in which a low-cost compliant 6-DOF pose-and-force sensing end-effector, Delta6, is mounted on both sides so that each manipulator behaves as an end-effector 6-DOF series elastic actuator (SEA). The leader runs a damping-only admittance loop with a 6-D biquad notch filter; the follower realizes a stiffness-damping impedance through a position-based outer loop with a PID wrench-to-pose mapping. Three time scales (hardware I/O, mid-rate impedance/admittance, low-rate teleoperation messages) are explicitly decoupled, enabling the same application to drive heterogeneous arms. On a Lite6/FR3 testbed at 150 Hz, the system tracks stably under delays up to $120\pm40$ ms and 1% packet loss, matches the prescribed virtual stiffness in contact, and shows a favorable cumulative energy signature in passivity-style tests.

</details>


### [143] [DEFLECT: Delay-Robust Execution via Flow-matching Likelihood-Estimated Counterfactual Tuning for VLA Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19294)
*Yixiang Zhu,Yonghao Chen,Rui Meng,Jingyu Guo,Jiaxiang Zou,Zijie Yang,Taowen Wang,Xinyu Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DEFLECT is an offline post-training refinement that converts inference latency into a label-free preference signal to upgrade asynchronous Vision-Language-Action (VLA) stacks, yielding robust gains in high-latency regimes and real-robot tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Asynchronous VLA inference causes prediction–execution misalignment: actions are conditioned on pre-inference observations but executed in drifted states. Long inference cycles (up to seven steps) degrade performance (rollover). A label-free, offline method is needed to extend the usable latency envelope without online rollouts or human labels.

Method: DEFLECT performs offline post-training refinement by constructing counterfactual fresh/stale action pairs from a frozen reference policy. These pairs are scored under the deployment-time conditioning via an implicit flow-matching likelihood-ratio surrogate, without human labels, reward models, or online rollouts. It acts as a near drop-in upgrade to existing async-VLA stacks by turning latency into a preference signal.

Result: In high-latency regimes (5–7 control steps), DEFLECT yields a +6.4 percentage-point increase in success rate. When transferred to a real-scale VLA at the longest delay, it achieves a +4.6 percentage-point gain. It also delivers consistent improvements on two real-robot tasks: a bimanual conveyor pick-and-place and a reactive whack-a-mole.

Conclusion: DEFLECT substantially extends the usable latency envelope of asynchronous VLA control and provides a label-free, offline refinement that improves robustness to latency without online data collection or human supervision.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies are typically deployed with asynchronous inference: the robot executes a previously predicted action chunk while the model computes the next one. This creates a prediction-execution misalignment: the chunk is conditioned on the observation taken before inference began, but executes in a physical state that has already drifted forward by several control steps; naive asynchronous rollover collapses from 89% to under 1% on Kinetix as the inference cycle covers up to seven control steps. We introduce DEFLECT, a fully offline post-training refinement that applies as a near drop-in upgrade to existing async-VLA stacks by converting latency itself into a label-free preference signal: counterfactual fresh/stale action pairs are constructed from a frozen reference policy and scored under the deployment-time conditioning via an implicit flow-matching likelihood-ratio surrogate, with no human labels, reward models, or online rollouts. DEFLECT substantially extends the usable delay envelope of async VLA control, with +6.4 success-rate gain in the high-latency regime (5-7 control steps), +4.6 when transferred to a real-scale VLA at the longest delay, and consistent improvements on two real-robot tasks (a bimanual conveyor pick-and-place and a reactive whack-a-mole).

</details>


### [144] [ContextFlow: Hierarchical Task-State Alignment for Long-Horizon Embodied Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19314)
*Shuhan Guo,Kun Zhang,Haifei Liu,Xingyu Gao,Yongqi Zhang,Yaqing Wang,Quanming Yao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ContextFlow provides an inspectable alignment framework for long-horizon embodied agents by modeling task stages as explicit contracts, converting runtime observations into evidence, and applying scoped updates to maintain task-frontier coherence across planning, monitoring, memory, and execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As agents delegate to stronger executors, the bottleneck shifts from local skill execution to maintaining a coherent task frontier. Task-state misalignment can cause unsupported handoffs, stage lock, executor-context mismatch, and unnecessary replanning.

Method: Model stages as explicit contracts, transform runtime observations into evidence packets, and apply scoped updates (continue, refine, transfer, promote, repair) to align planning, evidence, memory, and execution. Preserve local closed-loop control with specialist executors while making task-frontier alignment explicit and auditable.

Result: Experiments and demonstration traces on long-horizon embodied tasks show that evidence-grounded scoped updates can diagnose and mitigate recurring task-state failures, validating the effectiveness of ContextFlow.

Conclusion: ContextFlow enables auditable, contract-based task-frontier alignment, balancing specialist executors’ autonomy with explicit, inspectable coordination to reduce misalignment-related failures in long-horizon tasks.

Abstract: Long-horizon embodied agents increasingly delegate navigation, search, approach, and manipulation to specialist executors. As these executors become stronger, the main bottleneck shifts from local skill execution to maintaining a coherent task frontier across planning, monitoring, memory, and execution. We study task-state misalignment, a task-level consistency failure in which the planner's active stage, runtime evidence, remembered context, and delegated executor no longer justify the same next-step decision. This failure can lead to unsupported handoffs, stage lock, executor-context mismatch, and unnecessary replanning. We propose ContextFlow, an inspectable alignment framework that represents stages as explicit contracts, converts runtime observations into evidence packets, and applies scoped updates including continue, refine, transfer, promote, and repair. ContextFlow keeps specialist executors responsible for local closed-loop control while making task-frontier alignment explicit and auditable. Experiments and demonstration traces on long-horizon embodied tasks illustrate how evidence-grounded scoped updates diagnose and mitigate recurring task-state failures.

</details>


### [145] [Beyond Waypoints: Dual-Heatmap Grounding for Cross-Embodiment Semantic Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19420)
*Kaijie Yun,Yue Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces a Vision-Language Dual-Heatmap framework for in-FOV semantic navigation that replaces point regression with a navigation- and facing-heatmap pair, enabling continuous reachability signals and orientation constraints; backed by synthetic data and multimodal prompts; achieves state-of-the-art performance among ~8B baselines and improves cross-embodiment transfer and safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Point-based regression for navigation poorly handles spatial uncertainty and non-traversable targets, causing brittle, unsafe executions. There is a need to convert abstract semantic intent and interleaved text/image prompts into executable local goals that respect feasibility, safety, and orientation constraints, enabling robust cross-robot deployment.

Method: A unified Vision-Language model outputs two dense heatmaps: a navigation affordance heatmap indicating continuous reachable regions and a facing heatmap encoding orientation constraints. The heatmaps act as a differentiable semantic potential field feeding downstream local planners. The authors build an automated, foundation-model-assisted synthetic data pipeline and a comprehensive simulation benchmark, with ablations (e.g., feature fusion) and cross-robot evaluations (Jetbot, H1, Aliengo).

Result: The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance among comparable 8B baselines. Explicit heatmap predictions significantly improve the Affordance Rate (AR) and place targets in executable free space, mitigating the brittleness of point regression and enabling transfer across embodiments.

Conclusion: Heatmap-based semantic navigation provides a robust, transferable path from high-level prompts to executable actions, integrates naturally with local planners, and advances safe cross-embodiment navigation by offering a continuous, differentiable representation of feasibility and orientation constraints.

Abstract: Grounding open-ended semantic instructions into physically executable local goals is a fundamental challenge in human-robot interaction. While existing navigation frameworks often regress deterministic waypoints, this rigid formulation collapses spatial uncertainty and frequently targets non-traversable object centers, leading to severe execution failures. In this work, we focus on the practical setting of in-FOV semantic navigation, where a robot receives concise, interleaved multimodal (text and image) prompts. To bridge the gap between abstract semantic intent and physical reachability, we propose a unified Vision-Language framework that abandons single-point regression in favor of a Dual-Heatmap representation. Our framework predicts a navigation affordance heatmap that captures continuous reachable regions, coupled with a facing heatmap for orientation constraints. These dense outputs inherently function as a differentiable semantic potential field, integrating seamlessly with downstream local planners. To support this paradigm, we build a fully automated, foundation-model-assisted synthetic data pipeline and establish a comprehensive simulation benchmark. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance among comparable 8B baselines. Crucially, a feature-fusion study and simulation studies across diverse robot embodiments (Jetbot, H1, Aliengo) reveal that explicit heatmap prediction drastically improves the Affordance Rate (AR). By placing targets reliably in executable free space, our framework effectively mitigates the brittleness of point regression, offering a transferable path toward safe cross-embodiment semantic navigation.

</details>


### [146] [Neuromorphic Control of a Flapping-Wing Robot on Resource-Constrained Hardware](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19430)
*Rim El Filali,Chenrui Feng,Chao Gao,Weibin Gu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Hierarchical neuromorphic control using two lightweight SNNs on an ESP32 enables fully onboard, untethered flight of a butterfly-inspired FWMAV; achieves stable tracking with reduced latency and power versus an ANN baseline, demonstrating viable energy-efficient onboard control without specialized hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome nonlinear dynamics and SWaP constraints in flapping-wing micro air vehicles by leveraging low-power, low-latency neuromorphic computation for onboard state estimation and control.

Method: Two lightweight Spiking Neural Networks: one estimates state from raw sensory feedback; the other modulates a Central Pattern Generator for wing actuation. Trained via imitation learning and deployed onboard on an ESP32 (~$5).

Result: Stable pitch and heading tracking in untethered real-world flight. Inference latency reduced from 1059 μs to 680 μs (~36% faster). Inference power reduced from 0.033 W to 0.027 W (~18% saving) compared to a conventional ANN baseline.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the first fully onboard neuromorphic control for autonomous flight of a FWMAV and shows spike-based computation can meet SWaP constraints without specialized hardware, enabling energy-efficient autonomy.

Abstract: Flapping-Wing Micro Aerial Vehicles (FWMAVs) provide exceptional maneuverability and aerodynamic efficiency but pose significant challenges for onboard control due to nonlinear dynamics and stringent Size, Weight, and Power (SWaP) constraints, as exemplified by a butterfly-inspired robot less than 30 gram. To this end, we present a hierarchical neuromorphic control framework that enables fully onboard, closed-loop flight on a widely available, resource-constrained ESP32 microcontroller with a unit cost of approximately $5. Specifically, our method deploys two lightweight Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) onboard: one for state estimation from raw sensory feedback and another for control via modulation of a Central Pattern Generator (CPG) for wing actuation. Trained by imitation learning, the system achieves stable pitch and heading angle tracking during untethered real-world flight. Experimental results further reveal that the SNN-based controller reduces latency by 36% (1059us to 680us) and power by 18% (0.033W to 0.027W) for inference compared to the conventional Artificial Neural Network (ANN) baseline, demonstrating the viability of spike-based computation without specialized hardware. To the best of our knowledge, this work constitutes the first demonstration of fully onboard neuromorphic control for autonomous flight of a FWMAV, highlighting the potential of SNNs to enable energy-efficient autonomy under stringent SWaP constraints. Visual abstract: http://bit.ly/4nI8ECY

</details>


### [147] [Self-assembling Modular Aerial Robot for Versatile Aerial Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19431)
*Junichiro Sugihara,Masaki Kitagawa,Jinjie Li,Yunong Li,Takuzumi Nishio,Kei Okada,Moju Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Modular LEGION units enable in-flight self-assembly for cooperative aerial manipulation, forming end-to-end flying manipulators with zero-clearance interlocks; enabling switching between nimble flight and collective manipulation, demonstrated through pushing, pulling, rotating, grasping, and carrying in outdoor settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the trade-off between nimble navigation of small aerial robots and robust aerial manipulation of larger platforms by introducing reconfigurable, self-assembling modular robots.

Method: Use multiple identical aerial units with docking interfaces at both ends; achieve autonomous in-flight docking to form a multi-unit manipulator; control contact forces/torques to maintain a zero-clearance interlock; enable morphological switching between individual flight and collective manipulation; demonstrate core manipulation primitives and outdoor operation.

Result: Multiple units autonomously dock in flight; zero-clearance interlocks maintained; reliable aggregation and articulated motion outdoors; self-reconfigurability enables morphological switching between nimble flight and collective manipulation; demonstrated primitives include pushing, pulling, rotating, grasping, and carrying.

Conclusion: Self-organization of modular aerial robots enables swarms to actively participate in their environment, broadening aerial physical interaction capabilities and expanding the scope of aerial manipulation.

Abstract: Multirotor aerial robots excel at maneuvering in three-dimensional space, and recent advances enable nimble navigation in cluttered and confined environments, especially for small airframes. By contrast, platforms built for high-altitude work tend to be larger to deliver high thrust for stable physical interaction with the environment. However, these conflicting design requirements create a long-standing trade-off between nimble navigation and robust aerial manipulation. Here, we present LEGION units, which are reconfigurable modular aerial robots capable of in-flight self-assembly for cooperative manipulation, drawing inspiration from the self-organized collectives formed by ants. Each unit retains nimble maneuverability while joint-equipped docking interfaces at both ends enable end-to-end self-assembly into a flying manipulator. We show that multiple units autonomously dock in flight; once latched, they maintain a zero-clearance interlock by controlling the contact force and torque, enabling reliable aggregation and articulated motion even outdoors. We further show that self-reconfigurability enables morphological switching between nimble individual flight and collective articulated manipulation, while realizing core in-flight manipulation primitives including pushing, pulling, rotating, grasping, and carrying. LEGION's self-organization enables aerial robots, especially in swarms, to shift from passive observers to active participants in their environment, broadening the scope of aerial physical interaction.

</details>


### [148] [Closed-Loop Hybrid Digital Twin Platform for Connected and Automated Vehicle Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19490)
*Kanglong Quan,Zhebing Xia,Linfeng Jiang,Hao Yu,Ziheng Qiao,Dapeng Dong,Dongyao Jia*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Real-time hybrid digital twin for CAV validation by tightly coupling CARLA-SUMO co-simulation with a physical test site via low-latency V2X; middleware links a real vehicle as a shadow car and translates virtual commands to CAN messages; photogrammetry and cloud-edge enable scalable multi-user operation; results show stable sync and low-latency closed-loop control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between scalable simulation and real-world fidelity for validating connected and automated vehicles, capturing dynamic interactions with field data and hardware-in-the-loop constraints.

Method: Integrate CARLA-SUMO co-simulation with a physical test site through a custom middleware that maps the real CAV’s kinematic state to a shadow vehicle within the simulation and converts virtual control commands into CAN messages for closed-loop control; enable low-latency V2X communication; use photogrammetry for full-scale asset reconstruction and deploy a cloud-edge architecture for scalable, multi-user operation.

Result: Demonstrates stable synchronization between real and simulated components, effective closed-loop control with low latency, and practicality for multi-scenario CAV verification.

Conclusion: The platform delivers high-fidelity, scalable CAV validation by integrating real vehicles, high-fidelity simulators, and field data, enabling efficient multi-scenario verification prior to real-world deployment.

Abstract: Comprehensive and efficient validation of connected and automated vehicles (CAVs) is critical prior to real-world deployment. While simulation-based testing offers scalability, existing approaches often lack seamless integration with real vehicles and field data, limiting their fidelity in capturing dynamic, real-world interactions. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes a novel real-time hybrid digital twin platform. Its core innovation lies in the tight coupling of a high-fidelity CARLA-SUMO co-simulation with a physical test site and vehicle via a low-latency Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication link. A custom-developed middleware serves as the critical bridge, synchronizing a real CAV's kinematic state as a shadow vehicle in the simulation and translating virtual control commands into chassis-actuating Controller Area Network (CAN) messages for closed-loop control. Detailed implementation includes using photogrammetry for full-scale asset reconstruction and a cloud-edge collaborative architecture for scalable, multi-user operation. Experimental results demonstrate stable synchronization and effective closed-loop control with low latency, confirming the platform's practicality for multi-scenario CAV verification.

</details>


### [149] [CANINE: Coaching Visually Impaired Users for Interactive Navigation with a Robot Guide Dog](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19501)
*Cunjun Yu,Zishuo Wang,Anxing Xiao,Linfeng Li,David Hsu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CANINE is an automated coaching system that trains users for interactive navigation with a robot guide dog through personalized, adaptive verbal feedback. It uses a two-level architecture: high-level knowledge tracing to prioritize sub-skills, and low-level error inference via foundation models to generate targeted corrections. In a controlled study with blindfolded participants, CANINE improves learning efficiency and navigation performance versus generic instructions, with retention over two weeks and supportive case studies for real users.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The main challenge is subtle, complex human-robot coordination in robot-guided navigation. Generic verbal instructions are insufficient for effective learning; tailored coaching is needed to enable independent use and practical deployment.

Method: Two-level architecture: (1) high level uses knowledge tracing to track learner proficiency across sub-skills and prioritize training on weakest areas; (2) low level observes each practice episode, uses foundation models to infer error causes, and generates targeted verbal corrections adaptively. Evaluation includes a controlled study with blindfolded participants, a retention study after two weeks, and an exploratory case study with a visually impaired user; project page provided.

Result: CANINE significantly improves both learning efficiency and final navigation performance compared to generic verbal instructions. The retention study shows lasting skill improvement after two weeks. The case study confirms effectiveness with a visually impaired user and highlights design considerations for real-world deployment.

Conclusion: CANINE demonstrates effective automated coaching for interactive navigation with a robot guide dog, achieving adaptive, personalized training and improving learning outcomes. The work supports real-world deployment, while suggesting directions for deployment-ready refinements and broader evaluation.

Abstract: Robot guide dogs offer navigation assistance that greatly expands the independent mobility of the visually impaired, but their effective use requires subtle human-robot coordination that is difficult for users to learn from generic verbal instructions. To tackle this challenge, we present CANINE, an automated coaching system that trains users for interactive navigation with a robot guide dog, through personalized, adaptive verbal feedback. CANINE decomposes a complex coordination task into sub-skills and operates at two levels. At the high level, it decides what to train by tracking the learner's proficiency across sub-skills using knowledge tracing and prioritizing training on the weakest areas. At the low level, CANINE decides how to train each sub-skill by observing each human practice episode, using foundation models to infer the underlying causes of errors, and generating targeted verbal corrections adaptively. A controlled study with blindfolded participants, treated as a proxy population for quantitative evaluation, demonstrates that CANINE significantly improves both learning efficiency and final navigation performance compared to generic verbal instructions. We further validate CANINE through a retention study and an exploratory case study. The retention study shows lasting skill improvement after two weeks. The case study confirms CANINE's effectiveness in training a visually impaired user, while revealing additional design considerations for real-world deployment. Both are well aligned with the findings of the controlled study. Project page: https://cunjunyu.github.io/project/canine/

</details>


### [150] [ARC-RL: A Reinforcement Learning Playground Inspired by ARC Raiders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19503)
*Carlo Romeo,Andrew D. Bagdanov*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ARC-RL introduces four MuJoCo morphologies inspired by ARC Raiders (18-DoF tall hexapod Queen, 12-DoF Bastion, 18-DoF Tick, 12-DoF Leaper) with a unified observation, action space, and a single multi-component reward; includes hand-crafted CPg demonstrators and offline-to-online baselines to study RL methods under morphological diversity and animation-style constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a stylized yet morphologically diverse benchmark for legged locomotion that bridges game NPC design and robotics RL, enabling controlled comparisons of online and data-augmented RL algorithms across diverse morphologies.

Method: Four MuJoCo continuous-control environments with shared observation/action templates and a fixed closed-form reward function whose morphology-specific variation is limited to weightings; add hand-crafted Central Pattern Generator demonstrators as fixed references; perform controlled experiments comparing standard online algorithms (SAC, SPEQ, SOPE-EO) and data-augmented variants (SACfD, SPEQ-O2O, SOPE) to assess robustness to morphology and animation-style constraints.

Result: Empirical study characterizing how each RL paradigm copes with morphological diversity and animation-style constraints; findings suggest varying benefits of offline-to-online data across methods, informing the choice of algorithm and data-augmentation strategy for stylized morphologies.

Conclusion: ARC-RL provides a unified, stylized benchmarking playground for legged RL, enabling systematic evaluation of online vs. data-augmented methods and yielding insights into how morphology and animation constraints affect learning and transfer; the inclusion of CPg demonstrators offers priors for offline data collection.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning for legged locomotion has matured into a stack of multi-component reward functions and physics-engine benchmarks whose morphologies are uniformly derived from real commercial hardware. Game NPCs, however, are bound by stylistic constraints absent from sim-to-real robotics and routinely take the form of creatures with no real-robot counterpart. We introduce ARC-RL, a suite of four MuJoCo continuous-control environments featuring robotic morphologies inspired by the bestiary of ARC Raiders: the 18-DoF tall hexapod Queen, the 12-DoF armoured hexapod Bastion, the 18-DoF compact hexapod Tick, and the 12-DoF quadruped Leaper. All four robots share a unified observation template, action convention, simulation cadence, and a single closed-form multi-component reward function whose only per-morphology variation lives in a small set of weights and parameters. The reward fuses a velocity-tracking tent, a healthy survive bonus, a phase-locked gait-compliance bonus/cost pair, action regularisers, three safety penalties, and a posture anchor; no motion-capture data enters the reward at any point. We additionally provide hand-crafted Central Pattern Generator demonstrators per morphology, which serve both as fixed expert references and as sources of prior data for offline-to-online training. On this playground, we conduct a controlled empirical study comparing standard online algorithms (SAC, SPEQ, SOPE-EO) and methods augmented with prior data (SACfD, SPEQ-O2O, SOPE), and characterise how each paradigm copes with the playground's morphological diversity and animation-style stylistic constraints.

</details>


### [151] [SafeAlign-VLA: A Negative-Enhanced Safe Alignment Framework for Risk-Aware Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19524)
*Kefei Tian,Yuansheng Lian,Kai Yang,Xiangdong Chen,Shen Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SafeAlign-VLA introduces negative data into vision-language-action autonomous driving to improve safety in long-tail scenarios through counterfactual labeling and a two-stage training with contrastive anchors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end planners excel in common cases but struggle with safety-critical long-tail scenarios. Existing VLA methods primarily use positive demonstrations, limiting understanding of risky behaviors and safety boundaries. Incorporating negative samples can provide explicit failure feedback and boundaries.

Method: 1) Counterfactual safety pairing to generate structured safety labels and counterfactual positive trajectories in risky scenarios. 2) Negative-enhanced supervised fine-tuning for failure feedback and trajectory correction. 3) Anchor-based group relative policy optimization using positive/negative trajectories as contrastive anchors to steer sampling and penalize high-risk behaviors via group-relative advantages.

Result: NAVSIM v1 testset: 89.1 PDMS, +1.3% over baseline without negative data. DeepAccident: collision rate 3.36%; language accuracy 84.2%; risk prediction accuracy 85.8%.

Conclusion: The proposed negative-enhanced safe alignment framework effectively improves safety and robustness in VLA-based autonomous driving, validated on two benchmarks.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving systems excel in common scenarios but struggle with safety-critical long-tail cases. Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are promising due to their strong reasoning capabilities. However, most VLA-based approaches rely on positive expert demonstrations, rarely exploiting negative samples, leading to insufficient understanding of risky behaviors and safety boundaries. To address this limitation, we propose SafeAlign-VLA, a unified negative-enhanced safe alignment framework that incorporates negative data into supervised learning and reinforcement learning. First, we develop a counterfactual safety pairing paradigm to generate structured safety labels and counterfactual positive trajectories from risky scenarios via counterfactual reasoning. Then, a two-stage training strategy is adopted: negative-enhanced supervised fine-tuning for failure feedback and trajectory correction, followed by anchor-based group relative policy optimization that uses positive and negative trajectories as contrastive anchors to steer sampling and penalize high-risk behaviors via group-relative advantages. Experiments on NAVSIM and DeepAccident validate the proposed framework. SafeAlign-VLA achieves 89.1 PDMS on the NAVSIM v1 testset, improving over the baseline without negative data by 1.3%. On DeepAccident, it reduces the collision rate to 3.36%, while achieving 84.2% language accuracy and 85.8% risk prediction accuracy. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed negative-enhanced safe alignment framework for safe and robust autonomous driving.

</details>


### [152] [Learning-Accelerated Optimization-based Trajectory Planning for Cooperative Aerial-Ground Handover Missions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19562)
*Jingshan Chen,Bochen Yu,Henrik Ebel,Peter Eberhard*

Main category: cs.RO
