TL;DR: A learning-augmented trajectory planning framework uses a decoupled encoder-decoder LSTM to predict cooperative UAV-UGV handover trajectories, providing warm starts for a centralized optimizer, yielding substantial speedups with high success rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centralized trajectory optimization ensures dynamic feasibility and task-level optimality but is computationally expensive, limiting real-time applicability. A data-driven surrogate can provide informative initial guesses to accelerate convergence.

Method: Train a neural surrogate planner with decoupled encoder-decoder LSTMs to map task specifications to coordinated handover trajectories; these predictions serve as warm starts for a downstream centralized optimizer, accelerating convergence to feasible solutions.

Result: Benchmarks show more than a threefold speedup and a 100% optimization success rate compared with cold-start optimization, demonstrating effective synergy between learning-based inference and model-based refinement for heterogeneous multi-robot systems.

Conclusion: A learning-augmented planning framework enables fast and reliable trajectory generation for cooperative multi-robot missions by combining data-driven predictions with centralized optimization.

Abstract: This paper presents a learning-augmented trajectory planning framework for cooperative unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) and unmanned ground vehicle (UGV) handover missions. While centralized trajectory optimization ensures dynamic feasibility and task optimality, its high computational cost limits real-time applicability. We propose a neural surrogate planner utilizing decoupled encoder-decoder long short-term memory (LSTM) networks to generate coordinated handover trajectory predictions from the task specifications. These predictions serve as informed warm starts for the downstream centralized optimizer, thereby accelerating convergence to dynamically feasible solutions. Benchmark evaluations demonstrate that the learning-augmented planning framework achieves more than a threefold speedup and 100% optimization success rate compared to cold start optimization. The results indicate that combining data-driven inference with model-based refinement enables fast and reliable trajectory generation for heterogeneous multi-robot systems.

</details>


### [153] [PAPO-VLA: Planning-Aware Policy Optimization for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19580)
*Peizheng Guo,Jingyao Wang,Changwen Zheng,Wenwen Qiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PAPO-VLA introduces planning-aware optimization for vision-language-action models by identifying planning actions via action-trajectory analysis and weighting their influence with causal sufficiency/necessity, improving task reliability while maintaining trajectory optimization through GRPO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA policies function as both planner and executor; reliability hinges on correctly identifying and emphasizing planning actions in closed-loop manipulation, which existing methods rarely do.

Method: Identify planning actions by jointly considering action variation and trajectory outcome; estimate their importance using causal sufficiency and causal necessity; integrate this importance into GRPO advantage estimation to modulate optimization emphasis.

Result: Empirical experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of PAPO-VLA in improving planning-action awareness and overall policy performance.

Conclusion: Planning-aware optimization improves the reliability of VLA policies by explicitly recognizing and weighting planning actions, while preserving trajectory-level optimization.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models show promising ability in language-guided robotic tasks. However, making VLA policies reliable remains challenging, because a manipulation task is completed through closed-loop interaction, where each action affects subsequent execution. To analyze this problem, we revisit VLA policy during execution and argue that a VLA policy acts both as a planner, which makes task-oriented decisions that change the direction of execution, and as an executor, which realizes these decisions through dense continuous actions. This view suggests that improving VLA reliability requires particular attention to planning actions. Existing optimization methods can imitate actions or improve complete trajectories, but they usually do not explicitly identify planning actions or measure their importance for task success. To address this issue, we propose Planning-Aware Policy Optimization for VLA models (PAPO-VLA). PAPO-VLA first identifies planning actions by jointly considering action variation and trajectory outcome, then estimates their importance through causal sufficiency and causal necessity, and finally incorporates this importance into GRPO advantage estimation. In this way, more important planning actions receive stronger optimization emphasis, while the whole trajectory is still optimized by trajectory-level feedback. Experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of PAPO-VLA.

</details>


### [154] [Implicit Action Chunking for Smooth Continuous Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19592)
*Bosun Liang,Shuo Pei,Zirui Chen,Chuanzhi Fan,Chen Sun,Yuankai Wu,Huachun Tan,Yong Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DWS is an implicit action chunking framework that achieves temporal coherence in RL without enlarging the action space by using a dual-window design (execution window for smoothness and value window for TD targets) and a lightweight actor-side regularizer, outperforming SOTA baselines on control benchmarks including DM Control and autonomous driving tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explicit action chunking scales the policy output with horizon length and conflicts with step-wise, reactive control, leading to optimization difficulties and safety issues due to high-frequency oscillations. There is a need for temporal abstraction that preserves a fixed action space while ensuring smooth, safe control.

Method: Dual-Window Smoothing (DWS) introduces two synchronized windows: an execution window that enforces smooth physical control via deterministic modulation, and a value window that aligns TD targets over the horizon to correct critic bias from open-loop execution. It also adds a lightweight actor-side temporal regularizer based on first-order action differences to promote global continuity, enabling implicit action chunking without increasing action dimensionality.

Result: DWS outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on benchmarks such as the DeepMind Control Suite and industrial energy management tasks. In complex vision-based autonomous driving, it yields smoother control, reduced jitter, safer behavior, and a 100% success rate.

Conclusion: DWS successfully bridges temporal abstraction and reactive step-wise control by enabling implicit action chunking with a fixed action space, achieving improved stability, safety, and performance across diverse control domains.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning often produces high-frequency oscillatory control signals that undermine the safety and stability required for physical deployment. Explicit action chunking addresses this by predicting fixed-horizon trajectories but scales the policy output dimension proportionally with the horizon length, leading to optimization difficulties and incompatibility with standard step-wise interaction. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes Dual-Window Smoothing (DWS), an implicit action chunking framework for smooth continuous control. Unlike explicit methods, DWS enforces temporal coherence without expanding the action space. It uses a dual-window design: an execution window that ensures physical smoothness through deterministic modulation, and a value window that aligns temporal-difference targets over the horizon to correct critic bias caused by open-loop execution. DWS also includes a lightweight actor-side temporal regularizer based on first-order action differences to promote global continuity. This design effectively bridges the gap between temporal abstraction and reactive step-wise control. Experiments on benchmarks including the DeepMind Control Suite and industrial energy management tasks show that DWS outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines. In complex vision-based autonomous driving tasks, DWS achieves smoother control, safer behavior with reduced jitter, and attains a 100% success rate.

</details>


### [155] [MCNav: Memory-Aware Dynamic Cognitive Map for Zero-shot Goal-oriented Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19594)
*Jingyu Li,Zhe Liu,Wenxiao Wu,Li Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MCNav presents a memory-aware navigation framework using a dynamic cognitive map to exploit past observations, with goal re-validation and missed-goal re-exploration, plus blacklist and double-check; achieves SOTA on HM3Dv1/2 for instance-level navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot navigation methods often model the entire environment and rely on large language models, emphasizing exploration of new regions but underutilizing information from previously visited areas, leading to failures when targets are missed or misidentified.

Method: Introduces a dynamic cognitive map that stores efficiently queryable information about relevant objects in explored regions; two memory-aware exploration strategies: 1) goal re-validation to re-assess previously seen objects; 2) missed goal re-exploration to estimate the boundary of target presence in explored regions; stabilized by a blacklist mechanism to avoid repeated errors and a double-check mechanism for high-confidence confirmation.

Result: Empirical evaluation on HM3Dv1 and HM3Dv2 across three tasks shows state-of-the-art performance, especially in instance-level goal navigation.

Conclusion: Memory-aware navigation with a dynamic cognitive map substantially improves robustness and accuracy by reusing prior observations and preventing recurring errors.

Abstract: Navigating to instance-level targets in complex environments is a challenging problem. Many existing zero-shot methods achieve strong performance by modeling the entire environment and leveraging large language models for scene understanding. However, such strategies primarily focus on exploring new regions while lacking a deeper exploitation of information from previously explored areas. Consequently, when targets are missed or misidentified within previously visited regions, navigation failures occur frequently. To address these limitations, we propose MCNav, a memory-aware navigation framework with a dynamic cognitive map. This map stores efficiently queryable information about relevant objects in explored areas. Building on this memory structure, MCNav introduces two memory-aware exploration strategies: goal re-validation, which re-assesses previously seen objects to correct matching failures, and missed goal re-exploration, which estimates the likelihood that a target is present in an explored region from contextual cues. These strategies are further stabilized by a blacklist mechanism to prevent repeated errors and a double-check mechanism for high-confidence confirmation. We evaluate MCNav on the HM3Dv1 and HM3Dv2 datasets across three different tasks, where it achieves state-of-the-art performance, particularly on the instance-level goal navigation task.

</details>


### [156] [FlyMirage: A Fully Automated Generation Pipeline for Diverse and Scalable UAV Flight Data via Generative World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19600)
*Jinhan Li,Xijie Huang,Zhaoqi Wang,Yijin Wang,Weiqi Ge,Qiyi He,Mo Zhu,Fei Gao,Yuze Wu,Xin Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FlyMirage presents a scalable, automated pipeline for aerial Vision-Language Navigation data: LLM-driven scene design, 3D Gaussian Splatting world instantiation, automated exploration, semantic capture, and UAV-feasible trajectory planning to produce large, diverse, photorealistic datasets for embodied navigation models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aerial VLN datasets struggle to simultaneously achieve scale, diversity, and realism. Real scenes are costly; simulations often lack fidelity or diversity. There is a need for automated, scalable pipelines that deliver photorealistic, semantically rich data with feasible flight dynamics to train embodied navigation models.

Method: Use large language models as an environment designer to promote scene diversity. A generative world model creates high-fidelity 3D Gaussian Splatting scenes from these designs. Automate scene exploration and semantic information acquisition. Integrate a dynamically feasible planner for UAV trajectories. Generate large-scale, diverse, photorealistic aerial VLN data with realistic flight dynamics.

Result: A large-scale, diverse, and photorealistic aerial VLN dataset is generated, with dynamically feasible flying trajectories, intended to advance the development of next-generation embodied navigation models.

Conclusion: FlyMirage enables scalable, automated data generation for aerial VLN, reducing human labor and enabling realistic, diverse datasets with feasible flight dynamics. This supports progress in embodied navigation models, though future work should evaluate realism-to-task transfer, quantify diversity and coverage, and compare against existing simulators.

Abstract: In the field of Vision-Language Navigation (VLN), aerial datasets remain limited in their ability to combine scale, diversity, and realism, often relying on either costly real-world scenes or visually limited simulations. To address these challenges, we introduce FlyMirage, a highly scalable and fully automated data generation pipeline for aerial VLN. Our approach leverages large language models (LLM) as an environment designer to promote scene diversity, paired with a generative world model that instantiates these designs into high-fidelity 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) scenes. To substantially reduce human labor and ensure the feasibility of flight data, FlyMirage automates scene exploration and semantic information acquisition, and further integrates a dynamically feasible planner for uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) trajectory generation. Utilizing this toolchain, we generate a large-scale, diverse, and photorealistic aerial VLN dataset, with dynamically feasible flying trajectories, designed to support the development of next-generation embodied navigation models.

</details>


### [157] [HEAT: Heterogeneous End-to-End Autonomous Driving via Trajectory-Guided World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19631)
*Hoonhee Cho,Giwon Lee,Jae-Young Kang,Hyemin Yang,Heejun Park,Kuk-Jin Yoon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A trajectory-driven, unified end-to-end driving model with a latent world model improves cross-domain performance and enables a single model to work across heterogeneous datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain shifts across cities, sensors, and traffic patterns cause conflicting learning signals in joint multi-domain training; there is a need for domain-invariant representations of driving intent to enable robust cross-domain generalization.

Method: Introduce a trajectory-driven learning paradigm organized around planning trajectories; incorporate a world model that predicts future latent features conditioned on ego actions to improve feature consistency and mitigate domain biases.

Result: Empirical evaluation on nuScenes, NAVSIM, and the Waymo end-to-end dataset shows substantial improvements over existing methods across all domains; demonstrates that a single unified model can be trained on heterogeneous datasets while maintaining strong per-domain performance; code will be released publicly.

Conclusion: A step toward scalable real-world deployment of multi-domain, end-to-end autonomous driving by unifying training across heterogeneous domains with trajectory- and world-model-based representations.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving has emerged as a compelling alternative to traditional modular pipelines by directly mapping raw sensor data to driving actions. While recent approaches achieve strong performance on single-domain datasets, their performance degrades significantly when trained jointly across multiple heterogeneous domains. In practice, however, autonomous systems must operate across diverse environments with heterogeneous distributions, including different cities, sensor configurations, and traffic patterns, without domain-specific retraining. This gap highlights a key challenge in multi-domain learning: domain-specific variations across heterogeneous domains introduce conflicting learning signals, driving models toward compromised solutions that are suboptimal across domains. To address this, we propose a trajectory-driven learning paradigm that organizes training around planning trajectories, enabling the model to capture domain-invariant representations of driving intent. Furthermore, we incorporate a world model that predicts future latent features conditioned on ego actions, improving feature consistency and mitigating domain-induced biases. We evaluate our approach on three benchmarks, nuScenes, NAVSIM, and the Waymo end-to-end dataset, and show substantial improvements over existing methods across all domains. Our results demonstrate that a single unified model can be trained on heterogeneous datasets while maintaining strong performance within each domain, highlighting a step toward scalable real-world deployment. We will make our code publicly available.

</details>


### [158] [RoVLA: Multi-Consistency Constraints for Robust Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19678)
*Jingzhou Luo,Yifan Wen,Yongjie Bai,Xinshuai Song,Yang Liu,Liang Lin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoVLA proposes a robust VLA framework with three invariance constraints—Instructional Consistency, Evolutionary Consistency, and Observational Consistency—to enforce end-to-end stability under language, trajectory, and observation perturbations, showing improved robustness on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models rely on superficial correlations and struggle with observation changes and paraphrased instructions; end-to-end invariance constraints are needed to improve generalization and robustness.

Method: Introduce multi-consistency constraints IC, EC, OC during training; enforce stable grounding for paraphrased instructions, coherent action evolution, and pre/post perturbation prediction consistency; end-to-end policy learning with invariance losses; evaluate on LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin 2.0, real-world tasks; code release.

Result: RoVLA consistently outperforms strong baselines and demonstrates superior robustness across diverse task and observation shifts; results support effectiveness of multi-consistency learning for robust embodied control.

Conclusion: Multi-consistency learning is a viable path to robust VLA policies; enforcing semantic, temporal, and observational invariances reduces reliance on superficial cues and improves generalization; potential to extend to other embodied tasks.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong performance on embodied manipulation, yet they remain brittle under visual observation changes, paraphrased language instructions, and compounded perturbations. This limitation suggests that existing methods still rely heavily on shallow correlations in the training distribution, rather than learning stable couplings among task semantics, environment states, and action generation. Although recent efforts improve robustness through larger-scale training, post-training adaptation, or enhanced predictive modeling, they rarely enforce invariance-oriented consistency within the end-to-end policy itself. To address this issue, we propose RoVLA, a robust vision-language-action framework with multi-consistency constraints. RoVLA enforces consistency under three complementary transformations: instruction semantics, trajectory evolution, and observation perturbation. Specifically, Instructional Consistency (IC) promotes stable grounding under semantically equivalent instruction rewrites, Evolutionary Consistency (EC) preserves coherent action intent throughout the generation process, and Observational Consistency (OC) improves robustness to visual and proprioceptive perturbations by enforcing consistent predictions before and after targeted disturbances. By explicitly modeling these invariances during training, RoVLA reduces reliance on superficial correlations and improves robustness and generalization. Experiments on LIBERO-Plus, RoboTwin 2.0, and real-world manipulation tasks show that RoVLA consistently outperforms strong baseline methods and exhibits superior robustness under diverse task and observation shifts. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of multi-consistency learning for robust embodied control. Codes will be available at https://github.com/HCPLab-SYSU/RoVLA.

</details>


### [159] [D-CLING: Prior-Preserving Depth-Conditioned Fine-Tuning for Navigation Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19690)
*Shintaro Nakaoka,Takayuki Kanai,Kazuhito Tanaka*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel fine-tuning method for Navigation Foundation Models (NFMs) that uses a zero-initialized residual pathway to attach a trainable copy of the pre-trained backbone, inspired by ControlNet, to learn geometric cues while preserving prior knowledge. Evaluated on real-world navigation, showing robust long-horizon performance with minimal collisions and effective offline continual-learning behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome degradation of pre-trained generalization during in-domain fine-tuning for NFMs and enable efficient adaptation to novel environments or camera setups without eroding the pre-training priors.

Method: Attach a trainable copy of the pre-trained backbone via zero-initialized residual pathways, effectively creating a ControlNet-like structure that learns geometry-specific adjustments on top of the frozen or largely pre-trained features.

Result: Improved robust long-horizon navigation with fewer collisions and less human intervention in real-world tests; offline analysis indicates maintained or improved action-prediction capabilities beyond the fine-tuned data, suggesting favorable continual-learning properties.

Conclusion: The proposed DCLING-like fine-tuning approach successfully leverages large-scale pre-training for efficient in-domain geometry learning, preserving pre-trained generality while enabling better obstacle avoidance and goal-reaching in new setups.

Abstract: Navigation Foundation Models (NFMs) trained on large cross-embodied datasets have demonstrated powerful generalizability in various scenarios. Adopting in-domain fine-tuning for an NFM efficiently calibrates the visuomotor policy, promising further improvement even in a novel scenario. However, the fine-tuned models still suffer from poor obstacle avoidance or fail to properly reach the provided goals. Furthermore, model updates using a small subset of data typically erode the pre-trained prior, compromising the pre-training generalization. Consequently, fine-tuning deteriorates the capability of the model for robust and accurate navigation. In this work, we present a novel fine-tuning method that leverages large-scale pre-training while efficiently learning in novel setups, such as environments or camera configurations. In particular, inspired by ControlNet, we fine-tune an NFM by attaching a trainable copy of the pre-trained backbone using zero-initialized residual pathways, thereby learning geometric cues. This design enables the model to efficiently acquire in-domain geometry while preserving pre-trained knowledge across various behaviors. Despite its simplicity, our comprehensive evaluation of real-world navigation suggests that our proposal effectively enables robust long-horizon navigation with minimal collisions and human intervention. Additionally, our offline analysis shows that the proposed method maintains or further improves action prediction capabilities beyond the fine-tuned dataset, providing a key insight into continual learning for general navigation. The project page: https://toyotafrc.github.io/DCLING-Proj/

</details>


### [160] [Multi-Session Ground Texture SLAM in Low-Dynamic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19701)
*Kyle M. Hart,Brendan Englot*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: KL-divergence-based similarity scoring with loop-closure bias yields the best trajectory accuracy among three methods for multi-session ground-texture SLAM in low-dynamic environments; a new dataset with multi-session ground textures and ground-truth poses is provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable robust, lifelong SLAM in environments where ground texture is the sole reliable feature and changes across sessions are low but present, by evaluating methods to improve trajectory estimation and loop closure decisions.

Method: Evaluate three techniques for improving trajectory estimation in multi-session ground-texture SLAM, with KL divergence used as a similarity score and as bias for loop-closure confidence. Conduct empirical analysis on a newly introduced multi-session dataset containing ground-texture changes and high-precision pose data; perform in-depth exploration of KL divergence impact.

Result: Among the three methods, KL divergence-based similarity scoring and biasing loop-closure confidence gave the best improvement in trajectory estimation accuracy. The study provides a detailed analysis of all methods and clarifies the role of KL divergence. A new dataset with multi-session ground-texture changes and accurate poses is released for benchmarking.

Conclusion: KL-divergence-based loop-closure formulation offers the most promise for multi-session ground-texture SLAM in low-dynamic environments, and the released dataset enables community-wide evaluation and reproducibility.

Abstract: The simultaneous localization and mapping community has introduced a growing number of systems adapted for multi-session operations where the operational environment features low-dynamic changes that impact mapping, such as surface wear, weather phenomena, or seasonal change. These systems allow for lifelong operations by a robot within these environments. There is also growing interest in operations in environments where the unique ground texture is the only mapping feature available for use. These ground texture systems are not yet targeted for multi-session low-dynamic-change environments though. This work explores the impact of three different techniques on trajectory estimation accuracy in these multi-session low-dynamic ground texture environments. Of the three, the use of Kullback-Leibler Divergence, as a similarity score and a bias influencing loop closure confidence, is found to have the most success. We show an analysis of all three methods and a deeper exploration of the impact of Kullback-Leibler Divergence. We also introduce a dataset for use by the robotics community that contains multi-session images where the ground changes between sessions and also high-accuracy pose information for use in evaluation.

</details>


### [161] [KIO-planner: Attention-Guided Single-Stage Motion Planning with Dual Mapping for UAV Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19703)
*Dexing Yao,Haochen Li,Junhao Wei,Yifu Zhao,Yanxiao Li,Jiahui Xu,Jinxuan Hu,Lele Tian,Baili Lu,Zikun Li,Xu Yang,Sio-Kei Im,Dingcheng Yang,Yapeng Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An attention-guided single-stage UAV trajectory planner (KIO-planner) combines CBAM-enabled perception with a Dual Mapping framework (physical bounds activation + Geometric Safety Shield in depth-pixel space) to enforce kinodynamic feasibility without global maps, yielding low latency (~24 ms), fast speeds (up to 3.0 m/s), smoother trajectories, improved control efficiency, and larger worst-case obstacle clearance (0.76 m).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimization-based planners suffer from mapping latency and local minima in dense environments; end-to-end methods lack fine-grained geometric features and hard kinodynamic constraints, risking collisions near walls. A low-latency, safe, geometry-aware planner is needed for autonomous UAVs in confined spaces.

Method: Integrate a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) into the perception backbone to focus on critical edges and traversable space. Introduce Dual Mapping consisting of (1) physical bounds activation and (2) a deterministic Geometric Safety Shield operating in depth-pixel space to enforce kinodynamic feasibility and collision avoidance without global map fusion. Implemented in a single-stage planning framework and validated through high-fidelity simulations.

Result: KIO-planner achieves fast inference (~24 ms), speeds up to 3.0 m/s, smoother trajectories, and a 28.4% reduction in control cost compared with state-of-the-art baselines. The Dual Mapping raises the worst-case safety margin (minimum distance to obstacles) from 0.48 m to 0.76 m, indicating improved safety in highly constrained environments.

Conclusion: The proposed KIO-planner provides fast, smooth, and safer UAV navigation in confined spaces by combining attention-guided perception with a robust dual-mapping strategy that enforces kinodynamic feasibility and obstacle safety without relying on global maps.

Abstract: Autonomous UAV flight in confined, wall-dense environments requires low-latency and reliable motion planning under strict safety constraints. Traditional optimization-based planners suffer from mapping latency and easily fall into local minima when navigating through dense structural obstacles. Meanwhile, existing end-to-end learning methods struggle to extract fine-grained geometric features from raw depth images and lack hard kinodynamic constraints, leading to unpredictable collisions near walls. To address these issues, we propose KIO-planner, an attention-guided single-stage trajectory planning framework. First, we integrate a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) into the perception backbone to adaptively focus on critical structural edges and traversable space. Second, we introduce a novel Dual Mapping mechanism--comprising physical bounds activation and a deterministic Geometric Safety Shield in the depth-pixel space--to enforce kinodynamic feasibility and collision-free flight without global map fusion. Extensive high-fidelity simulated experiments demonstrate that KIO-planner enables highly agile navigation at speeds up to 3.0 m/s. Compared to the state-of-the-art baseline, KIO-planner achieves lower inference latency (approximately 24 ms) and generates significantly smoother trajectories, reducing control cost by 28.4%. Most notably, our Dual Mapping substantially increases the worst-case safety margin, measured by minimum distance to obstacles, from 0.48 m to 0.76 m, ensuring fast, smooth, and safer navigation in highly constrained environments.

</details>


### [162] [Beyond Imitation: Learning Safe End-to-End Autonomous Driving from Hard Negatives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19771)
*Junli Wang,Zhihua Hua,Xueyi Liu,Zebin Xing,Haochen Tian,Kun Ma,Hangjun Ye,Guang Chen,Long Chen,Qichao Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BeyondDrive presents a failure-aware imitation learning framework for end-to-end autonomous driving. It combines a flow-matching based negative trajectory generator, diversity-aware sampling to cover failure modes, and a Repulsive Distance Loss to create discriminative safety boundaries. Applied to Latent TransFuser, it achieves 89.7 PDMS on NAVSIMv1, outperforms prior SOTA, and shows generalization across architectures with strong zero-shot transfer on HUGSIM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard imitation learning minimizes geometric distance to expert trajectories, assuming proximity implies safety. This creates an objective mismatch where near-expert trajectories can differ in safety outcomes (recoverable vs. collision). There is a need to explicitly model safety failures and learn discriminative safety boundaries.

Method: 1) A flow matching-based negative trajectory generator synthesizes safety-critical yet expert-proximate trajectories to model safety asymmetry. 2) A diversity-aware sampling strategy mitigates mode collapse and expands coverage of diverse failure modes during negative trajectory generation. 3) A Repulsive Distance Loss attracts predictions toward expert demonstrations while repelling from hard negative trajectories, creating discriminative safety boundaries in trajectory space. The approach is evaluated on the uni-modal baseline Latent TransFuser, with NAVSIMv1 closed-loop benchmark and zero-shot transfer to HUGSIM benchmark, showing cross-architecture generalization.

Result: Achieves 89.7 PDMS on NAVSIMv1, outperforming prior state-of-the-art methods. Demonstrates effective generalization across autonomous driving architectures, including multi-modal planners, and strong zero-shot transfer to HUGSIM.

Conclusion: The framework effectively addresses the objective mismatch in imitation learning for autonomous driving by incorporating failure-aware learning components. It yields improved safety performance, robust cross-architecture generalization, and zero-shot transfer capability, suggesting broader applicability to safety-critical imitation tasks.

Abstract: Existing imitation learning methods for end-to-end autonomous driving predominantly learn from successful demonstrations by minimizing geometric deviations from expert trajectories. This paradigm implicitly assumes that spatial proximity implies behavioral safety, leading to a critical objective mismatch: trajectories with nearly identical imitation losses may exhibit drastically different safety outcomes, where one remains recoverable while the other results in collision. To address this limitation, we propose BeyondDrive, a failure-aware imitation learning framework that jointly learns from successful and failed driving behaviors. First, we introduce a flow matching-based negative trajectory generator that synthesizes safety-critical yet expert-proximate trajectories, enabling explicit modeling of safety asymmetry. Second, we develop a diversity-aware sampling strategy that mitigates mode collapse and improves coverage of diverse failure modes during negative trajectory generation. Third, we propose a Repulsive Distance Loss that simultaneously attracts predictions toward expert demonstrations while repelling them from hard negative trajectories, thereby establishing discriminative safety boundaries in trajectory space. Applied to the uni-modal baseline Latent TransFuser, BeyondDrive achieves 89.7 PDMS on the NAVSIMv1 closed-loop benchmark, outperforming prior state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, BeyondDrive generalizes effectively across different autonomous driving architectures, including multi-modal planners, and further demonstrates strong zero-shot transferability on the HUGSIM benchmark.

</details>


### [163] [Justifying bio-inspired robotics research: A taxonomy of strategies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19840)
*Margaret J. Zhang,Justin Ting,Talia Y. Moore*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Lacks systematic approach to bio-inspired design; proposes a taxonomy of motivations to guide robotics research and funding decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Historical inconsistency between motivations and methods in bio-inspired design leads to poor predictability and potential disappointment; a framework is needed to justify approaches and assess value.

Method: The authors construct and describe a taxonomy of motivations for bio-inspired design and discuss expected contributions from different approaches.

Result: A taxonomy is proposed outlining distinct motivational categories and the likely contributions associated with them.

Conclusion: The taxonomy provides a structured basis for evaluating bio-inspired designs, aligning expectations, and guiding funding and research decisions in robotics.

Abstract: For most of human history, we have not thought systematically about how and why we incorporate aspects of the natural world into our designs. The lack of a systematic approach has resulted in inconsistencies in motivations and methods that make it difficult to predict or evaluate the success of bio-inspired design. This mismatch between expectations and results can lead to disappointment when a reader considers a bio-inspired design to be superficial, weak, or incomplete. This is especially true in the field of Robotics, in which similarity to a biological system might be the driving motivation for construction. In an effort to assist robotics researchers justify their specific bio-inspired approach and to assist funding program managers with discerning the value of different bio-inspired approaches, here we propose a taxonomy of motivations for bio-inspired design and describe the potential significant contributions that are likely to result from different approaches.

</details>


### [164] [Trajectory Planning and Control near the Limits: an Open Experimental Benchmark on the RoboRacer Platform](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19881)
*Mattia Piccinini,Patrick Zambiasi,Aniello Mungiello,Mattia Piazza,Felix Jahncke,Johannnes Betz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Modular benchmarking framework for high-acceleration autonomous driving combining time-optimal planning, online velocity replanning, geometric tracking, and a model-structured NN for steering inverse dynamics; validated on a 1:10 RoboRacer with ablations showing MS-NN improves tracking and stability, online replanning boosts lap times; code and data released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a reproducible, modular platform to evaluate and compare trajectory planning and control methods under extreme acceleration, and to study interactions between components.

Method: Develop a modular suite including: (i) time-optimal raceline generation, (ii) online time-optimal velocity replanning, (iii) geometric path-tracking controllers, and (iv) a model-structured neural network (MS-NN) to learn steering inverse dynamics. Deploy on a 1:10 RoboRacer across two circuits, performing ablations with cautious vs aggressive racelines to assess individual modules and their combinations.

Result: MS-NN significantly improves steering tracking accuracy, reduces steering oscillations, and is physically interpretable; online velocity replanning improves lap times by compensating execution errors and enables higher speeds and accelerations.

Conclusion: The framework provides a reproducible benchmark for high-acceleration autonomous driving research and demonstrates clear performance gains from the MS-NN and online replanning; resulting code, datasets, and results are publicly available for future work.

Abstract: We present a modular framework to benchmark new and existing methods for trajectory planning and control in high-acceleration maneuvers that push autonomous driving to the limits. Our framework includes time-optimal raceline generation, online time-optimal velocity replanning, geometric path tracking controllers, and a new model-structured neural network (MS-NN) to learn the inverse dynamics for steering control. We deploy our framework on a 1:10-scale RoboRacer platform, using two circuits. Through several ablations with cautious and aggressive racelines, we study the performance of single modules and their combinations. We show that our MS-NN significantly improves tracking accuracy, decreases steering oscillations, and is physically interpretable. Moreover, online velocity replanning improves lap times by compensating for execution errors, and enables the vehicle to safely reach higher speeds and accelerations. To support future research, our code, datasets, videos and results are publicly available at https://roboracer-benchmark.github.io/planning_control_benchmark/.

</details>


### [165] [Beyond Action Residuals: Real-World Robot Policy Steering via Bottleneck Latent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19919)
*Dongjie Yu,Kun Lei,Zhennan Jiang,Jia Pan,Huazhe Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Z-Perturbation Reinforcement Learning (ZPRL) introduces a compact latent bottleneck (via a variational information bottleneck) to steer pretrained manipulation policies during online RL, improving sample efficiency and performance by learning residuals in latent space rather than actions or weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in adapting offline-pretrained imitation policies to online RL without destabilizing exploration or requiring full policy finetuning; mitigate dataset coverage gaps and deployment mismatch.

Method: Augment offline pretrained policy with a VIB module to extract a task-relevant latent; freeze base policy during online RL; learn residual perturbations on the latent; decoded representation conditions the frozen action generator; instantiate on flow-matching policies; evaluate on 8 sim tasks and 4 real-world tasks.

Result: ZPRL improves sample efficiency and final performance over post-training baselines; in real world, avg success rate increased by 33.7% over imitation base policies; smoother exploration than action residual method.

Conclusion: A compact, task-aligned bottleneck latent provides an effective interface for online RL adaptation of pretrained policies; suggests latent-space perturbations can be superior to action-space or weight-space adjustments.

Abstract: Pretrained imitation policies have become a strong foundation for robot manipulation, but they often require online improvement to overcome execution errors, limited dataset coverage, and deployment mismatch. A central question is therefore how reinforcement learning (RL) should adapt policies after offline pretraining. Existing lightweight methods commonly apply residual corrections directly in action space, but this often leads to noisy and poorly structured exploration. In this work, we propose Z-Perturbation Reinforcement Learning (ZPRL), an approach that steers pretrained policies through a compact bottleneck latent rather than through policy weights or output actions. During offline training, we augment the policy with a plug-and-play variational information bottleneck (VIB) module to extract a task-relevant latent interface from observation embeddings. During online finetuning, the base policy is frozen and RL learns only a residual perturbation on this latent, whose decoded representation conditions the frozen action generator. We instantiate ZPRL on flow-matching policies and evaluate it on eight simulation tasks and four real-world tasks. Across diverse manipulation settings, ZPRL improves both sample efficiency and final performance over strong post-training baselines. In the real world, ZPRL improves the average success rate on four tasks by 33.7% over imitation base policies while producing smoother exploration behaviors than an action residual counterpart. These results suggest that a compact, task-aligned bottleneck latent provides an effective interface for online RL adaptation. More videos can be found at https://manutdmoon.github.io/ZPRL/.

</details>


### [166] [RoHIL: Robust Human-in-the-Loop Robotic Reinforcement Learning Against Illumination Variations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19924)
*Shuoqin Zhang,Yixin Xiong,Xiru Gao,Kai Liu,Ke Wang,Xichuan Zhou,Zhe Hu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoHIL proposes offline fine-tuning for HIL-RL under lighting shifts using a world-model-based image relighter to synthesize relit videos, an Illumination-Retention Replay to preserve source data, and an anchored Bellman-actor regularizer to constrain drift, achieving cross-workstation illumination robustness without re-collecting data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HIL-RL systems trained in one workstation fail when environment lighting changes; collecting new demonstrations is costly; naive fine-tuning forgets source policy. A cross-domain, data-efficient solution is needed that preserves source performance while adapting to shifted lighting.

Method: Three components: (i) image relighter that synthesizes source trajectories under multiple HDRI-based virtual illumination; (ii) Illumination-Retention Replay (IRR), a data-level replay that interleaves relit adaptation transitions with original-light retention transitions to maintain Bellman coverage; (iii) anchored Bellman-actor regularizer to constrain representation and policy drift from the source policy.

Result: Across four real-robot manipulation tasks with substantial illumination variation, RoHIL significantly improves shifted-light performance where standard HIL-RL collapses, while preserving source-workstation performance; eliminates the need to re-collect data and retrain for every new workstation and environment.

Conclusion: RoHIL enables offline, data-efficient cross-workstation HIL-RL adaptation to lighting shifts by combining a relighting world model, data-level anti-forgetting replay, and an anchored policy regularizer, reducing deployment costs while maintaining source-task performance.

Abstract: Human-in-the-loop reinforcement learning systems achieve near-perfect success on the workstation where they are trained, but collapse when the same robot is moved to a workstation a few meters away due to shifts in the visual input distribution caused by new lamp positions and window light. Re-collecting demonstrations and re-running HIL on every workstation is incompatible with deployment, and naively fine-tuning on shifted-light data triggers catastrophic forgetting of the source workstation. To close this cross-domain gap, we present RoHIL, an offline fine-tuning framework that uses no extra real-robot interaction. RoHIL combines (i) a world-model-based image relighter that re-synthesises the visual stream of source-workstation trajectories under multiple virtual HDRI environments, leaving actions and rewards real; (ii) Illumination-Retention Replay (IRR), a data-level anti-forgetting mechanism that interleaves relit adaptation transitions with original-light retention transitions to preserve source-workstation Bellman coverage; and (iii) an anchored Bellman-actor regulariser that constrains representation and policy drift from the original source-workstation policy. Across four real-robot manipulation tasks under significant cross-workstation illumination variations, RoHIL substantially improves shifted-light performance where standard HIL-RL collapses, while preserving source-workstation performance, eliminating the need to re-collect data and retrain for every new workstation and environment. Project page: https://anonymous4365.github.io/RoHIL/

</details>


### [167] [TravExplorer: Cross-Floor Embodied Exploration via Traversability-Aware 3-D Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19958)
*Han Zheng,Zhe Chen,Yudong Huang,Haoran Liu,Jinghao Wang,Ming Yang,Tong Qin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TravExplorer is a cross-floor embodied exploration framework that fuses zero-shot semantic guidance with traversability-aware 3-D planning to perform cross-floor object search, using a unified memory map, traversable frontiers, and foothold-guided 3-D search; validated on large-scale sims and real-world trials, outperforming ObjectNav baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real indoor environments involve multiple floors, stairs, landings, and vertical overlaps; existing ZSON methods are largely planar/single-floor and struggle with cross-floor navigation.

Method: 1) Unified volumetric map distinguishing occupied structures and support surfaces; 2) Extract traversable frontiers from floors, stairs, landings; 3) FOV-aware active perception to resolve incomplete observations; 4) Lightweight guidance aligning open-vocabulary segmentation with faster image-to-text spatial maps; 5) Hierarchical planner for target-aware frontier touring over object hypotheses, traversable frontiers, stair landmarks; 6) Executable cross-floor motions via foothold-guided 3-D search and vertically constrained local trajectory optimization.

Result: In 4,195 simulated episodes across HM3D and MP3D, TravExplorer shows consistent advantages over representative ObjectNav baselines; 50 real-world trials on a Unitree Go2 validate cross-floor open-vocabulary target search without prior maps or intervention.

Conclusion: TravExplorer demonstrates effective cross-floor exploration by integrating semantic guidance with geometry-based planning, reduces latent semantic reasoning latency, and generalizes to real-world settings, with code release planned.

Abstract: Zero-shot Object Navigation (ZSON) has shown promise for open-vocabulary target search in unseen environments, yet most existing systems remain tied to planar representations and single-floor assumptions. These assumptions become inadequate in real buildings, where navigation involves floors, stairs, landings, and vertically overlapping spaces. This article presents TravExplorer, a cross-floor embodied exploration framework that couples zero-shot semantic guidance with traversability-aware 3-D planning. TravExplorer maintains a unified volumetric map that distinguishes occupied structures from robot-reachable support surfaces and extracts traversable frontiers from connected support surfaces, including floors, stairs, and landings. A FOV-aware active perception strategy further resolves incomplete observations during cross-floor traversal. To reduce semantic-reasoning latency, a lightweight guidance module aligns a probabilistic instance map from online open-vocabulary segmentation with a spatial value map from fast image-to-text matching. Based on these geometric and semantic memories, a hierarchical planner performs target-aware frontier touring over object hypotheses, traversable frontiers, and stair landmarks, and generates executable cross-floor motions through foothold-guided 3-D search and vertically constrained local trajectory optimization. Experiments over 4,195 simulated episodes on HM3D and MP3D demonstrate consistent advantages over representative ObjectNav baselines. Fifty real-world trials on a Unitree Go2 further validate open-vocabulary target search across single-floor and cross-floor indoor environments without prior maps or human intervention. The code will be released at https://github.com/wuyi2121/TravExplorer.

</details>


### [168] [CEER: Compliant End-Effector and Root Control as a Unified Interface for Hierarchical Humanoid Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19981)
*Xinyuan Luo,Xingrui Chen,Xunjian Yin,Hongxuan Wu,Boxi Xia,Zhuoqun Chen,Jinzhou Li,Boyuan Chen,Xianyi Cheng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CEER introduces a compliant EE-root control abstraction for modular humanoid loco-manipulation, enabling planner-compatible, compliance-aware whole-body control via a teacher-student distilled low-level policy, achieving accurate end-effector tracking and stable manipulation with plug-and-play planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manipulation is inherently contact-rich and long-horizon, requiring compliant, whole-body control. Joint-space tracking is often insufficient for complex tasks; modular, planner-friendly interfaces can enable scalable, long-horizon loco-manipulation.

Method: Define an EE-root task-space abstraction (root motion commands + end-effector targets) for compliant whole-body control within a hierarchical planner. Use a teacher-student framework to distill a general motion-tracking controller into a low-level EE-root policy. Build a hierarchical system that couples heterogeneous planners and modules through the EE-root interface, enabling varied tasks without retraining the whole-body policy.

Result: 3.3 cm end-effector tracking accuracy with reduced jerk; stable contact-rich manipulation under teleoperation; up to 70% success in simulated single-object loco-manipulation tasks in room-scale environments; demonstrated in simulation and on hardware.

Conclusion: Compliant EE-root control provides a practical, modular abstraction for humanoid loco-manipulation, enabling plug-and-play integration of diverse skills within a hierarchical planning framework.

Abstract: Humanoid robots have achieved impressive locomotion performance, yet contact-rich and long-horizon manipulation remains a major bottleneck. Manipulation is inherently contact-rich and demands compliant whole-body control for stable interaction, while its diversity and long-horizon nature favor modular, planner-compatible interfaces over joint-space tracking.
  We propose CEER, a compliant end-effector-root (EE-root) control abstraction for modular humanoid loco-manipulation within a hierarchical planning framework. CEER enables compliance-aware whole-body control in an interpretable task space defined by root motion commands and end-effector pose targets, and supports plug-and-play integration with heterogeneous high-level planners. A teacher-student framework is adopted to distill a general motion-tracking controller into a low-level policy that consumes only EE-root commands.
  We further construct a hierarchical system that integrates heterogeneous planners and task modules through the EE-root interface, enabling diverse manipulation tasks without retraining the underlying whole-body policy. Experiments in simulation and on hardware demonstrate 3.3 cm end-effector tracking accuracy with substantially reduced jerk compared to baselines, stable contact-rich manipulation under teleoperation, and up to 70% success in simulated single-object loco-manipulation tasks within a room-scale environment. These results indicate that compliant EE-root control provides a practical abstraction for humanoid loco-manipulation, enabling modular and scalable integration of diverse skills.

</details>


### [169] [Beyond Binary Success: A Diagnostic Meta-Evaluation Framework for Fine-Grained Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19986)
*He-Yang Xu,Pengyuan Zhang,Zongyuan Ge,Xiaoshuai Hao,Serge Belongie,Xin Geng,Yuxin Peng,Xiu-Shen Wei*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MetaFine is a diagnostic meta-evaluation framework to diagnose fine-grained manipulation along understanding, perception, and controlled behavior, using a compositional task graph to unify benchmarks, revealing bottlenecks in visual encoders and enabling targeted improvements; includes hybrid real-sim validation and aims to shift from ranking to diagnosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-grained manipulation requires tightly coupled local grounding, high-fidelity spatial perception, and constraint-respecting motor execution. Conventional benchmarks rely on binary success rates, inflating capabilities and masking architectural bottlenecks, which hampers real-world deployment.

Method: Construct MetaFine around a compositional task graph that absorbs heterogeneous external benchmarks into diagnostic scenarios of varying complexity under a unified protocol. Evaluate state-of-the-art vision-language-action models, perform targeted causal interventions to identify bottlenecks, and use limited real-world rollouts to calibrate scalable simulation-based estimates for stable physical benchmarking.

Result: Diagnostic evaluation exposes dimension-specific failures not visible under traditional metrics. A key bottleneck is the visual encoder's ability to preserve local spatial structure; improving it unlocks previously inaccessible manipulation capabilities without changing downstream policies. Hybrid real-sim validation enables scalable yet grounded benchmarking.

Conclusion: Shifting evaluation from ranking to diagnosis renders benchmarking actionable for repairing the layered capacities underlying physical dexterity. MetaFine and its benchmarks/resources will be publicly released at metafine.github.io.

Abstract: Fine-grained manipulation marks a regime where global scene context no longer suffices, and success hinges on the tight coupling of local attribute grounding, high-fidelity spatial perception, and constraint-respecting motor execution. However, current embodied AI benchmarks collapse these capacities into binary success rates, systematically inflating reported capabilities by up to 70% and masking the architectural bottlenecks that impede real-world deployment. We introduce MetaFine, a diagnostic meta-evaluation framework that disentangles manipulation competency along three axes: understanding, perception, and controlled behavior. Built on a compositional task graph, MetaFine absorbs heterogeneous external benchmarks and reconstructs them into diagnostic scenarios of varying complexity under a unified protocol. Evaluating state-of-the-art vision-language-action (VLA) models through this lens exposes severe dimension-specific failures invisible to conventional metrics. Through targeted causal intervention, we identify the visual encoder's ability to preserve local spatial structure as a key bottleneck for fine-grained precision: improving it directly unlocks previously inaccessible manipulation capabilities without modifying downstream policies. MetaFine further supports hybrid real-sim validation, using limited paired real-world rollouts to calibrate scalable simulation-based estimates for more stable physical benchmarking. By shifting evaluation from ranking to diagnosis, MetaFine turns benchmarking into an actionable compass for repairing the layered capacities underlying genuine physical dexterity. The MetaFine framework, benchmarks, and supporting resources will be publicly released at our project page: https://metafine.github.io/.

</details>


### [170] [Minimalist Visual Inertial Odometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19990)
*Francesco Pasti,Jeremy Klotz,Nicola Bellotto,Shree K. Nayar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A minimalist four-sensor photodiode system with IMU and a learned model can provide robust planar odometry, bypassing full camera-based VIO. By encoding speed via Gabor-filtered photodiode signals and jointly training mask parameters with a Temporal Convolutional Network in a physics-based simulator, the approach yields continuous planar trajectories with a differential-drive robot without real-world fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce resource demands of visual-inertial odometry while maintaining robust motion estimation. Demonstrate that a tiny sensing footprint (four photodiodes) can capture enough cueing about speed when combined with learning and physics-based modeling.

Method: Four downward-facing photodiodes feed signals modulated by learnable optical Gabor masks. A Temporal Convolutional Network decodes speed from these four measurements. Mask parameters and the TCN are trained jointly in a physically-grounded simulator. The IMU provides angular speed; combining these yields planar motion.

Result: A prototype sensor mounted on a differential-drive robot tracks reference ground truth across diverse indoor/outdoor settings with little to no real-world fine-tuning, demonstrating robust planar odometry from minimalist sensing.

Conclusion: Minimalist sensing, when paired with a learned decoder and physics-based training, can achieve accurate and efficient planar odometry, offering a resource-friendly alternative to camera-heavy VIO for differential-drive platforms.

Abstract: Visual-Inertial Odometry(VIO), which is critical to mobile robot navigation, uses cameras with a large number of pixels. Capturing and processing camera images requires significant resources. This work presents a minimalist approach to planar odometry, demonstrating that just four visual measurements and an IMU can provide robust motion estimation for differential-drive robots. Our key insight is that four downward-facing photodiodes that sense the world through optical Gabor masks produce signals that encode speed. Based on this, we jointly optimize the mask parameters alongside a Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) using a physically-grounded simulator. The resulting model decodes speed from just the four measurements produced by the photodiodes. Pairing these estimates with the angular speed from an IMU yields a continuous planar trajectory. We validate our approach with a prototype sensor mounted on a differential drive robot. Across diverse indoor and outdoor terrains, our system closely tracks the reference ground truth without any real-world fine-tuning. Our work shows that minimalist sensing enables efficient and accurate planar odometry.

</details>


### [171] [Topology-Optimized Pneumatic Soft Actuator: Design and Experimental Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20101)
*Sumit Mehta,Konstantinos Poulios*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: 3D nonlinear topology optimization for soft elastomeric pneumatic actuators; extends a density- and porohyperelasticity-based framework from 2D to 3D, yielding two manufacturable designs validated experimentally.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Design of soft actuators needing large deformations where nonlinear effects and fabrication constraints complicate optimization; aims to connect computational design with manufacturability.

Method: Extend existing 2D density- and porohyperelasticity-based topology optimization to 3D; optimize bending under prescribed actuation pressure with two strain limits; account for very large deformations during optimization; fabricate via stereolithography; validate numerically and experimentally.

Result: Two optimized 3D actuator designs were produced and validated both numerically and experimentally; bending performance under target pressure matched predictions.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of a 3D nonlinear topology optimization framework for designing manufacturable soft pneumatic actuators that can undergo large deformations, with successful SLA fabrication and experimental validation.

Abstract: This paper demonstrates the computational design of soft elastomeric pneumatic actuators using nonlinear topology optimization. An existing density- and porohyperelasticity-based topology optimization framework was extended from 2D to 3D and used to generate two manufacturable actuator designs, which were then studied numerically and experimentally. For both designs, the objective was to maximize the bending response for a prescribed actuation pressure under two different allowable strain limits. A key advantage of the employed topology optimization framework is that it can consistently, during the optimization, account for the very large deformations induced upon pressurization. The two optimized 3D designs were fabricated using stereolithography and experimentally tested to validate their performance.

</details>


### [172] [Hamilton--Jacobi Reachability for Spacecraft Collision Avoidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20138)
*Larry Hui,Jordan Kam,William Su,Jianshu Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HJ reachability-based collision avoidance for two satellites in the same circular orbit using RTN coordinates and planar HCW dynamics; backward reachable sets define unsafe states under worst-case disturbances; zero-sum game with bounded adversarial disturbance; integrated with supervisory hybrid control for provable safety and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide provable safety guarantees for satellite collision avoidance in close-proximity operations, leveraging rigorous reachability analysis and a supervisory control framework that can scale to multiple scenarios while complying with FCC orbital separation standards.

Method: Model relative motion with RTN coordinates under planar HCW dynamics. Formulate a zero-sum differential game with Player 1 (controlled satellite) and Player 2 (bounded adversarial disturbance with unknown intent). Compute backward reachable sets via Hamilton-Jacobi reachability to identify states from which collision is unavoidable under worst-case disturbances. Use the resulting sets within a supervisory hybrid control logic to decide when evasive maneuvers must be executed.

Result: Backward reachable sets delineate the unsafe region of the relative state where collision cannot be avoided; states outside this region admit collision-free trajectories under optimal play. The approach yields safety guarantees and integrates with a supervisory control framework to manage evasive actions, aiming for scalability to larger satellite constellations.

Conclusion: HJ reachability provides a rigorous, scalable framework for relative-safety analysis in two-satellite proximity operations. The combination of HCW dynamics, RTN framing, and a zero-sum differential game offers provable collision-avoidance guarantees and a practical path to real-time supervisory control in space environments.

Abstract: This article presents a Hamilton--Jacobi (HJ) reachability framework for a two--satellite collision avoidance problem operating in the same circular orbit, where relative motion is modeled in the radial--tangential--normal (RTN) frame using planar Hill--Clohessy--Wiltshire (HCW) dynamics. We define the target state space as unsafe relative configurations in the orbit plane corresponding to minimum separation requirements consistent with Federal Communications Commission (FCC) orbital standards. The interaction between spacecraft is formulated as a zero--sum differential game, where Player 1 is the controlled satellite and Player 2 is modeled as a bounded adversarial disturbance with unknown intent. We present the HJ formulation and compute backward reachable sets that characterize relative states from which collision cannot be avoided under worst-case disturbances, while states outside this set admit provably collision-free trajectories. These reachable sets are integrated with supervisory hybrid control logic to determine when evasive maneuvers must be initiated, enabling mathematically grounded safety guarantees for scalability.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [173] [Position: Let's Develop Data Probes to Fundamentally Understand How Data Affects LLM Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18801)
*Shiqiang Wang,Herbert Woisetschläger,Hans Arno Jacobsen,Mingyue Ji*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes data probes—synthetic sequences from defined random processes—to systematically study how data characteristics affect LLM behavior across training, tuning, alignment, and in-context learning, using information-theoretic concepts like typical sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current data-centric methods rely on expensive empirical experimentation and heuristics; there is a need for principled understanding of how data properties drive LLM performance, generalization, and robustness.

Method: Define random-process-generated synthetic sequences (data probes) and examine LLM responses across workflow stages; analyze their statistical properties with theoretical concepts (typical sets) to reveal data-characteristics effects.

Result: Conceptual framework and advocacy for a data-probe methodology; suggests a path toward foundational insights rather than relying solely on large-scale empirical datasets.

Conclusion: The data-probe approach offers a principled avenue to uncover the role of data in LLM training and inference, potentially guiding dataset design and evaluation beyond heuristic practices.

Abstract: Data is fundamental to large language models (LLMs). However, understanding of what makes certain data useful for different stages of an LLM workflow, including training, tuning, alignment, in-context learning, etc., and why, remains an open question. Current approaches rely heavily on extensive experimentation with large public datasets to obtain empirical heuristics for data filtering and dataset construction. These approaches are compute intensive and lack a principled way of understanding the essence of how specific data characteristics drive LLM behavior. In this position paper, we advocate for the need of developing systematic methodologies for generating synthetic sequences from appropriately defined random processes, with the goal that these sequences can reveal useful characteristics when they are used in one or multiple stages of the LLM workflow. We refer to such sequences as data probes. By observing LLM behavior on data probes, researchers can systematically conduct studies on how data characteristics influence model performance, generalization, and robustness. The probing sequences exhibit statistical properties that can be viewed using theoretical concepts, such as typical sets, which are generalized to describe the behaviors of LLMs. This data-probe approach provides a pathway for uncovering foundational insights into the role of data in LLM training and inference, beyond empirical heuristics.

</details>


### [174] [Operationalizing Document AI: A Microservice Architecture for OCR and LLM Pipelines in Production](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18818)
*Yao Fehlis,Benjamin Bengfort,Zhangzhang Si,Vahid Eyorokon,Prema Roman,Patrick Deziel,Devon Slonaker,Steve Veldman,Ben Johnson,Joyce Rigelo,Michael Wharton,Steve Kramer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a production-oriented microservice architecture for document understanding pipelines combining classification, OCR, and LLM-based field extraction, with empirical production insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between model research and production deployment in document understanding by providing concrete architectural patterns that scale to thousands of pages per hour.

Method: Describe design decisions: hybrid classification, separation of GPU-bound inference from CPU orchestration, asynchronous processing for IO-bound tasks, and independent horizontal scaling; implement and evaluate pipelines; use batch profiling to measure latency, throughput, and bottlenecks.

Result: A scalable microservice architecture for large-scale document pipelines; deployment experience achieving thousands of multi-page documents per hour; two key findings: OCR dominates end-to-end latency, and system concurrency is capped by shared GPU inference capacity rather than worker count.

Conclusion: Offers practical architectural patterns for productionizing document understanding models beyond academic benchmarks, guiding resource allocation and component structuring to improve latency and throughput.

Abstract: Academic research tends to focus on new models for document understanding creating a wide gap in the literature between model definition and running models at production scale. To close that gap, we present a microservice architecture that encapsulates pipelines of multiple models for classification, optical character recognition (OCR), and large language model structured field extraction as well as our experience running this pipeline on thousands of multi-page documents per hour. We describe our primary design decisions, including a hybrid classification, separation of GPU-bound inference from CPU-bound orchestration, use of asynchronous processing for the many IO-bound operations in the pipeline, and an independent, horizontal scaling strategy. Using batch profiling, we identified two surprising qualitative findings that shape production deployments: OCR, not language-model parsing, dominates end-to-end latency, and the system saturates at a concurrency determined by shared GPU-inference capacity rather than worker count. Our goal is to provide practitioners with concrete architectural patterns for building document understanding systems that work beyond the benchmark; effectively operationalizing models in production.

</details>


### [175] [Evaluating the Utility of Personal Health Records in Personalized Health AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18937)
*Rory Sayres,Kejia Chen,Ayush Jain,Matthew Thompson,Jonathan Richina,Xiang Yin,Jimmy Hu,Fan Zhang,Bob Lou,Mike Sanchez,Ines Mezerreg,Meredith Schreier,Hamsa Subramaniam,I-Ching Lee,Yugang Jia,Daniel Mcduff,Yossi Matias,Avinatan Hassidim,Dale Webster,Yun Liu,Jackie Barr,Quang Duong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PHR-contextualized LLM responses improve helpfulness across query types; introduces SHARP-based evaluation and a new error framework to identify gaps like temporal disorientation and confabulations; suggests potential safety and personalization gains but notes limitations and need for monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage patient-owned PHR data to improve LLM health Q&A while ensuring safety and accuracy; address potential confusion from complex records; fill gap in patient understanding.

Method: Use Gemini 3.0 to answer 2,257 queries drawn from three distributions; compare responses without PHR, with basic PHR summary, with full clinical notes; 1,945 de-identified PHRs; evaluate with SHARP framework and a newly developed error framework; autoraters for the full set; clinician ratings for a subset (n=95); raters aware of PHR context.

Result: Significant improvements in helpfulness with PHR context across all query types (p<0.001); potential gains in safety, accuracy, relevance, and personalization; framework identifies gaps in LLM understanding of complex PHRs (e.g., temporal disorientation, confabulations).

Conclusion: PHR data can assist a broad range of user needs; the evaluation framework provides a method to monitor gaps in LLM answers conditioned on PHR context; supports further work to realize benefits and mitigate risks.

Abstract: Patient-managed Personal Health Records (PHRs) promises to empower patients to better understand their health; but information in the record is complex, potentially hindering insights. In this study, we assess the potential of large language models (LLMs, Gemini 3.0 Flash) to provide helpful answers to user health queries, when provided clinical data from PHRs as context. A total of 2,257 user queries were drawn from 3 different distributions to represent patient questions: shorter web search queries, longer questions derived from templates of chatbot conversations, and questions patients asked to their healthcare team (patient calls). Queries were matched with de-identified PHRs (from a pool of 1,945). Gemini responses were generated (1) without PHR context; (2) with a basic summary of demographics, conditions, and medications; (3) with full, extensive clinical notes. For evaluation, we leveraged an existing rating framework (SHARP), and developed a new framework for specific error modes when interpreting PHRs. Evaluation was performed using autoraters for the full set, and with clinician ratings for a subset (n=95), with both sets of raters knowing the full PHR context. We see significant improvements in the helpfulness of answers to all question types with PHR data (p < 0.001, paired t-test). We also observe potential gains in safety, accuracy, relevance and personalization of answers. Our PHR evaluation framework further identifies gaps in LLM understanding of particular aspects of complex PHRs, such as temporal disorientation, and rare but meaningful confabulations. These results suggest potential for PHR data to help people with a wide range of user needs; and provide a framework for monitoring for gaps in LLM answers based on PHR context. This study motivates further work to assess and realize potential benefits to users from understanding their health records.

</details>


### [176] [Learn-by-Wire Training Control Governance: Bounded Autonomous Training Under Stress for Stability and Efficiency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19008)
*Anis Radianis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LBW-Guard is a bounded autonomous governance layer above AdamW that stabilizes LLM training under stress, improving final perplexity and speed, without replacing the optimizer. It maintains trainability under aggressive LR stress while baselines like gradient clipping fail to reproduce the effect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training instability and wasted compute under aggressive learning-rate, scale, and runtime stress hinder LLM training. The paper proposes a governance layer that observes telemetry and applies bounded control to the optimizer without altering the objective.

Method: Implement LBW-Guard as a bounded autonomous layer above AdamW. It monitors training telemetry, identifies instability-sensitive regimes, and applies bounded control to optimizer execution. Evaluation on a Qwen2.5-7B setup with WikiText-103, including comparisons across model sizes (3B, 7B, 14B), LR-stress tests, gradient-clipping baselines, and a TinyLlama-1B sanity check.

Result: In the 7B setting, LBW-Guard reduces final perplexity from 13.21 to 10.74 (18.7% improvement) and speeds end-to-end time from 392.54s to 357.02s (1.10x). Under LR stress, AdamW yields perplexities of 1885.24 at LR=3e-3 and 659.76 at LR=1e-3, while LBW-Guard remains trainable with perplexities 11.57 and 10.33, respectively. Gradient-clipping baselines do not reproduce these improvements.

Conclusion: Stability-sensitive LLM training can benefit from a governance plane above the optimizer. Bounded runtime control can preserve productive compute under stress while remaining distinct from optimizer replacement and local gradient suppression.

Abstract: Modern language-model training is increasingly exposed to instability, degraded runs, and wasted compute, especially under aggressive learning-rate, scale, and runtime-stress conditions. This paper introduces Learn-by-Wire Guard (LBW-Guard), a bounded autonomous training-control governance layer that operates above AdamW. Rather than replacing the optimizer update rule, LBW-Guard observes training telemetry, interprets instability-sensitive regimes, and applies bounded control to optimizer execution while preserving fixed training objectives.
  We evaluate LBW-Guard in a Qwen2.5-centered stress-and-robustness suite using WikiText-103, with Qwen2.5-7B as the empirical anchor, model-size comparisons against Qwen2.5-3B and Qwen2.5-14B, learning-rate stress tests, gradient-clipping baselines, and a no-LoRA TinyLlama-1B full-parameter sanity check. In the 7B reference setting, LBW-Guard reduces final perplexity from 13.21 to 10.74, an 18.7% improvement, while reducing end-to-end time from 392.54s to 357.02s, a 1.10x speedup. Under stronger learning-rate stress, AdamW degrades to 1885.24 final perplexity at LR=3e-3 and 659.76 at LR=1e-3, whereas LBW-Guard remains trainable at 11.57 and 10.33, respectively. Gradient-clipping baselines do not reproduce this effect.
  These results support a scoped systems conclusion that stability-sensitive LLM training can benefit from a governance plane above the optimizer. LBW-Guard provides evidence that bounded runtime control can preserve productive compute under stress while remaining distinct from optimizer replacement and local gradient suppression.

</details>


### [177] [AgentNLQ: A General-Purpose Agent for Natural Language to SQL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19010)
*Olena Bogdanov,Yeunji Jung,Chandra Dhir,Pareekshitreddy Gaddam,Saurabh Jain,Lakshmi Tumati,Vijay Parthasarathy,Anup Shirgaonkar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multi-agent NL2SQL framework with schema enrichment achieving 78.1% semantic accuracy on BIRD; optimized orchestrator and context-aware metadata improve accuracy and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
