Motivation: NL2SQL accuracy remains below human expert SQL writers; there is a need for architectures that effectively leverage LLMs with semantically enriched schemas and business rules to improve reliability and generalizability across domains.

Method: An optimized multi-agent orchestrator that plans, orchestrates, reflects, and self-corrects to generate SQL; an advanced schema enrichment method that adds context-aware metadata; evaluation on BIRD and BIRD-SQL benchmarks to demonstrate accuracy and cross-domain generalizability.

Result: Achieves 78.1% semantic accuracy on the BIRD benchmark and demonstrates generalizability across domains as shown on the BIRD-SQL benchmark.

Conclusion: Semantic enrichment of schemas combined with a reflective, multi-agent planning framework can substantially improve NL2SQL accuracy and robustness, with promising cross-domain applicability.

Abstract: Natural language to SQL (NL2SQL) conversion is an important problem for researchers and enterprises due to the ubiquitous importance of relational databases in broad-ranging practical problems. Despite the rapid advancements in the capabilities of LLMs, NL2SQL has not reached parity in accuracy with human expert SQL writers, hence needing additional improvements in NL2SQL algorithms. This study presents a new multi-agent method for NL2SQL that achieves 78.1% semantic accuracy on the BIg Bench for LaRge-scale Database (BIRD) benchmark. Our method leverages a semantically enriched representation of user-provided schema, adds user-provided business rules, and produces accurate SQL queries. The main contributions of this study are (a) We designed an optimized new orchestrator in a multi-agent solution that uses LLMs to plan, orchestrate, reflect, and self-correct to generate accurate SQL queries, (b) We developed an advanced schema enrichment method that creates context-aware metadata to improve accuracy, and (c) We demonstrated the accuracy and generalizability of the method across different domains and datasets by evaluating it on the BIRD-SQL benchmark.

</details>


### [178] [KAN-MLP-Mixer: A comprehensive investigation of the usage of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) for improving IMU-based Human Activity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19031)
*Mengxi Liu,Sizhen Bian,Vitor Fortes,Francisco Calatrava Nicolas,Daniel Geißler,Maximilian Kiefer-Emmanouilidis,Bo Zhou,Paul Lukowicz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hybrid KAN-MLP HAR model combines KAN input embedding with MLP feature mixing and a LarctanKAN classifier, achieving ~5.33% relative macro-F1 improvement over a pure-MLP baseline across eight HAR datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KANs offer precise function learning on clean data but underperform on noisy real-world data; MLPs are robust to noise and computationally efficient. The paper seeks a strategy to fuse their strengths for robust HAR in wearable sensing.

Method: Systematically explore placements of KAN modules in deep HAR networks; propose a hybrid architecture with a KAN-based input embedding layer, MLP layers for intermediate feature mixing, and a specialized LarctanKAN module for final activity classification; evaluate on eight public HAR datasets, comparing against standalone KAN and MLP baselines and integrating the hybrid approach into other state-of-the-art HAR architectures.

Result: Average macro F1 relative improvement of 5.33% over the pure-MLP model; hybrid outperforms standalone KAN and MLP baselines; the strategy consistently boosts performance when integrated into other SOTA HAR architectures.

Conclusion: A carefully orchestrated combination of KAN and MLP components yields more robust and accurate HAR models for real-world wearable sensing environments, with potential applicability to diverse HAR architectures.

Abstract: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) have demonstrated an exceptional ability to learn complex functions on clean, low-dimensional data but struggle to maintain performance on noisy and imperfect real-world datasets. In contrast, conventional multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) are far more tolerant to noise and computationally efficient. Replacing all MLP components with KANs in HAR models often degrades accuracy and computation efficiency, highlighting an open challenge: how to combine KANs' precision with MLPs' noise robustness and efficiency. To address this, we systematically explore various placements of KAN modules within deep HAR networks and propose a hybrid architecture that strategically synergizes the strengths of both paradigms, which uses a KAN-based input embedding layer, retains MLP layers for intermediate feature mixing, and introduces a specialized LarctanKAN module for final activity classification. Across eight public HAR datasets, the hybrid KAN-MLP model achieves an average macro F1 score relative improvement of 5.33\% compared pure-MLP model, significantly outperforming standalone KAN and MLP baselines. Furthermore, integrating this hybrid strategy into other state-of-the-art HAR architectures consistently boosts their performance. Our findings demonstrate that a carefully orchestrated combination of KAN, MLP, or other conventional neural components yields more robust and accurate HAR models for real-world wearable sensing environments.

</details>


### [179] [Trustworthy Agent Network: Trust in Agent Networks Must Be Baked In, Not Bolted On](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19035)
*Yixiang Yao,Yuhang Yao,Xinyi Fan,Jiechao Gao,Jie Wang,Minjia Zhang,Srivatsan Ravi,Carlee Joe-Wong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A2A networks require a design-time trust framework; a four-pillar framework is proposed to govern trust in autonomous agent-to-agent coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous LLM agents coordinating in A2A networks create systemic vulnerabilities (adversarial composition, semantic misalignment, cascading failures); existing alignment methods are insufficient.

Method: Proposes a comprehensive conceptual framework with four design pillars to situate trust in A2A systems; a vision/taxonomy paper.

Result: Conceptual framework and four pillars; no empirical results; qualitative contribution to architecture/design of trustworthy A2A networks.

Conclusion: Trustworthiness must be engineered from inception; retrofitting protocols is inadequate; adopt architectural design-time framework for robust A2A coordination.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of Large Language Models has given rise to autonomous LLM-based agents capable of complex reasoning and execution. As these agents transition from isolated operation to collaborative ecosystems, we witness the emergence of the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) network, a paradigm where heterogeneous agents autonomously coordinate to solve multi-step tasks. While these networks may offer better task performance compared to simply using one agent to complete the entire task, they introduce systemic vulnerabilities, such as adversarial composition, semantic misalignment, and cascading operational failures, that existing agent alignment techniques cannot address. In this vision paper, we argue that the trustworthiness of A2A networks cannot be fully guaranteed via retrofitting on existing protocols that are largely designed for individual agents. Rather, it must be architected from the very beginning of the A2A coordination framework. We present a comprehensive conceptual framework that situates trust in A2A systems through four design pillars.

</details>


### [180] [Interference-Aware Multi-Task Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19042)
*Ying-Hua Huang,Rui Fang,Hsi-Wen Chen,Ming-Syan Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes an interference-aware, multi-task unlearning framework that uses task-aware gradient projection and instance-level gradient orthogonalization to remove supervision for designated data while preserving performance across tasks. Addresses coupling between forget/retain sets in shared backbones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multi-task models with shared representations, forgetting data for one task can inadvertently affect other tasks or instances due to parameter coupling; existing single-task unlearning methods do not account for cross-task interference.

Method: Introduce interference-aware framework combining: (1) task-aware gradient projection to constrain updates to task-specific subspaces, and (2) instance-level gradient orthogonalization to reduce conflicts between forget signals and retain signals, enabling full-task and partial-task unlearning.

Result: On two multi-task computer vision benchmarks spanning five tasks, the method achieves effective unlearning with strong generalization, reducing unlearning interference scores (UIS) by 30.3% (full-task) and 52.9% (partial-task) over the strongest baseline.

Conclusion: The interference-aware approach successfully mitigates cross-task and cross-instance interference in multi-task unlearning, enabling reliable removal of designated supervision while preserving performance.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove the contribution of designated training data from a trained model while preserving performance on the remaining data. Existing work mainly focuses on single-task settings, whereas modern models often operate in multi-task setups with shared backbones, where removing supervision for one task or instance can unintentionally affect others. We introduce multi-task unlearning with two settings: full-task unlearning, which removes a target instance from all tasks, and partial-task unlearning, which removes supervision only from selected tasks. We show that shared parameters couple the forget and retain sets, causing task-level interference on non-target tasks and instance-level interference on other instances. To address this issue, we propose an interference-aware framework that combines task-aware gradient projection, which constrains updates within task-specific subspaces, with instance-level gradient orthogonalization, which reduces conflicts between forget and retain signals. Experiments on two multi-task computer vision benchmarks across five tasks show that our method achieves effective unlearning while maintaining strong generalization, reducing UIS compared with the strongest baseline by 30.3% in full-task unlearning and 52.9% in partial-task unlearning.

</details>


### [181] [Embedding by Elicitation: Dynamic Representations for Bayesian Optimization of System Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19093)
*Zhiyuan Jerry Lin,Benjamin Letham,Samuel Dooley,Maximilian Balandat,Eytan Bakshy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces ReElicit, a Bayesian optimization framework for tuning system prompts under aggregate feedback using embedding by elicitation and a Gaussian process surrogate to map prompts into an adaptive latent feature space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable sample-efficient, black-box optimization of natural-language prompts when feedback comes only as scalar aggregate scores, without per-example labels or critiques.

Method: LLM-based elicitation of a compact, interpretable feature space from task descriptions and past evaluated prompts with scalar scores; a Gaussian process surrogate models the relationship between prompts in this learned space; an acquisition function selects target feature vectors which the LLM realizes as deployable prompts; the feature space is re-elicited as new evaluations arrive to adapt to the history.

Result: Across ten system-prompt optimization tasks with a 30-evaluation budget, ReElicit achieves the strongest aggregate performance among aggregate-only baselines when evaluated on offline benchmark accuracy as a proxy for aggregate feedback; demonstrates that LLMs can serve as adaptive semantic representation builders for Bayesian optimization over natural-language artifacts.

Conclusion: LLMs can function as adaptive semantic representations for BO on NL prompts, enabling efficient, aggregate-feedback–driven prompt optimization and dynamic representation adaptation.

Abstract: System prompts are a central control mechanism in modern AI systems, shaping behavior across conversations, tasks, and user populations. Yet they are difficult to tune when feedback is available only as aggregate metrics rather than per-example labels, failures, or critiques. We study this aggregate feedback setting as sample-constrained black-box optimization over discrete, variable-length text. We introduce ReElicit, a Bayesian optimization framework based on \emph{embedding by elicitation}. Given a task description, previously evaluated prompts, and scalar scores, an LLM elicits a compact, interpretable feature space and maps prompts into it. Leveraging a probabilistic Gaussian process surrogate, an acquisition function then selects target feature vectors, which the LLM realizes and refines into deployable system prompts. Re-eliciting the feature space as new evaluations arrive lets the representation adapt to the observed prompt-score history. We evaluate the setting using offline benchmark accuracy as a controlled aggregate proxy: the optimizer observes one scalar score per prompt and no per-example labels, errors, or critiques. Across ten system prompt optimization tasks with a 30 total evaluation budget, ReElicit achieves the strongest aggregate performance profile among representative aggregate-only prompt-optimization baselines. These results suggest that LLMs can serve as adaptive semantic representation builders, not only prompt generators, for Bayesian optimization over natural-language artifacts.

</details>


### [182] [DecisionBench: A Benchmark for Emergent Delegation in Long-Horizon Agentic Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19099)
*Yuxuan Gao,Megan Wang,Yi Ling Yu,Zijian Carl Ma,Ao Qu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DecisionBench provides a fixed benchmark substrate for emergent delegation in long-horizon agentic workflows, with a fixed task suite, peer-model pool, delegation interface, annotation layer, and multi-axis metrics; key findings show minimal quality differences across awareness conditions, substantial routing fidelity variation driven by delivery channel, and a large unrealized headroom relative to a counterfactual perfect-delegation ceiling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable principled, cross-condition evaluation of orchestration strategies in agent marketplaces by isolating the effects of routing awareness and interface design on long-horizon delegation performance.

Method: Define a standardized substrate consisting of: fixed task suites (GAIA, tau-bench, BFCL multi-turn), a fixed peer-model pool (23 models across 7 vendor families, 11 models in the pool), a delegation interface (call_model with optional read_profile channel), a deterministic skill-annotation layer, and a multi-axis metric suite (quality, cost, latency, delegation rate, routing fidelity-at-k, vendor self-preference, counterfactual-delegation ceiling). Conduct a five-condition reference sweep over the full pool (n=23,375 task instances) and report results (mean quality across four awareness conditions, routing fidelity-at-1 across conditions, and the counterfactual ceiling). Release all artifacts (substrate, annotation layer, reference suite, analysis pipeline, and archives).

Result: Three benchmark-level findings: (i) mean end-task quality is statistically indistinguishable across the four awareness conditions (|beta| ≤ 0.010, p ≥ 0.21), indicating quality-only evaluation misses the orchestration signal; (ii) routing fidelity-at-1 ranges from 7.5% to 29.5% across conditions at near-equal mean quality, with the delivery channel (on-demand tool vs. preloaded description) dominating content; (iii) a counterfactual ceiling shows perfect delegation would exceed measured performance by 15–31 percentage points on every suite, revealing substantial unrealized headroom for future orchestration methods. The authors release the substrate, annotation layer, reference intervention suite, analysis pipeline, and 220 per-condition run archives.

Conclusion: DecisionBench provides a concrete, extensible benchmark for evaluating long-horizon agentic delegation, highlighting that quality alone is insufficient to capture orchestration dynamics, identifying routing fidelity as a key bottleneck influenced by content delivery, and uncovering substantial headroom against a theoretical perfect-delegation ceiling. The framework supports extending evaluation with learned routers, richer peer memories, adaptive profile construction, and multi-step delegation.

Abstract: We introduce DecisionBench, a benchmark substrate for emergent delegation in long-horizon agentic workflows. The substrate fixes a task suite (GAIA, tau-bench, BFCL multi-turn), a peer-model pool (11 models, 7 vendor families), a delegation interface (call_model plus an optional read_profile channel), a deterministic skill-annotation layer, and a multi-axis metric suite covering quality, cost, latency, delegation rate, routing fidelity-at-k, vendor self-preference, and a counterfactual-delegation ceiling. The substrate is agnostic to how peer information is generated or delivered, so learned routers, richer peer memories, adaptive profile construction, and multi-step delegation can all be evaluated against it. We characterize the substrate with a five-condition reference sweep on the full pool (n=23,375 task instances). Three benchmark-level findings emerge: (i) mean end-task quality is statistically indistinguishable across the four awareness conditions (|beta| <= 0.010, p >= 0.21), so quality-only evaluation would miss the orchestration signal; (ii) routing fidelity-at-1 ranges from 7.5% to 29.5% across conditions at near-equal mean quality, with delivery channel (on-demand tool vs. preloaded description) dominating description content; (iii) a counterfactual ceiling places perfect delegation 15-31 percentage points above measured performance on every suite, locating large unrealized headroom for future orchestration methods. We release the substrate, annotation layer, reference intervention suite, analysis pipeline, and 220 per-condition run archives.

</details>


### [183] [POLAR-Bench: A Diagnostic Benchmark for Privacy-Utility Trade-offs in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19127)
*Qiaoyuan Zheng,Yiqu Yang,Qi Gao,Imanol Schlag*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: POLAR-Bench evaluates policy-aware privacy in LLM agents by pitting a trusted model with a privacy policy against an adversarial third-party to assess how well protected attributes are withheld while maintaining task utility across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs gain access to private user data and operate via third-party systems, there is a need to ensure agents follow user-defined privacy intents even under adversarial probing. Existing models show leakage gaps, and smaller on-device/open-weight models may be more vulnerable.

Method: A trusted model with a privacy policy negotiates with a third-party model that adversarially probes for task-relevant and protected attributes. Evaluation spans 10 domains and 7,852 samples. Privacy and utility are scored by a deterministic set-membership metric, while varying privacy policy dimensions and attack strategies along two orthogonal axes, yielding a 5x5 diagnostic surface per model.

Result: Frontier models withhold over 99% of protected attributes; smaller open-weight models in the 1–30B range leak more than 50% in worst cases. The benchmark localizes where intent-following breaks down and identifies priority targets for privacy alignment across model families and deployment regimes.

Conclusion: POLAR-Bench provides a diagnostic framework to pinpoint privacy-alignment gaps by analyzing how policy adherence deteriorates under adversarial probing, especially across model size regimes, guiding targeted privacy improvements.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly have access to private user data and act on the user's behalf when interacting with third-party systems. The user defines what may and must not be shared, and the agent must robustly follow that intent even when third-party systems behave adversarially. We introduce POLAR-Bench (Policy-aware adversarial Benchmark), in which a trusted model with a privacy policy and a task converses with a third-party model that adversarially probes for both task-relevant and protected attributes. Across 10 domains and 7,852 samples, we score privacy and utility by deterministic set-membership and vary privacy policy dimension and attack strategy along two orthogonal axes, producing a 5 times 5 diagnostic surface per model. Our results reveal a sharp split: current frontier models withhold over 99% of protected attributes, while smaller open-weight models in the 1--30B range, the class users most commonly run as their own trusted agent on-device or via private inference, score notably worse, with the weakest leaking over half. POLAR-Bench thus localizes where each model's intent-following breaks down, providing a foothold for privacy alignment where it matters most.

</details>


### [184] [Progressive Autonomy as Preference Learning: A Formalization of Trust Calibration for Agentic Tool Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19151)
*Changkun Ou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Trust calibration for agentic tool use is framed as preference-learning via a Gaussian-process classifier; a policy gateway uses a latent risk-tolerance function with probit feedback to decide when to escalate actions to humans, fitting Preferential Bayesian Optimization but with a classification objective (allow/block/ask) rather than design optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce unsafe autonomous action and unnecessary human workload by selectively escalating actions based on quantified human risk tolerance.

Method: Model latent risk tolerance with a Gaussian process; observe approve/deny via probit likelihood; escalate where uncertainty is highest; treat as Preferential Bayesian Optimization.

Result: Recognizes the approach as an instance of Preferential Bayesian Optimization, leveraging its approximate GP classification and uncertainty-driven querying; the objective shifts from design optimization to action-space classification (allow/block/ask).

Conclusion: A principled, sample-efficient framework for trust calibration in agentic decision-making, enabling targeted human oversight and safer autonomous action.

Abstract: We formalize trust calibration for agentic tool use (deciding when an automated agent's proposed action may execute autonomously versus require human approval) as a preference-learning problem. A policy gateway maintains a Gaussian-process posterior over a latent human risk-tolerance function, observed through a probit likelihood on binary approve/deny feedback, and escalates to the human exactly where the approval outcome is most uncertain. We show this is structurally an instance of Preferential Bayesian Optimization, inheriting its inference machinery (approximate Gaussian-process classification) and its sample-efficiency argument (uncertainty-targeted querying), while differing in objective: classifying an action space into allow/block/ask regions rather than optimizing a design.

</details>


### [185] [How Far Are We From True Auto-Research?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19156)
*Zhengxin Zhang,Ning Wang,Sainyam Galhotra,Claire Cardie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ResearchArena enables off-the-shelf agents to perform full research loops; manuscript-only reviews look optimistic but artifact-aware evaluation reveals serious experimental rigor weaknesses; none of the 117 papers meet top-tier acceptance; indicates gap to true auto-research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no systematic assessment of whether agent-generated papers are credible beyond manuscript quality; need to quantify quality across ideation, experimentation, writing, and self-refinement.

Method: Deploy ResearchArena scaffold with three agent families (Claude Code Opus 4.6, Codex GPT-5.4, Kimi Code K2.5) over 13 CS seeds and 3 trials per agent-domain; produce 117 papers; evaluate via manuscript-only SAR, artifact-aware PR, and human meta-review; analyze failure modes (fabricated results, underpowered experiments, plan/execution mismatch); compare to baselines (FARS, ICLR 2025 submissions).

Result: SAR suggests competitive manuscript-level quality for Claude Code; but artifact-aware PR scores drop; manual audits reveal major technical gaps; failure modes vary by agent with substantial spread (~15x); none reach top-tier acceptance; engineered to show limitations of current auto-research.

Conclusion: Manuscript-only signals are insufficient to judge auto-research; current systems are not yet capable of automated, credible, reproducible scientific papers; robust evaluation and safeguards are needed to bridge the gap to true auto-research.

Abstract: Recent auto-research systems can produce complete papers, but feasibility is not the same as quality, and the field still lacks a systematic study of how good agent-generated papers actually are. We introduce ResearchArena, a minimal scaffold that lets off-the-shelf agents (Claude Code using Opus 4.6, Codex using GPT-5.4, and Kimi Code using K2.5) carry out the full research loop themselves (ideation, experimentation, paper writing, self-refinement) under only lightweight guidance. Across 13 computer science seeds and 3 trials per agent-domain pair, ResearchArena yields 117 agent-generated papers, each evaluated under three complementary lenses: a manuscript-only reviewer (SAR), an artifact-aware peer review (PR) in which agents inspect the workspace alongside the manuscript, and an human conducted meta-review. Under SAR alone the picture is optimistic: Claude Code obtains the highest score, outperforms Analemma's FARS, and matches the weighted-average human ICLR 2025 submission, suggesting that minimally scaffolded agents can produce papers that look competitive on manuscript-only review. Manual inspection, however, reveals this picture is overstated: SAR scores are poorly aligned with its actual acceptance decisions and reward plausible framing without verifying experimental substance. Under artifact-aware PR scores drop sharply, and manual auditing identifies experimental rigor as the major bottleneck, decomposing into three failure modes (fabricated results, underpowered experiments, and plan/execution mismatch) that are highly agent-dependent: Codex 5%/8% paper-vs-artifact mismatch / fabricated references versus Kimi Code 77%/72%, a $\sim$15$\times$ spread that tracks distinct research personas the agents develop. None of the 117 agent-generated papers reaches the acceptance bar of a top-tier venue. This suggests that we are still gapped from the true auto-research.

</details>


### [186] [Hallucination as Exploit: Evidence-Carrying Multimodal Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19192)
*Guijia Zhang,Hao Zheng,Harry Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Evidence-carrying multimodal agents (ECA) prevent hallucination-to-action by requiring external, typed certificates to authorize tool actions, converting perception errors into verifiable residuals and reducing unsafe actions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate hallucination-to-action conversion in multimodal agents where perceptual errors enable privileged actions; enable verifiable authorization rather than trusting free-form model output.

Method: Decompose each tool call into action-critical predicates; obtain typed certificates from constrained DOM/OCR/AX verifiers; a deterministic gate grants privileges only those certificates support; use content-derived certificates; red-team against 1,900 attacks; implement four hardening steps; evaluate on end-to-end pipelines and browser PoC; HACR audits; oracle-certificate replay; compare with baselines.

Result: Gate bypass reduced from 15% to 1.3% after hardening; 0% unsafe-action rate on a 200-task end-to-end pipeline (95% CI upper bound 2.67%); 0% unsafe actions on 120-task browser PoC (upper bound 4.3%). Naive agents reach unsafe action in 100% of 500 stratified task keys; prompt-only defense 49.6%. Oracle replay sanity checks; neural judge baselines remain bypassable under the threat model.

Conclusion: Model language can propose actions, but external evidence must authorize them; ECA provides a principled architecture to align capability with verifiable permission.

Abstract: Multimodal agents use screenshots, documents, and webpages to choose tool calls. When a false visual claim triggers a click, email, extraction, or transfer, hallucination becomes an authorization failure rather than an answer-quality error. We formalize this failure mode as hallucination-to-action conversion: an unsupported perceptual claim supplies the precondition that makes a privileged action appear permitted. We propose evidence-carrying multimodal agents (ECA), which treat free-form model text as inadmissible evidence. ECA decomposes each tool call into action-critical predicates, obtains typed certificates from constrained DOM/OCR/AX verifiers, and lets a deterministic gate grant only the privileges those certificates support. The architecture does not hide perception error; it converts opaque model belief into named verifier, schema, and implementation residuals. Verifier red-teaming over 1,900 attacks exposes this residual directly: four targeted hardening steps reduce gate bypass from 15% to 1.3%. With content-derived certificates, ECA obtains 0% unsafe-action rate on a 200-task end-to-end pipeline (Wilson 95% upper bound 2.67%) and a 120-task browser proof-of-concept (upper bound 4.3%). A direct HACR audit on 500 stratified task keys shows that unsupported action-critical claims reach unsafe execution for naive agents (100.0%) and prompt-only defense (49.6%), but not for ECA. Oracle-certificate replay on 7,488 GPT-5.4 benchmark traces serves as a gate-correctness sanity check, and neural judge baselines remain bypassable under the same threat model. The resulting principle is simple: model language may propose actions, but external evidence must authorize them.

</details>


### [187] [Not all uncertainty is alike: volatility, stochasticity, and exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19215)
*Payam Piray*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Uncertainty from volatility and stochasticity have opposite effects on exploration; CAUSE provides a closed-form, noise-aware exploration bonus derived from control-as-inference, improving performance in heterogeneous-noise environments and linking exploration to noise-inference asymmetry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reconcile how different sources of uncertainty shape exploration, especially when latent reward dynamics drift (volatility) vs noisy observations (stochasticity).

Method: Extend the Gittins index framework to Gaussian state-space bandits with latent dynamics; derive CAUSE via control-as-inference; analytically establish monotonicity with respect to volatility and stochasticity; compare to standard exploration strategies and rested-bandit policies; demonstrate in simulations.

Result: Volatility increases optimal exploration while stochasticity suppresses it; CAUSE yields superior performance in heterogeneous-noise environments; it inherits the same monotonicities; reversed exploration can occur under pathological noise inference.

Conclusion: Learning and exploration are governed by the same noise-inference asymmetry; the framework predicts implications for psychiatric conditions and provides guidance for exploration strategies in non-stationary and noisy environments.

Abstract: Adaptive decision-making in biological and artificial intelligence requires balancing the exploitation of known outcomes with the exploration of uncertain alternatives. Although prior work suggests that uncertainty generally promotes exploration, it has typically treated distinct sources of environmental uncertainty as equivalent. We consider environments with latent reward states that drift over time (volatility) and are observed through noisy outcomes (stochasticity). Both increase posterior uncertainty, yet we show they drive optimal exploration in opposite directions: volatility enhances it, stochasticity suppresses it. We establish this asymmetry formally by extending the Gittins index framework to Gaussian state-space bandits with latent dynamics. We further derive Cause-Aware Uncertainty-Sensitive Exploration (CAUSE), a closed-form exploration bonus obtained via control-as-inference that inherits the same monotonicities. CAUSE outperforms standard exploration strategies in environments with heterogeneous noise structure, and also improves on a Gittins-per-arm policy whose rested-bandit optimality does not transfer to restless settings. Learning and exploration are governed by the same noise-inference asymmetry, and the framework predicts that pathological noise inference produces \emph{reversed} rather than merely impaired exploration, with implications for computational accounts of psychiatric conditions.

</details>


### [188] [SimGym: A Framework for A/B Test Simulation in E-Commerce with Traffic-Grounded VLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19219)
*Han Li,Vibhor Malik,Zahra Zanjani Foumani,Alberto Castelo,Shuang Xie,Ailin Fan,Keat Yang Koay,Yuanzheng Zhu,Meysam Feghhi,Ronie Uliana,Zhaoyu Zhang,Angelo Ocana Martins,Mingyu Zhao,Francis Pelland,Jonathan Faerman,Nikolas LeBlanc,Aaron Glazer,Andrew McNamara,Zhong Wu,Lingyun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SimGym is a framework to simulate A/B tests for e-commerce storefronts using vision-language model agents in live browsers, deriving personas from production data, enabling rapid, traffic-grounded simulations with guardrails; achieves 77% directional alignment with real add-to-cart shifts and reduces cycles to under an hour.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: A/B testing is slow and traffic-intensive; a simulation framework can accelerate experimentation while minimizing exposure of real users to candidate variants.

Method: Three components: (a) traffic-grounded persona generation from clickstream data; (b) live-browser VLM agent architecture with multimodal perception, episodic memory, guardrails; (c) evaluation protocol comparing simulated outcomes to observed real-buyer behavior; validation on UI theme changes across storefronts/categories.

Result: Agents show strong agreement with real behavior, 77% directional alignment for add-to-cart shifts; cycle time reduced from weeks to under an hour.

Conclusion: SimGym enables rapid, safe, and directionally accurate A/B testing simulations in e-commerce by bridging production data with live-browser VLM agents.

Abstract: A/B testing remains the gold standard for evaluating modifications to e-commerce storefronts, yet it diverts traffic, requires weeks to reach statistical significance, and risks degrading user experience. We present SimGym, a framework for simulating A/B tests on e-commerce storefronts using vision-language model (VLM) agents operating in a live browser. The framework comprises three key components: (a) a traffic-grounded persona generation pipeline that derives per-shop buyer archetypes and intents from production clickstream data; (b) a live-browser agent architecture that combines multimodal perception over visual and browser-structured observations with episodic memory and guardrails to conduct coherent shopping sessions across control and treatment storefronts; and (c) an evaluation protocol that compares simulated outcome shifts with observed shifts in real buyer behavior. We validate SimGym on A/B tests of visually driven UI theme changes from a major e-commerce platform across diverse storefronts and product categories. Empirical results show that SimGym agents achieve strong agreement with observed outcome shifts, attaining 77% directional alignment with add-to-cart shifts observed across interface variants in real-buyer traffic. It reduces experimental cycles from weeks to under an hour, enabling rapid experimentation without exposing real buyers to candidate variants.

</details>


### [189] [Can Large Language Models Revolutionize Survey Research? Experiments with Disaster Preparedness Responses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19229)
*Yan Wang,Ziyi Guo,Christopher McCarty*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Five-stage LLM integration framework for disaster-survey data; Anchored Marginal Theory-Informed LLM (A-TLM) outperforms classical imputation baselines in MNAR, with strong bias control; retrieval around PMT knowledge graph improves performance and reduces hallucination, though subgroups may exhibit unseen biases, necessitating subgroup bias auditing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Survey research faces declining response rates, sample bias, nonresponse and AI-assisted fraud in online panels; there is a need for rigorous evaluation of LLMs across the full survey workflow, especially in disaster contexts where data quality is critical.

Method: Propose a five-stage workflow: questionnaire design, sample selection, pilot testing, missing-data imputation, and post-collection analysis. Build a Protection Motivation Theory (PMT)-constrained co-occurrence knowledge graph. Develop seven LLM configurations spanning zero-shot, retrieval-augmented baselines, and theory-informed variants. Central model A-TLM uses PMT-informed anchors. Use the 2024 Hurricane Milton preparedness survey (n=946) as testbed. Evaluate with RMSE and MAE; analyze bias and subgroup variation. Implement retrieval around PMT causal structure and integrate evidence in a single model call. Demonstrate a knowledge-graph chatbot with grounded refusal to control hallucination.

Result: A-TLM achieves lower RMSE than IPW/MI, MICE+PMM, and missForest under block-wise MNAR (S4 RMSE 1.439 vs. 1.496). Signed bias near zero (-0.121) compared with larger absolute bias for baselines (e.g., -0.631 for random forest). Retrieval-constrained PMT knowledge-graph approach with unified inference outperforms unstructured retrieval (MAE 0.993 vs. 1.097). Subgroup errors may mask aggregate bias; propose subgroup-stratified bias auditing. A retrieval-constrained chatbot reduces hallucinations through grounded refusals.

Conclusion: Integrating PMT-informed knowledge graphs with retrieval-constrained LLM inference yields superior imputation performance and manageable hallucination in disaster survey workflows. However, near-zero aggregate bias can mask subgroup disparities, underscoring the need for stratified bias auditing as standard reporting practice.

Abstract: Survey research faces mounting structural challenges: declining response rates, sample bias, block-wise missingness among at-risk respondents, and AI-assisted fraudulent completions in online panels. Large language models (LLMs) have been proposed as a remedy, yet rigorous evaluations across the full survey workflow remain scarce, particularly in disaster contexts where data quality matters most. We present and evaluate a five-stage framework for LLM integration covering questionnaire design, sample selection, pilot testing, missing-data imputation, and post-collection analysis, using the 2024 Hurricane Milton preparedness survey of Florida residents (n=946) as a shared empirical testbed. We introduce a Protection Motivation Theory (PMT)-constrained co-occurrence knowledge graph and develop seven LLM configurations spanning zero-shot inference, retrieval-augmented baselines, and novel theory-informed variants. Our proposed Anchored Marginal Theory-Informed LLM (A-TLM) outperforms all three classical imputation baselines (IPW/MI, MICE+PMM, missForest) on RMSE under disaster-relevant block-wise MNAR conditions (S4 RMSE 1.439 vs. 1.496 for the next-best), while achieving near-zero signed bias (-0.121) where the random-forest imputer produces the largest absolute bias (-0.631). Organizing retrieval around PMT causal structure and integrating all evidence in a single model call outperforms unstructured retrieval and staged sequential inference (MAE 0.993 vs. 1.097 for standard RAG). We document that near-zero aggregate bias can mask opposing subgroup errors and propose subgroup-stratified bias auditing as a reporting standard. A retrieval-constrained knowledge-graph chatbot demonstrates that hallucination is architecturally manageable through grounded refusal.

</details>


### [190] [Causal Evidence for Attention Head Imbalance in Modality Conflict Hallucination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19250)
*Jinrui Jiang,Zhangtai Wu,Zhen Wu,Xinyu Dai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Two opposing groups of attention heads govern hallucination in multimodal LLMs: driving heads (broadly distributed with strong aggregate influence) and resisting heads (few high-importance heads). A causal intervention MACI suppresses driving heads under modality conflict, yielding major hallucination reductions on MMMC and zero-shot transfer to SCI-SemanticConflict, indicating a robust, transferable mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand why visual evidence loses to textual premises in MLLMs; identify mechanistic drivers of modality-conflict hallucination via head-level causality; develop an inference-time intervention to mitigate it.

Method: Path-patching causal analysis of attention heads across five open-source MLLMs; categorize heads into driving vs resisting; ablation experiments; design conditional MACI that suppresses driving heads when conflict detected; evaluate on MMMC and transfer to SCI-SemanticConflict.

Result: Identified two opposite causal head groups; driving heads are more distributed and heavier; resisting heads are concentrated in a small set; ablations show opposing effects; MACI reduces hallucination with favorable accuracy trade-off and transfers zero-shot to SCI-SemanticConflict.

Conclusion: The hallucination tendency arises from an imbalanced routing structure favoring erroneous premises; a targeted, conflict-aware intervention can effectively curb hallucinations with cross-model transfer, suggesting practical applicability for robust MLLMs.

Abstract: Modality-conflict hallucination occurs when multimodal large language models (MLLMs) prioritize erroneous textual premises over contradictory visual evidence. To understand why visual evidence fails to prevail during generation, we take a mechanistic perspective and examine which internal components drive or resist this failure. We perform head-level causal analysis using path patching across five open-source MLLMs and identify two groups of attention heads with opposing causal roles: hallucination-driving heads and hallucination-resisting heads. We find a consistent asymmetry: driving effects are more broadly distributed and carry greater aggregate weight, whereas resisting effects concentrate in a small number of high-importance heads. Ablation experiments further confirm that these groups exert opposing effects during generation: distributed driving influence and localized resistance together form an imbalanced routing structure that biases generation toward the erroneous premise. Motivated by this finding, we propose MACI (Modality-conflict-Aware Causal Intervention), a conditional intervention that suppresses causally identified hallucination-driving heads only when conflict is detected. Across five MLLMs, MACI achieves the largest hallucination reduction among compared inference-time baselines on the MMMC benchmark with a favorable hallucination-accuracy trade-off, and transfers zero-shot to the SCI-SemanticConflict test.

</details>


### [191] [AQuaUI: Visual Token Reduction for GUI Agents with Adaptive Quadtrees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19260)
*Yuankai Li,Tinghui Zhu,Ha Min Son,Zhe Zhao,Xin Liu,Muhao Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Training-free, inference-time quadtree-based token reduction (AquaUI) for GUI agents that preserves spatial structure and boosts speed with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GUI screenshots have highly non-uniform information density; existing methods either require retraining or rely on attention-based token compression that ignores GUI layout. A compact, inference-time approach is needed to exploit spatial redundancy without retraining.

Method: Per-image adaptive quadtree construction; merge tokens per leaf and keep one representative token per leaf; preserve token spatial positions to maintain position encodings; a conditional quadtree refines the current tree using previous trees to leverage temporal continuity across successive GUI frames; training-free during inference.

Result: Empirical evaluation on standard grounding and navigational benchmarks shows improved accuracy-efficiency trade-offs. On GUI-Owl-1.5-32B-Instruct, achieves up to 13.22% speedup and 29.52% fewer visual tokens while retaining 99.06% of full-token performance.

Conclusion: Inference-time token reduction via a conditional adaptive quadtree effectively exploits GUI spatial redundancy, enabling faster GUI agent reasoning without retraining and preserving essential spatial structure.

Abstract: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have recently emerged as promising backbones for GUI-agent models, where high-resolution GUI screenshots are introduced to the prompts at each iteration step. However, these screenshots exhibit highly non-uniform spatial information density: large regions may carry little information and are visually homogeneous, while key text and icons may require high visual fidelity. Existing approaches to this problem either require additional training or rely on attention-based token compression, ignoring the structured layout and spatial redundancy of GUI screenshots. To fill the gap, this paper proposes AquaUI, a training-free inference-time token reduction method for GUI agent models that utilizes the non-uniform information density in screenshots. AQuaUI constructs an adaptive quadtree on each screenshot input and keeps one representative merged token per leaf of the quadtree. AQuaUI preserves the spatial positions of retained tokens throughout the pipeline to ensure that all position-encoding stages remain consistent. To further improve temporal consistency across multi-step GUI interactions, we propose a conditional quadtree algorithm that leverages the continuity between consecutive screenshots within a single request. Specifically, it refines the current quadtree using previous quadtrees as references, helping preserve fine-grained regions across static or mildly shifted GUI states. We implement AQuaUI on state-of-the-art GUI agent models and conduct experiments on standard grounding and navigational benchmarks. AQuaUI consistently shows improved accuracy-efficiency trade-offs over prior baselines. Notably, on GUI-Owl-1.5-32B-Instruct, AQuaUI achieves up to 13.22% speedup and 29.52% fewer visual tokens while retaining 99.06% of full-token performance, suggesting that the spatial redundancy of GUI screenshots can be exploited at inference without retraining.

</details>


### [192] [Swimming with Whales: Analysis of Power Imbalances in Stake-Weighted Governance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19264)
*Yuzhe Zhang,Manvir Schneider,Qin Wang,Davide Grossi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Power imbalances in stake-weighted PoS governance are analyzed both analytically and empirically using the Penrose-Banzhaf index; perfect power-stake alignment is generally unattainable but can be approximated in expectation under certain conditions, with Project Catalyst data illustrating real-world imbalances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify and understand how stake concentration translates into voting power in stake-weighted governance, and identify conditions under which power aligns with stake or deviates, to inform governance design.

Method: Analytical: derive results on power-vs-stake using the Penrose-Banzhaf index and show conditions for approximate alignment in expectation. Empirical: analyze a real-world on-chain governance dataset (Project Catalyst) to measure observed power distributions and compare to stake shares.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows that full alignment between power and relative stake is generally unattainable, but can be approximated in expectation under specific conditions. Empirical analysis on Project Catalyst reveals detailed patterns of power imbalances consistent with the theory, highlighting practical implications for stake-weighted governance.

Conclusion: Stake-weighted governance in PoS is susceptible to power concentration and misalignment with stake. The results provide conditions under which alignment may hold in expectation and offer empirical insight into the magnitude and nature of distortions, informing design choices to mitigate undue influence.

Abstract: Voting methods weighted by stakes are the fundamental governance paradigm in Proof-of-Stake (PoS) blockchains. Such a paradigm is known to be prone to power distortions: a few users possessing large stakes may completely control decision making, even without owning the totality of the stakes. We study this phenomenon through the lens of computational social choice, focusing on the extent of power imbalances in stake-weighted voting when power is quantified using the Penrose-Banzhaf power index. Our work presents both analytical and empirical contributions. Analytically, we demonstrate that while a perfect alignment between power and relative stake ownership is generally unattainable, it can be approximated in expectation under specific conditions. Empirically, using data from a real-world on-chain governance system (Project Catalyst), we provide a more fine-grained understanding of the power imbalances that are likely to occur in current stake-weighted governance systems.

</details>


### [193] [MOCHA: Multi-Objective Chebyshev Annealing for Agent Skill Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19330)
*Md Mehrab Tanjim,Jayakumar Subramanian,Xiang Chen,Branislav Kveton,Subhojyoti Mukherjee,Anlan Zhang,Sungchul Kim,Somdeb Sarkhel,Sunav Choudhury*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MOCHA is a multi-objective optimization method for tuning LLM agent skills under platform constraints, using Chebyshev scalarization and exponential annealing to cover the full Pareto front (including non-convex regions). It outperforms baselines across six tasks and yields more Pareto-optimal variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Skills in LLM agents are structured artifacts constrained by routing truncation, progressive disclosure, and limited context windows. Optimizing them is inherently multi-objective; existing methods either ignore trade-offs or collapse them to a single weighted objective, missing non-convex Pareto regions.

Method: MOCHA applies Chebyshev scalarization to maintain Pareto-front coverage and uses exponential annealing to shift from exploration to exploitation. It shares a uniform multi-objective mutation operator across methods; baselines receive identical per-objective textual feedback.

Result: Across six diverse agent-skill tasks, MOCHA yields a 7.5% relative mean-correctness improvement over the strongest baseline (up to 14.9% on FEVER and 10.4% on TheoremQA). Baselines fail to improve the seed skill on 4 of 6 tasks after 1000 rollouts, whereas MOCHA breaks through on every task and discovers about twice as many Pareto-optimal variants.

Conclusion: MOCHA successfully covers non-convex regions of the multi-objective landscape, delivering Pareto-optimal skill variants and consistent performance gains. This demonstrates the value of Chebyshev scalarization with annealing for structuring LLM-agent skill optimization under platform constraints.

Abstract: LLM agents organize behavior through skills - structured natural-language specifications governing how an agent reasons, retrieves, and responds. Unlike monolithic prompts, skills are multi-field artifacts subject to hard platform constraints: description fields are truncated for routing, instruction bodies are compacted via progressive disclosure, and co-resident skills compete for limited context windows. These constraints make skill optimization inherently multi-objective: a skill must simultaneously maximize task performance and satisfy platform limits. Yet existing prompt optimizers either ignore these trade-offs or collapse them into a weighted sum, missing Pareto-optimal variants in non-convex objective regions. We introduce MOCHA (Multi-Objective Chebyshev Annealing), which replaces single-objective selection with Chebyshev scalarization - covering the full Pareto front, including non-convex regions - combined with exponential annealing that transitions from exploration to exploitation. In our experiments across six diverse agent skills - where all methods share the same multi-objective mutation operator and baselines receive identical per-objective textual feedback - existing optimizers fail to improve the seed skill on 4 of 6 tasks: 1000 rollouts yield zero progress. MOCHA breaks through on every task, achieving 7.5% relative improvement in mean correctness over the strongest baseline (up to 14.9% on FEVER and 10.4% on TheoremQA) while discovering twice as many more Pareto-optimal skill variants.

</details>


### [194] [Agentic Trading: When LLM Agents Meet Financial Markets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19337)
*Yihan Xia,Panpan You,Taotao Wang,Fang Liu,Han Qi,Xiaoxiao Wu,Shengli Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Audit-oriented systematic review of 77 studies on LLM-based trading agents; primary subset shows protocol incomparability and reproducibility gaps; using an Architecture-Capability-Adaptation lens; main contributions are evidence ledger, reproducibility audit, and reporting checklist.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Map evidence, evaluate evaluation protocols, and identify bottlenecks hindering comparability and reproducibility in LLM-driven trading agents.

Method: Protocol-coded snapshot review of studies through 2026-03-09; 77 studies included; 19 in the primary empirical subset (Action Output + Closed-Loop Evaluation); 58 background studies; coded dimensions include time-consistency of protocols, transaction-cost models, survivorship/universes, execution timing/semantics, and reproducibility levels (R0–R3).

Result: Central finding: protocol incomparability. In the primary subset, only 2/19 report time-consistent split protocols; 1/19 report explicit transaction-cost model; 1/19 document universe/survivorship handling; 11/19 report execution timing/semantics; 15/19 coded as R0; no study reaches R3 reproducibility. An Architecture-Capability-Adaptation lens is used; emphasis on evidence ledger, reproducibility audit, and reporting checklist. Architectural experimentation is growing; bottlenecks are comparable evaluation protocols, execution semantics, and reproducible artifacts.

Conclusion: Standardized evaluation protocols and reproducible artifacts are needed; research is expanding in architecture and capability exploration, but comparability remains the immediate bottleneck; the study foregrounds evidence ledger, reproducibility auditing, and reporting practices.

Abstract: A growing body of work explores how Large Language Models (LLMs) can be embedded in trading systems as agents that perceive market information, retrieve context, reason about decisions, emit tradable actions, and adapt under market feedback. This paper reframes LLM-based trading agents as expert-system decision pipelines and presents an audit-oriented evidence map of 77 included studies in a protocol-coded snapshot screened through 2026-03-09. A primary empirical subset (n=19) satisfies the minimum boundary of Action Output plus Closed-Loop Evaluation; the remaining 58 included studies are retained as background and design context. The central empirical finding is protocol incomparability: within the primary subset, only 2/19 studies report extractable time-consistent split protocols, 1/19 reports an explicit transaction-cost model, 1/19 documents universe or survivorship handling, 11/19 report execution timing or semantics, 15/19 are coded as R0, and no study reaches R3 reproducibility. We therefore use Architecture-Capability-Adaptation as a working analytical lens rather than a validated taxonomy, and we foreground the evidence ledger, reproducibility audit, and reporting checklist as the main contributions. The resulting survey shows that architectural experimentation is expanding rapidly, while comparable evaluation protocols, execution semantics, and reproducible artifacts remain the field's immediate bottlenecks.

</details>


### [195] [Generative Recursive Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19376)
*Junyeob Baek,Mingyu Jo,Minsu Kim,Mengye Ren,Yoshua Bengio,Sungjin Ahn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GRAM reframes recursive reasoning models as probabilistic latent trajectories, enabling multiple hypotheses and scalable inference; it uses amortized variational inference to train a latent-variable generative model with conditional and unconditional generation, outperforming deterministic baselines on structured reasoning tasks and implying unconditional generation capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of deterministic RRMs that follow a single latent trajectory and converge to a single solution. Introduce probabilistic multi-trajectory reasoning to capture multiple hypotheses, alternative strategies, and scalable inference via recursive depth and parallel trajectory sampling.

Method: Propose Generative Recursive reAsoning Models (GRAM): a latent-variable generative framework where reasoning unfolds as stochastic latent trajectories with shared transition functions. Enables both p_theta(y|x) for conditional reasoning and p_theta(x) for unconditional generation. Trained with amortized variational inference. Supports multiple trajectories through recursive depth control and parallel sampling.

Result: Outperforms deterministic recurrent and recursive baselines on structured reasoning and multi-solution constraint satisfaction tasks. Demonstrates the ability to generate data unconditionally (p_theta(x)) in addition to conditional reasoning (p_theta(y|x)).

Conclusion: GRAM enables probabilistic, multi-trajectory recursive reasoning, allowing multiple hypotheses and scalable inference via depth and parallel sampling. It broadens RRMs from single-trajectory deterministic models to a generative latent-variable framework with both conditional and unconditional generation.

Abstract: How should future neural reasoning systems implement extended computation? Recursive Reasoning Models (RRMs) offer a promising alternative to autoregressive sequence extension by performing iterative latent-state refinement with shared transition functions. Yet existing RRMs are largely deterministic, following a single latent trajectory and converging to a single prediction. We introduce \emph{Generative Recursive reAsoning Models (GRAM)}, a framework that turns recursive latent reasoning into probabilistic multi-trajectory computation. GRAM models reasoning as a stochastic latent trajectory, enabling multiple hypotheses, alternative solution strategies, and inference-time scaling through both recursive depth and parallel trajectory sampling. This yields a latent-variable generative model supporting conditional reasoning via $p_θ(y \mid x)$ and, with fixed or absent inputs, unconditional generation via $p_θ(x)$. Trained with amortized variational inference, GRAM improves over deterministic recurrent and recursive baselines on structured reasoning and multi-solution constraint satisfaction tasks, while demonstrating an unconditional generation capability. \href{https://ahn-ml.github.io/gram-website/}{https://ahn-ml.github.io/gram-website}

</details>


### [196] [Conflict-Resilient Multi-Agent Reasoning via Signed Graph Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19418)
*Longgang He,Longzhu He,Daojing He,Chaozhuo Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SIGMA introduces a signed graph-informed multi-agent reasoning framework that models trust, conflict, and neutral relations among agents through a confidence-weighted signed graph, enabling conflict-aware message passing and robust aggregation to improve accuracy and resilience across diverse MAS configurations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based MAS suffer from naive aggregation that assumes uniform cooperation. They propagate errors when signals conflict and lack explicit modeling of conflicting inter-agent relations and structural patterns, leading to fragile reasoning.

Method: Select a relevant and diverse subset of agents, build a structured signed interaction graph with confidence-weighted edges, perform conflict-aware signed message passing to reinforce trustworthy signals and suppress conflicts, and apply a structure- and conflict-aware weighted aggregation to produce coherent predictions.

Result: Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets with various LLM backbones and MAS configurations show SIGMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in accuracy and conflict-resilient performance.

Conclusion: Modeling trust, conflict, and neutral relations via a signed graph enables more reliable, globally consistent reasoning in MAS, reducing error propagation from conflicting signals.

Abstract: LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) have demonstrated strong reasoning and decision-making capabilities that consistently surpass those of single LLM agents. However, their performance often suffers from naive aggregation mechanisms that assume uniformly cooperative interactions. Upon close inspection, we observe that existing graph-based MAS frameworks (1) propagate errors when conflicting signals arise without control, and (2) lack explicit modeling of conflicting inter-agent relations as well as structural awareness, failing to identify reliable interaction patterns. To bridge this gap, we introduce SIGMA, a novel SIgned Graph-informed Multi-Agent reasoning framework that explicitly captures trust, conflict, and neutral relations among agents via a signed relational graph. Specifically, given a query, SIGMA first selects a set of relevant and diverse agents, then constructs a structured signed interaction graph with confidence-weighted edges. Reasoning proceeds through conflict-aware signed message passing, which reinforces information from trustworthy agents while suppressing conflicting signals, and terminates with a structure- and conflict-aware weighted aggregation to yield globally consistent and conflict-resilient predictions. Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets, across multiple LLM backbones and diverse multi-agent configurations, demonstrate that SIGMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving notable gains in both accuracy and conflict-resilient performance.

</details>


### [197] [What and When to Distill: Selective Hindsight Distillation for Multi-Turn Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19447)
*Xiaozhe Li,Tianyi Lyu,Yang Li,Yichuan Ma,Peiji Li,Linyang Li,Qipeng Guo,Dahua Lin,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SERL selectively leverages environment feedback to guide per-step updates in long-horizon RL for LLM agents, improving success rates on ALFWorld and WebShop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon credit assignment is hard; existing methods rely on trajectory-level rewards or proxy signals and fail to exploit per-step environmental feedback, especially in multi-turn tasks with diverse feedback.

Method: SERL uses five feedback sources and two insertion granularities. Task reward sets update direction; environment feedback adjusts placement and magnitude to emphasize critical actions, effectively reweighting updates.

Result: On ALFWorld (90.0%) and WebShop (80.1%), SERL outperforms strong RL and distillation baselines.

Conclusion: Grounded, action-relevant feedback at meaningful points improves credit assignment and performance, outperforming indiscriminate use of longer or richer context.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning can train LLM agents from sparse task rewards, but long-horizon credit assignment remains challenging: a single success-or-failure signal must be distributed across many actions. Existing methods rely on trajectory-level rewards or proxy signals, without fully leveraging per-step environmental feedback. Multi-turn agent settings are underexplored, where feedback can include error messages, page changes, observations, or reference trajectories. We systematically study five feedback sources and two insertion granularities and introduce SERL, a selective environment-reweighted learning framework. SERL uses the task reward to determine update direction, while environment feedback adjusts placement and magnitude, focusing on critical actions. On ALFWorld and WebShop, SERL achieves 90.0% and 80.1% success, outperforming strong RL and distillation baselines. Analysis shows that grounded, action-relevant feedback at meaningful points consistently outperforms indiscriminate use of longer or richer context.

</details>


### [198] [Generative Auto-Bidding with Unified Modeling and Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19457)
*Mingming Zhang,Feiqing Zhuang,Na Li,Shengjie Sun,Xiaowei Chen,Junxiong Zhu,Fei Xiao,Keping Yang,Lixin Zou,Chenliang Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GUIDE introduces a unified explore-safeguard-select pipeline for automated bidding by combining a Decision Transformer with a Q-value guided exploration and an Inverse Dynamics Module that provides a safe fallback; yields improvements on public data, simulations, and large-scale Taobao deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of prior methods: rule-based methods lack adaptability; RL approaches model MDP but struggle with long-term dependencies; generative models lack explicit exploration-safety mechanisms, causing inefficient exploration and higher financial risk.

Method: Use Decision Transformer to model historical actions and environment transitions; Q-value module enforces regularization guiding exploration; IDM uses DT-predicted future states to infer robust actions as a safe fallback; final action selected by Q-value module balancing exploration/safety; ends with explore-safeguard-select pipeline.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across tasks; in real deployment on Taobao shows +4.10% GMV, +1.40% clicks, +1.66% cost, +3.52% ROI.

Conclusion: GUIDE effectively unifies efficiency and safety in automated bidding, with strong industrial applicability demonstrated by large-scale deployment, suggesting this integrated architecture as a promising direction for safe, efficient online bidding.

Abstract: Automated bidding is central to modern digital advertising. Early rule-based methods lacked adaptability, while subsequent Reinforcement Learning approaches modeled bidding as a Markov Decision Process but struggled with long-term dependencies. Recent generative models show promise, yet they lack explicit mechanisms to balance exploration and safety, relying solely on action perturbations or trajectory guidance without a safety fallback. This results in inefficient exploration and elevated financial risk for advertising platforms.
  To address this gap, we propose GUIDE (Generative Auto-Bidding with Unified Modeling and Exploration), a framework that synergistically integrates directed exploration with a safe fallback mechanism. GUIDE employs a Decision Transformer (DT) to jointly model historical bidding actions and environmental state transitions. A Q-value module guides the DT's exploration via regularization constraints, while an Inverse Dynamics Module (IDM) leverages DT-predicted future states to infer robust, behaviorally consistent actions as a safe policy fallback. The Q-value module then adaptively selects the final action between these two options, balancing exploration and safety. Together, these components form an integrated "explore-safeguard-select" pipeline that unifies efficiency and safety.
  We conduct extensive experiments on public datasets, in simulated auction environments, and through large-scale online deployment on Taobao, a leading Chinese advertising platform. Results show GUIDE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across all scenarios. In real-world deployment, GUIDE achieves notable gains: +4.10% ad GMV, +1.40% ad clicks, +1.66% ad cost, and +3.52% ad ROI, demonstrating its effectiveness and strong industrial applicability.

</details>


### [199] [Beyond Mode Collapse: Distribution Matching for Diverse Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19461)
*Xiaozhe Li,Yang Li,Xinyu Fang,Shengyuan Ding,Peiji Li,Yongkang Chen,Yichuan Ma,Tianyi Lyu,Linyang Li,Dahua Lin,Qipeng Guo,Qingwen Liu,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DMPO addresses mode collapse in on-policy reinforcement learning by shifting from reverse KL (mode-seeking) to forward KL (mode-covering) via distribution matching. It achieves sustained exploration and improved performance on NP-Bench and related tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-policy methods like GRPO tend to collapse to a single high-reward trajectory, reducing diversity and exploration due to reverse KL minimization. A strategy to retain a diverse set of solutions is needed.

Method: DMPO constructs a group-level target distribution over sampled trajectories proportional to their rewards and aligns the policy distribution to this (forward KL) target, enabling mode-covering without sampling from the intractable global target distribution.

Result: DMPO achieves higher Quality Ratios than GRPO on NP-Bench: 43.9% vs 40.1% (text-based) and 43.1% vs 38.4% (vision-based); relative improvements of 9% and 12%. Additional gains observed in mathematical reasoning (+2.0%) and out-of-domain tasks (+2.3%).

Conclusion: Distribution matching offers a principled, practical approach to prevent mode collapse in on-policy RL and yields sustained exploration and broader generalization across modalities.

Abstract: On-policy reinforcement learning methods like GRPO suffer from mode collapse: they exhibit reduced solution diversity, concentrating probability mass on a single solution once discovered and ceasing exploration of alternative strategies. We show this stems from reverse KL minimization's mode-seeking behavior, which reinforces the first high-reward trajectory found rather than maintaining a distribution over multiple diverse solutions. We propose DMPO (Distribution-Matching Policy Optimization), which prevents mode collapse through principled approximation of forward KL minimization. DMPO constructs a group level target distribution over sampled trajectories proportional to their rewards, then aligns the policy distribution to this target. This provides mode-covering behavior without requiring sampling from the intractable global target distribution, enabling sustained exploration throughout training. We validate DMPO on NP-hard combinatorial optimization, where exponentially many feasible solutions exist but only a few approach optimality, an ideal testbed for evaluating exploration. DMPO achieves 43.9% Quality Ratio on text-based NP-Bench (vs. GRPO's 40.1%) and 43.1% on vision-based NP-Bench (vs. 38.4%), demonstrating 9% and 12% relative improvements respectively. These gains generalize to mathematical reasoning (+2.0%) and out-of-domain tasks (+2.3%), showing that diversity-preserving training enhances general reasoning capabilities across modalities. Our work establishes distribution matching as a practical, principled approach to preventing mode collapse in on-policy RL, with consistent quality improvements demonstrating sustained exploration across diverse reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [200] [Attention-Guided Reward for Reinforcement Learning-based Jailbreak against Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19485)
*Zheng Lin,Zhenxing Niu,Haoxuan Ji,Yuzhe Huang,Haichang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LRMs are vulnerable to jailbreak attacks; attack success correlates with attention patterns; an RL-based jailbreak leverages attention signals in rewards; diverse persuasion strategies boost ASR; experiments across five LRMs and three benchmarks show superior effectiveness, efficiency, and transferability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address safety risks of exposing internal reasoning in LRMs and the vulnerability of LRMs to jailbreaks. The work investigates how attention patterns relate to jailbreak success and introduces an RL-guided, attention-aware attack with broadened action space to improve effectiveness and generalization.

Method: Propose an RL-based jailbreak framework that incorporates attention signals into the reward function and adds diverse persuasion strategies to expand the action space. Evaluate on five open-source/closed-source LRMs across three benchmarks, comparing against existing jailbreak approaches.

Result: Significant improvement in attack success rate (ASR) over baselines. The method demonstrates higher effectiveness, efficiency, and transferability across models and benchmarks.

Conclusion: Attention patterns are predictive of jailbreak success in LRMs. An RL-informed attack that exploits these signals achieves strong performance, underscoring safety concerns for LRMs and motivating the development of defenses and robust prompt/attention-control mechanisms.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in solving complex problems by generating structured, step-by-step reasoning content. However, exposing a model's internal reasoning process introduces additional safety risks; for example, recent studies show that LRMs are more vulnerable to jailbreak attacks than standard LLMs. In this paper, we investigate jailbreak attacks on LRMs and reveal that the attack success rate (ASR) is closely correlated with LRMs' attention patterns. Specifically, successful jailbreaks tend to assign lower attention to harmful tokens in the input prompt, while allocating higher attention to those tokens in the reasoning content. Motivated by this finding, we propose a novel jailbreak method for LRMs that leverages reinforcement learning (RL) to enhance attack effectiveness, explicitly incorporating attention signals into the reward function design. In addition, we introduce diverse persuasion strategies to enrich the RL action space, which consistently improves the ASR. Extensive experiments on five open-source and closed-source LRMs across three benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves substantially higher ASR, outperforming existing approaches in terms of effectiveness, efficiency, and transferability.

</details>


### [201] [Position: The Turing-Completeness of Real-World Autoregressive Transformers Relies Heavily on Context Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19514)
*Guanyu Cui,Zhewei Wei,Kun He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Formalizes a fixed-system setting for autoregressive Transformers and contrasts it with the scaling-family view; argues that Turing-completeness results typically rely on scaling, not fixed-system deployment; shows context management critically determines the computational power of real-world models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve a common misinterpretation that Transformers are Turing-complete by distinguishing fixed-system deployment from scaling analyses and by clarifying the role of context management in computational power.

Method: Formal definition of a fixed-system setting; critical analysis of existing scaling-family proofs; examination of how context-management methods affect computational capability; argument that different context-management regimes yield different power levels.

Result: A concrete fixed-system framework for analyzing real-world LLMs; clarification that scaling-family proofs do not establish Turing-completeness for fixed systems; demonstration that context management materially alters computational power, supporting the centrality of context management in practical Transformer capabilities.

Conclusion: Future work should adopt the fixed-system perspective to assess LLM computational power; context-management design is a crucial determinant of what autoregressive Transformers can compute, more so than unbounded scaling alone.

Abstract: Many works make the eye-catching claim that Transformers are Turing-complete. However, the literature often conflates two distinct settings: (i) a fixed Transformer system setting, in which a fixed autoregressive Transformer is coupled with a fixed context-management method to process inputs of different lengths step by step, and (ii) a scaling-family setting, in which a family of different models (with increasing context-window length or numerical precision) is used to handle different input lengths. Existing proofs of Transformer Turing-completeness are frequently established in setting (ii), whereas real-world LLM deployment and the standard notion of Turing-completeness correspond more naturally to setting (i). In this paper, we first formalize the fixed-system setting, thereby providing a concrete characterization of how real-world LLMs operate. We then argue that results proved in the scaling-family setting provide theoretically meaningful resource bounds but do not establish Turing-completeness, thereby clarifying a common misinterpretation of existing results. Finally, we show that different context-management methods can yield sharply different computational power, and we advocate the position that context management is a central component that critically determines the computational power of real-world autoregressive Transformers.

</details>


### [202] [BLINKG: A Benchmark for LLM-Integrated Knowledge Graph Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19518)
*Carla Castedo,Enrique Iglesias,Manuel Lama,Alberto Bugarin-Diz,Maria-Esther Vidal,David Chaves-Fraga*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BLINKG is introduced as a benchmark to evaluate LLMs' ability to map heterogeneous data sources to ontology terms for KG construction; experiments show LLMs are promising but struggle in complex scenarios; the work defines requirements for (semi-)automated LLM-driven KG construction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aligning input schemas with ontology terms is time-consuming and error-prone, and there is no standardized framework to evaluate LLM-based mapping capabilities. A benchmark is needed to quantify and compare LLM performance and guide research.

Method: Develop BLINKG consisting of scenarios with increasing complexity drawn from real-world use cases; conduct extensive experiments with state-of-the-art LLMs to assess their mapping/correspondence capabilities and establish evaluation metrics; analyze findings to derive requirements for automated/semi-automated KG construction.

Result: LLMs show promising capabilities but performance drops in more complex scenarios; the benchmark enables systematic assessment and comparative analysis of LLMs for KG construction and reveals areas needing improvement.

Conclusion: BLINKG provides a concrete evaluation framework for LLM-driven KG construction, outlines requirements for achieving (semi-)automated processes, and opens new directions for research in this area.
