Result: On long-context reasoning and information aggregation, PEEK improves over baselines by 6.3-34.0% with 93-145 fewer iterations and 1.7-5.8x lower cost than ACE. On context learning, it improves solving rate by 6.0-14.0% and rubric accuracy by 7.8-12.1%, at 1.4x lower cost than ACE. Gains generalize across LMs and architectures.

Conclusion: A context map provides persistent, reusable orientation knowledge about recurring external contexts, enabling more accurate and efficient interaction for long-context LLM workloads.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly operate over long and recurring external contexts, like document corpora and code repositories. Across invocations, existing approaches preserve either the agent's trajectory, passive access to raw material, or task-level strategies. None of them preserves what we argue is most needed for repeated same-context workloads: reusable orientation knowledge (e.g., what the context contains, how it is organized, and which entities, constants, and schemas have historically been useful) about the recurring context itself. We introduce PEEK, a system that caches and maintains this orientation knowledge as a context map: a small, constant-sized artifact in the agent's prompt that gives it a persistent peek into the external context. The map is maintained by a programmable cache policy with three modules: a Distiller that extracts transferable knowledge from inference-time signals, a Cartographer that translates it into structured edits, and a priority-based Evictor that enforces a fixed token budget. On long-context reasoning and information aggregation, PEEK improves over strong baselines by 6.3-34.0% while using 93-145 fewer iterations and incurring 1.7-5.8x lower cost than the state-of-the-art prompt-learning framework, ACE. On context learning, PEEK improves solving rate and rubric accuracy by 6.0-14.0% and 7.8-12.1%, respectively, at 1.4x lower cost than ACE. These gains generalize across LMs and agent architectures, including OpenAI Codex, a production-grade coding agent. Together, these results show that a context map helps long-context LLM agents interact with recurring external contexts more accurately and efficiently.

</details>


### [229] [Probabilistic Tiny Recursive Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19943)
*Amin Sghaier,Ali Parviz,Alexia Jolicoeur-Martineau*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PTRM adds Gaussian-noise-based stochastic exploration to Tiny Recursive Models at test time, generating parallel trajectories that explore diverse solution basins and are chosen via the model's Q head. It achieves large accuracy gains with no retraining, using only 7M parameters and negligible cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deterministic TRMs can converge to suboptimal solutions; existing test-time perturbation/voting methods require task-specific augmentations. A task-agnostic, scalable, test-time exploration framework is needed to improve reasoning accuracy without retraining.

Method: At each deep recursion step, PTRM injects Gaussian noise to create multiple stochastic trajectories. These trajectories run in parallel and explore different solution basins. The final selection among trajectories is made using the model's Q head (the early-stopping signal from TRM). No retraining or task-specific augmentation is required.

Result: PTRM yields substantial accuracy gains across benchmarks: Sudoku-Extreme improves from 87.4% to 98.75%. Pencil Puzzle Bench tasks improve from 62.6% to 91.2%. On Pencil Puzzle Bench, PTRM reaches 91.2% accuracy, nearly double frontier LLMs (55.1%), with cost <0.0001x and using only 7M parameters.

Conclusion: Probabilistic TRM provides a task-agnostic, test-time compute scaling method that enhances TRM performance with minimal parameter overhead and no retraining, achieving strong gains across diverse reasoning benchmarks.

Abstract: Tiny Recursive Models (TRM) solve complex reasoning tasks with a fraction of the parameters of modern large language models (LLMs) by iteratively refining a latent state and final answer. While powerful, their deterministic recursion can lead to convergence at suboptimal solutions, without escape mechanism. A common workaround relies on task-specific input perturbations at test time combined with answer aggregation via voting. We introduce Probabilistic TRM (PTRM), a task-agnostic framework for test-time compute scaling that addresses this limitation through stochastic exploration. PTRM injects Gaussian noise at each deep recursion step, enabling parallel trajectories to explore diverse solution basins, and selects among them using the model's existing Q head (used for early stopping in the original TRM). Without requiring retraining or task-specific augmentations, PTRM enables substantial accuracy gains across benchmarks, including Sudoku-Extreme (87.4% to 98.75%) and on various puzzles from Pencil Puzzle Bench (62.6% to 91.2%). On the latter, PTRM achieves nearly double the accuracy of frontier LLMs (91.2% vs. 55.1%) at less than 0.0001x the cost, using only 7M parameters.

</details>


### [230] [GeoX: Mastering Geospatial Reasoning Through Self-Play and Verifiable Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20006)
*Kyeongjin Ahn,Seungeon Lee,Krishna P. Gummadi,Meeyoung Cha*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GeoX, a self-play framework for spatial reasoning in geospatial VLMs, uses executable programs and a verifier to learn spatial logic with verifiable rewards, reducing annotation needs while boosting performance and releasing a self-play geospatial benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the high cost of annotating the combinatorial space of geospatial questions and enable robust image-grounded spatial reasoning without large-scale curated datasets.

Method: A single multimodal policy generates executable spatial programs and solves them via abduction, deduction, and induction over spatial primitives and an image understanding tool. A verifier executes each program to produce a reward signal, which is used to train the policy (and verifier) with reinforcement learning. The approach also includes a self-play-derived geospatial understanding benchmark.

Result: Base vision-language models improve by up to 5.5 points on average, matching or surpassing baselines trained on millions of curated data.

Conclusion: GeoX demonstrates effective self-play for geospatial reasoning, reducing reliance on human annotations while achieving competitive or superior performance and providing a reusable benchmark for geospatial understanding.

Abstract: Geospatial reasoning requires solving image-grounded problems over the complex spatial structure of a scene. However, developing this capability is hindered by the cost of annotating a vast and combinatorial question space. We propose GeoX, a self-play framework that acquires spatial logic through executable programs that yield verifiable rewards, without relying on large-scale human-curated data Given a satellite or aerial image, our framework employs a single multimodal policy that proposes spatial problems as executable programs and solves them under three reasoning modes-abduction, deduction, and induction-over spatial primitives and an image understanding tool. A verifier executes each program to covert a reward signal that jointly optimizes the two roles via reinforcement learning. GeoX consistently improves its base VLMs by up to 5.5 points on average, matching or exceeding conventional baselines trained on millions of curated data. Along-side the proposed method, we release a benchmark for geospatial understanding accumulated through self-play.

</details>


### [231] [When Skills Don't Help: A Negative Result on Procedural Knowledge for Tool-Grounded Agents in Offensive Cybersecurity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20023)
*Samuel Jacob Chacko,James Hugglestone,Chashi Mahiul Islam,Xiuwen Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reanalysis of a large MCP-grounded autonomous CTF study shows Skills provide little marginal benefit in offensive cybersecurity; higher documentation richness maps to ablations; the key missing variable is environment-feedback bandwidth, which reduces reliance on Skills. A falsifiable hypothesis is proposed and a replication pipeline will be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify when agent Skills help or harm performance, by reexamining a 180-run controlled study across four documentation conditions and extending insights to a domain with limited Skills benchmarks (offensive cybersecurity).

Method: Reanalyze a published 180-run controlled experiment of an MCP-grounded autonomous Capture-the-Flag agent under four documentation conditions with increasing lines of documentation (55, 1,478, 1,976, 4,147). Interpret these as No-Skills, Experiential-Skills, Curated-Skills, and Comprehensive-Skills ablations. Assess performance deltas, statistical significance (p=0.71, χ^2; p=0.25, Cochran–Armitage trend test; five of six Cohen's h below 0.2), and consider timing-side-channel effects. Frame design implications and propose a falsifiable hypothesis; plan to release the reanalysis pipeline for replication.

Result: In offensive cybersecurity, the marginal benefit of Skills collapses: the No-Skills vs Full-Skills delta is 8.9 percentage points and not statistically significant. The spread is small and often within noise. Five of six Cohen's h values are below 0.2. The authors attribute this to environment-feedback bandwidth: when the tool layer provides strict, schema-validated, low-latency observations, the environment provides the procedural correction signals Skills normally provide. This diminishes Skills' value and can even degrade performance in timing-sensitive settings. The work proposes a falsifiable hypothesis and discusses implications for designing compound AI systems; a reanalysis pipeline will be released to support replication.

Conclusion: Environment-feedback bandwidth is the missing variable that mediates Skills effectiveness. With high-quality, low-latency observations from the environment, Skills become less necessary, and in some settings may hinder performance. The authors outline design implications for multi-agent/compound AI systems and offer a replication path through an released reanalysis pipeline.

Abstract: Agent Skills, structured packages of procedural knowledge loaded into an LLM agent at inference time, are widely reported to improve task pass rates by an average of 16.2~percentage points across diverse domains. Yet the same benchmarks show wide variance, with 16 of 84 tasks suffering negative deltas when Skills are introduced. The community has not yet articulated a clean mechanism for \emph{when} Skills help and when they are merely redundant overhead. We re-analyze a recently published 180-run controlled study of an MCP-grounded autonomous Capture-the-Flag (CTF) agent under four documentation conditions of increasing richness (55, 1{,}478, 1{,}976, and 4{,}147 lines), and show that these conditions correspond almost exactly to a No-Skills, Experiential-Skills, Curated-Skills, and Comprehensive-Skills ablation. In offensive cybersecurity, a domain not deeply covered by existing Skills benchmarks, the marginal benefit of Skills collapses. The spread between the no-Skills and full-Skills conditions is only 8.9~pp ($p = 0.71$, $χ^2$; $p = 0.25$, Cochran--Armitage trend test; five of six pairwise Cohen's $h$ values fall below the $0.2$ small-effect threshold). We argue that the missing variable is \emph{environment-feedback bandwidth}. When an agent's tool layer returns strict, schema-validated, low-latency observations, the environment itself supplies the procedural correction signal that Skills are normally needed to provide. As a result, the marginal benefit of curated Skills diminishes substantially, and, in some cases (e.g., our timing side-channel setting), actively degrades performance. We articulate a falsifiable hypothesis, sketch its design implications for compound AI systems, and will release the reanalysis pipeline to support replication.

</details>


### [232] [AutoResearchClaw: Self-Reinforcing Autonomous Research with Human-AI Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20025)
*Jiaqi Liu,Shi Qiu,Mairui Li,Bingzhou Li,Haonian Ji,Siwei Han,Xinyu Ye,Peng Xia,Zihan Dong,Congyu Zhang,Letian Zhang,Guiming Chen,Haoqin Tu,Xinyu Yang,Lu Feng,Xujiang Zhao,Haifeng Chen,Jiawei Zhou,Xiao Wang,Weitong Zhang,Hongtu Zhu,Yun Li,Jieru Mei,Hongliang Fei,Jiaheng Zhang,Linjie Li,Linjun Zhang,Yuyin Zhou,Sheng Wang,Caiming Xiong,James Zou,Zeyu Zheng,Cihang Xie,Mingyu Ding,Huaxiu Yao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoResearchClaw is a multi-agent autonomous research pipeline that integrates five mechanisms to iteratively challenge hypotheses and learn from failures, outperforming a baseline AI Scientist v2 on ARC-Bench, with explicit human-in-the-loop modes and cross-run learning, positioning itself as a research amplifier.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous research systems are largely linear pipelines with single-agent reasoning, stop on failures, and no cross-run experience transfer, limiting iterative scientific discovery. There is a need for multi-agent debate, self-healing execution, verifiable reporting, human-in-the-loop oversight, and cross-run knowledge reuse to better emulate real scientific practice.

Method: (1) Structured multi-agent debate for hypothesis generation and result analysis; (2) a self-healing executor with a Pivot/Refine decision loop to turn failures into information; (3) verifiable result reporting to prevent fabrication and hallucinations; (4) human-in-the-loop collaboration with seven intervention modes spanning autonomy to step-by-step oversight; (5) cross-run evolution to convert past mistakes into future safeguards.

Result: On ARC-Bench, a 25-topic experiment-stage benchmark, AutoResearchClaw outperforms AI Scientist v2 by 54.7%. A human-in-the-loop ablation across seven intervention modes shows that targeted collaboration at high-leverage decision points yields better performance than both full autonomy and exhaustive step-by-step oversight.

Conclusion: AutoResearchClaw should be viewed as a research amplifier that augments human scientific judgment rather than replaces it; code is publicly available at the given GitHub URL.

Abstract: Automating scientific discovery requires more than generating papers from ideas. Real research is iterative: hypotheses are challenged from multiple perspectives, experiments fail and inform the next attempt, and lessons accumulate across cycles. Existing autonomous research systems often model this process as a linear pipeline: they rely on single-agent reasoning, stop when execution fails, and do not carry experience across runs. We present AutoResearchClaw, a multi-agent autonomous research pipeline built on five mechanisms: structured multi-agent debate for hypothesis generation and result analysis, a self-healing executor with a \textsc{Pivot}/\textsc{Refine} decision loop that transforms failures into information, verifiable result reporting that prevents fabricated numbers and hallucinated citations, human-in-the-loop collaboration with seven intervention modes spanning full autonomy to step-by-step oversight, and cross-run evolution that converts past mistakes into future safeguards. On ARC-Bench, a 25-topic experiment-stage benchmark, AutoResearchClaw outperforms AI Scientist v2 by 54.7%. A human-in-the-loop ablation across seven intervention modes reveals that precise, targeted collaboration at high-leverage decision points consistently outperforms both full autonomy and exhaustive step-by-step oversight. We position AutoResearchClaw as a research amplifier that augments rather than replaces human scientific judgment. Code is available at https://github.com/aiming-lab/AutoResearchClaw.

</details>


### [233] [Neurosymbolic Learning for Inference-Time Argumentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20098)
*Gabriel Freedman,Adam Dejl,Adam Gould,Mansi,Lihu Chen,Jianqi Jiang,Francesca Toni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ITA is a trainable neurosymbolic framework for ternary claim verification that uses formal argumentation semantics to generate and score arguments, enabling true/false/uncertain predictions that are faithful and inspectable. It improves over argumentative baselines and competes with non-argumentative baselines on two datasets, with predictions deterministically derived from argumentative structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In high-stakes domains, binary truth judgments are often insufficient; uncertainty and transparent, faithful explanations are essential. A neurosymbolic approach that integrates formal argumentation with language models can produce interpretable, faithful verdicts.

Method: Introduce inference-time argumentation (ITA): a trainable framework where formal argumentation semantics assign base scores to generated arguments; these guides LLM training (arguments are generated and scored) and are used to compute ternary predictions (true/false/uncertain). Training optimizes the quality of argumentative predictions; inference uses the arguments and scores to derive deterministic, inspectable verdicts.

Result: On two ternary claim verification datasets, ITA outperforms purely argumentative baselines and is competitive with non-argumentative direct-prediction models, while ensuring that verdicts are computed deterministically from explicit argumentative structures.

Conclusion: ITA demonstrates that coupling trainable argument generation with formal argumentation semantics yields both predictive performance and interpretable, faithful verdicts. It suggests a viable direction for neurosymbolic, interpretable NLP in high-stakes tasks.

Abstract: Claim verification is an important problem in high-stakes settings, including health and finance. When information underpinning claims is incomplete or conflicting, uncertain answers may be more appropriate than binary true or false classifications. In all cases, faithful explanations of the considerations determining the final verdict are crucial. We introduce inference-time argumentation (ITA), a trainable neurosymbolic framework for ternary claim verification in which a formal argumentation semantics giving the strength of claims is used both (i) to guide LLM training as models learn to generate arguments and assign them base scores (representing intrinsic strengths) and (ii) to compute ternary (true/false/uncertain) predictions from generated, scored arguments. As a result, at training time, argument generation and scoring can be optimised according to the quality of the induced argumentative predictions. Moreover, at inference time, the final prediction is faithful, by construction, to the arguments and scores determining the verdict, rather than being justified by a potentially unfaithful post-hoc reasoning trace as in conventional reasoning models. We finally show that, on two datasets for ternary claim verification, ITA improves upon argumentative baselines and can perform competitively against non-argumentative direct-prediction baselines, while providing verdicts that are computed deterministically from explicit, inspectable argumentative structures.

</details>


### [234] [Using Aristotle API for AI-Assisted Theorem Proving in Lean 4: A Formalisation Case Study of the Grasshopper Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20120)
*Gabriel Rongyang Lau*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI-assisted Lean 4 formalization of Grasshopper/IMO 2009 Problem 6 yields four verified local lemmas but leaves the main theorem unproved; illustrates that local proof search can succeed while global combinatorial counting remains unresolved; provides a reproducible case study of limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess the evidential status of AI-generated formal proofs and isolate where local verification stops short of global conclusions.

Method: Lean 4 case study formalization using an Aristotle API proof attempt; generated generalized theorem; four verified helper lemmas; main theorem left with a single unresolved sorry; analysis of which parts are verified vs unverified; produce a reproducible artifact.

Result: Verified components: four local lemmas establishing partial sum equalities, limited impact of adjacent swaps, and a maximality-forbidden-set fact; main global counting lemma not encoded; summary points to global counting step; artifact demonstrates gap between local proof search success and global combinatorial reasoning.

Conclusion: Highlights a central limitation in AI-assisted formalization: local proof search can succeed while the required global bookkeeping remains unresolved; provides a reproducible Lean artifact and precise content analysis; suggests need for integrated global counting lemmas or improved search guidance.

Abstract: AI-assisted theorem proving can now generate substantial Lean developments for olympiad-level mathematics, but the evidential status of such developments depends on which declarations are actually verified. This paper reports a Lean 4 formalization case study of an Aristotle API proof attempt for the Grasshopper problem, originally posed as IMO 2009 Problem 6. The generated artifact states a generalized Lean version of the theorem, contains four verified helper lemmas for local components of a maximality and adjacent-swap exchange strategy, and leaves the main theorem grasshopper closed directly by one unresolved sorry. The verified components establish that the final partial sum equals the total sum, that an adjacent transposition can affect only the relevant intermediate partial sum, that the changed partial sum has the expected form, and that maximality at a position admitting an adjacent successor swap forces a corresponding forbidden-set membership fact. The Aristotle output summary identifies the intended remaining mathematical step as the global counting step needed to show that these membership facts produce at least n distinct forbidden values, contradicting the cardinality assumption |M| < n; the Lean source itself does not reduce the main theorem to a separately encoded counting lemma. This case study gives an inspectable example of a central limitation in AI-assisted formalization, namely that local proof search can succeed while the global combinatorial bookkeeping required for a theorem remains unresolved. The paper contributes a reproducible Lean artifact and a precise analysis of its verified and unverified proof content.

</details>


### [235] [Not Every Rubric Teaches Equally: Policy-Aware Rubric Rewards for RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20164)
*Utkarsh Tyagi,Xingang Guo,MohammadHossein Rezaei,Daniel George,Anas Mahmoud,Jackson Lee,Bing Liu,Yunzhong He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: POW3R introduces a policy-aware rubric reward that preserves human rubric weights while adaptively weighting criteria during training via rollout-level contrast, improving rubric metrics and data efficiency over vanilla rubric RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static rubric aggregations conflate human importance with current usefulness of criteria, causing misaligned optimization. Many criteria may be saturated or unreachable, while discriminative criteria are not necessarily the highest-weighted. A method is needed to separate what matters for the final answer from what aids learning.

Method: POW3R maintains rubric targets and category balance while dynamically adjusting criterion-level reward weights during training. It uses rollout-level contrast to emphasize criteria that currently differentiate the policy outputs, thereby making the rubric reward more informative without changing the evaluation target (GRPO).

Result: Across three base policies and two datasets spanning multimodal and text-only settings, POW3R wins 24 of 30 base-policy/metric comparisons, improves mean rubric reward and strict completion over vanilla GRPO with rubric rewards, and achieves the same performance plateau in 2.5–4× fewer training steps.

Conclusion: Rubric rewards should distinguish what should matter in the final answer from what can teach the current policy; policy-aware, dynamic weighting yields better quality and efficiency in rubric-based RL.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has made post-training highly effective when correctness can be checked automatically. However, many important model behaviors require satisfying several qualitative criteria at once. Rubric-based rewards address this setting by grading prompt-specific criteria and aggregating them into a scalar reward. Yet standard static aggregations conflate a criterion's human-assigned importance with its current usefulness as an optimization signal. We show that this assumption breaks down in rubric RL: many important criteria are already saturated or currently unreachable, while criteria that distinguish rollouts are not necessarily those with the largest human weights. We introduce POW3R, a policy-aware rubric reward framework that preserves human weights and category balance as the rubric objective while adapting criterion-level reward weights during training. POW3R uses rollout-level contrast to emphasize criteria that currently separate the policy's outputs, making the GRPO reward more informative without changing the underlying evaluation target. Across three base policies on two datasets spanning multimodal and text-only settings, POW3R wins $24$ of $30$ base-policy/metric comparisons, improving both mean rubric reward and strict completion (the fraction of prompts whose response satisfies every required rubric criterion) over vanilla GRPO with rubric rewards, and reaches the same plateau in $2.5$--$4\times$ fewer training steps. Rubric rewards should therefore distinguish what should matter in the final answer from what can teach the current policy.

</details>


### [236] [HaorFloodAlert: Deseasonalized ML Ensemble for 72-Hour Flood Prediction in Bangladesh Haor Wetlands](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20167)
*Salma Hoque Talukdar Koli,Fahima Haque Talukder Jely,Md. Samiul Alim,Md. Zakir Hossen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A deseasonalized ML ensemble forecasts 72-hour flood probability for Sunamganj Haor; integrates a SAR-based upstream lead time; achieves high accuracy and provides tiered alerts and crop damage estimator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backwater flood dynamics in haor wetlands are poorly captured by riverine-focused systems; need timely, basin-specific forecasting to protect boro rice.

Method: Develop HaorFloodAlert, an ensemble of RF and XGBoost that is deseasonalized; use temperature as a seasonal cheat; construct upstream Barak River Sentinel-1 SAR proxy from Silchar to provide ~36 h lead; apply Otsu thresholding for SAR change detection; implement three-tier alert pipeline; BRRI-calibrated boro rice damage estimator.

Result: LOOCV accuracy 89.6%, recall 87.5%, AUC-ROC 0.943 on 77 Sentinel-1 events; SAR proxy yields 84-91% spatial match; ensemble weights RF 0.5625 and XGBoost 0.4375; 36 h lead; 72-hour forecast; 3-tier alert.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective integration of deseasonalized ML with SAR-based upstream monitoring to forecast haor floods, enabling actionable alerts and crop-impact estimation; suitable for near-term operational deployment, with caveats regarding seasonal confounds and data availability.

Abstract: Flash floods in Bangladesh's haor wetlands show up with almost no warning. They wreck the annual boro rice harvest. Current setups, built for riverine floods, miss backwater dynamics entirely. These basins are flat. Water does not behave like it does on the Brahmaputra.
  We built HaorFloodAlert, a deseasonalized machine learning ensemble that forecasts 72-hour flood probability for the Sunamganj Haor (approximately 8,000 km2). Temperature was acting as a seasonal cheat code - it inflated accuracy by 6.9 pp just because floods happen in warm months. We caught that. We also built an upstream Barak River Sentinel-1 SAR proxy from Silchar, Assam, giving about 36 hours of lead time. Otsu-thresholded SAR change detection validates at 84-91 percent spatial match.
  The operational ensemble (RF 0.5625 + XGBoost 0.4375) hits 89.6 percent LOOCV accuracy, 87.5 percent recall, and 0.943 AUC-ROC on 77 real Sentinel-1 events. A three-tier alert pipeline and a BRRI-calibrated boro rice damage estimator are included.

</details>


### [237] [A Methodology for Selecting and Composing Runtime Architecture Patterns for Production LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20173)
*Vasundra Srinivasan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes the stochastic-deterministic boundary (SDB) as the core primitive for production LLM agents, organizing runtime design into three concerns and six patterns, plus a five-step methodology, diagnostics, and a runnable reference implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Treat the boundary between stochastic LLM outputs and deterministic systems as a first-class architectural object to improve reliability and long-run stability in production agents.

Method: Define SDB, classify runtime concerns (Coordination, State, Control), catalog six patterns (hierarchical delegation; scatter-gather + saga; event-driven sequencing; shared state machine; supervisor + gate; human in the loop); map patterns to distributed-systems lineage; develop five-step pattern selection methodology; diagnostic for failures; define replay divergence; perform stylized reliability decomposition; apply to five workloads; provide one runnable 90-day contract-renewal agent.

Result: Catalog of six runtime patterns across conversational, autonomous, long-horizon agents; five-step pattern-selection methodology; diagnostic procedure; failure mode 'replay divergence'; reliability decomposition; empirical application to five workloads; one runnable reference implementation.

Conclusion: As model variance declines, pattern choice and SDB strength become key levers for long-run reliability; the SDB is a load-bearing primitive; the framework offers practical guidance and a runnable example for deployment.

Abstract: Production LLM agents combine stochastic model outputs with deterministic software systems, yet the boundary between the two is rarely treated as a first-class architectural object. This paper names that boundary the stochastic-deterministic boundary (SDB): a four-part contract among a proposer, verifier, commit step, and reject signal that specifies how an LLM output becomes a system action. We argue that the SDB is the load-bearing primitive of production agent runtimes.
  Around this primitive, we organize agent runtime design into three concerns: Coordination, State, and Control. We present a catalog of six runtime patterns that compose the SDB differently across conversational, autonomous, and long-horizon agents: hierarchical delegation, scatter-gather plus saga, event-driven sequencing, shared state machine, supervisor plus gate, and human in the loop. For each pattern, we trace its lineage to distributed-systems concepts and identify what changes when the worker is stochastic.
  The paper contributes a five-step methodology for selecting runtime patterns, a diagnostic procedure that maps production failures to pattern weaknesses, and a failure mode called replay divergence, in which LLM-based consumers of a deterministic event log produce different downstream outputs under model-version or prompt changes. A stylized reliability decomposition separates per-call model variance from architectural momentum, motivating the claim that as model variance decreases, pattern choice and SDB strength become increasingly important levers for long-run reliability. We apply the methodology to five workloads and provide one runnable reference implementation for a 90-day contract-renewal agent.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [238] [Dimensional Balance Improves Large Scale Spatiotemporal Prediction Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18793)
*Jing Chen,Shixiang Pan,Yujie Fan,Haocheng Ye,Haitao Xu,Wenqiang Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes ST-Balance to harmonize spatial and temporal features for spatiotemporal forecasting using low-rank spatial embedding and extended temporal horizon; demonstrates strong cross-domain gains on traffic, weather, and epidemic data; code available on GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses bottlenecks in spatiotemporal forecasting: incremental gains and limited cross-domain transferability. Uses spatial/temporal entropy as diagnostic indicators of mismatch rather than guarantees to improve forecasting.

Method: A scalable adaptive framework. Spatial dimensionality compressed via low-rank matrix embedding to preserve essential spatial structure. Temporal horizon extended to capture long-range dependencies and reduce cumulative errors from temporal heterogeneity. Entropy mismatch used as a diagnostic to guide harmonization of spatial and temporal features.

Result: Empirical results show substantial accuracy gains across urban traffic, meteorological, and epidemic datasets, with broad applicability across evaluated domains. Code available at GitHub.

Conclusion: Entropy-based diagnostic signals guide a harmonized, scalable spatiotemporal representation, yielding cross-domain improvements and suggesting broad applicability beyond the studied domains.

Abstract: Accurate spatiotemporal pattern analysis is critical in fields such as urban traffic, meteorology, and public health monitoring. However, existing methods face performance bottlenecks, typically yielding only incremental gains and often exhibiting limited cross-domain transferability. We analyze this bottleneck through spatial and temporal entropy measures, which are used as diagnostic indicators of spatiotemporal complexity mismatch rather than as guarantees that entropy alignment alone yields better forecasting. Empirically, larger mismatch is often accompanied by higher prediction uncertainty, especially under a fixed model-capacity budget. Guided by this diagnostic, we propose a scalable, adaptive framework that harmonizes spatial and temporal feature representations. Spatial dimensionality is compressed via low-rank matrix embedding to preserve essential structure, while an extended temporal horizon captures long-range dependencies and mitigates cumulative errors arising from temporal heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on urban traffic, meteorological, and epidemic datasets demonstrate substantial accuracy gains and broad applicability across the evaluated domains, suggesting that the framework is promising for a wide range of spatiotemporal tasks beyond the current study. The code is available on GitHub at https://github.com/ST-Balance/ST-Balance.

</details>


### [239] [Robust Basis Spline Decoupling for the Compression of Transformer Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18794)
*Joppe De Jonghe,Van Tien Pham,Mariya Ishteva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a B-spline-based decoupling framework (R-CMTF-BSD) for tensor-factorized single-layer decoupling, improving numerical stability and expressiveness while enabling parameter-efficient neural network compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decoupling models multivariate functions via linear transformations and univariate nonlinearities. Existing tensor-based decoupling uses polynomial/piecewise-linear parametrizations, which can be numerically unstable or insufficiently expressive. A stable, flexible representation is desired for structured NN compression.

Method: Formulates a constrained coupled matrix-tensor factorization and solves it with a robust alternating least-squares algorithm (R-CMTF-BSD), incorporating normalization and Tikhonov regularization. Uses B-spline bases with local support and adjustable smoothness to represent internal nonlinearities.

Result: Validated on synthetic data and transformer model compression experiments; achieves substantial parameter reduction while maintaining competitive accuracy in Vision and Swin Transformer architectures.

Conclusion: B-spline decoupling offers a numerically stable, expressive approach for structured NN compression; the R-CMTF-BSD algorithm is a promising tool for efficient, parameter-light neural networks.

Abstract: Decoupling is a powerful modeling paradigm for representing multivariate functions as compositions of linear transformations and univariate nonlinear functions. A single-layer decoupling can be viewed as a fully connected neural network with a single hidden layer and flexible activation functions, providing a direct link with neural networks. Because of this, the use of decoupling methods has gained increasing attention in neural network domains, particularly compression, since it enables structured approximations with reduced parameter complexity. Existing tensor-based decoupling methods typically rely on polynomial or piecewise-linear parameterizations of the internal nonlinear functions, which can suffer from numerical instability or limited expressiveness. In this work, we introduce a B-spline-based decoupling framework that generalizes these existing approaches. By exploiting the local support and flexible smoothness control of B-splines, the proposed formulation yields a more numerically stable and expressive representation. We derive a constrained coupled matrix-tensor factorization and propose a robust alternating least-squares algorithm, called R-CMTF-BSD, incorporating normalization and Tikhonov regularization. The proposed method is validated through experiments on synthetic data and transformer model compression. Results on the Vision and Swin Transformer architectures demonstrate that B-spline decoupling enables substantial parameter reduction while maintaining competitive accuracy, making the R-CMTF-BSD algorithm a promising tool for structured neural network compression.

</details>


### [240] [HELLoRA: Hot Experts Layer-Level Low-Rank Adaptation for Mixture-of-Experts Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18795)
*Jia Wei,Zhonghao Zhang,Ping Chen,Qianyang li,Yancheng Pan,Shaoxun Wang,Ziyi Qiu,Longxiang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Activation-aware, layer-level Low-Rank Adaptation (HELLoRA) for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) reduces trainable parameters and adapter FLOPs while boosting performance; extended HELLoRI under extreme budgets; shows consistent gains across multiple MoE backbones and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods focus on dense architectures; MoE models offer sparse activation patterns that can be exploited to reduce adaptation cost and maintain or improve performance.

Method: Attach LoRA modules only to the most frequently activated experts at each layer (Hot-Experts Layer-level LoRA, HELLoRA). To stress-test under tight budgets, combine with LoRI (freeze up-projection and sparsify the down-projection) to form HELLoRI. Evaluate across three MoE backbones (OlMoE-1B-7B, Mixtral-8x7B, DeepSeekMoE) and three task families (math reasoning, code generation, safety alignment).

Result: HELLoRA reduces trainable parameters and adapter FLOPs while improving accuracy; relative to vanilla LoRA on OlMoE: uses 15.7% of trainable parameters, 38.7% fewer FLOPs, 1.9x higher training throughput, and 9.2% accuracy improvement. On DeepSeekMoE, HELLoRA outperforms LoRA while using only 23.2% of LoRA’s trainable parameters. Across all three MoE backbones and task families, HELLoRA consistently outperforms strong PEFT baselines.

Conclusion: Activation-aware (hot-expert) placement is an effective and practical strategy to scale PEFT for MoE language models, with HELLoRA and HELLoRI delivering substantial parameter/compute savings and performance gains.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) dominates parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models, yet most variants target dense architectures. Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale parameters at near-constant per-token compute, and their sparse activation patterns create untapped opportunities for more efficient adaptation. We propose Hot-Experts Layer-level Low-Rank Adaptation (HELLoRA), which attaches LoRA modules only to the most frequently activated experts at each layer. This simple mechanism reduces trainable parameters and adapter-induced FLOPs while improving downstream performance, an effect we attribute to a form of structured regularization that preserves pretrained expert specialization. To stress-test HELLoRA under extreme parameter budgets, we further compose it with LoRI to form HELLoRI, which freezes the up-projection and sparsifies the down-projection. Across three MoE backbones, namely OlMoE-1B-7B, Mixtral-8x7B, and DeepSeekMoE, and three task families covering mathematical reasoning, code generation, and safety alignment, HELLoRA consistently outperforms strong PEFT baselines. Relative to vanilla LoRA on OlMoE, HELLoRA uses 15.7% of the trainable parameters, reduces adapter FLOPs by 38.7%, achieves 1.9x the training throughput, and improves accuracy by 9.2%. On DeepSeekMoE, HELLoRA outperforms LoRA while using only 23.2% of its trainable parameters. These results demonstrate that activation-aware adapter placement is an effective and practical route to scaling PEFT for MoE language models.

</details>


### [241] [UCCI: Calibrated Uncertainty for Cost-Optimal LLM Cascade Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18796)
*Varun Kotte*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Calibration-first router UCCI uses isotonic regression to map token-level margin uncertainty to per-query error probability, enabling cost-minimizing escalation thresholds; demonstrates ~31% inference-cost reduction and improved calibration on production NER workloads, outperforming entropy thresholding, split-conformal routing, and FrugalGPT-like learned thresholds; end-to-end routing on measured latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce inference cost in LLM cascades by reliable, workload-tuned routing without ad-hoc per-workload thresholds, leveraging calibrated uncertainty estimates.

Method: Calibrate margin-based uncertainty via isotonic regression to obtain per-query error probability; select escalation threshold by constrained cost minimization; prove threshold optimality under three assumptions; show sample complexity ECE O(n^{-1/3}); perform on production NER workload with 4B/12B models on H100; compare to baselines.

Result: 31% cost reduction (CI 27%-35%), micro-F1 0.91 on 75k queries; ECE reduced from 0.12 to 0.03; outperforms entropy thresholding, split-conformal routing, FrugalGPT thresholds; end-to-end routing with measured latency.

Conclusion: Calibration-first routing with isotonic regression yields cost-optimal cascade decisions under stated assumptions, with strong calibration improvements and practical gains on real workloads; isotonic calibration offers favorable sample efficiency.

Abstract: LLM cascades and model routing promise lower inference cost by sending easy queries to a small model and escalating hard ones to a large model, but most deployed routers use uncalibrated confidence scores and require per-workload threshold tuning. We present UCCI, a calibration-first router that maps token-level margin uncertainty to a per-query error probability via isotonic regression and selects the escalation threshold by constrained cost minimization. Under three explicit assumptions, threshold policies on the calibrated score are cost-optimal, and isotonic calibration achieves O(n^{-1/3}) sample complexity for expected calibration error (ECE). On a production named entity recognition workload of 75,000 queries served by 4B and 12B instruction-tuned LLMs on H100 GPUs, UCCI cuts inference cost by 31% (95% CI: [27%, 35%]) at micro-F1 = 0.91 while reducing ECE from 0.12 to 0.03. At the same operating point, UCCI beats entropy thresholding, split-conformal routing, and a FrugalGPT-style learned threshold. All cascade results use end-to-end routing on actual model outputs and measured H100 latency, not simulated routing from global accuracies or nominal API prices.

</details>


### [242] [Simply Stabilizing the Loop via Fully Looped Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18797)
*Rao Fu,Zixuan Yang,Jiankun Zhang,Jing Ma,Hechang Chen,Yu Li,Yi Chang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fully Looped Transformer stabilizes training and improves performance for Looped Transformer by introducing two parameter-free modifications, enabling up to 12 iterations and achieving up to 13.2% gains on downstream tasks, with inference-time loop count as a compute budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling model performance via iterations (looped transformers) without increasing parameters or context length faces training instability from gradient oscillation and residual explosion; the work aims to stabilize training and unlock larger loop budgets.

Method: Two parameter-free modifications: (1) Fully Looped Architecture distributes inter-loop signals across all layers to mitigate residual explosion; (2) Attention Injection reuses the existing attention block to suppress gradient oscillation.

Result: Training remains stable up to 12 loop iterations (baseline looped models collapse earlier). In milder settings, Fully Looped Transformer improves average downstream-task performance by up to 13.2%. It also enables adaptability to test-time budgets via varying loop iterations at inference.

Conclusion: The approach stabilizes training, enhances downstream performance, and provides preliminary adaptability across compute budgets, offering a viable path to scaling via iteration without increasing parameters or context length.

Abstract: Scaling model performance typically requires increasing model size. Looped Transformer offers a compelling alternative by iteratively reusing the same Transformer blocks, trading additional computation for improved performance without increasing parameter count or context length. Because the number of loop iterations can be adjusted at inference, it also provides a natural mechanism for balancing performance and test-time compute. However, Looped Transformer still suffers from training instability when the number of loop iterations increases. Our analysis reveals that this instability stems from two sources: gradient oscillation and residual explosion. To address these two problems, we propose the Fully Looped Transformer, which introduces two parameter-free modifications: (1) Fully Looped Architecture, which distributes inter-loop signals across all layers to mitigate residual explosion; (2) Attention Injection, which reuses the existing attention block to suppress gradient oscillation. These modifications stabilize training dynamics, enabling the Fully Looped Transformer to be trained stably up to 12 loop iterations, whereas other baseline looped models collapse in this regime. In milder settings where Looped Transformer does not collapse, Fully Looped Transformer still improves average downstream-task performance by up to 13.2\%. Overall, our experiments demonstrate that Fully Looped Transformer improves training stability, enhances downstream performance, and provides preliminary adaptability under different test-time compute budgets by varying loop iterations at inference.

</details>


### [243] [Accurate Evaluation of Quickest Changepoint Detectors via Non-parametric Survival Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18798)
*Taiki Miyagawa,Akinori F. Ebihara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Non-parametric Kaplan-Meier-inspired estimators (KM-ARL and KM-ADD) for average run length and average detection delay in quickest changepoint detection under finite and irregular sequence lengths; uses a survival-analysis analogy to model truncation; provides bias bounds and asymptotic unbiasedness without extrapolation; validated on simulated and real data; code in Python.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ARL and ADD are standard but assume long, regular sequences; in real-world data sequences are finite and irregular, making classical estimators unreliable. A robust, non-parametric approach is needed.

Method: Develop KM-ARL and KM-ADD by mapping detection probabilities under sequence truncation to Kaplan-Meier estimators; derive bias bounds; prove asymptotic unbiasedness when extrapolation is not required; provide theoretical guarantees.

Result: Empirical results on simulated and real datasets show improved robustness to sequence truncation, better interpretability, and practical utility; accompanied by ready-to-use Python implementations.

Conclusion: Non-parametric KM-based estimators offer robust ARL/ADD estimation under finite/irregular sequences and facilitate empirical model selection; code available for practitioners.

Abstract: We propose non-parametric estimators for the average run length (ARL) and average detection delay (ADD) in quickest changepoint detection (QCD) under finite and irregular sequence lengths. Although ARL and ADD are widely used as optimality criteria in theoretical and simulation studies, their application to real-world datasets is hindered by limited and irregular sequence lengths. To address this issue, we propose non-parametric estimators for the ARL and ADD, termed KM-ARL and KM-ADD, by drawing an analogy between QCD and survival analysis to model detection probabilities under sequence truncation. We derive estimation bias bounds and prove that they are asymptotically unbiased unless extrapolation is required. Experiments on simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate their practical utility, enhancing robustness against limited and irregular sequence lengths, improving interpretability, and facilitating empirical, intuitive model selection. Our Python code is provided at https://github.com/TaikiMiyagawa/Kaplan-Meier-Average-Run-Length, offering ready-to-use implementations for practitioners.

</details>


### [244] [ReCrit: Transition-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Scientific Critic Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18799)
*Wanghan Xu,Yuhao Zhou,Hengyuan Zhao,Shuo Li,Dianzhi Yu,Zhenfei Yin,Yaowen Hu,Fengli Xu,Wanli Ouyang,Wenlong Zhang,Lei Bai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReCrit is a transition-aware RL framework that improves critic interactions in scientific reasoning by decomposing interaction into four quadrants (Correction, Sycophancy, Robustness, Boundary), using transition-aware rewards, and scalable asynchronous rollout. It yields significant gains in Critic accuracy on ChemBench, TRQA, EarthSE benchmarks across LLMs, with ablations favoring transition-aware rewards over final-answer rewards. Code available at GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In scientific reasoning, a user’s criticism can cause a valid initial solution to become incorrect. Traditional final-answer accuracy metrics miss this inter-turn transition issue; there is a need to train critics to maintain correct reasoning through interaction.

Method: Decompose critic interaction into four quadrants: Correction, Sycophancy, Robustness, Boundary. Use transition-aware reinforcement learning with rewards that reward correction and robustness, penalize sycophancy, and treat persistent errors as weak boundary signals. Employ dynamic asynchronous rollout with tail-adaptive completion to reduce rollout waiting. Train on three scientific-reasoning benchmarks (ChemBench, TRQA, EarthSE) and evaluate critic accuracy.

Result: On Qwen3.5-4B, Critic accuracy improved from 38.15 to 51.49; on Qwen3.5-9B, from 45.40 to 55.59. Ablations indicate final-answer rewards offer little interaction-level gain; transition-aware rewards and quadrant weighting yield stronger signals and greater improvements. Code available at https://github.com/black-yt/ReCrit.

Conclusion: Transition-aware, quadrant-based RL for critic interaction improves stability and accuracy in scientific reasoning tasks, offering practical rollout strategies and demonstrating substantial gains over baselines.

Abstract: Large language models can fail in critic interaction not only by answering incorrectly, but also by abandoning an initially correct scientific solution after user criticism. This is especially risky in scientific reasoning, where user criticism can turn a valid answer into an incorrect one. We frame critic interaction as an inter-turn correctness-transition problem rather than a final-answer accuracy problem, and identify three challenges: transition awareness, decoupling useful correction from harmful sycophancy, and scalable rollout. We propose ReCrit, a transition-aware reinforcement learning framework that decomposes Initial-to-Critic behavior into four quadrants: Correction, Sycophancy, Robustness, and Boundary. ReCrit rewards correction and robustness, penalizes sycophancy, and treats persistent errors as weak boundary signals. To make interaction training practical, ReCrit further uses dynamic asynchronous rollout with tail-adaptive completion to reduce rollout waiting. On three scientific reasoning benchmarks, ChemBench, TRQA, and EarthSE, ReCrit improves average Critic accuracy from 38.15 to 51.49 on Qwen3.5-4B and from 45.40 to 55.59 on Qwen3.5-9B. Ablations show that final-answer rewards provide little interaction-level gain, while transition-aware rewards and quadrant weighting produce more distinguishable training signals and larger net Critic-stage improvement. The code is available at https://github.com/black-yt/ReCrit .

</details>


### [245] [Theory-optimal Quantization Based on Flatness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18800)
*Xiusheng Huang,Zhe Li,Xuanwu Yin,Lu Wang,Yequan Wang,Dong Li,Emad Barsoum,Kang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Bidirectional Diagonal Quantization (BDQ) to disperse activation outlier magnitudes in post-training quantization, supported by a Flatness metric and theoretical optimization. Demonstrates state-of-the-art results for ultra-low-bit quantization on large LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Activation outliers severely degrade low-bit quantization performance in LLMs. Existing linear transforms only partially mitigate this issue; a unified metric and optimization framework are needed to systematically disperse outliers and improve quantization accuracy.

Method: Define Flatness to characterize outlier distributions; derive the theoretical optimum with respect to Flatness; propose BDQ that applies learned diagonal transformations (one on each side) to weight matrices to spread outliers across dimensions in a bidirectional manner; focuses on post-training quantization without full retraining.

Result: BDQ achieves less than 1% accuracy drop in W4A4 quantization on LLaMA-3-8B; in a harder W2A4KV16 setting, it reduces the performance gap by 39.1% on the DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-70B model compared with state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: BDQ provides a principled framework linking outlier distribution (Flatness) to quantization error and demonstrates that learned diagonal matrix transformations can effectively disperse outliers, setting a new benchmark for post-training quantization of large LLMs.

Abstract: Post-training quantization has emerged as a widely adopted technique for compressing and accelerating the inference of Large Language Models (LLMs). The primary challenges in LLMs quantization stem from activation outliers, which significantly degrade model performance especially at lower bit precision. While recent approaches attempt to mitigate outliers through linear transformations across feature dimensions, our analysis reveals that the transformed weights and activations still exhibit persistent outlier patterns with concentrated magnitude distributions. In this paper, we first model the mathematical relationship between quantization error and outliers, and then introduce a new metric Flatness to quantify the distribution of outliers. Based on this, we derive the theoretical optimal solution with respect to Flatness. Building on these insights, we propose Bidirectional Diagonal Quantization (BDQ), a novel post-training quantization framework that effectively disperses outlier patterns through optimized matrix transformations. BDQ strategically distributes outlier magnitudes across matrix dimensions via learned diagonal operations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BDQ establishes a new quantization benchmark. It achieves less than 1\% accuracy drop in W4A4 quantization on the LLaMA-3-8B model. In the more challenging W2A4KV16 experiment, compared to state-of-the-art approaches, BDQ reduces the performance gap by 39.1\% on the DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-70B model.

</details>


### [246] [PROWL: Prioritized Regret-Driven Optimization for World Model Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18803)
*Ahmet H. Güzel,Jenny Seidenschwarz,Benjamin Graham,Jonathan Sadeghi,Jeffrey Hawke,Jack Parker-Holder,Ilija Bogunovic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PROWL introduces a KL-constrained adversarial curriculum to actively uncover high-error trajectories for diffusion-based world models, using a Prioritized Adversarial Trajectory (PAT) buffer to focus training on unresolved failures, yielding robustness gains and exposing reward-hacking under weak constraints; data collection is selective and adversarially guided rather than passive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation:  Passive demonstrations under-sample high-impact, rare transitions critical for robust planning. To improve robustness, models must be trained on informative failure cases discovered actively rather than relying on natural occurrence.

Method:  Train a policy with KL constraint to maximize the world model's prediction error while remaining close to the behavior policy. Continuously fine-tune the diffusion-based world model on these adversarial trajectories. Use a PAT buffer to re-rank trajectories by prediction error, action fidelity, and learning progress to prioritize unresolved failures. Implemented in MineRL framework.

Result: Compared to models trained on passive data, PROWL yields improved robustness on held-out out-of-distribution trajectories, reveals reward-hacking under weak constraints, and demonstrates that balancing exploratory failure discovery with behavioral regularization is critical for effective adversarial world-model training.

Conclusion: Selective, adversarial data generation paired with continual retraining can significantly enhance the robustness of scalable world models, suggesting that larger datasets help, but targeted, informative data collection is crucial.

Abstract: Modern action-conditioned video world models achieve strong short-horizon visual realism, yet remain unreliable on rare, interaction-critical transitions that dominate downstream planning and policy performance. Because passive demonstration data systematically under-samples these high-impact regimes, improving robustness requires actively eliciting model failures rather than relying on their natural occurrence. We introduce a KL-constrained adversarial curriculum in which a policy is trained to expose high-error trajectories of a diffusion-based world model while remaining close to the behavior distribution. The world model is continuously fine-tuned on these adversarially discovered trajectories, yielding an adversarial training loop that converts rare failures into a stable, near-distribution training signal without drifting into out-of-distribution exploitation. To maintain pressure on unresolved weaknesses as the model improves, we propose a Prioritized Adversarial Trajectory (PAT) buffer that re-ranks trajectories based on prediction error, action fidelity, and learning progress, focusing training on unresolved failure modes rather than repeatedly revisiting solved cases. We implement our approach in the MineRL framework and evaluate it on held-out out-of-distribution trajectories; PROWL improves robustness over models trained on passive data alone, reveals reward-hacking behaviors under weak behavioral constraints, and demonstrates that effective adversarial world-model training critically depends on balancing exploratory failure discovery with explicit behavioral regularization. Our results suggest that scalable world models benefit not only from larger datasets, but also from selectively generating informative training data.

</details>


### [247] [Adaptive Multi-Scale Goodness Aggregation for Forward-Forward Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18804)
*Salar Beigzad,Vansh Verma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends the Forward-Forward algorithm with multi-scale goodness aggregation and training dynamics to boost stability, robustness, and generalization in local-learning neural networks, reporting modest accuracy gains on MNIST-family datasets with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations of the original Forward-Forward framework—specifically stability, robustness, generalization, and efficiency—by enriching goodness estimation across multiple representation scales and by better training dynamics.

Method: Introduce AMSGA: (1) multi-scale goodness aggregation across local, intermediate, and global representations; (2) adaptive curriculum-guided hard negative mining; (3) layer-dependent adaptive thresholds; (4) warm-up cosine annealing learning-rate schedule; designed to preserve memory-friendly, biologically plausible local learning.

Result: Empirical evaluation on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST shows consistent improvements over baseline FF, achieving up to +1.45% on MNIST and +1.50% on Fashion-MNIST without significant computational overhead.

Conclusion: Local learning methods can become substantially more competitive when goodness estimation and training dynamics are carefully designed; AMSGA strengthens FF while maintaining its memory-efficient, biologically plausible properties.

Abstract: We propose Adaptive Multi-Scale Goodness Aggregation (AMSGA), a novel extension of the Forward-Forward (FF) algorithm designed to improve stability, robustness, and generalization in local-learning neural networks. AMSGA addresses several limitations of the original FF framework by introducing multi-scale goodness aggregation across local, intermediate, and global representations; adaptive curriculum-guided hard negative mining; layer-dependent adaptive thresholds; and a warm-up cosine annealing learning-rate schedule for improved optimization stability. Together, these modifications strengthen the FF paradigm while preserving its biologically plausible and memory-efficient properties. Experiments on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST demonstrate consistent performance improvements over the baseline FF algorithm, achieving up to +1.45% improvement on MNIST and +1.50% improvement on Fashion-MNIST without significant computational overhead. Our results suggest that local learning methods can become substantially more competitive when goodness estimation and training dynamics are carefully designed.

</details>


### [248] [Block-Based Double Decoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18807)
*Asher Labovich,Benjamin Bradley,Vanessa Alexander,Chaitanya Harsha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Block-based double decoders offer decoder-only training efficiency with encoder-decoder inference efficiency, achieving full supervision via block-based doubly-causal attention and static sequence packing; they outperform encoder-decoders and closely track decoder-only models, reducing KV-cache memory and per-token compute by >=2/3.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve limited scalability of encoder-decoder pretraining due to sparse supervision and dynamic sequence lengths; combine the training efficiency of decoder-only models with the inference efficiency of encoder-decoder architectures.

Method: Introduce block-based doubly-causal attention masks and static sequence packing in a transformer with two decoders; train with full supervision; evaluate on scaling laws and inference metrics; compare to encoder-decoders and decoder-only baselines.

Result: In scaling-law experiments, outperform encoder-decoders across scales and closely track decoder-only performance; at inference, reduce KV-cache memory and per-token compute by at least two-thirds without losing prefill caching or other inference optimizations.

Conclusion: Block-based double decoders provide a practical middle ground achieving scalable training efficiency and inference efficiency, enabling competitive performance with reduced memory/compute.

Abstract: Encoder-decoder models offer substantial inference-time savings over decoder-only models, but their pretraining objectives suffer from sparse supervision and dynamic sequence lengths, keeping them out of practice at scale. We propose block-based double decoders, a novel transformer architecture that utilizes doubly-causal block-based attention masks to train with full loss supervision and static sequence packing, combining decoder-only training efficiency with encoder-decoder inference efficiency. In scaling law experiments, block-based double decoders strongly outperform encoder-decoders and closely track decoder-only models across scales. At inference time, they cut KV-cache memory and per-token compute by at least 2/3 without sacrificing prefill caching or other existing inference optimizations available to decoder-only models.

</details>


### [249] [Compositional Literary Primitives in Instruction-Tuned LLMs: Cross-Architectural SAE Features for Self, Style, and Affect](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18808)
*Joao Paulo Cavalcante Presa,Savio Salvarino Teles de Oliveira*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sparse autoencoder analysis of mid-depth residual streams reveals four feature classes controlling affective output in two instruction-tuned LLMs, with Llama 3.1 achieving full 27-category emotion coverage and Gemma 2 achieving 23, under a 5-judge forced-choice framework across a 27-category taxonomy; a three-stage validation pipeline implies robust, low-compute evidence of compositional emotion control, but with cross-architecture asymmetries in how affect is signaled.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover compositional, controllable primitives governing affective expression in instruction-tuned LLMs by analyzing mid-layer representations with sparse autoencoders, and to validate whether a limited set of latent features can explain broad emotion coverage.

Method: Apply sparse autoencoders to mid-depth residual streams of two LLMs (Llama 3.1 8B-Instruct and Gemma 2 9B-IT) to extract four feature classes: naming-gates, an eleven-self cluster of first-person registers, stylistic register modulators, and compositional emotions that emerge from multi-feature steering. Evaluate affective coverage using a forced-choice 5-LLM judge panel across a 27-category Cowen-Keltner taxonomy. Include random-judge controls and cross-architectural comparisons. Validate via a three-stage pipeline (logit-lens, LLM-rate, 5-LLM judge). Report compute details (single-GPU, ~15 minutes per emotion-feature cycle).

Result: Llama reaches full 27/27 emotion coverage by combining naming-gates, multi-feature recipes, and single self-feature steering; Gemma reaches 23/27 with adoration as the single residual strict-fail. Under random judging, per-cell pass probability is ~1e-3 with negligible two-seed false positives, indicating the results are not due to chance. An asymmetry exists: judges agree more on Llama outputs than Gemma's for the same generations, attributable to direct naming of affect in Llama vs evocative signaling via scene in Gemma. Both models harbor self-features that function as both registers and emitters, including a most-RLHF-loaded self-feature per architecture that modulates persona or affect depending on regime. 

Conclusion: The study demonstrates a compositional, latent-feature architecture for affect in instruction-tuned LLMs, showing near-complete emotion coverage in one model and substantial coverage in another, with robust, low-cost validation. It highlights the role of self-features as dual-role registers/emitters and reveals cross-architecture differences in how affect is signaled. The three-stage validation pipeline provides a practical framework for evaluating emergent affective capabilities in LLMs.

Abstract: We characterize a compositional architecture of literary primitives in two instruction-tuned large language models (Llama 3.1 8B-Instruct and Gemma 2 9B-IT) via sparse autoencoders on mid-depth residual streams. Four feature classes emerge: naming-gates that promote lexical tokens of a target affect, an eleven-self cluster of first-person register features, stylistic register modulators (show-don't-tell and defamiliarization), and compositional emotions that arise only from multi-feature steering. Under a forced-choice 5-LLM judge panel applied to a 27-category emotion taxonomy (Cowen-Keltner), Llama reaches full 27/27 coverage by combining naming-gates, multi-feature recipes, and single self-feature steering; Gemma reaches 23/27 with adoration as the single residual strict-fail. Under random judging, the per-cell pass probability is on the order of $10^{-3}$ and the expected number of two-seed false-positive cells across the catalog is negligible, so the observed coverage is not consistent with chance. A cross-architectural asymmetry sits in the strict-versus-soft judge contrast: on the same generations, judges agree more often on Llama outputs than on Gemma outputs because Llama outputs name the target affect more directly while Gemma outputs evoke it through scene and imagery. Both architectures contain self-features that serve simultaneously as register markers and as emotion emitters, including a single most-RLHF-loaded self-feature per architecture that intensifies the institutional Helper-AI persona at one operating regime and produces affect-categorizable output at the same calibrated coefficient. Methodologically, the paper presents a three-stage validation pipeline (logit-lens, LLM-rate, 5-LLM judge) with documented anti-patterns; the total compute is single-GPU and about 15 minutes per emotion-feature discovery cycle.

</details>


### [250] [Metric-Gradient Projection for Stable Multi-Agent Policy Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18809)
*Zuyuan Zhang,Sizhe Tang,Mahdi Imani,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HPML projects the joint multi-agent update field onto a metric-gradient (potential) component in an L2 vector-field space, yielding a stable, Lyapunov-supported update direction that improves MARL stability/performance with a plug-in projection layer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In general-sum MARL, coupled policy updates create a non-potential, cyclic component that destabilizes learning. Existing stabilization strategies modify local updates; HPML seeks a global, geometric decomposition to isolate and follow the gradient (potential) part.

Method: Formulate the joint update field as an L2 vector field and compute a Hodge-type projection onto the closest metric-gradient flow via a variational (Poisson-type) problem. Implement realizations through graph-based and amortized neural networks to recover projected directions from samples, enabling a plug-in projection layer in MARL pipelines.

Result: The projected dynamics admit a Lyapunov potential and come with equilibrium-gap bounds that include an explicit non-potentiality term. Empirical evaluations validate the mechanism and show that incorporating HPML as a projection layer in CTDE benchmarks yields improved stability and higher normalized returns.

Conclusion: HPML provides a principled, geometry-based projection of the joint update field onto a metric-gradient component, serving as a complementary stabilization layer for MARL that leverages global structure to improve stability and performance.

Abstract: General-sum multi-agent learning is often governed by a stacked update field in which each agent's policy update changes the optimization landscape faced by the others. This coupling can entangle an integrable component of collective improvement with cyclic interaction dynamics, leading to slow or unstable multi-agent learning. Existing approaches, such as regularization, credit assignment, and consensus methods, stabilize MARL through local or algorithmic modifications; HPML complements them by projecting the joint update field onto a metric-gradient component. We introduce \textbf{HPML} (\textbf{H}odge-\textbf{P}rojected \textbf{M}ulti-agent \textbf{L}earning), which views the joint update field of a multi-agent system as an element of an $L^2$ space of vector fields and computes a Hodge-type projection onto the closest metric-gradient potential flow. HPML follows the projected component as the update direction, yielding the closest metric-gradient field under the chosen metric and sampling measure. The projection is defined variationally, characterized by a Poisson-type equation, and implemented through graph-based and amortized neural realizations that recover projected directions from samples. We show that the projected dynamics admit a Lyapunov potential and yield equilibrium-gap bounds with an explicit additive non-potentiality term. Controlled experiments validate the geometric mechanism, and CTDE benchmarks show improved stability and normalized return when HPML is used as a plug-in projection layer in MARL pipelines.

</details>


### [251] [D-PACE: Dynamic Position-Aware Cross-Entropy for Parallel Speculative Drafting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18810)
*Tianyu Wu,Yu Yao,Zhenting Qi,Han Zheng,Zhuohan Wang,Haoran Ma,Lawrence Liao,Himabindu Lakkaraju,Ju Li,Yilun Du*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dynamic, position-aware cross-entropy loss (D-PACE) improves multi-token speculative decoding by weighting training signals per draft position based on the expected accepted draft length, aligning optimization with which positions limit acceptance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-token drafters use fixed, position-dependent weights that do not adapt as the drafter improves and acceptance limits shift; there is a need for training signals that track the evolving per-position bottlenecks to accelerate inference.

Method: derive per-position training weights from a differentiable surrogate of expected accepted draft length; define D-PACE loss that emphasizes positions currently limiting acceptance; no architectural/inference changes, but training signal reweighting guides the drafter to improve bottleneck positions.

Result: Across six benchmarks, two Qwen3-4B draft depths, two decoding temperatures, and two additional target models, D-PACE yields consistent improvements in wall-clock speedup and average emitted length, with 2.3% training-time overhead and no changes to the drafter architecture or inference procedure.

Conclusion: D-PACE effectively adapts training focus to evolving bottleneck positions in speculative multi-token drafting, improving efficiency without altering models or inference and providing robust gains across varied settings.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by having a small drafter propose tokens that a larger target model verifies in parallel. Recent diffusion-based parallel drafters such as DFlash predict the full B-token block in one forward pass, enabling deeper drafters and longer accepted blocks. However, existing multi-token drafter objectives often use fixed position-dependent weighting schedules, such as head-dependent weights or block-position decays, which do not adapt as the positions limiting acceptance change during training. To address this, we derive per-position training weights from a differentiable surrogate of expected accepted draft length, matching the weight of each position to its log-probability gradient contribution. The resulting loss, D-PACE (Dynamic Position-Aware Cross-Entropy), shifts training signal toward positions that currently limit acceptance as the drafter improves. Across six benchmarks, two Qwen3-4B draft depths, two decoding temperatures, and two additional target models, D-PACE consistently improves both wall-clock speedup and average emitted length, with 2.3\% measured training-time overhead and no changes to the drafter architecture or inference procedure.

</details>


### [252] [PASC: Pipeline-Aware Conformal Prediction with Joint Coverage Guarantees for Multi-Stage NLP and LLM Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18812)
*Varun Kotte*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PASC is a pipeline-aware split conformal method that reduces multi-stage joint coverage to a single quantile of the joint max nonconformity score, yielding finite-sample, distribution-free joint coverage guarantees across K stages; it outperforms Bonferroni and independent CP on NER->NED->typing pipelines and scales to K up to 6, with faster runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty quantification in multi-stage NLP and LLM pipelines suffers from stage-wise calibration or overly conservative union bounds; there is a need for joint coverage guarantees with finite-sample, distribution-free properties.

Method: Reduce the joint multi-stage coverage problem to a single scalar conformal prediction problem based on the maximum nonconformity score across stages; obtain finite-sample, distribution-free guarantee that all K stages are covered with probability at least 1-α; requires a single quantile computation and yields efficient, scalable calibration.

Result: Empirical evaluation on a three-stage NER→NED→entity-typing pipeline (CoNLL-2003) shows end-to-end coverage: PASC 96.4% vs Bonferroni 93.4% and independent CP 86.5%; average prediction set size 1.083. Under distribution shift to WNUT-17 and WikiNEuRal, PASC maintains target coverage while independent CP collapses to 59%. PASC runs 1.7× faster than Bonferroni and scales to K=6 stages (independent CP drops to 0.53 coverage). The approach applies similarly to compound LLM systems and agent pipelines.

Conclusion: The joint-maximum-score reduction provides a practical, finite-sample, distribution-free path to joint coverage across multi-stage pipelines, with clear efficiency and scalability advantages and broad applicability to complex AI systems.

Abstract: Modern NLP and LLM systems are pipelines: named entity recognition (NER) -> entity disambiguation (NED) -> entity typing, retrieval-augmented generation (retriever -> reader), and agentic chains of planner -> tool -> critic. Errors compound across stages, but existing uncertainty quantification methods either calibrate each stage independently (no joint coverage) or apply a Bonferroni union bound (joint coverage, but conservative). We present PASC (Pipeline-Aware Split Conformal), which reduces multi-stage joint coverage to a single scalar conformal prediction problem on the joint maximum nonconformity score. PASC provides a finite-sample distribution-free guarantee that all K stages are simultaneously covered with probability at least 1 - alpha, and is nearly tight up to a 1/(n+1) factor. On a three-stage NER -> NED -> entity-typing pipeline over CoNLL-2003, PASC achieves 96.4% end-to-end coverage versus 93.4% for Bonferroni and 86.5% for independent CP, at identical average prediction set size (1.083).
