Under distribution shift to WNUT-17 Twitter and WikiNEuRal Wikipedia data, PASC empirically maintains the target coverage in the tested shift settings while independent CP collapses to 59%. PASC requires a single quantile computation, runs 1.7x faster than Bonferroni, and scales to K = 6 stages where independent CP drops to 0.53 end-to-end coverage. The same joint-maximum-score reduction applies directly to compound LLM systems and agent pipelines.

</details>


### [253] [Composition of Memory Experts for Diffusion World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18813)
*Sebastian Stapf,Pablo Acuaviva Huertos,Aram Davtyan,Paolo Favaro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A diffusion-based, memory-augmented world model using a product-of-experts to combine short-term, long-term, and spatial memory, addressing memory scalability and fidelity in RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the memory-trade-off in world models (local detail vs. long-context fidelity) by decoupling future-past consistency from any single architecture and enabling scalable, long-horizon planning.

Method: Diffusion-based framework integrating heterogeneous memory models via a contrastive product-of-experts. Three specialised experts: (1) short-term memory for fine local dynamics, (2) long-term memory storing episodic history in external diffusion weights with lightweight test-time finetuning, (3) spatial long-term memory enforcing geometric/spatial coherence. The design avoids mode collapse and scales to long contexts without quadratic cost.

Result: Empirically improves temporal consistency, recall of past observations, and navigation performance across simulated and real-world benchmarks; demonstrates a new paradigm for memory-augmented diffusion world models.

Conclusion: Proposes a scalable, memory-augmented diffusion framework that decouples past-future consistency from a single architecture and leverages specialized experts to achieve robust long-horizon modeling without quadratic attention costs.

Abstract: World models aim to predict plausible futures consistent with past observations, a capability central to planning and decision-making in reinforcement learning. Yet, existing architectures face a fundamental memory trade-off: transformers preserve local detail but are bottlenecked by quadratic attention, while recurrent and state-space models scale more efficiently but compress history at the cost of fidelity. To overcome this trade-off, we suggest decoupling future-past consistency from any single architecture and instead leveraging a set of specialized experts. We introduce a diffusion-based framework that integrates heterogeneous memory models through a contrastive product-of-experts formulation. Our approach instantiates three complementary roles: a short-term memory expert that captures fine local dynamics, a long-term memory expert that stores episodic history in external diffusion weights via lightweight test-time finetuning, and a spatial long-term memory expert that enforces geometric and spatial coherence. This compositional design avoids mode collapse and scales to long contexts without incurring a quadratic cost. Across simulated and real-world benchmarks, our method improves temporal consistency, recall of past observations, and navigation performance, establishing a novel paradigm for building and operating memory-augmented diffusion world models.

</details>


### [254] [How Faithful Is Trajectory-Based Data Attribution? Error Sources, Remedies, and Practical Guidelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18814)
*Junwei Deng,Pingbang Hu,Suliang Jin,Hao Lu,Jiachen T. Wang,Shichang Zhang,Jiaqi W. Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Systematic error analysis of trajectory-based data attribution. Decomposes total error into config-, algorithm-, and system-level sources. Proposes AdamW-aware attribution (AdamW-influence), a closed-form proxy for first-order Taylor error, and a K-step look-ahead framework. Delivers actionable guidelines for data selection with improved fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of comprehensive error analysis in trajectory-based data attribution, thereby improving faithfulness and reliability in applications such as data selection, data valuation, and model diagnosis.

Method: Decompose attribution error into three levels; (i) identify optimizer mismatch as a dominant config-level error and introduce AdamW-influence; (ii) analyze first-order Taylor approximation to isolate learning-rate and trajectory-length effects and derive a closed-form error proxy; (iii) unify offline and online data selection strategies under a K-step look-ahead framework and provide practical guidelines.

Result: AdamW-influence yields substantial performance gains (10% to >300% in Spearman correlation) across MLP, CNN, GPT-2, and Llama 3.2-1B; a computable error proxy is proposed; a unified, actionable data-selection recipe is offered.

Conclusion: Provides a first systematic error-analysis framework for trajectory-based data attribution, with concrete remedies and guidelines that make these methods more faithful and deployable in practice.

Abstract: Trajectory-based data attribution methods estimate the influence of training samples on model predictions by unrolling the training trajectory. They are widely used in applications such as data selection, data valuation, and model diagnosis, but there is a lack of comprehensive error analysis of these methods, raising concerns about method faithfulness and hindering reliable deployment. In this work, we provide the first systematic analysis of error sources in trajectory-based data attribution, together with concrete remedies to mitigate them and practical guidelines for downstream use. We organize the total error into three categories, config-level, algorithm-level, and system-level. We make three contributions. First, we identify optimizer mismatch as the dominant config-level error: existing methods derive their attribution under the assumption of SGD, even for models trained with the modern de facto optimizer AdamW. We propose AdamW-influence to fully account for AdamW's optimization dynamics, yielding improvements from 10% to over 300% in Spearman correlation between estimated and ground-truth influence across four settings spanning MLP, CNN, GPT-2, and Llama 3.2-1B. Second, we isolate the remaining algorithm-level error arising from the first-order Taylor approximation, identify the learning rate and trajectory length as factors governing the error magnitude, and derive a closed-form error proxy that can be evaluated along the original trajectory without retraining. Third, we translate these insights into practical guidelines for data selection by unifying offline and online strategies under a K-step look-ahead framework. Under this framework, online selection with a short horizon often matches or exceeds offline, and the optimal horizon can be tuned jointly with the learning rate. Together, these results turn the framework into an actionable selection recipe for practitioners.

</details>


### [255] [DynaTrain: Fast Online Parallelism Switching for Elastic LLM Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18815)
*Yuanqing Wang,Yuchen Zhang,Hao Lin,Junhao Hu,Chunyang Zhu,Quanlu Zhang,Boxun Li,Guohao Dai,Zhi Yang,Daning Cheng,Yunquan Zhang,Yu Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DynaTrain enables sub-second online reconfiguration of distributed LLM training across arbitrary parallelism, using a Virtual Parameter Space to map configurations to a deterministic space and fast transfer/scheduling mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic resource fluctuations, RLHF phase shifts, and cluster elasticity render static execution models suboptimal; rapid, correct reconfiguration is required.

Method: Introduce Virtual Parameter Space (VPS) to unify training states; a state routing-and-transition layer for rank-local transfers with a memory-aware, deadlock-free schedule; and an Elastic Device Manager to overlap new-world construction with ongoing training to mask topology changes.

Result: Demonstrated on dense and MoE models up to 235B: reconfiguring a 70B dense model in <2s and a 235B MoE model in 4.36s; outperforms checkpoint-based and elastic systems by up to three orders of magnitude; preserves correctness.

Conclusion: DynaTrain enables fast, correct online reconfiguration for large-scale dynamic training, enabling adaptive parallelism without sacrificing training progress.

Abstract: Modern large language model (LLM) training is inherently dynamic: resource fluctuations, RLHF phase shifts, and cluster elasticity continually reshape the optimal parallelism layout, posing a significant challenge to existing training frameworks built around a static execution model. We present DynaTrain, a distributed training system for sub-second, online reconfiguration across arbitrary multi-dimensional parallelism. At its core, we propose a Virtual Parameter Space (VPS) abstraction that unifies all distributed training states under one logical coordinate space, turning any parallelism configuration into a deterministic mapping and collapsing complex transition into manageable geometric intersections. On top of VPS, a state routing-and-transition layer executes rank-local transfers under a memory-aware, deadlock-free schedule, and an Elastic Device Manager overlaps new-world construction with ongoing training to mask topology-change cost. On dense and MoE models up to 235B parameters, DynaTrain reconfigures a 70B dense model in under 2s and a 235B MoE model in 4.36s, outperforming state-of-the-art checkpoint-based and elastic systems by up to three orders of magnitude while preserving correctness.

</details>


### [256] [Symmetry in the Wild: The Role of Equivariance in Neural Fluid Surrogates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18816)
*Patryk Rygiel,Julian Suk,Kak Khee Yeung,Christoph Brune,Jelmer M. Wolterink*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Equivariant neural surrogates for CFD show domain-dependent benefits; AB-GATr is an E(3)-equivariant surrogate for surface/volume data; equivariance helps hemodynamics but can hurt strongly aligned aerodynamics; explicit equivariance outperforms augmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need scalable, accurate CFD surrogates for large/high-resolution meshes; leverage inductive biases like symmetry, but understand when they help given distributional alignment.

Method: Develop Anchored-Branched Geometric Algebra Transformer (AB-GATr) that enforces E(3) symmetry and models coupled surface/volume quantities; evaluate across automotive aerodynamics and blood flow benchmarks with varying data alignment.

Result: Equivariance degraded performance on strongly aligned aerodynamics datasets; beneficial on diverse hemodynamics benchmarks; AB-GATr outperforms data-augmentation-based symmetry learning across benchmarks.

Conclusion: Equivariance is not universally beneficial; it provides clear advantages in problems lacking strong symmetry, and explicit symmetry preservation is advantageous for generalization in large-scale CFD surrogates.

Abstract: Neural surrogates enable orders-of-magnitude acceleration of computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations, with the potential to transform engineering and healthcare workflows. Neural surrogate use in real-world applications requires addressing scalability to large, high-resolution surface and volume meshes, as well as to bespoke architectures, and accounting for limited training data through the use of inductive biases. Group-equivariant architectures are a principled way to introduce such bias, yet they can be detrimental when the learning problem itself breaks symmetry, for example, due to strong distributional alignment in the dataset. In this work, we investigate under which conditions equivariance improves generalization in neural CFD surrogates across tasks with increasing levels of distributional alignment and realism, covering automotive aerodynamics and blood flow (hemodynamics). To systematically assess the added value of equivariance at the limit of problem scaling, we introduce the Anchored-Branched Geometric Algebra Transformer (AB-GATr), a neural surrogate that integrates scalability and symmetry preservation to efficiently model coupled surface and volume quantities in an $E(3)$-equivariant manner. We find that on strongly aligned aerodynamics datasets, i.e., those that break symmetry, enforcing equivariance can degrade in-distribution performance. In contrast, across hemodynamic benchmarks with diverse geometries and varying alignment, equivariance is consistently beneficial. Moreover, across all benchmarks, the explicit equivariance of AB-GATr reliably outperforms implicit symmetry learning through data augmentation. Our findings showcase that equivariance is not universally beneficial across domains, yet it brings tangible advantages in problems lacking strong data regularities.

</details>


### [257] [Multi-Token Residual Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18817)
*Yufeng Xu,Zishuo Bao,Qian Wang,Zeshen Zhang,Haoqi Zhang,Bowen Peng,Ang Li,Rahul Chalamala,Yucheng Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MRP enables dependency-aware multi-token denoising in diffusion language models by predicting residual logits between adjacent denoising steps from the backbone's hidden states, allowing a single forward pass to denoise multiple tokens. It supports direct decoding (lossy, tunable speed) and speculative decoding (lossless, verification against the backbone). Demonstrates up to 1.42x lossless speedup on SDAR models (1.7B, 4B, 8B) for reasoning and code generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DLMs offer a tradeoff between parallelism and quality; current practice controls the number of tokens decoded per step via a confidence threshold, leading to quality degradation as more tokens are denoised per step. A method that increases tokens per step without significant quality loss can accelerate inference.

Method: Introduce Multi-token Residual Prediction (MRP), a lightweight module that predicts the residual between adjacent denoising steps using the backbone's hidden states. This enables dependency-aware multi-token denoising within a single forward pass, avoiding a second backbone pass. Two inference modes are proposed: (1) direct decoding, using corrected logits without verification for a tunable speed-quality tradeoff, and (2) speculative decoding, which verifies MRP proposals against the backbone to achieve lossless acceleration.

Result: Empirical evaluation on SDAR models at 1.7B, 4B, and 8B scales across reasoning and code generation benchmarks shows up to 1.42× lossless speedup in SGLang.

Conclusion: MRP enables efficient, dependency-aware multi-token denoising within one backbone forward pass and provides both lossy and lossless acceleration modes; it achieves substantial speedups with minimal or no quality loss on diverse SDAR model scales and tasks.

Abstract: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) generate text by iteratively denoising masked token sequences, offering a tradeoff between parallelism and quality compared to autoregressive models. In current practice, the number of tokens decoded per step is controlled by a confidence threshold, and quality degrades monotonically as more tokens are denoised per step. We introduce Multi-token Residual Prediction (MRP), a lightweight module that enables dependency-aware multi-token denoising within a single backbone forward pass. MRP exploits a key property of the denoising process: the logit distributions at adjacent denoising steps are remarkably similar. Rather than running the backbone a second time to obtain the next-step logits, MRP predicts the residual between steps from the backbone's hidden states, effectively denoising more tokens per backbone forward at a fraction of the cost. We deploy MRP in two inference modes: direct decoding, which uses the corrected logits without verification for a tunable quality--speed tradeoff; and speculative decoding, which verifies MRP's proposals against the backbone for lossless acceleration. Experiments on SDAR models at the 1.7B, 4B, and 8B scales across reasoning and code generation benchmarks demonstrate up to $1.42\times$ lossless speedup in SGLang.

</details>


### [258] [Efficient Conditioning Why Pseudo Observation Batch Bayesian Optimization Works When It Does not](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18819)
*Kumbha Nagaswetha,Rabi Pathak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified, conditioning-based theory explains Constant Liar (CL), Kriging Believer (KB), and fantasy models for batch Bayesian optimization, showing Gaussian Processes enable efficient conditioning that yields provably distinct batch points for monotone acquisition functions; these models are unified as a single mechanism differing only in lie-value distribution and linked to Local Penalization (LP) and, qualitatively, to Determinantal Point Processes (DPPs); a practical diagnostic (Structural Diversity Diagnostic, SDD) tests surrogate compatibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no unifying theory explaining why lie-based batch surrogates perform well or fail; identifying the key surrogate property (efficient conditioning) clarifies when and why these methods work and how they relate to LP and DPPs across acquisition strategies.

Method: Prove that Gaussian Processes have efficient conditioning under data augmentation, leading to closed-form updates; show batch-point separation of order l for any acquisition monotone in posterior uncertainty (EI, UCB, PI) and analogous behavior for Thompson Sampling; unify CL, KB, and fantasy as instances of a single conditioning mechanism with different lie-value distributions; connect to LP (explicit penalization) and DPPs (qualitative relation); introduce Structural Diversity Diagnostic (SDD) to test surrogate compatibility; conduct experiments on Hartmann6D, Ackley8D, Levy10D, and SVM hyperparameters; compare CL/KB against LP-greedy, extend joint qEI conditioning to Multiquadric RBF networks, and examine parametric surrogates (random forests, neural networks) for diversity and cost.

Result: The theory’s predictions align with experiments: CL/KB’s implicit penalty matches or outperforms explicit LP hidden conditioning, with convergence comparable to joint qEI efficient conditioning; effectiveness extends to Multiquadric RBFs; parametric surrogates can degenerate batch diversity (RFs), while neural nets regain diversity only at ~15x GP-conditioning cost; results robust across different initial data and observation noise.

Conclusion: A unified, conditioning-based framework explains the success of lie-based batch BO surrogates, and the SDD provides a practical test for surrogate compatibility; GP-based efficient conditioning remains broadly applicable and outperforms many parametric alternatives in maintaining batch diversity, though some surrogates require substantial additional cost to recover diversity.

Abstract: Constant Liar (CL), Kriging Believer (KB), and fantasy models are widely used for batch selection in parallel Bayesian Optimization, yet a unified theory explaining their effectiveness and conditions under which they fail has been lacking. We identify efficient conditioning as the key surrogate property the ability to update predictions in closed form when data is augmented. We prove that Gaussian Processes satisfy this requirement, producing provably distinct batch points with separation of order l, and that this holds for any acquisition function monotonically non decreasing in posterior uncertainty (EI, UCB, PI), with qualitatively similar behavior for Thompson Sampling. We unify CL, KB, and fantasy models as instances of a single conditioning mechanism differing only in the lie value distribution, and draw quantitative connections to Local Penalization (LP) and qualitative connections to Determinantal Point Processes (DPPs). To disentangle model structure from optimizer randomness, we introduce the Structural Diversity Diagnostic (SDD), a reusable methodology for testing surrogate compatibility. Experiments on Hartmann6D, Ackley 8D, Levy10D, and SVM hyperparameter tuning validate all theoretical predictions: CL or KBs implicit penalty matches or outperforms explicit LP greedy conditioning achieves convergence on par with joint qEI efficient conditioning extends to Multiquadric RBF networks; and parametric surrogates produce degenerate batches even when fully retrained (random forests), while neural networks regain diversity only at 15x the wall clock cost of GP conditioning. Robustness is confirmed across multiple initial datasets and under observation noise.

</details>


### [259] [Emergence of Frontier Superposition: Möbius attractor and Cascade Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18820)
*Hongyu Gu,Jingwen Fu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how superposition reasoning can emerge in Transformers for graph reachability, showing that symmetry induces a Möbius attractor, and introduces Cascade Supervision to maintain gradient signals; end-to-end training is insufficient, and experiments corroborate the predicted gradient/prediction performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address whether gradient descent can reach an equal-weight frontier in a symmetry-rich model for reachability, bridging architectural dynamics and supervision.

Method: Architectural reduction to a 1D Möbius map under Sn symmetry; identification of a codimension-one manifold of global optima; propose Cascade Supervision (selectivity bootstrap, gradient persistence across depth, per-step discrimination); theoretical decay analysis of end-to-end gradients; empirical verification on Erdős-Rényi graphs comparing final-step cosine similarity between target states.

Result: Existence of Möbius attractor and global optima manifold; end-to-end gradients decay and fail to reach the manifold; Cascade Supervision enables near-perfect alignment with the theoretical optimum; experimental cosines: ≈0.37 vs ≈0.69, matching within 0.02 at each step.

Conclusion: Combining Möbius attractor dynamics with Cascade Supervision yields emergence of superposition-based reasoning; end-to-end supervision is provably insufficient due to gradient vanishing; cascade supervision is necessary for depth-safe learning.

Abstract: Superposition allows Transformers to reason in depth, carrying an entire reasoning frontier in parallel through a bounded-depth forward pass instead of unrolling serial chain-of-thought tokens. While Zhu et al. (2025) hand-crafted an equal-weight breadth-first frontier in a single residual stream for graph reachability, it remained open whether gradient descent could ever find this target amidst permutation-symmetric saddles.
  We close this gap on Reachability-by-Superposition over Erdős-Rényi graphs by isolating architectural and supervisional contributions. Architecturally, we identify a Möbius attractor: under $S_n$-symmetry in the tree regime, layerwise dynamics reduce to a 1D Möbius map whose zero set is a codimension-one manifold of global optima containing the equal-weight superposition state.
  On the supervision side, we identify Cascade Supervision: a loss class whose backward pass simultaneously delivers (A) selectivity bootstrap, (B) gradient persistence across depth, and (C) per-step discrimination (e.g., \mathcal{L}_{sup} and \mathcal{L}_{node}). End-to-end supervision fails condition (B) and is provably insufficient: internal gradients at layer c decay as (np)^{-(D-c-2)/2} in the graph fan-out and stall before the manifold is reached.
  Our thesis: Möbius attractor + Cascade Supervision = emergence of superposition reasoning. The parameter-free decay law predicts a final-step cosine of 0.35 vs. 0.71 (end-to-end vs. cascade) at depth D=3; experiments confirm 0.37 vs. 0.69, matching within 0.02 at every step.

</details>


### [260] [Quantum Adversarial Machine Learning: From Classical Adaptations to Quantum-Native Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18821)
*Roozbeh Razavi-Far,Mohammad Meymani,Erfan Mahmoudinia,Dorsa Vazirzade,Peyman Paknezhad,Fateme Ghasemi,Saeed Saravani,Somayeh Nikkhoo,Kimia Haghjooei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive survey of quantum adversarial machine learning, covering vulnerabilities, attack types, defenses, theory, trends, and challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand vulnerabilities of quantum ML systems and develop robust defenses against adversarial threats in quantum-enabled machine learning.

Method: Systematic literature survey: taxonomy of attacks and defenses, theoretical foundations, synthesis of emerging trends, and identification of critical challenges.

Result: A synthesized overview that categorizes attack surfaces, defense strategies, and gaps in the current literature, providing a coherent map of the quantum adversarial ML landscape.

Conclusion: Quantum adversarial ML is a vital, rapidly evolving field; progress hinges on rigorous taxonomy, benchmarks, and robust defense mechanisms to ensure trustworthy quantum-enhanced ML.

Abstract: Machine learning has revolutionized numerous industrial domains. Despite recent advances, machine learning models remain vulnerable to adversarial threats. Adversarial machine learning is a field that studies these vulnerabilities to build robust machine learning models. Quantum machine learning is an interdisciplinary field that bridges quantum computing and classical machine learning. While quantum machine learning shows potentials to outperform classical machine learning in complex tasks such as regression, classification, and generative modeling, it remains vulnerable to adversarial attacks. Given the recent advancements in quantum computing and machine learning, the quantum adversarial machine learning field has emerged to study the vulnerabilities of quantum machine learning, possible attacks, and novel quantum-enhanced defense strategies. In this survey, we provide a detailed overview on quantum adversarial machine learning and explore the existing attacks and countermeasures. We also review the theoretical underpinnings of this area, emerging trends, and critical challenges.

</details>


### [261] [Hybrid-LoRA: Bridging Full Fine-Tuning and Low-Rank Adaptation for Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18822)
*Chengqian Zhang,Wei Zhu,Kyumin Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid-LoRA: a hybrid post-training framework that uses full fine-tuning on a small subset of modules and LoRA on the rest under a fixed budget, guided by a Hybrid-LoRA Score for module sensitivity to low-rank adaptation. Claims nearly match FFT with 10% full-FT module budget, outperforming PEFT baselines by up to 5.65% (avg 4.36%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the computational and memory costs of RLVR post-training for large language models while preserving or approaching full fine-tuning performance on complex reasoning tasks.

Method: Propose Hybrid-LoRA: apply full fine-tuning selectively to a subset of modules less amenable to LoRA, while applying LoRA to remaining modules. Introduce Hybrid-LoRA Score to rank modules by sensitivity to low-rank adaptation under a fixed parameter budget.

Result: Empirical results show that Hybrid-LoRA closely matches full fine-tuning performance with only 10% of the modules fully tuned, while other modules use LoRA. Outperforms four state-of-the-art PEFT post-training baselines by up to 5.65% and on average 4.36% over the best baseline.

Conclusion: Hybrid-LoRA offers a practical balance between performance and efficiency in RLVR post-training by strategically combining FFT and LoRA, guided by a module-sensitivity ranking.

Abstract: Post-training has become essential for adapting large language models (LLMs) to complex downstream behaviors, including instruction following, preference alignment, and multi-step reasoning. Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has recently emerged as a particularly effective post-training paradigm for improving reasoning capabilities, with critic-free algorithms such as GRPO and GSPO enabling scalable optimization. However, RLVR post-training with full fine-tuning (FFT) requires substantial GPU memory and incurs high training costs. Although parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, such as Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), effectively reduce computational costs, they often suffer from a noticeable performance gap compared to full fine-tuning in post-training for complex reasoning tasks. In this paper, we propose Hybrid-LoRA, an efficient hybrid post-training framework that selectively applies full fine-tuning to a small subset of modules less suited to low-rank adaptation, while adapting the remaining components with LoRA. We introduce a novel Hybrid-LoRA Score to rank candidate modules according to their sensitivity to low-rank adaptation under a fixed parameter budget. Experiments show that Hybrid-LoRA closely matches full fine-tuning performance under a 10% full fine-tuning module budget, with the remaining candidate modules adapted by LoRA, consistently outperforming four state-of-the-art PEFT post-training baselines, achieving improvements of up to 5.65% and on average 4.36% over the best baseline.

</details>


### [262] [Not All Tokens Are Worth Caching: Learning Semantic-Aware Eviction for LLM Prefix Caches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18825)
*Shaoke Fang,Ziang Li,Wenfei Wu,Jiatong Ji,Qingsong Liu,Ruizhi Pu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAECache is a semantic-aware, online-learning eviction policy for LLM prefix caches that uses per-token-type weighting and a multi-queue design to improve TTFT and adapt to workload variability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM prompts exhibit up to 756x variation in token reuse rates across token types; uniform eviction policies (e.g., LRU) waste memory and increase recomputation.

Method: Introduce a multi-queue architecture with task-specific queues and tailored priority metrics; implement semantic-aware token weighting learned online via eviction feedback; deploy a fully adaptive online learning framework for all parameters (timing, decay, queue weights, meta-parameters).

Result: Empirical evaluation across heterogeneous workloads shows 1.4x–2.7x TTFT gains over production baselines; fixed-parameter variants can drop up to 2.7x under workload mismatch.

Conclusion: Adaptive, semantics-aware eviction yields robust improvements and avoids performance degradation due to workload shifts; the approach generalizes beyond static policies.

Abstract: Prefix caching is a key optimization in Large Language Model (LLM) serving, reusing attention Key-Value (KV) states across requests with shared prompt prefixes to reduce expensive prefill computation. However, its benefit depends critically on the eviction policy as GPU memory is scarce, and existing policies such as LRU largely treat cached blocks uniformly. This view ignores a fundamental property of LLM prompts: not all tokens are equally worth caching. We show that different token types within a prompt, including system prompts, user queries, tool outputs, model responses, and chain-of-thought reasoning, exhibit up to 756x variation in reuse rates, yet no existing eviction policy exploits this signal. In this paper, we present SAECache (Semantic-Adaptive Eviction for prefix caches), a semantic-adaptive prefix cache eviction policy that addresses this gap through three innovations: (1) a multi-queue architecture that routes KV blocks to task-specific queues with tailored priority metrics, capturing both session reuse in multi-turn requests and structural reuse in templated single-turn requests; (2) a semantic-aware token weighting mechanism that learns the reuse value of different token types online through eviction feedback; and (3) a fully adaptive online learning schema for all parameter updates, including log-normal timing parameters, position decay power, queue weights, and meta-parameters, which eliminates manual tuning and enables automatic adaptation to deployment-specific workload characteristics. Through extensive evaluation across heterogeneous workloads, we demonstrate that SAECache achieves 1.4x-2.7x TTFT improvement over production-style baselines, while fixed-parameter alternatives can degrade by up to 2.7x under workload mismatch -- a failure mode our adaptive approach avoids entirely.

</details>


### [263] [The Routing and Filtering Structure of Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18826)
*Shafayeth Jamil,Rehan Kapadia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A decomposition of QK^T into routing (skew-symmetric, low rank) and filtering (symmetric) reveals a spectral cascade; S-D attention enables stable, layer-norm-free training, linearizable regions, and cascade-based architectures that cut attention parameters substantially with small perplexity shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To disentangle routing and filtering in self-attention, quantify spectral properties across many heads and models, and derive stable, efficient parametrizations that improve interpretability and scalability.

Method: Analyzed 1776 heads across five pretrained transformers; introduced S-D attention as a diagnostic, stability-guaranteed parameterization (Re(λ) ≤ 0); trained without layer normalization; measured effective rank and spectral cascade; performed linearization experiments (first seven layers of 125M S-D attention) and compared with standard attention; explored ELU+1 linear attention; evaluated cascade-allocated architectures vs baseline.

Result: Routing operates at low rank; effective rank ~2 at the first layer; a six-scale spectral cascade varying from 7M to 355M parameters; linearizing the first seven layers costs <5% perplexity; standard attention collapses under the same intervention; deeper models widen the linearizable region; replacing the first four layers with ELU+1 linear attention reaches within 1.4% of baseline at full head dimension; cascade-allocated architectures reduce attention parameters by 47–65% with a perplexity increase of 3.9–8.4%.

Conclusion: The decomposition makes the spectral budget legible and actionable; routing-filtering separation enables stable training without normalization and guides efficient, cascade-based architectural designs.

Abstract: The attention interaction matrix $QK^{\top}$ contains two entangled computations: a skew-symmetric component that redistributes information between positions (routing) and a symmetric component that scales mutual relevance (filtering). We decompose 1776 heads across five pretrained transformers and find routing operating at low rank, well below the routing capacity allocated by the weight kernel. We introduce $S$-$D$ attention as a diagnostic parameterization that disentangles routing from filtering by construction with guaranteed stability ($\mathrm{Re}(λ) \le 0$) and trains stably without layer normalization. When disentangled and unnormalized, routing self-organizes into a spectral cascade, effective rank $2$ at the first layer, expanding with depth across six scales from 7M to 355M parameters. The cascade predicts where attention can be simplified: linearizing the first seven layers of 125M $S$-$D$ attention costs ${<}5\%$ perplexity, whereas standard attention collapses under the same intervention. The linearizable region widens with depth. Replacing the first four layers with ELU+1 linear attention reaches within $1.4\%$ of baseline at full head dimension. Cascade-allocated architectures trade attention parameters for perplexity ($47\%-65\%$ fewer attention parameters at $+3.9\%$ to $+8.4\%$ PPL). The routing-filtering decomposition makes the spectral budget legible; the cascade makes it actionable.

</details>


### [264] [Lossless Anti-Distillation Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18829)
*Zibo Diao,Jingchu Gai,Xinyue Ai,Zhang Zhang,Zhenyu He,Di He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lossless anti-distillation sampling (LADS) scheme that seeds each generation from a private, query-content–dependent seed and visit-count; benign users experience identical distributions, while multi-account distillers share randomness within semantic buckets, reducing sample diversity and slowing generalization; proven to degrade distillers' convergence rate; experiments show strong protection with no loss to individual-user fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Frontier models face distillation threats via cheaper student models trained on harvested outputs; existing defenses trade off quality or are circumventable; need a defense that preserves benign-user quality while harming distillers.

Method: Introduce LADS: generation randomness derived from a private seed determined by query semantic content and user-query count; benign users get independent samples per visit; distillers with multiple accounts sharing latent randomness in the same semantic bucket induces correlation and reduces generalization; use uniform convergence theory to bound degradation in the distiller's generalization gap for unconditional and conditional generation.

Result: LADS substantially degrades distilled students' performance while maintaining exact statistical fidelity for individual users; experiments on image generation, mathematical reasoning, and code generation corroborate the defense.

Conclusion: LADS offers a provable, lossless-for-benign-users defense against multi-account distillation by shaping sampling randomness; preserves user experience while weakening distillers' generalization.

Abstract: Frontier commercial generative models face a growing threat from distillation, whereby a distiller harvests generated responses and trains a competing model of its own at drastically lower cost. Existing defenses either rely on modifying the models outputs, thereby sacrificing response quality for benign users, or on behavioral detection methods, which can be readily circumvented by distributing queries across multiple accounts. In this work, we propose Lossless Anti-Distillation Sampling (LADS), a novel sampling scheme specifically designed to counter multi-account distillation while maintaining a lossless experience for benign users. Concretely, LADS derives the randomness underlying each generation from a private seed determined by the semantic content of the query and the number of times the user has queried the model. By construction, every benign user receives a response independently sampled from the original model at each visit, and thus experiences no distortion. In contrast, for a distiller, different accounts share latent randomness whenever their queries fall in the same semantic bucket. As a result, the harvested data becomes correlated, potentially reducing sample diversity and degrading generalization. Using uniform convergence theory, we show that LADS provably degrades the convergence rate of the distillers generalization gap relative to standard i.i.d. sampling in both unconditional and conditional generation settings. Experiments on image generation, mathematical reasoning, and code generation confirm that LADS substantially degrades the performance of distilled students while preserving exact statistical fidelity for individual users.

</details>


### [265] [In-Context Learning Operates as Concept Subspace Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18830)
*Wei Tang,Xinyan Jiang,Fakhri Karray,Lijie Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Structured demonstrations enable a low-dimensional, task-aligned concept subspace that mediates recoverable in-context learning (ICL); the predictive power sits in a compact activation subspace rather than full circuit recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test whether structured demonstrations induce low-dimensional concept inference and identify whether a compact, task-aligned subspace governs recoverable ICL behavior across models and tasks.

Method: Develop a concept-subspace framework for ridge and least-squares ICL proxies; prove exact prediction decomposition into concept-coordinate regression and off-subspace leakage under block-diagonal/near-block-diagonal covariance; empirically test with CounterFact-derived multi-relations prompts on Llama-3-8B; perform subspace patching by removing/patching subspaces and swapping concepts; replicate on Qwen2.5-7B and cross-lingual rule tasks; compare with random and cross-task matched-rank controls.

Result: Identified a 68–73-dimensional subspace within the 4096-d residual stream that accounts for ~78.8% of the clean–corrupted accuracy gap; patching the complementary subspace yields 0% improvement; concept swaps redirect predictions toward injected relations; controls largely ineffective; consistent patterns across additional models and tasks like Qwen2.5-7B and cross-lingual tasks.

Conclusion: Concept subspaces are compact, task-aligned mediators of recoverable ICL behavior in structured task families, supporting low-dimensional inference without implying full circuit reconstruction.

Abstract: Regression and Bayesian accounts of in-context learning (ICL) explain how demonstrations can induce predictors, while mechanistic analyses often identify compact activation directions that steer prompted behavior. However, it remains unclear whether structured demonstrations induce low-dimensional concept inference. We study this question through a concept-subspace view of ICL, in which tasks vary only along intrinsic concept coordinates, although inputs are observed in a high-dimensional ambient space. For ridge and least-squares ICL proxies, prediction decomposes exactly into concept-coordinate regression and off-subspace leakage. Under block-diagonal or near-block-diagonal covariance assumptions, the leading estimation and nuisance-sensitivity terms scale with the dimension of the concept subspace, while residual effects are controlled by cross-subspace coupling. This separation gives a mechanistic prediction: recoverable task information should concentrate in a low-dimensional, task-aligned activation subspace. On CounterFact-derived multi-relation prompts with Llama-3-8B, a 68--73-dimensional subspace of the 4096-dimensional residual stream restores 78.8% of the clean--corrupted accuracy gap, whereas patching the complementary subspace restores 0%. Concept swaps redirect predictions toward injected relations, while random and cross-task matched-rank controls are largely ineffective. Additional experiments on Qwen2.5-7B and a controlled cross-lingual rule task show the same qualitative pattern. These results support concept subspaces as compact, task-aligned mediators of recoverable ICL behavior in structured task families, without implying full-circuit recovery.

</details>


### [266] [Precision Tracked Transformer via Kalman Filtering, Kriging and Process Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18832)
*Bo Long,Deepak Agarwal,Jelena Markovic-Voronov,Yi Wang,Liuqing Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bayesian Filtering Transformer (BFT) adds principled uncertainty handling to Transformers by interpreting attention as precision-weighted kriging, residuals as Kalman updates with adaptive gain, and FFN as a dynamics model for precision propagation; uses REML for observation precision; a drop-in replacement with negligible overhead that improves sequential recommendation and LLM robustness under noisy data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers treat all tokens with uniform confidence, ignoring uncertainty from cold-start items, heterogenous signal quality, and noisy supervision/retrieval scenarios. Introducing a probabilistic, uncertainty-aware layer can improve accuracy and robustness in real-world tasks.

Method: Replace standard Transformer components with probabilistic equivalents: attention becomes precision-weighted kriging; residual connection as Kalman update with adaptive gain; FFN as a dynamics model propagating precision via a Jacobian and process-noise rule. Observation precision is learned via a parameter-free REML estimator with a conjugate Bayesian prior. The approach is a drop-in replacement, adding negligible overhead.

Result: Empirical gains across domains: in sequential recommendation, BFT improves six benchmarks across three architectures, with largest gains for cold-start users and rare items. In LLM fine-tuning with noisy data, BFT improves robustness under token-label corruption and retrieval-based noisy context (RAG distractors). A single modification—restoring precision—yields substantial gains in both classical sequence models and modern LLM regimes.

Conclusion: BFT provides a principled, low-overhead mechanism to inject uncertainty into Transformer-based models, yielding improved performance and robustness across tasks. Restoring and calibrating observation precision appears to be the key factor driving gains.

Abstract: The Transformer is the foundational building block of modern AI, yet offers no principled handling of \emph{uncertainty}, which is prevalent in real applications: cold-start tokens with sparse histories in sequential recommendation, heterogeneous signal quality in language models, and attention sinks induced by unconstrained softmax. Every token is treated with uniform confidence. We show this uniformity is a degenerate case of our \emph{Bayesian Filtering Transformer} (BFT): attention becomes precision-weighted kriging, the residual connection becomes a Kalman update with adaptive gain, and the FFN becomes a dynamics model propagating precision via a Jacobian--plus--process-noise rule. Observation precision comes from a parameter-free Restricted Maximum Likelihood (REML) estimator with a conjugate Bayesian prior. BFT replaces any Transformer layer with negligible overhead. On sequential recommendation, BFT applied to three major architectures yields significant gains on six benchmarks, with the largest improvements on cold-start users and rare items where uncertainty is highest. On supervised fine-tuning of large language models with noisy data, BFT improves robustness in two regimes: noisy supervision (token-label corruption in question answering) and noisy context (retrieval-augmented QA with real RAG distractors). A single principled modification -- restoring precision -- unlocks substantial headroom across both classical sequence-modeling and modern LLM regimes.

</details>


### [267] [Automated Big Data Quality Assessment using Knowledge Graph Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18833)
*Hadi Fadlallah,Rima Kilany,Mitri Haber,Ali Jaber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A knowledge-graph-embedding framework for context-aware automated data quality assessment using a KG of contextual characteristics and quality operations; integrates diverse representations and edge weights; evaluated on real-world radiation sensor data with AmpliGraph.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address context-insensitive data quality assessment by leveraging knowledge graphs and embeddings to model context and quality rules/dimensions, enabling tailored DQA plans.

Method: Construct a knowledge graph linking contextual data characteristics with data quality rules and dimensions; use KG embeddings to predict missing edges between the input dataset's context and relevant quality rules/dimensions; inject numerical edge attributes as weights; integrate diverse representations from literature; evaluate with AmpliGraph on LAEC-CNRS radiation sensor dataset to generate a comprehensive DQA plan.

Result: Demonstrates capability to generate a comprehensive, context-specific data quality assessment plan for the input dataset; shows improved contextual understanding over strict matching; weights enable weighted quality measurements.

Conclusion: KG embeddings with weighted edges effectively enable context-aware data quality assessment and planning; validated on real-world data, suggesting broader applicability to context-driven quality management.

Abstract: Automated data quality assessment is crucial for managing big data, but existing solutions face challenges in achieving accurate context-aware assessment. This paper presents a novel knowledge-based approach to enhance automated data quality assessment. Our approach utilizes knowledge graph embeddings to predict missing edges between the input dataset's context representation and the relevant quality rules and dimensions within a knowledge graph representing contextual data characteristics and the required quality assessment operations. We surpass conventional practices by integrating diverse representations within the knowledge graph, drawing insights from contextual information from a thorough literature investigation. This integration allows us to develop a comprehensive and context-specific data quality assessment plan tailored to each context. Leveraging the knowledge graph improves our understanding of the input dataset's context, overcoming the limitations of traditional methods that rely solely on strict matching and overlook contextual characteristics. By injecting numerical edge attributes, we assign corresponding weights to each predicted quality measurement, providing a comprehensive data quality assessment plan for the input dataset.
  To evaluate our approach, we leverage AmpliGraph, a framework developed and benchmarked by AccentureLabs. The evaluation involves employing a real-world radiation sensors dataset provided by the Lebanese Atomic Energy Commission (LAEC-CNRS). The results obtained from this evaluation demonstrate the capability of our solution to generate a comprehensive data quality assessment plan for the given input dataset.

</details>


### [268] [StampFormer: A Physics-Guided Material-Geometry-Coupled Multimodal Model for Rapid Prediction of Physical Fields in Sheet Metal Stamping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18835)
*Jiajie Luo,Mohamed Mohamed,Osama Hassan,Haosu Zhou,Yingxue Zhao,Haoran Li,Xinrun Li,Zhutao Shao,Yang Long,Nan Li,Jichun Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StampFormer integrates geometry and material properties via MAGN and HMEIU into a Swin-UNet backbone to predict FEA fields for sheet metal stamping with sub-second speed and low error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient design validation for sheet metal forming is hindered by FEA being time-consuming. Scalar-based surrogates miss full field information, while image-based models often ignore material properties. A multimodal approach leveraging geometry and stress–strain data aims to yield fast, accurate FEA predictions.

Method: Three components: Material-Augmented Geometric Network (MAGN) fuses geometric and material data; Hierarchical Material Embedding Injection Unit (HMEIU) injects material embeddings at multiple levels; an adapted Swin-UNet backbone processes the fused data to predict FEA fields (thinning, major/minor strain, plastic strain, displacement). Evaluated on crossmember stamping with steel and aluminum using two simulation datasets.

Result: The model achieves high-fidelity predictions for 2D fields (thinning, major/minor strain, plastic strain) and 3D displacement, with average relative error < 8.5% across the four 2D fields and mean squared error < 1.2 mm^2 for displacement, and runs in under one second per prediction when compared to ground-truth FEA.

Conclusion: StampFormer offers a practical, efficient multimodal framework enabling real-time manufacturability assessments by jointly leveraging geometry and material properties to predict FEA outcomes.

Abstract: Traditional sheet metal forming relies on time-consuming and expensive Finite Element Analysis (FEA) for design validation, a process that significantly prolongs design cycles. While surrogate models offer faster iteration, current approaches have limitations: scalar-based methods cannot capture comprehensive field-based FEA results, while existing image-based models often ignore the critical role of material properties by focusing solely on geometry. To address this gap, we develop a physics-guided deep learning framework, namely StampFormer, which simultaneously uses component geometry and material stress-strain responses to predict FEA outcomes. The StampFormer framework uses three core components to process data. A Material-Augmented Geometric Network (MAGN) first fuses geometric and material data. This information is then integrated at various levels by a Hierarchical Material Embedding Injection Unit (HMEIU) before being processed by the primary network backbone, an adapted Swin-UNet. We evaluated our model on the stamping of a crossmember panel with two simulation datasets for steel and aluminium panels, and results demonstrate that StampFormer provides high-fidelity predictions of critical physical fields - including thinning, major strain, minor strain, plastic strain, and displacement - in under a second. Compared with ground truth FEA, our model achieved an average relative error of less than 8.5% on the four 2D fields and a mean squared error of less than 1.2 mm2 for the 3D displacement field. In summary, we introduce a practical and efficient framework that integrates multimodal information, namely geometry and material properties, to provide fast and accurate predictions, enabling designers to perform real-time manufacturability assessments.

</details>


### [269] [Spectral Gradient Surgery for Domain-Generalizable Dataset Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18836)
*Minyoung Oh,Najeong Chae,Jae-Young Sim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DGDD with Spectral Gradient Surgery improves OOD generalization of distilled datasets by disentangling class-discriminative and domain-specific gradient signals in the spectral domain, and augmenting DM updates with shared and diversity-promoting components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dataset distillation (DD) assumes train and test distributions match, a rarely true condition. Post-hoc domain generalization (DG) on distilled data is inefficient because synthetic sets lack natural diversity. There is a need for a principled DG approach tailored to DD that preserves efficiency.

Method: Introduce Domain Generalizable Dataset Distillation (DGDD) and study it on Distribution Matching (DM). Propose Spectral Gradient Surgery (SGS) that analyzes cross-domain domain-wise gradients in the spectral domain to identify shared (class-discriminative) versus domain-specific components. SGS augments the DM update with two gradients: reinforce cross-domain shared components and promote diversity within the distilled dataset, enabling plug-and-play integration with existing DM methods.

Result: SGS substantially improves OOD generalization across diverse-scale benchmarks while remaining compatible with and easy to integrate into existing DM methods.

Conclusion: DGDD with SGS provides a practical, plug-and-play mechanism to disentangle gradient signals for robust OOD generalization in dataset distillation, offering a principled pathway to extend DD beyond IID assumptions.

Abstract: Dataset Distillation (DD) synthesizes a compact synthetic dataset that preserves the training utility of a full dataset. However, its standard formulation assumes that test data follow the same distribution as training data, an assumption that rarely holds in practice. A straightforward extension-applying post-hoc Domain Generalization (DG) techniques to distilled data-is ill-suited because existing DG methods rely on the natural diversity of real datasets, which compact synthetic sets inherently lack, while also incurring substantial augmentation overhead that conflicts with the efficiency objective of dataset distillation. To address this limitation, we introduce Domain Generalizable Dataset Distillation (DGDD), a new problem setting that explicitly targets out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization of distilled datasets. We study this problem through a widely adopted DD baseline of Distribution Matching (DM). We attribute the OOD vulnerability of DM to the entanglement of class-discriminative and domain-specific information within the compressed synthetic set, and propose Spectral Gradient Surgery (SGS) to disentangle the two. The key insight of SGS is that cross-domain agreement among domain-wise gradients in the spectral domain reveals which gradient components are shared across source domains-and are therefore class-discriminative-and which are domain-specific. Based on this observation, SGS augments the standard DM update with two complementary gradients: one that reinforces cross-domain shared components and another that explicitly promotes diversity within the distilled dataset. Extensive experiments on diverse-scale benchmarks demonstrate that SGS substantially improves OOD generalization while remaining plug-and-play compatible with existing DM methods.

</details>


### [270] [VCR: Learning Valid Contextual Representation for Incomplete Wearable Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18837)
*Yuxuan Weng,Wenhan Luo,Qijia Shao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VCR is a self-supervised framework for robust multimodal health monitoring under missing modalities. It uses an orthogonal tokenizer to disentangle shared semantics from modality-specific residuals and a missing-aware mixture-of-experts backbone, reconstructing only shared components to avoid hallucinations, thereby improving performance and robustness when modalities are present or missing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In real-world health monitoring, multimodal data often suffer from missing modalities and limited labels. Existing methods that reconstruct entire missing signals can hallucinate modality-specific details and harm robustness. There is a need for representations that preserve information integrity while being robust to missing modalities.

Method: VCR introduces an orthogonal tokenizer to enforce strict disentanglement of latent representations by rectifying latent manifolds and applying a geometric projection, separating each modality into shared semantics and modality-specific residuals. The resulting tokens feed a missing-aware mixture-of-experts backbone that adapts to varying modality availability. The learning objective reconstructs only the shared components of missing modalities to avoid non-inferable details.

Result: Across multiple health monitoring tasks, VCR consistently improves performance and robustness under full, single-missing, and multiple-missing modality settings compared with strong supervised and self-supervised baselines.

Conclusion: Disentangling shared and modality-specific information with an orthogonal tokenizer and aligning reconstruction to shared components yields robust representations for multimodal health monitoring under modality missingness, reducing hallucinations and improving generalization across missingness scenarios.

Abstract: Wearable devices enable continuous health monitoring from multimodal signals, but real-world deployment is hindered by limited labeled data and pervasive sensor incompleteness. While large-scale self-supervised pretraining reduces label dependence, most existing methods assume full modality availability. Current approaches for handling modality missingness often reconstruct entire absent signals, which can encourage hallucinating modality-specific details that are not inferable from the observed sensor signals and degrade robustness. We propose VCR, a self-supervised framework that learns to extract valid representations robust to modality missingness. VCR employs an orthogonal tokenizer to enforce strict orthogonal disentanglement by rectifying latent manifolds and applying a geometric projection, separating each modality into shared semantics and modality-specific residuals. This design preserves complete information integrity while serving as a structural foundation for robust learning under modality missingness. The resulting tokens are processed by a missing-aware mixture-of-experts backbone that adapts to varying patterns of modality availability. By constraining the objective to reconstruct only the shared components of missing modalities, VCR effectively mitigates hallucinations of non-inferable modality-specific details. Across multiple health monitoring tasks, VCR consistently improves performance and robustness under full, single-missing, and multiple-missing modality settings compared with strong supervised and self-supervised baselines.

</details>


### [271] [Lying Is Just a Phase: The Hidden Alignment Transition in Language Model Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18838)
*Adil Amin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A phase-transition in model coupling between reasoning and truthfulness occurs at a family-dependent critical size (~3.5B parameters). Below Nc, reasoning and truthfulness anticorrelate; above Nc, they cooperate. Architecture, data curation, and training details shift Nc; width normalization removes the anticorrelation; most models have no competing attention heads; an ODE can predict held-out models; a public tool and dashboard diagnose coupling phase and suggest interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To go beyond loss-based scaling laws and understand how core capabilities interact as models scale, and to identify levers that steer this interaction toward cooperative behavior.

Method: Empirical survey of 63 base models across 16 families; define a coupling measure between reasoning and truthfulness; analyze effects of architecture, curated data, training recipe; apply width normalization; inspect attention head competition; fit sparse-regression ODE to predict coupling; validate on held-out Llama-2; provide code, data, and an interactive dashboard.

Result: Discovered a regime change with Nc ≈ 3.5B (with bootstrap 95% CI). Below Nc, anticorrelation; above Nc, cooperation. Architecture, data curation, and training affect Nc independently. Specific interventions: curated training eliminates the dip (Qwen), Gemma-4 achieves high coupling via distillation/architecture, Phi matches web-trained coupling via data curation. Width normalization removes anticorrelation. Most models show zero competing heads. ODE cross-predicts held-out with 5.6% error. Diagnostic uses public benchmark scores. The cooperative regime persists to frontier (r=+0.72 across 34 models). Public release.

Conclusion: Coupling behavior is governed by an output-projection bottleneck; the coupling diagnostic is practical using public benchmarks; interventions like data curation, width adjustment, and benchmark rotation can steer models toward the cooperative regime; an open-source tool supports diagnosing and predicting coupling across models.

Abstract: Scaling laws predict loss from compute but not how capabilities interact. We measure the coupling between reasoning and truthfulness across 63 base models from 16 families and find a regime change invisible to loss curves: below a family-dependent critical scale $N_c$, capabilities anticorrelate; above it, they cooperate. $N_c \approx 3.5$B parameters [2.9B, 13.4B] (bootstrap 95% CI), but model size is not the only variable that determines phase. Architecture, data curation, and training recipe each shift $N_c$ independently: curated training eliminated the coupling dip between Qwen generations ($0.025 \to 0.830$ at matched scale), Gemma-4 at 4B achieves coupling 0.871, characteristic of 13B+ standard-trained models, through distillation and architectural innovation, and Phi at 1B matches web-trained coupling at 10B through data curation alone. Width normalization eliminates the anticorrelation across all tested families, supporting an output-projection bottleneck. Internally, 38 of 40 models show zero competing attention heads. A sparse-regression ODE cross-predicts held-out Llama-2 at 5.6% error. The diagnostic requires no model internals -- only public benchmark scores across a model family. The cooperative regime extends to the frontier ($r = +0.72$, 34 models, 10 labs). Code, data, and an open-source activation-steering tool for any open-weight model are released alongside an interactive dashboard that diagnoses any model's coupling phase, suggests concrete interventions (data curation, width, benchmark rotation), and provides ODE scaling predictions, frontier diagnostics, and eigenstructure analysis: https://zehenlabs.com/cape/.

</details>


### [272] [An Integrated Forecasting Prototype for Emergency Department Boarding Time to Support Proactive Operational Decision Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18839)
*Orhun Vural,Abdulaziz Ahmed,Ferhat Zengul,James Booth,Bunyamin Ozaydin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multi-horizon ED boarding time forecasting using DLinear and NLinear with external data sources; outperforms baselines across 6–24h horizons; robust under extreme congestion; an MLOps prototype supports deployment and retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ED overcrowding and boarding prolong care delays; predicting boarding time ahead enables proactive resource allocation and bed management.

Method: Real-world data from a university urban hospital combined with external contextual data (weather, holidays, major local events). A multi-horizon forecasting framework predicts boarding time at 6, 8, 10, 12, and 24 hours. Models compared: decomposition-based Linear (DLinear) and normalization-based Linear (NLinear). Evaluation includes normal and extreme congestion scenarios. An MLOps web app prototype provides data ingestion, forecast visualization, experimentation, and retraining capability.

Result: DLinear and NLinear demonstrated superior predictive performance across horizons and remained robust under high-congestion scenarios. The approach achieved better accuracy than baselines (exact metrics not provided). The MLOps prototype indicates practical translation of forecasts into operational practice.

Conclusion: A multi-horizon time-series forecasting framework using DLinear/NLinear with external data effectively predicts ED boarding time, enabling proactive decision making to alleviate congestion. The MLOps component supports deployment and ongoing model maintenance, suggesting readiness for real-world adoption.

Abstract: Overcrowding in emergency departments (ED) remains a persistent operational challenge worldwide, causing delays in care delivery and downstream congestion. ED boarding time, defined as the duration admitted patients remain in the ED while awaiting inpatient bed placement, is a key indicator of this congestion. Predicting ED boarding time in advance enables proactive operational decision making before congestion escalates. We developed and evaluated a multi-horizon time series forecasting framework to predict ED boarding time at 6, 8, 10, 12, and 24-hour horizons. Real-world data from a university-affiliated urban hospital in the United States were utilized and integrated with external contextual data sources, including weather, holidays, and major local events. Decomposition-based Linear (DLinear) and Normalization-based Linear (NLinear) time series forecasting deep learning models showed superior performance across multiple horizons. Models were also evaluated under extreme congestion scenarios characterized by elevated boarding times. In addition, a Machine Learning Operations (MLOps) web application prototype was developed to support translation of the forecasting framework into practice through integrated data ingestion, forecast visualization, experimentation, and retraining.

</details>


### [273] [The Growing Pains of Frontier Models: When Leaderboards Stop Separating and What to Measure Next](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18840)
*Adil Amin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Frontier model leaderboards hide cross-release interactions; a per-release h-field residual analysis combined with a population-coupling trend diagnoses capability emphasis, predicts which measurements/tests are most informative next, enabling axis rotation. Across 34 models from 10 labs (2024–2026), capabilities cooperate (r=+0.72, p<1e-6) but vary by lab/time; a three-level playbook, per-lab measurement-priority, and seven falsifiable predictions with an interactive dashboard are provided for the next 12 months.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Move beyond single-axis frontier rankings to understand how capabilities reinforce or trade off across releases, enabling proactive benchmark design and model development by diagnosing emphasis shifts and informing axis rotation.

Method: Decompose paired SWE-bench and GPQA Diamond scores into a population-coupling trend and per-release residual (h-field). Analyze 34 models from 10 labs (2024–2026), including six open-weight architectures, identify second capability transitions, and assess cross-lab coupling dynamics. Derive a three-level playbook (locate, diagnose, rotate), provide per-lab measurement-priority tables, and forecast via ODE-based scaling; validate with April 2026 releases and offer an interactive dashboard for phase classification, h-field diagnostics, and seven timestamped predictions.

Result: Demonstrates positive but lab-dependent capability cooperation (r=+0.72, p<1e-6; rising to +0.75 in out-of-sample tests). DeepSeek shifts from reasoning-rich to coding-first; Google maintains reasoning emphasis; Anthropic oscillates between coding and recovery. A second capability transition at 30–72B is observed; SWE-bench saturates while HLE and instruction-following retain discriminatory spread. Six open-weight architectures corroborate axis-rotation potential. Five April 2026 releases confirm diagnostic stability. Slopes of per-lab coupling vary up to ~5×. Provides a playbook, per-lab tables, seven falsifiable predictions, and an interactive dashboard at https://zehenlabs.com/cape/.

Conclusion: The work offers a practical, falsifiable framework to locate, diagnose, and rotate measurement axes across frontier releases, with actionable guidance and forward-looking predictions, supported by longitudinal, multi-lab data and an interactive tool.

Abstract: Leaderboards rank frontier models on independent axes but do not reveal whether capabilities reinforce or trade off across releases -- and at the frontier, this interaction is the more informative signal. We decompose paired SWE-bench and GPQA Diamond scores into a population coupling trend and per-release residual ($h$-field) that diagnoses capability emphasis and identifies which measurement or stress test is most informative next. Across 34 models from 10 labs (2024--2026), capabilities cooperate ($r = +0.72$, $p < 10^{-6}$), but cooperation varies by lab and over time: DeepSeek reversed from reasoning-rich to coding-first ($h$: $+11.2 \to -4.7$, 15.9-pp swing); Google maintains consistent reasoning emphasis; Anthropic oscillates between coding excursions and recovery. Cooperation is not static -- it cascades. Six open-weight architectures confirm a second capability transition at 30--72B, and SWE-bench is now saturating while HLE and instruction-following retain discriminatory spread -- signaling the next axis rotation. We provide a three-level playbook (locate, diagnose, rotate), a per-lab measurement-priority table, and seven falsifiable predictions with timestamped criteria for the next 12 months of frontier releases. Per-lab coupling slopes vary $5\times$ (Google $1.15$ vs. DeepSeek $0.23$), quantifying how efficiently each recipe converts coding gains into reasoning. Five April 2026 releases confirm the diagnostic out of sample ($r$ rises from $+0.72$ to $+0.75$). An interactive dashboard provides phase classification with actionable recommendations, $h$-field diagnostics, per-lab coupling trajectories, ODE-based scaling predictions, benchmark rotation guidance, self-steering demo, and live tracking of all seven predictions: https://zehenlabs.com/cape/.

</details>


### [274] [From Cumulative Constraints to Adaptive Runtime Safety Control for Nonstationary Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18841)
*Timofey Tomashevskiy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A runtime safety shield (CPSS) converts a cumulative safety budget into adaptive, context-aware state-level constraints to enforce per-step safety in nonstationary RL; it provides finite-horizon safety guarantees and selective interventions, demonstrated on nonstationary highway merging tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cumulative safety constraints fail to prevent unsafe actions in nonstationary or continual RL where risk varies by context; fixed state thresholds can be too conservative or too weak. A mechanism is needed to adapt safety enforcement to regime changes while respecting budgets.

Method: Constraint Projection Safety Shield tracks remaining safety budget, projects it into a time-varying admissible risk threshold, and filters actions whose predicted safety cost exceeds the active threshold. The threshold is updated online using contextual signals to tighten enforcement in demanding regimes and relax it when budget permits.

Result: The shield ensures per-state threshold satisfaction for executed actions, yields finite-horizon cumulative safety bounds, and provides a degradation bound tied to intervention frequency and reward distortion. Empirically, CPSS reduces proximity-based safety violations and increases separation margins in nonstationary highway merging tasks, intervening selectively.

Conclusion: Adaptive budget-to-threshold projection is a practical method to convert cumulative safety specifications into effective local safety control for continual reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Safety in reinforcement learning is often specified through cumulative cost constraints, but these trajectory-level guarantees do not directly prevent unsafe individual decisions, especially under nonstationarity. In continual and nonstationary settings, the difficulty is amplified because the risk associated with the same action can vary across contexts, while a fixed state-level threshold may be either too conservative or too weak. We propose Constraint Projection Safety Shield (CPSS), a runtime mechanism that converts a cumulative safety budget into adaptive state-level control constraints during execution. CPSS tracks the remaining safety budget, projects it into a time-varying admissible risk threshold, and filters policy actions whose predicted safety cost exceeds the active threshold. The threshold is adjusted online using contextual signals so that enforcement becomes stricter in more demanding or rapidly changing regimes and less restrictive when the available safety budget is sufficient. We analyze the resulting shielded policy and show that the mechanism guarantees per-state threshold satisfaction for executed actions, induces finite-horizon cumulative cost bounds, and yields a performance degradation bound in terms of intervention frequency and per-step reward distortion. We evaluate CPSS in nonstationary highway merging scenarios using highway-env. Across multiple seeds, CPSS substantially reduces proximity-based safety violations and increases separation margins while intervening selectively rather than dominating the learned policy. These results support adaptive budget-to-threshold projection as a practical way to transform cumulative safety specifications into effective local safety control for continual reinforcement learning systems.

</details>


### [275] [Safe Continual Reinforcement Learning under Nonstationarity via Adaptive Safety Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18842)
*Timofey Tomashevskiy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LILAC+ proposes three adaptive safety mechanisms for safe continual RL under nonstationarity, combining context-based constraints, adaptation-speed constraints, and budget-to-state enforcement; evaluated in driving sims; shows reduced safety violations under distribution shift with competitive task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety in reinforcement learning under distribution shift and nonstationarity is challenging; fixed safety constraints fail to adapt to changing environments; there is a need for adaptive mechanisms that respond to context, rate of change, and safety budget.

Method: Integrates three adaptive mechanisms: (1) context-based safety constraints using inferred and predicted environmental context; (2) adaptation-speed constraints that tighten safety when environmental change outruns the agent’s adaptation; (3) budget-to-state safety enforcement that converts cumulative safety requirements into local state-level constraints for runtime enforcement; evaluated in simulated driving under stationary, seen nonstationary, and unseen nonstationary conditions.

Result: Adaptive constraints substantially reduce safety violations under distribution shift while maintaining competitive task performance compared with unconstrained and fixed-constraint baselines.

Conclusion: Safe continual reinforcement learning requires adaptive constraint mechanisms that respond to current state information and to predicted context, adaptation demand, and remaining safety budget; LILAC+ demonstrates a unified framework achieving proactive and reactive safety adaptation.

Abstract: Safe reinforcement learning in nonstationary environments requires safety mechanisms that adapt as environmental conditions change. Standard safe reinforcement learning methods often assume fixed constraints or stable environmental conditions, which can become inadequate under distribution shift. We propose LILAC+, a framework for safe continual reinforcement learning under nonstationarity that combines three adaptive safety mechanisms: context-based safety constraints, adaptation-speed constraints, and budget-to-state safety enforcement. Context-based constraints adjust safety requirements using inferred and predicted environmental context. Adaptation-speed constraints tighten safety requirements when the rate of environmental change exceeds the agent's ability to adapt safely. Budget-to-state enforcement converts cumulative safety requirements into local state-level control constraints that can be enforced at decision time. Together, these mechanisms provide a unified approach for proactive and reactive safety adaptation in continual reinforcement learning. We evaluate the framework in simulated driving environments under stationary, seen nonstationary, and unseen nonstationary conditions. The results show that adaptive safety constraints substantially reduce safety violations under distribution shift while maintaining competitive task performance compared with unconstrained and fixed-constraint baselines. These findings suggest that safe continual reinforcement learning requires adaptive constraint mechanisms that respond not only to current state information but also to predicted environmental context, adaptation demand, and remaining safety budget.

</details>


### [276] [TEMPO: Temporal Enforcement via Mode-Separated Policy Optimization for Trustworthy LLM Backtesting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18843)
*Zeyu Zhang,Bradly C. Stadie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TEMPO introduces temporal discipline to prevent post-cutoff knowledge leakage in backtesting LLMs by separating a leakage-avoidance mode from a task-performance mode, using a two-mode reward and a GRPO-based training pipeline to converge to leak-free, improved task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluation validity is compromised when models leak post-cutoff information into reasoning.
