Prompt constraints may fail when leakage is causally tied to predictions, and instance-specific temporal compliance means erasing knowledge is not viable. There is a need for models to learn to use temporally valid evidence conditioned on the cutoff date.

Method: TEMPO uses two-mode rewards: a leakage mode that drives post-cutoff claims to zero as a prerequisite before a performance mode optimizes the task; and a GRPO-based training pipeline that enables discovery of temporally valid reasoning strategies. The approach enforces temporal discipline rather than erasing knowledge, aiming for monotonic leakage reduction and convergence to a leak-free optimum.

Result: Across three prediction tasks and two models, TEMPO reduces leakage from 2–13% to 0.6–3.7% in all conditions, with task performance improving 6–13% when strong pre-cutoff signals exist and remaining stable when the task is inherently hard. The authors prove monotonic leakage decrease and convergence to a leak-free optimum, and show leakage reduction accompanied by performance gains.

Conclusion: TEMPO provides a principled method to enforce temporal compliance in LLM reasoning, improving evaluation validity and task performance once compliance is achieved, with empirical results across tasks/models and a theoretical guarantee of leakage reduction.

Abstract: Backtesting large language models on historical events requires reasoning exclusively from information available before a specified cutoff date. Yet models routinely leak post-cutoff knowledge from pre-training into their reasoning, inflating apparent accuracy and undermining evaluation validity. Prompt-based constraints fail when suppressed content is causally related to the prediction, and knowledge unlearning cannot address this problem because temporal compliance is instance-specific: the same fact may be legitimate evidence for one cutoff date and a violation for another. Rather than erasing knowledge, the model must learn temporal discipline: selecting evidence conditioned on each instance's cutoff date. We propose TEMPO (Temporal Enforcement via Mode-separated Policy Optimization), which trains this discipline via two contributions: (1) a two-mode reward where a leakage mode drives post-cutoff claims to zero as a hard prerequisite before a performance mode optimizes task performance; and (2) a GRPO-based training pipeline that enables the model to discover temporally valid reasoning strategies. We prove that training monotonically decreases leakage, converges to the leak-free optimum, and improves task performance once compliance is achieved. On three prediction tasks and two models, TEMPO reduces leakage from 2~13% to 0.6~3.7% across all conditions, with task performance improving 6~13% where strong pre-cutoff signals exist and maintained where the prediction task is inherently difficult from valid information alone.

</details>


### [277] [Graph-Driven Cross-Industry Real-Time Monitoring Framework for Anti-Money Laundering Detection in Converged Mobility-Energy Supply Chain Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18844)
*Rong Liu,Xiaojun Xiao,Zhanqing Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph-driven real-time cross-industry AML monitoring (GCRMF) for travel–energy supply chains using CIHG, Temporal Dual-GAT, meta-path subgraph reasoning with contrastive learning, and online self-supervised adaptation, yielding >17.8% F1 gain and lower false positives over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-industry supply chain finance creates high AML risk; traditional GNN methods struggle with heterogeneous, evolving cross-industry networks and collusive fraud; need real-time, self-adapting detection framework.

Method: 1) Build cross-industry heterogeneous graph (CIHG) spanning travel platforms, energy suppliers, fintechs. 2) Use Temporally Dual-GAT to encode industry semantics and dynamic capital flows over time. 3) Propose meta-path subgraph reasoning with contrastive learning and hierarchical graph sampling to detect coordinated fraud. 4) Employ self-supervised online learning for continual adaptation to new laundering strategies.

Result: Experimental results show >17.8% relative F1 improvement over existing GNN baselines in cross-industry settings and a significant reduction in false positives; demonstrates robustness and real-time capability.

Conclusion: GCRMF effectively integrates heterogeneous cross-industry data, leveraging temporal graph attention and meta-path reasoning with online self-supervision to improve accuracy and reduce false alarms, suitable for real-time AML monitoring in connected travel–energy ecosystems.

Abstract: With the deep integration of the travel and energy industries, cross-industry supply chain finance has gradually become a high-risk field of hidden money laundering incidents. For this reason, this work proposes a graph-driven cross-industry real-time anti-money laundering monitoring framework (GCRMF) for integrated travel - energy supply chain networks. First, a cross-industry heterogeneous graph (CIHG) covering new energy vehicle rental platforms, energy suppliers, fintech institutions, etc., is constructed, and industry semantics are integrated through temporarily Dual-GAT (Temporal Dual-Graph Attention Network), dynamically encoding capital flow paths and evolution features over time. Subsequently, in order to identify the structural fraud behavior together produced by colluding subjects, a meta-path subgraph reasoning module based on contrastive learning and hierarchical graph sampling is proposed to enhance the discrimination capability of cross-industry recurring money laundering behavior. Meanwhile, a self-supervised online learning mechanism is adopted for real-time adaptation and continuous optimization to new money laundering strategies. The experimental results show that compared with existing graph neural network methods in cross-industry scenarios, GCRMF improves the performance by more than 17.8% of F1 score and greatly reduces the false positive rate.

</details>


### [278] [First-Passage Prediction of Grokking Delay: ACalibrated Law under AdamW with Causal Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18845)
*Truong Xuan Khanh,Truong Quynh Hoa,Luu Duc Trung,Phan Thanh Duc*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A quantitative, architecture-aware first-passage time law predicts grokking delay under AdamW, tying delay to norm dynamics and a geometry-based angular threshold; calibrated per architecture, it predicts delays well across tasks and architectures, but transfer to large language models remains open.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify and predict grokking delays under AdamW and to understand the roles of parameter-norm growth and angular reachability in memory formation.

Method: Derive T_grok - T_mem = (1/(2 κ_LL η λ)) log(V_mem/V_star) with V_t = ||θ_t||^2, V_star a architecture-dependent threshold, and κ_LL capturing AdamW corrections; treat the first-passage of V_t as necessary but not sufficient; establish a quantile-margin theorem requiring both norm separation V_mem>V_post and angular reachability α_star = arcsin(C / sqrt(V_T_mem)); calibrate κ_LL and V_star from a single hyperparameter cell; calibrate C from NTK feature map and validation-margin quantile; validate across 26 held-out runs (MAPE 17.7% over 41x delays), MLPs (MAPE 18.0%, N=34), and cross-task extension (MAPE 23.3%, N=46, 43.5x range); report structured residual with V_star/V_mem stability (CV ~14%); compare causality via interventions (freeze norm or remove weight decay eliminates grokking).

Result: The law yields accurate delay predictions across architectures and tasks (MAPEs in 17-23%), reveals a stable V_star/V_mem pattern within architectures, and links grokking to a geometry of the NTK-feature space via α_star; causal interventions that fix norm or remove weight decay suppress grokking, confirming the necessity of the dual norm and angular requirements; κ_LL is architecture-specific with modest cross-architecture variability (~2x) beyond depth; generalization to natural-language scale models remains untested.

Conclusion: An empirical, architecture-aware first-passage framework for grokking delay under AdamW is established, with strong cross-architecture predictive performance for algorithmic tasks; the norm-and-angle criteria form a structured, testable explanation, though cross-domain transfer to NLP-scale models is an open question.

Abstract: We give the first quantitative prediction of grokking delay under AdamW. Treating the delay as a first-passage time, we derive a closed-form law T_grok - T_mem = (1 / 2 kappa_LL eta lambda) log(V_mem / V_star), where V_t = ||theta_t||^2 is the squared parameter norm, V_star is an architecture-dependent threshold, and kappa_LL absorbs the AdamW correction to the clean-SGD contraction rate 2 eta lambda. Calibrating (kappa_LL, V_star) on a single hyperparameter cell predicts grokking delays on 26 held-out runs with MAPE 17.7% over a 41x delay range; the law generalises to MLPs (MAPE 18.0%, N=34) and degrades to 23.3% on cross-task extension (N=46, 43.5x range), with a structured residual in which V_star / V_mem stays comparatively stable within architecture (CV about 14% on the 1L transformer).
  First-passage of V_t is necessary but not sufficient. A quantile-margin theorem establishes that positive delay requires both norm separation V_mem > V_post and angular reachability of a threshold alpha_star = arcsin(C / V_T_mem^(1/2)), where C is computable from the empirical NTK feature map and the validation-margin quantile. Calibrating C on modulus p=89 predicts alpha_star = 47.2 degrees at p=97 (observed 47.8 degrees, error 1.3%) as a prior cross-cell prediction. Causal interventions that freeze the norm or remove weight decay at memorisation eliminate grokking (0/6 vs. 3/3 baseline), trapping the angular displacement near 12 degrees.
  kappa_LL is empirically measured per architecture rather than derived from (beta_1, beta_2, epsilon); within-architecture CV stays at most 15% across four architectures, but values differ by about 2x between architectural variants beyond depth alone. Empirical scope is algorithmic tasks (modular arithmetic, sparse parity) under AdamW; whether the law transfers to natural-language scale models is open.

</details>


### [279] [EUPHORIA: Efficient Universal Planning via Hybrid Optimization for Robust Industrial Robotic Assembly](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18872)
*Shih-Yu Lai,Chia-Ching Yen,Yang-Ting Shen,Peter Yichen Chen,Yu-Lun Liu,Bing-Yu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified planner, EUPHORIA, for architectural robotic assembly that combines meta-geometric encoding via graph hypernetworks, physics-informed graph transformer with SAC and DEM-based physics-bias attention, kinematics-aware sequencing, and a differentiable residual stability correction layer to enable few-shot adaptability and energy-efficient, modular planning on complex topologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome retraining bottlenecks of specialized planners and inefficiencies from decoupled sequencing and motion by creating a single framework with universal adaptability and energy-aware planning for complex architectural geometries.

Method: 1) Meta-Geometric Encoder with Graph Hypernetworks generates policy parameters from minimal support data for parameter-level adaptation to complex topologies without gradient-based retraining. 2) Physics-Informed Graph Transformer trained with Soft Actor-Critic (SAC); introduces Physics-Bias Attention by modulating attention with contact forces from Discrete Element Method (DEM) simulations to emphasize structurally critical connections. 3) Kinematics-Aware Sequencing; SAC objective penalizes high-energy transitions to improve runtime efficiency. 4) Residual Stability Correction; differentiable optimization layer that refines coarse assembly actions by minimizing an energy-stability cost before execution, bridging Sim2Real gap.

Result: Experiments report significant reduction in energy consumption compared to decoupled baselines and state-of-the-art success rates on unseen, non-standard geometries with only a few-shot support set, indicating strong generalization and efficiency gains.

Conclusion: EUPHORIA integrates meta-learning, physics-informed attention, and residual optimization into a cohesive planner, achieving universal few-shot adaptability and dynamic efficiency for robotic architectural assembly.

Abstract: Robotic assembly in architectural construction faces a persistent bottleneck: existing planners are either highly specialized, requiring prohibitive retraining for every new geometric design, or operationally inefficient, treating structural sequencing and kinematic motion as disjoint processes. We present EUPHORIA, a unified framework that achieves universal few-shot adaptability and dynamic efficiency through a hybrid optimization strategy. To overcome the retraining bottleneck, we propose a Meta-Geometric Encoder based on Graph Hypernetworks: unlike standard contrastive learning, which performs only feature-level recognition, our hypernetwork dynamically generates policy parameters from a minimal support set, enabling parameter-level adaptation to complex topologies (e.g., domes, arches) without gradient-based retraining. For structural reasoning, we introduce a Physics-Informed Graph Transformer trained via Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), with a Physics-Bias Attention mechanism that modulates attention scores using contact forces from Discrete Element Model (DEM) simulations, guiding the planner toward structurally critical connections. We further ensure operational efficiency through Kinematics-Aware Sequencing, where the SAC objective penalizes high-energy transitions. Finally, we bridge the Sim2Real gap via Residual Stability Correction, a differentiable optimization layer that fine-tunes coarse assembly actions by minimizing a joint energy-stability cost prior to execution. Experiments show that EUPHORIA significantly reduces energy consumption over decoupled baselines and achieves state-of-the-art success rates on unseen, non-standard geometries with minimal few-shot examples, fusing meta-learning, physics-informed attention, and residual optimization into a cohesive, generalized planner.

</details>


### [280] [Lost and Found in Translation: Variational Diagnostics for Neural Codebook Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18846)
*Yusuke Hayashi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces the neural codebook channel K_{e→d}(j|i) as a diagnostic for encoder–decoder mismatch in VAEs, with a Bernoulli–KL certificate bounding off-diagonal leakage. Demonstrates the certificate across multiple benchmarks, yielding an audit-ready reporting unit and linking mismatched decoding to a visible failure mode in deep generative models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VAE diagnostics verify latent usage but not whether the decoder reads latent codes in the encoder’s intended way. This mismatch is akin to mismatched decoding in classical communication, which can degrade performance but remains undetectable with standard metrics.

Method: Define K_{e→d}(j|i) as the encoder-to-decoder reading probability, derive a Bernoulli–KL certificate d_bin(1−A || arη_p) ≤ arΔ controlled by the variational gap, and prove a marginal-impossibility result: no combination of marginal histograms, entropies, active-code counts, or mutual information fixes K_{e→d}. Validate via audits on: (i) four sklearn datasets with finite grids (exact, multiple seeds/pairs), (ii) a 2D model where the bound is non-vacuous (2.71× observed disagreement) with the four-term identity closing to 1e-4, (iii) MNIST with importance-sampling control, and (iv) a VQ-VAE achieving Â=1.000. The outputs form an audit-ready package (K_{e→d}, A, R_eff, R, AU).

Result: Empirically, the bound holds across tested setups: the sklearn datasets meet the bound for all seeds/pairs; the 2D model yields a non-vacuous certificate, and the four-term identity closes to 1e−4; MNIST is controllable under importance sampling; the VQ-VAE attains the predicted limit Â=1.000. The authors provide an end-to-end auditing unit.

Conclusion: The framework makes mismatched decoding visible inside a single deep generative model, offering a practical, audit-ready diagnostic that connects classical communication theory to modern VAEs and reveals latent–decoder incompatibilities that standard metrics miss.

Abstract: Classical communication systems fail not only through random noise but also when transmitter and receiver use incompatible operational codebooks. Variational autoencoders (VAEs) train an encoder $q_φ$ and decoder $p_θ$ jointly, and practitioners treat the resulting latent space as a discrete code -- for clustering, conditional generation, and mechanistic interpretability. Yet standard VAE diagnostics -- ELBO, active units, mutual information, and code histograms -- certify only whether this code is used, never whether the decoder reads each latent under the encoder's code.
  We close this gap with the neural codebook channel $K_{e\to d}(j\mid i)$, a coupled encoder-decoder diagnostic whose off-diagonal mass is bounded by an architecture-free Bernoulli-KL certificate $d_{\mathrm{bin}}(1-\mathcal{A} \,\|\, \barη_p) \le \barΔ$ controlled by the variational gap. The certificate is the operational specialization of the classical KL chain rule under disintegration to the encoder-decoder disagreement event, complemented by a constructive marginal-impossibility result: no combination of marginal histograms, entropies, active-code counts, or mutual information determines $K_{e\to d}$.
  We audit the certificate on four sklearn datasets (finite-grid exact, 5/5 seeds, 20/20 pairs satisfy the bound), a 2D model where the bound is non-vacuous at $2.71\times$ the observed disagreement and the four-term identity closes within $10^{-4}$, MNIST under importance-sampling control, and a VQ-VAE attaining the predicted limit $\hat{\mathcal{A}}=1.000$. The package $(K_{e\to d}, \mathcal{A}, R_{\mathrm{eff}}, R, \mathrm{AU})$ is an audit-ready reporting unit. More broadly, the framework makes mismatched decoding -- a failure mode classical communication theory named decades ago -- visible inside a single deep generative model.

</details>


### [281] [Transformers Linearly Represent Highly Structured World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18847)
*Roman Kniazev,Nathanaël Fijalkow*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: 8-layer Transformer solving Sudoku develops a row/column/box–oriented world model and a sparse naked-single circuit; mechanistic interpretability reveals end-to-end algorithmic reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether sequential reasoning traces lead to internal representations aligned with domain structure and whether such representations are interpretable.

Method: Train an 8-layer Transformer on Sudoku solving traces and perform mechanistic interpretability analyses to uncover internal circuits and representations; identify world-model substructures and dedicated neurons for naked singles.

Result: Found a substructure world model organized around rows/cols/boxes rather than per-cell state; identified dedicated neurons in the final MLP that detect when exactly one digit is possible for a cell and promote it; the circuit is sparse, monosemantic, and interpretable.

Conclusion: Domain-constraint algebra shapes emergent representations; mechanistic tools can recover a complete algorithmic account of transformer-based reasoning on a combinatorial task.

Abstract: Do transformers, when trained on sequential reasoning traces, build internal models of the underlying task? And if so, does the structure of those internal representations mirror the structure of the domain? We train an 8-layer transformer on Sudoku solving traces and perform a mechanistic analysis of its internal computation. We establish two results. First, the model builds a substructure world model: it does not represent the board state cell by cell, as a human analyst would expect, but organizes information around the rows, columns, and boxes that Sudoku's constraints act on. Second, we identify a naked-single circuit: a small set of dedicated neurons in the final MLP layer, each individually detecting when exactly one digit remains possible for a specific cell, and reliably promoting that digit. These findings show that the geometry of an emergent world model is shaped by the constraint algebra of the domain, not its surface presentation, and that the resulting decision circuit is sparse, monosemantic, and fully interpretable. More broadly, they demonstrate that mechanistic interpretability tools can recover an end-to-end algorithmic account of how a transformer solves a combinatorial reasoning task.

</details>


### [282] [Sampling-Based Safe Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19469)
*Luca Vignola,Bruce D. Lee,Manish Prajapat,Manuel Wendl,Melanie Zeilinger,Andreas Krause,Yarden As*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Model-based safe RL (SBSRL) that enforces safety across multiple uncertain dynamics samples; uses uncertainty-constrained exploration (no explicit bonuses); provides high-probability safety guarantees and finite-time sample complexity; scalable to deep ensembles for high-dimensional control; validated in simulation and hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safe exploration is essential for real-world RL deployments but difficult due to uncertain dynamics and safety constraints. Existing approaches struggle to provide practical guarantees in continuous domains. This work aims to deliver formal safety guarantees and practical sample efficiency.

Method: SBSRL enforces safety by considering a finite set of dynamics samples and solving a constrained optimization that approximates worst-case uncertainty. An exploration strategy bounds epistemic uncertainty instead of using exploration bonuses. Regularity conditions yield high-probability safety guarantees and finite-time guarantees for recovering near-optimal policy. The approach extends to deep ensembles to scale to high-dimensional control.

Result: Empirically, SBSRL achieves safe and efficient exploration in both simulation and real robotic hardware. The method readily extends to practical deep-ensemble implementations that scale to high-dimensional continuous control problems.

Conclusion: The paper delivers a practical safe RL framework with formal safety and sample-complexity guarantees, bridging theory and real-world applications, and scalable to complex, high-dimensional settings.

Abstract: Safe exploration remains a fundamental challenge in reinforcement learning (RL), limiting the deployment of RL agents in the real world. We propose Sampling-Based Safe Reinforcement Learning (SBSRL), a model-based RL algorithm that maintains safety throughout the learning process by enforcing constraints jointly across a finite set of dynamics samples. This formulation approximates an intractable worst-case optimization over uncertain dynamics and enables practical safety guarantees in continuous domains. We further introduce an exploration strategy based on constraining epistemic uncertainty, eliminating the need for explicit exploration bonuses. Under regularity conditions, we derive high-probability guarantees of safety throughout learning and a finite-time sample complexity bound for recovering a near-optimal policy. Empirically, SBSRL achieves safe and efficient exploration both in simulation and in real robotic hardware, and readily extends to practical deep-ensemble implementations that scale to high-dimensional continuous control problems.

</details>


### [283] [Exact Linear Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18848)
*Weinuo Ou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Exact Linear Attention (ELA) achieves linear-time attention with exact results via kernel decomposition, addressing gradient explosion and token attention dilution in previous linear-attention methods. It introduces kernel-constrained, non-negative, discriminative kernels and three engineering modules—Hyper Link, Memory Lobe, and routing-score bias for MoEs—to enhance efficiency, memory flow, interpretability, and semantic alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard attention is Quadratic in sequence length, making long-sequence modeling expensive. Previous linear-attention approaches trade accuracy for speed and suffer gradient instability and attention dilution. There is a need for an exact, linear-time mechanism with principled constraints and practical architectural enhancements to improve training dynamics and interpretability.

Method: Leverages exact kernel decomposition to obtain linear-time attention without approximation errors. Proposes kernel families such as Hadamard Exp Kernel, Summation Squared Euclidean Distance Kernel, and Subtraction Squared Euclidean Distance Kernel. Enforces kernel constraints: non-negativity, discriminability, and geometric interpretability. Adds three engineering modules: (1) Hyper Link—replaces residual connections to mitigate gradient degradation, (2) Memory Lobe—bidirectional linear attention capturing transformation flow across layers for qualitative memory and implicit reinforcement learning, (3) routing-score bias—bias mechanism for Mixture of Experts to improve interpretability and semantic alignment.

Result: Claims linear computational complexity for attention with exact results (no approximation error). Addresses gradient explosion and token attention dilution observed in prior linear attention methods. Kernel constraints aim to improve discriminability and interpretability; Hyper Link, Memory Lobe, and routing-score bias are proposed to enhance gradient flow, memory across layers, and interpretability/semantic alignment in MoE settings.

Conclusion: ELA provides a principled framework for exact, linear-time attention and introduces architectural enhancements that improve training dynamics, memory, and interpretability, expanding the practicality of exact linear attention for deep Transformer and MoE architectures.

Abstract: This paper introduces Exact Linear Attention (ELA), a mechanism that achieves linear computational complexity for Transformer attention by leveraging the exact decomposition property of kernel functions, without any approximation error. It identifies and addresses gradient explosion and token attention dilution in prior linear attention methods by imposing kernel constraints that ensure non-negativity, discriminability, and geometric interpretability. Several kernel functions are proposed, including the Hadamard Exp Kernel, Summation Squared Euclidean Distance Kernel, and Subtraction Squared Euclidean Distance Kernel. Beyond the core attention formulation, the paper presents three engineering innovations: a Hyper Link structure that replaces traditional residual connections to mitigate gradient degradation, a Memory Lobe module based on bidirectional linear attention that captures transformation flow across layers to implement qualitative memory and an implicit reinforcement learning paradigm, and a routing score based bias mechanism for Mixture of Experts to improve interpretability and semantic alignment.

</details>


### [284] [INSIGHTS: Demonstration-Based Summaries of Time Series Predictors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18849)
*Bar Eini Porat,Rom Gutman,Uri Shalit,Ofra Amir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces INSIGHTS, a model-agnostic method for global explanations of time-series models by constructing informative, diverse sample summaries and domain-aware utility to select representative time series.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global explanations for time-series models are underdeveloped; current work emphasizes local attributions. There is a need for simple, efficient, transparent global explanations that stakeholders can readily adopt.

Method: Propose INSIGHTS (model-agnostic, user-centric). It generates sample summaries and uses utility functions to balance importance and diversity of time-series samples, capturing domain-specific aspects (e.g., exceeding domain norms) to create informative subsets. Evaluation includes experiments, interviews, and a user study.

Result: INSIGHTS constructs comprehensive, diverse time-series subsets and summaries that are manageable for individual evaluation. Domain experts prefer it for providing stable understanding of model behavior and sample quality. User-study participants show enhanced understanding of the model’s overall behavior with INSIGHTS-based summaries.

Conclusion: INSIGHTS offers a practical, stable, and interpretable approach for global explanations of time-series models by focusing on informative, diverse sample summaries and domain-aligned utilities, facilitating adoption by stakeholders.

Abstract: Explainability methods have progressed rapidly, but global explanations for time-series models remain underdeveloped, with most approaches focusing on local, instance-level attributions. We introduce INSIGHTS, a model-agnostic, user-centric approach for providing global explanations of time series models. Our approach prioritizes simplicity, efficiency, and transparency in its design, ensuring that stakeholders can readily adopt its outputs. While current methods focus on local explanations, INSIGHTS generates sample summaries that offer a comprehensive overview of model behavior. It balances the importance and diversity of time series samples to create informative subsets using utility functions that capture domain-specific aspects of time series behavior, such as exceeding domain norms. We evaluate INSIGHTS through experiments, interviews, and a user study. Our results indicate INSIGHTS effectively constructs comprehensive, diverse time series subsets, producing summaries manageable for individual evaluation. It is preferred by domain experts for its ability to provide a stable understanding of model behavior and the quality of the samples identified. Moreover, user study participants presented with INSIGHTS-based summaries exhibit an enhanced understanding of the model's overall behavior.

</details>


### [285] [STRIDE: Learnable Stepwise Language Feedback for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18851)
*Junjie Zhang,Guozheng Ma,Shunyu Liu,Zetian Hu,Yongcheng Jing,Ting-En Lin,Yongbin Li,Dacheng Tao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STRIDE: a learnable stepwise language feedback framework that replaces scalar rewards with outcome-based supervision, co-training a generator and a generative verifier to redirect intermediate reasoning steps, achieving strong gains and enabling zero-pass-rate improvements on complex reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the limitations of scalar reward signals and costly intermediate annotations in RL for LLM reasoning; provide rich, scalable, localizable feedback via a learnable verifier to improve policy learning beyond existing external critics.

Method: STRIDE jointly trains a generator and a generative verifier. It uses only outcome-based rewards, with the verifier providing stepwise, natural-language critiques that localize failures. The generator uses these critiques to redirect reasoning trajectories at intermediate steps, ensuring harmless policy improvement even with noisy or suboptimal verifier feedback.

Result: Empirical results on diverse reasoning benchmarks show STRIDE outperforms state-of-the-art baselines and achieves breakthroughs on zero-pass-rate problems where scalar methods yield no learning signal; ablation studies confirm the value of learnable stepwise language feedback and the verifier’s training.

Conclusion: Learnable, stepwise language feedback via STRIDE offers scalable, annotation-free RL supervision for LLM reasoning, enabling robust policy improvement and broader domain coverage through trajectory redirection and outcome-based rewards.

Abstract: Recent advances in Reinforcement Learning (RL) have underscored its potential for incentivizing reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing step-level efforts suffer from costly annotations that limit domain coverage, while scalar scores further impose an information bottleneck, offering insufficient semantic bandwidth to improve intermediate decisions. Alternative language-critique approaches, which rely on frozen or external critics, provide richer textual feedback but lack the scalability needed for sustained policy improvement. In this work, we propose language-driven stepwise trajectory redirection, termed as STRIDE, a novel training framework that shifts process supervision from scalar rewards to learnable stepwise language feedback. Specifically, we co-train a generator and a generative verifier using only outcome-based rewards, eliminating external annotations, while delivering sustained policy improvement through jointly aligned verifier training. The verifier's stepwise language critiques explicitly localize and explain failures, enabling the generator to redirect reasoning trajectories at intermediate steps toward alternative decisions. The trajectory redirection design guarantees harmless policy improvement, even under noisy or suboptimal verifier feedback. Experiments on diverse reasoning benchmarks show that STRIDE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, as well as achieving breakthroughs on zero-pass-rate problems where scalar methods yield no learning signal in our ablation studies, demonstrating the effectiveness of learnable stepwise language feedback for enhancing LLM reasoning.

</details>


### [286] [Robust Checkpoint Selection for Multimodal LLMs via Agentic Evaluation and Stability-Aware Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18852)
*Qinwu Xu,Zhuoheng Li,Jessie Salas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Uncertainty-aware, multi-stage checkpoint selection for multimodal LLMs using curated real-world data, LLM judgments, and multi-stage ranking; highlights OCR readability as key to evaluation validity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small performance gaps between checkpoints and noisy signals make static benchmarks unreliable; there is a need for robust uncertainty estimation and real-world alignment, especially in OCR-heavy scenarios.

Method: Proposes a multi-stage evaluation framework: pointwise filtering, listwise ranking, and pairwise comparison; incorporates subsampling-based confidence estimation and percentile-based scoring; leverages curated real-world data and LLM-based judgments; emphasizes data quality (OCR readability).

Result: Framework aims to yield robust checkpoint ranking under evaluation uncertainty; improves reliability of conclusions in the presence of noise; demonstrates that OCR readability critically affects evaluation validity.

Conclusion: A robust, uncertainty-aware, multi-stage evaluation framework enhances checkpoint selection for multimodal LLMs, with a key emphasis on data quality and OCR readability for valid evaluation.

Abstract: Checkpoint selection for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) presents significant challenges when performance differentials are marginal and evaluation signals are prone to noise. Existing methodologies rely heavily on static benchmarks or pointwise scoring, which frequently misalign with in-the-wild usage and lack robust uncertainty estimation, particularly in OCR-heavy scenarios. In this work, we formulate checkpoint selection as a robust decision problem under evaluation uncertainty. We propose a multi-stage framework that integrates curated real-world data, structured LLM-based judgment, and multi-stage ranking protocols. The evaluation system orchestrates progressive refinement via pointwise filtering, listwise ranking, and pairwise comparison. To enhance reliability, we introduce subsampling-based confidence estimation and a percentile-based scoring formulation that captures distributional characteristics while penalizing tail failures. Furthermore, we demonstrate that data quality, specifically OCR readability, is a critical determinant of evaluation validity.

</details>


### [287] [INAR-VL: Input-Aware Routing for Edge-Cloud Vision-Language Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18853)
*Ahmed Šabanović,Paul Joe Maliakel,Ivona Brandić*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: INAR-VL is a lightweight edge-cloud routing system for multimodal inference that partitions vision-language tasks between edge and cloud using simple complexity signals, achieving substantial latency and energy savings with minimal cloud-level accuracy loss in visual question answering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge deployment of VLMs faces a latency-accuracy tradeoff due to limited edge model capacity and communication delays; image quality and reasoning complexity vary across inputs, making static edge/cloud placement suboptimal.

Method: A two-tier deployment where complementary VLMs reside at edge and cloud. INAR-VL uses lightweight image and text complexity signals to drive routing and model selection, handling simple queries locally and offloading complex ones to the cloud.

Result: Evaluation on visual question answering shows 36% of requests processed at the edge, 24% lower latency, 26% lower energy consumption, and 97% of cloud-level accuracy preserved.

Conclusion: Adaptive edge-cloud routing with input complexity signals can significantly reduce latency and energy while maintaining near-cloud accuracy for multimodal inference.

Abstract: Edge deployment of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) faces a tradeoff between latency and accuracy: cloud execution provides high-quality predictions but incurs communication delay and energy cost, while edge-only execution is faster but less accurate due to limited model capacity. This trade-off is further complicated by heterogeneity in image quality and reasoning complexity, making static placement suboptimal. We present INAR-VL, a lightweight edge-cloud routing system for multimodal inference in a two-tier deployment. INAR-VL maintains complementary VLMs across edge and cloud and uses lightweight image and text complexity signals to guide routing and model selection, executing simple queries locally while offloading complex ones when beneficial. Evaluation on visual question answering shows that INAR-VL executes 36% of requests on the edge, reduces latency by 24%, lowers energy by 26%, and preserves 97% of cloud-level accuracy.

</details>


### [288] [Evaluating Memory Condensation Strategies for Coding Agents in Data-Driven Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18854)
*Renuka Chintalapati,Sid Raskar,Anurag Acharya,Jared Willard,Patrick Emami,Sameera Horawalavithana*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Eight memory-condensation strategies were evaluated with GPT-4o on 60 DiscoveryBench tasks across six scientific domains (480 evaluations). No condenser notably improved hypothesis quality; LLM condensers raised token costs (24–94%); masking tool-call outputs yielded an 8.6% net token savings. The optimal condenser is domain- and task-length dependent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a systematic benchmark guiding memory-condensation strategy selection for long-running, context-heavy AI-driven scientific discovery tasks.

Method: Empirical evaluation of eight memory-condensation strategies using GPT-4o on 60 DiscoveryBench tasks spanning six domains (480 evaluations total). Metrics include hypothesis quality, token cost, and net savings.

Result: No condenser significantly altered hypothesis quality; LLM-based condensers increased token costs by 24–94%; masking tool-call outputs achieved an 8.6% net savings.

Conclusion: Best condensation strategy is domain- and task-length dependent; no single approach dominates across all scientific discovery tasks.

Abstract: Coding agents accumulate extensive context during long-running tasks, yet fixed context windows force practitioners to choose between truncation and task failure. While numerous memory condensation strategies have been proposed, from simple sliding windows to LLM-generated summaries, no systematic comparison exists to guide strategy selection, especially in scientific discovery tasks. We evaluate eight memory condensation strategies using GPT-4o on sixty DiscoveryBench tasks spanning six scientific domains (480 total evaluations). We find that no condenser significantly alters hypothesis quality, while LLM-based condensers increase token costs by 24-94 percent, and masking tool-call outputs achieves an 8.6 percent net savings. We also observe that the optimal condenser for data-driven scientific discovery varies by scientific domain and task length.

</details>


### [289] [Delta Attention Residuals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18855)
*Cheng Luo,Zefan Cai,Junjie Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Delta Attention Residuals attend over layer deltas (h_{i+1}-h_i) instead of cumulative states, reducing routing collapse and increasing attention contrast; yields consistent perplexity gains (1.7–8.2%) across 220M–7.6B models and enables easy fine-tuning of pretrained checkpoints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address redundancy in standard Attention Residuals where attending over cumulative hidden states leads to low-contrast, near-uniform attention weights in deeper layers, causing routing collapse and limited selective state routing.

Method: Replace attention targets with deltas v_i = h_{i+1}-h_i at sublayer and block granularity; measure attention contrast and routing efficacy; evaluate across model scales (220M–7.6B) and tasks; demonstrate conversion of pretrained checkpoints to Delta ARs via standard fine-tuning; provide code.

Result: Delta ARs yield higher-contrast attention distributions (max weight ~0.6 vs ~0.2 for standard ARs), improved routing efficacy, and 1.7–8.2% validation perplexity gains across tested scales; outperform standard residuals and Attention Residuals; Delta ARs can convert pretrained checkpoints via fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Focusing on deltas for cross-layer routing mitigates routing collapse, enables more selective and effective state routing, and is robust across scales; practical for deployment with support for converting existing checkpoints.

Abstract: Attention Residuals replace standard additive residual connections with learned softmax attention over previous layer outputs, enabling selective cross-layer routing. However, standard Attention Residuals still attend over cumulative hidden states in previous layers, which are highly redundant. We show that this redundancy leads to routing collapse in deeper layers: attention weights become low-contrast and closer to uniform (max weight ${\approx}$0.2), limiting the model's ability to select informative states in previous layers. This raises a key but underexplored design question: what layer-wise representations should be routed in Attention Residuals? To answer this question, we propose Delta Attention Residuals, which attend over deltas -- the change introduced by each sublayer ($\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{h}_{i+1} - \mathbf{h}_i$) -- instead of cumulative states. Delta representations are structurally diverse and yield higher-contrast attention distributions (max weight ${\approx}$0.6), enabling more selective and effective routing across layers. This principle applies at both per-sublayer and block granularity. Across all tested scales (220M--7.6B), Delta Attention Residuals consistently outperform both standard residuals and Attention Residuals, with 1.7--8.2\% validation perplexity gains. Delta Attention Residuals also enables converting pretrained checkpoints into Delta Attention Residuals via standard fine-tuning. Code is available at https://github.com/wdlctc/delta-attention-residuals-code.

</details>


### [290] [SPHERICAL KV: Angle-Domain Attention and Rate-Distortion Retention for Efficient Long-Context Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18856)
*Anay Chauhan,Gurucharan Marthi Krishna Kumar,Arion Das,Amit Dhanda,Vinija Jain,Aman Chadha,Amitava Das*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spherical KV reduces KV residency and decoding bandwidth in long-context inference by treating KV allocation as a rate-distortion problem, via Angle-Domain Attention (ADA) with spherical keys and Rate-Distortion Retention (RDR) for tiered precision under a fixed budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context inference is constrained by growing resident memory and memory traffic in KV caches; existing techniques (eviction, windowing, quantization, offloading) only partially mitigate the bottleneck, especially when compressed states must be reconstructed into dense vectors during decoding.

Method: ADA stores keys in a spherical parameterization (radius + angle codes) and computes attention logits directly from codes without reconstructing dense keys, preserving a paged, block-local, fusion-friendly decode path. RDR jointly selects keep/drop decisions and precision tiers per token/head under a fixed budget to create tier-homogeneous pages with lightweight metadata, reducing KV residency and coalesced reads.

Result: The abstract presents a deployment-oriented mechanism to reduce KV residency while preserving decode efficiency; no empirical results are provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: ADA and RDR together offer a practical framework for reducing memory and bandwidth pressure in long-context inference, with attention geometry-guided compression and rate-distortion optimization; open questions include accuracy impact, memory/latency gains, and implementation details.

Abstract: Long-context inference is increasingly constrained by the KV cache: resident memory grows with context length, and decoding becomes limited by repeated High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) streaming rather than arithmetic. Existing methods such as eviction, windowing, quantization, and offloading reduce footprint, but often leave the critical-path bottleneck only partially addressed, especially when compressed states must still be reconstructed into dense vectors during decoding.
  We present Spherical KV, a long-context inference method that treats KV allocation as a rate-distortion problem grounded in attention geometry for efficient decoding. The method is built on two ideas: (i) represent directional information cheaply in the decode hot loop, and (ii) allocate retention and precision according to estimated future utility. Its first component, Angle-Domain Attention (ADA), stores keys in a spherical parameterization consisting of a scalar radius and compact angle codes, and computes attention logits directly from these codes without reconstructing dense keys. This preserves a paged, block-local, fusion-friendly decode path and directly targets HBM traffic in realistic serving settings. Its second component, Rate-Distortion Retention (RDR), jointly chooses keep/drop decisions and precision tiers per token and head under a fixed budget, producing tier-homogeneous pages with lightweight metadata and coalesced reads. Together, ADA and RDR provide a deployment-oriented mechanism for reducing KV residency while preserving decode efficiency.

</details>


### [291] [When Individually Calibrated Models Become Collectively Miscalibrated](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18858)
*Zhaohui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Individually calibrated predictors can become collectively miscalibrated when aggregate predictions interact strategically under Brier-score aggregation with positively correlated beliefs. A Price of Anarchy > 1 arises due to under-reporting by each agent. A VCG-based aggregation aligns incentives, achieving dominant-strategy incentive compatibility and near-optimal performance, with strong robustness in real datasets and under distribution shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibrated ensemble predictions are crucial in multi-agent settings where learners are trained on overlapping data. The work investigates whether calibration is preserved under aggregation when agents act strategically, and whether mechanism design can restore calibration and performance.

Method: Theoretically analyzes Brier-score-based aggregation with positively correlated beliefs. Proves PoA > 1 when Cov(b_i,b_j) > 0. Uses a canonical setting (n=5, ρ=0.5, base rate p=0.3) to illustrate PoA of false-negative rate up to 7.25x. Proposes a VCG-based aggregation to align incentives, attaining dominant-strategy incentive compatibility (DSIC) and near-optimality. Empirically evaluates on NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, and Credit Card Fraud datasets, comparing standard aggregation to VCG, and exploring adaptive weighting under distribution shifts.

Result: Theoretical result: PoA > 1 under positive correlation for Brier-score aggregation. Empirical PoA observed as high as 7.25x in false negatives. VC G-based aggregation achieves DSIC and near-optimal performance, with robustness and comparable accuracy to baseline methods. Adaptive weighting yields further gains under distribution shift.

Conclusion: Calibration is not preserved under strategic interaction in multi-agent ensembles. Mechanism design (VCG) can restore incentive compatibility and robustness, offering a practical approach for secure and reliable ensemble prediction in data-sparse or adversarial environments.

Abstract: Probabilistic prediction systems often aggregate probability estimates from multiple models into a single decision. A common assumption is that if each model is individually calibrated, the aggregate prediction will also be well calibrated. We show that this assumption fails in multi-agent settings: individually calibrated predictors can become collectively miscalibrated when their predictions interact strategically, in the game-theoretic sense of Brier-optimal local response, even without deliberate coordination.
  This phenomenon arises naturally when agents are independently trained on overlapping data. We prove that under Brier-score-based aggregation with positively correlated beliefs, each agent's individually optimal report systematically underestimates the positive-class probability, yielding a Price of Anarchy greater than one whenever Cov(b_i, b_j) > 0.
  In a canonical setting (n = 5 agents, pairwise correlation = 0.5, base rate = 0.3), the empirically measured PoA in false-negative rate reaches 7.25x. In contrast, VCG-based aggregation aligns incentives by rewarding marginal contribution, achieving dominant-strategy incentive compatibility and near-optimal performance.
  Experiments on three real-world datasets (NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, Credit Card Fraud) show that VCG provides strong robustness while maintaining comparable accuracy. It performs particularly well in data-sparse and adversarial settings, and adaptive weighting further improves performance under distribution shift.

</details>


### [292] [TwinRouterBench: Fast Static and Live Dynamic Evaluation for Realistic Agentic LLM Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18859)
*Pei Yang,Wanyi Chen,Tongyun Yang,Pengbin Feng,Jiarong Xing,Wentao Guo,Yuhang Yao,Yuhang Han,Hanchen Li,Xu Wang,Zeyu Wang,Jie Xiao,Anjie Yang,Liang Tian,Lynn Ai,Eric Yang,Tianyu Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TwinRouterBench introduces a two-track, step-level router benchmark for long-horizon LLM workflows, enabling offline deterministic scoring and end-to-end live validation of routing strategies across multiple datasets; code is open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon LLM applications accrue high costs when routing each call to the cheapest sufficient model. Existing benchmarks focus on single-shot prompts and do not expose intermediate, router-visible prefixes or test downstream task success when substituting cheaper models, nor rely on online evaluator judges. A step-level, multi-track benchmark is needed to evaluate cost-efficiency without sacrificing task success in realistic agent trajectories.

Method: Static track: 970 router-visible prefixes from 520 instances across SWE-bench, BFCL, mtRAG, QMSum, PinchBench; each paired with an execution-verified target tier via a downgrade-and-cascade protocol; scoring is deterministic arithmetic over tier labels, trajectory membership, and token costs (no online LLM judge). Dynamic track: a harness that runs routers on the full 500-case SWE-bench Verified suite; this paper reports a 100-case held-out evaluation; at each LLM call the router selects a specific model from a locked pool; success is measured by official task resolution and realized API spend. Two tracks enable fast offline iteration and end-to-end live validation; code and data at GitHub.

Result: The abstract presents the benchmark design and an initial held-out evaluation for the dynamic track (100 cases) with offline deterministic scoring in the static track, but does not report numerical results. The contribution is the benchmark itself, plus an experiment setup enabling reproducible evaluation.

Conclusion: TwinRouterBench provides a reproducible, two-track framework to evaluate step-level routing decisions in long-horizon LLM workflows, facilitating cost-aware model selection while ensuring task success; the authors provide public code/data for implementation and benchmarking.

Abstract: LLM routing matters most in long-horizon applications such as coding agents, deep research systems, and computer-use agents, where a single user request triggers many model calls. Routing each call to the cheapest sufficient model can cut costs without sacrificing quality, yet existing router benchmarks evaluate routers only on one-shot prompts. They never expose the router-visible prefix at an intermediate agent step, never test whether a cheaper replacement preserves downstream task success, and often rely on online LLM judges at evaluation time. We introduce TwinRouterBench, a step-level routing benchmark with two tracks. The static track provides 970 router-visible prefixes from 520 instances across SWE-bench, BFCL, mtRAG, QMSum, and PinchBench, each paired with an execution-verified target tier estimated under a released downgrade-and-cascade protocol; scoring is deterministic arithmetic over tier labels, trajectory membership, and token costs, with no online evaluator-side LLM judge. The dynamic track supplies a harness that runs routers on the full 500-case SWE-bench Verified suite; in this paper we report a 100-case held-out evaluation disjoint from the static SWE supervision split. At each LLM call the router selects a concrete model from a locked pool, and success is measured by official task resolution and realized API spend. The two tracks support fast offline iteration followed by end-to-end validation under live agent execution. Code and data are available at https://github.com/CommonstackAI/TwinRouterBench.

</details>


### [293] [From Llama to Cria: Scaling Down Neural Networks via Neuron-Level Spectral Structural Importance Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18860)
*Yongyu Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a graph-signal-processing-based neuron pruning framework that evaluates spectral structural importance of neurons, prunes low-importance neurons iteratively, and uses a final recovery fine-tuning to preserve performance; achieves compact networks on CIFAR-10 and SST-2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce neural network size with principled pruning that respects the internal topological structure among neurons, leveraging graph signal processing to quantify neuron importance across layer transformations.

Method: During inference, record hidden states and model neurons as graph nodes with hidden states as graph signals. Construct layer-wise input and output graphs to capture neuron relationships before/after each layer. Assess spectral structural importance via transformations between these graphs using spectral graph theory. Prune low-importance neurons iteratively until target compression; perform a final recovery fine-tuning instead of fine-tuning after every pruning step.

Result: Experiments on CIFAR-10 and SST-2 show effective pruning of low-importance neurons, yielding compact networks with competitive performance after the recovery fine-tuning stage.

Conclusion: A principled, graph-signal-processing-based spectral structural analysis provides an effective framework for neuron pruning that preserves task performance while reducing model size, validated by empirical results.

Abstract: This paper proposes a neuron pruning framework based on neuron-level spectral structural importance evaluation. Given a trained neural network, we record the hidden states of each hidden layer during inference and model neurons as graph nodes, with hidden states treated as graph signals. Using ideas from graph signal processing, we infer layer-wise input and output graphs that characterize the structural relationships among neurons before and after each layer transformation. We then evaluate the spectral structural importance of neurons by analyzing the transformation between these graphs based on spectral graph theory. Neurons with high spectral structural importance are regarded as strongly involved in the internal representation transformation and are therefore preserved, while neurons with low importance scores are selected as pruning candidates. The pruning process is conducted iteratively until a predefined effective parameter reduction target is reached. Instead of fine-tuning after every pruning step, the proposed strategy first removes low-importance neurons to obtain a compact architecture and then applies a final recovery fine-tuning stage to restore task performance. By connecting neuron pruning with graph signal processing and spectral structural analysis, the proposed framework offers a principled way to reduce neural network size while maintaining solution quality. Experimental results on CIFAR-10 image classification and SST-2 sentiment classification show that our method can effectively remove low-importance neurons and achieve compact networks with competitive performance after recovery fine-tuning.

</details>


### [294] [Towards Family-Grouped Hierarchical Federated Learning on Sub-5KB Models: A Feasibility Study of Privacy-Preserving ECG Monitoring for Ultra-Resource-Constrained Wearables](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18862)
*Hangyu Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Three-tier Family-Grouped Hierarchical Federated Learning (Family-FL) enables privacy-preserving, low-communication FL for ultra-resource-constrained wearables by intra-family aggregation before a global sync, paired with a tiny INT8-quantized CNN-LSTM (~669 params) suitable for microcontrollers. Evaluation on MIT-BIH shows substantial communication savings (76.7% vs FedAvg; 0.31% of FedAvg) with competitive accuracy (≈92%; macro-F1 ≈0.48) and reliable ventricular arrhythmia detection (F1 ≈0.80) but remains simulation-based with limited dataset (47 subjects), no hardware deployment, reduced rare-class sensitivity, and no differential privacy guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The work addresses privacy, communication efficiency, and resource constraints in continuous ECG monitoring on wearables. FL is appealing for privacy, but standard FL incurs high communication; microcontrollers require compact models. The approach combines a hierarchical FL structure with a tiny, quantized model to meet device constraints while preserving privacy boundaries at the family level.

Method: Proposes a three-tier architecture: intra-family aggregation (privacy boundary) followed by global synchronization (FedAvg-like); designs a hardware-constrained Tiny CNN-LSTM with 669 parameters, INT8 quantized to fit STC32G12K128-class MCUs (~4.65KB Flash, ~2.95KB RAM). Evaluates on MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database with multiple seeds; measures accuracy, macro-F1, per-class F1 for ventricular arrhythmia; compares communication volume to FedAvg.

Result: Family-FL reduces communication volume by 76.7% relative to FedAvg while maintaining comparable accuracy. Family-FL-Tiny achieves 91.9% ±1.2% accuracy with macro-F1 0.483 ±0.031; total communication is 0.31% of FedAvg. Per-class F1 for ventricular arrhythmia around 0.80, indicating reliable detection of the critical class.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the technical feasibility of privacy-preserving FL on ultra-resource-constrained microcontrollers via simulation. Limitations include no hardware deployment, validation on a single dataset (MIT-BIH, 47 subjects), reduced sensitivity for rare classes, and lack of formal differential privacy guarantees; future work should address hardware experiments, broader datasets, and DP/privacy guarantees.

Abstract: Cardiovascular disease remains the leading cause of death worldwide, and early detection of arrhythmias through continuous ECG monitoring on wearable devices can prevent life-threatening events. Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training by keeping raw ECG data on device, yet standard FL incurs prohibitive communication overhead and standard deep learning models cannot fit on ultra-low-power microcontrollers. We propose Family-Grouped Hierarchical Federated Learning (Family-FL), a three-tier architecture that uses the family as a natural privacy boundary for intra-family aggregation before global synchronization. We further design a hardware-constrained Tiny CNN-LSTM architecture with only 669 parameters, INT8-quantized to occupy merely 4.65KB Flash and 2.95KB RAM, meeting the constraints of STC32G12K128-class microcontrollers. Experiments on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database (mean of 5 independent runs with different seeds) demonstrate that Family-FL reduces communication volume by 76.7% compared to FedAvg while maintaining comparable accuracy. Family-FL-Tiny achieves 91.9 +/- 1.2% accuracy with macro-F1 of 0.483 +/- 0.031, reducing total communication to 0.31% of FedAvg. The model achieves reliable ventricular arrhythmia detection (per-class F1 = 0.80), the most clinically critical abnormality for home-based preliminary screening. These results demonstrate the technical feasibility of privacy-preserving federated learning on ultra-resource-constrained microcontrollers through simulation-based evaluation. We honestly discuss limitations: no hardware deployment, single-dataset validation (MIT-BIH, 47 subjects), reduced rare-class sensitivity, and absence of formal differential privacy guarantees.

</details>


### [295] [SAGE: Shaping Anchors for Guided Exploration in RLVR of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18864)
*Chanuk Lee,Minki Kang,Sung Ju Hwang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reverse-KL regularization in RLVR stabilizes training but constrains exploration, anchoring policies to the reference distribution. SAGE reshapes the reverse-KL anchor with a guide q(x,y) to expand empirical support, achieving gains in pass@1 and pass@k on challenging math reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether RLVR yields genuine new reasoning abilities or merely improves sampling efficiency; prior work attributes limited gains to structural aspects of RLVR objectives, particularly reverse-KL that constrains exploration and limits alternative reasoning modes.

Method: Introduce SAGE, a framework that reshapes the reverse-KL anchor distribution through a guide function q(x,y), enabling controllable expansion of empirical support without abandoning stability or triggering reward hacking; avoids simply removing or replacing KL.

Result: Empirical results show consistent improvements in both pass@1 and pass@k on difficult mathematical reasoning benchmarks; code for SAGE is provided.

Conclusion: SAGE provides a principled mechanism to expand empirical coverage by shaping the anchor distribution, enabling the discovery of broader reasoning strategies while maintaining training stability; it addresses the efficiency-coverage trade-off in RLVR for serious reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Recent studies observe that reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) reliably improves pass@1 on reasoning tasks, yet often fails to yield comparable gains in pass@k, raising the question of whether RLVR genuinely enables large language models to acquire novel reasoning abilities or merely enhances the efficiency of sampling reasoning modes already present in the base model. Prior analyses largely support the latter view, attributing this limitation to structural properties of standard RLVR objectives that result in insufficient exploration pressure. In this work, we argue that a central structural constraint arises from reverse-KL regularization, which stabilizes training but inherently anchors the policy to the reference distribution, thereby suppressing the emergence of alternative reasoning modes. However, we show that neither removing the KL term nor replacing it with forward-KL provides a satisfactory solution, as both disrupt the efficiency-coverage trade-off by either inducing reward hacking or allocating probability mass to off-target regions. To resolve this tension, we propose SAGE, a principled framework that enables controllable empirical support expansion by reshaping the reverse-KL anchor distribution itself through a guide function q(x,y), achieving consistent improvements in both pass@1 and pass@k across challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks. Our code is available at https://github.com/tally0818/SAGE.

</details>


### [296] [From Sparsity to Simplicity: Enabling Simpler Sequential Replacements via Sparse Attention Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18865)
*Yuxin Ren,Maxwell D Collins,Miao Hu,Huanrui Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sparsity-aware, layer-wise distillation enables efficient replacement of attention in pretrained ViTs by substituting only certain layers with sequential modules guided by attention sparsity, achieving speedups with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quadratic token interactions in self-attention are expensive; naive replacement with sequential modules hurts performance, suggesting a need to explore selective, sparsity-driven replacement within pretrained transformers.

Method: A plug-and-play layer-wise distillation framework is used to approximate and replace attention in pretrained ViTs. The authors test controlled group-wise replacements under a fixed budget. They impose attention sparsity via AViT-style token retention and perform sparsity-guided distillation for sequential replacing models.

Result: Dense-to-sequential replacements incur larger accuracy losses; substituting sparser layers incurs far smaller drops. Increasing teacher sparsity consistently reduces the student–teacher gap. The method yields reduced parameter size and latency while maintaining accuracy due to sparsity-guided guidance.

Conclusion: Attention sparsity-aware replacement is a viable path to efficient attention substitution in vision transformers, enabling reduced compute with controlled accuracy loss through selective layer replacement guided by sparsity.

Abstract: Self-attention serves as the core foundation of large-scale transformer pretraining, but its quadratic token interaction cost makes inference expensive. Replacing attention with simpler sequential modules is appealing, yet naive substitution is often lossy, especially at larger scales. This paper revisits attention replacement through the lens of sparsity. Based on the observation of diverse sparsity patterns across transformer layers, we posit that pretrained transformers decompose the complex token dependency across tokens into various sequence-to-sequence mappings of diverse complexities, where some layer functionalities can be approximated and replaced with much simpler sequential modules without loss. We evaluate this premise using a plug-and-play layer-wise distillation framework to approximate and replace attention functionalities in pretrained vision transformer models. Controlled group-wise replacements under a fixed training budget reveal a clear pattern: substituting layers with sparser attention incurs substantially smaller accuracy drops than replacing denser ones. We further impose explicit attention sparsity on the pretrained ViT via AViT-style token retention and perform sparsity-guided distillation for sequential replacing models, where we see increasing teacher sparsity consistently reduces the student-teacher gap. The proposed method achieves efficient attention replacement for reduced parameter size and latency through the guidance of attention sparsity.

</details>


### [297] [FLUIDSPLAT: Reconstructing Physical Fields from Sparse Sensors via Gaussian Primitives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18866)
*Huaxi Huang,Meng Li,Zhengqing Gao,Xi Zhou,Xiaoshui Huang,Xiao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces FLUIDSPLAT, a sensor-conditioned model that reconstructs continuous flow fields from sparse surface sensors using K anisotropic Gaussian primitives forming a partition-of-unity scaffold. The framework provides a theoretical rate O(K^{-s/d}) for ideal estimators under Sobolev smoothness s, yields a bias-variance tradeoff with optimal K*, and adds a residual decoder to alleviate variance bottlenecks. Empirically, it achieves state-of-the-art results on cylinder-flow benchmarks and shows 11–23% improvements over strong baselines on AirfRANS with 8 sensors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve interpretability and sample efficiency in reconstructing continuous flow fields from sparse surface data, addressing the lack of spatially explicit representations and formal guidance on how representation capacity should scale with observations.

Method: Model constructs K anisotropic Gaussian primitives that form a partition-of-unity scaffold to represent the flow field in a spatially explicit manner. The paper analyzes an idealized Gaussian primitive estimator, deriving an approximation rate O(K^{-s/d}) for Sobolev-s smooth fields and a squared-risk decomposition with bias O(K^{-2s/d}) and variance O(σ^{2}K/N) when N noisy observations are available. The optimal primitive count is K* ~ (N/σ^{2})^{d/(2s+d)}, revealing a variance bottleneck for sparse sensing. To mitigate this, FLUIDSPLAT pairs the scaffold with a state-conditioned residual decoder. Evaluation on a standard cylinder-flow benchmark and AirfRANS with 8 surface-pressure sensors demonstrates strong performance.

Result: The model achieves the best mean error across surface-sensor layouts on cylinder-flow benchmarks and reduces error by 11–23% over the strongest baseline on AirfRANS with 8 sensors across three splits.

Conclusion: FLUIDSPLAT provides an interpretable, sensor-conditioned primitive-based representation with a theoretically grounded scaling relationship between observation count, noise, and primitive count. It delivers strong empirical performance but exposes a variance bottleneck that motivates a residual decoding augmentation; the approach harmonizes interpretability with accuracy for sparse-sensor flow reconstruction.

Abstract: Reconstructing continuous flow fields from sparse surface-mounted sensors is central to aerodynamic design, flow control, and digital-twin instrumentation. Existing neural methods for this task typically encode sensor readings into implicit latent codes with little spatial interpretability and limited formal guidance on how representational capacity should scale with observation count. Inspired by 3D Gaussian Splatting, we introduce FLUIDSPLAT, a sensor-conditioned model that predicts K anisotropic Gaussian primitives forming a partition-of-unity scaffold, a spatially explicit and interpretable intermediate representation of the flow. For an idealized Gaussian primitive estimator, we prove an $O(K^{-s/d})$ approximation rate for fields with Sobolev smoothness $s$; incorporating $N$ noisy observations yields a squared-risk decomposition with bias $O(K^{-2s/d})$ and variance $O(σ^{2}K/N)$.Balancing the two yields $K^{*}\!\sim\!(N/σ^{2})^{d/(2s+d)}$: primitive count cannot grow freely under sparse sensing, revealing a variance bottleneck that motivates complementing the scaffold with a state-conditioned residual decoder. On a standard cylinder-flow benchmark, FLUIDSPLAT achieves the best mean error across all surface-sensor layouts; on AirfRANS with 8 surface-pressure sensors, it reduces error by 11-23% over the strongest baseline across three standard splits.

</details>


### [298] [EVA-0: Test-Time Model Evolution with Only Two Forward Passes per Sample](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18867)
*Guohao Chen,Shuaicheng Niu,Geng Li,Yunbei Zhang,Shilin Shan,Chunyan Miao,Jianfei Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EVA-0: a BP-free zeroth-order test-time adaptation framework for edge-efficient model evolution with a strict two-forward budget, addressing shortcuts, weight drift, and direction estimation; achieves 14x faster adaptation than FOA on ImageNet-C/ViT-Base.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for efficient test-time adaptation without backpropagation to enable deployment on edge devices and quantized models. Zeroth-order methods face issues such as shortcut solutions, uncontrolled weight drift, and poor update-direction estimation.

Method: EVA-0 introduces three components: (1) loss scale-invariance to prevent shortcut solutions; (2) anchor-guided optimization to constrain weight drift; (3) sample-wise symmetric two-sided perturbation to estimate update direction. The framework uses only two forward passes per sample, enabling inference and adaptation without backpropagation.

Result: On ImageNet-C with ViT-Base, EVA-0 outperforms BP-based DeYO and BP-free FOA, while delivering a 14× speed-up over FOA.

Conclusion: A minimal zeroth-order adaptation framework with strict two-forward-budget is effective for test-time model evolution, offering competitive performance and substantial speed-ups over previous BP-free methods.

Abstract: Test-time model evolution offers a promising way for deployed models to improve from unlabeled test-time experience, yet most existing methods depend on backpropagation (BP), which incurs substantial memory overhead and makes them difficult to deploy on edge devices, quantized models, specialized accelerators, or black-box models. In this work, we study test-time model evolution under a strict two-forward budget, a setting that pushes adaptation toward highly efficient real-world deployment. We reveal three key obstacles in zeroth-order test-time optimization: susceptibility to shortcut solutions, uncontrolled weight drift, and ineffective update direction estimation. To overcome them, we propose EVA-0, a minimal zeroth-order adaptation framework that: 1) keeps the loss scale-invariant to prevent shortcut solutions; 2) devises an anchor-guided optimization strategy to alleviate weight drift; 3) uses sample-wise symmetric two-sided perturbation for update direction estimation and inference. EVA-0 requires no BP and performs both inference and adaptation within only two forward passes per sample. Results on ImageNet-C & ViT-Base show that EVA-0 outperforms both BP-based DeYO and BP-free FOA, while achieving a 14x speed-up over FOA. Code will be released.

</details>


### [299] [MO-CAPO: Multi-Objective Cost-Aware Prompt Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18869)
*Jan Büssing,Moritz Schlager,Timo Heiß,Tom Zehle,Matthias Feurer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MO-CAPO is a multi-objective prompt optimization algorithm that jointly optimizes LLM performance and inference cost using budget-aware optimization, yielding diverse, cost-efficient Pareto fronts that outperform NSGA-II baselines and complement single-objective optimizers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are highly sensitive to prompt design and existing multi-objective approaches either optimize solely for performance or rely on inefficient NSGA-II-based methods. There is a need for an efficient, deployment-aware, multi-objective prompt optimizer.

Method: Propose MO-CAPO, a multi-objective optimization algorithm that (i) jointly optimizes performance and inference cost; (ii) uses budget allocation to improve cost efficiency; (iii) defines a deployment-oriented cost objective capturing the full LLM inference profile; (iv) evaluates on four tasks and three LLMs; (v) benchmarks against an NSGA-II-based MO method and state-of-the-art single-objective optimizers; (vi) uses noisy R2 and approximation gap to assess generalization and robustness.

Result: MO-CAPO consistently yields strong, robust, and diverse Pareto front approximations and is cost-efficient. It outperforms the NSGA-II baseline on 8 of 12 cases in noisy R2 and achieves competitive performance often at a substantially lower budget. The solution sets span diverse performance-cost trade-offs not captured by single-objective optimizers, while top-performing prompts remain competitive with single-objective solutions. The paper also introduces evaluation of generalization/robustness via noisy R2 and approximation gap.

Conclusion: MO-CAPO enables practitioners to select from an efficiently discovered set of prompts with different performance-cost trade-offs, offering robust Pareto fronts and efficient deployment-oriented cost modeling for multi-objective prompt optimization.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across a wide range of tasks but are highly sensitive to prompt design, motivating the need for automatic prompt optimization. Existing methods predominantly focus on performance alone, ignoring competing objectives such as inference cost or latency. At the same time, existing work on multi-objective prompt optimization relies on off-the-shelf NSGA-II, ignoring optimization efficiency. As a remedy, we introduce MO-CAPO, a novel multi-objective prompt optimization algorithm that jointly optimizes performance and inference cost while leveraging budget allocation for cost-efficient optimization. We further propose a deployment-oriented cost objective that captures the full computational profile of LLM inference. We evaluate our approach across four tasks and three LLMs and compare it to an NSGA-II-based multi-objective method and state-of-the-art single-objective prompt optimizers. Results show that MO-CAPO consistently identifies strong, robust, and diverse Pareto front approximations while maintaining cost-efficiency. It outperforms the NSGA-II baseline on 8 out of 12 cases in terms of the noisy R2 metric and achieves competitive performances often already at a considerably lower budget. The discovered solution sets span diverse performance-cost trade-offs that are omitted by single-objective optimizers, yet the top-performance candidates remain competitive with single-objective solutions. Additionally, we conduct the first evaluation of multi-objective machine learning experiments that considers generalization and robustness through noisy R2 and approximation gap, enabling a more realistic assessment of solution quality. MO-CAPO enables practitioners to select from an efficiently discovered set of multiple prompts offering different trade-offs between performance and cost.

</details>


### [300] [Multi-Headed Transformer Architectures as Time-dependent Wasserstein Gradient Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18870)
*Alex Massucco,Leonardo Del Grande,Marcello Carioni,Christoff Brune,Carola-Bibiane Schönlieb*

Main category: cs.LG
