TL;DR: This work reframes multi-head transformer data flow as time-dependent gradient flows of an attention-based interaction energy, proving stability and convergence results (ω-limit stationary points, Γ-convergence) and validating with experiments in both autonomous-like and non-autonomous regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between theoretical gradient-flow models and practical transformer architectures by incorporating time-varying weights and an interaction-energy formulation of attention.

Method: Model data flow as time-dependent gradient flows of an interaction energy; allow head- and layer-specific time-varying weights with no initialization constraints; analyze ω-limit points under a weight-integrability assumption; study stability under perturbations and Γ-convergence; perform numerical experiments to verify energy-dissipation and regime behavior.

Result: Under suitable integrability assumptions, every ω-limit point is a stationary point of the limiting interaction energy; gradient flows depend continuously on initial data and are unique; perturbed energies Γ-converge to the unperturbed energy, yielding convergence of the corresponding gradient flows; numerical experiments confirm energy-dissipation and clarify asymptotics in autonomous-like and non-autonomous regimes.

Conclusion: The time-dependent gradient-flow framework provides rigorous insights into the stability and convergence of transformer-like dynamics with non-autonomous weight schemes, aligning with observed energy-dissipation and offering a principled tool for understanding and potentially designing attention-driven architectures.

Abstract: In recent years, transformer architectures have revolutionized the field of language processing, opening the door to previously unforeseen possibilities. However, from a theoretical point of view, the mathematical models proposed in the literature often lack direct contact with the actual architectures and depend on strong simplifying assumptions. In this paper, we reduce this gap by modelling the data flow in multi-headed transformer architectures as time-dependent gradient flows for a suitable interaction energy capturing the design of the attention mechanism. The explicit dependence on time allows us to consider different weights for each head and for each layer, without imposing constraints on the initialization method. Moreover, we prove that, under a suitable integrability assumption on the evolution of the weights, each element of the $ω$-limit set of the gradient flows is a stationary point of the interaction energy at a limiting weight distribution. Finally, we analyse the stability of the gradient flows considering perturbations of both the initial data and the weights. Specifically, on the one hand, we study the robustness of the proposed models with respect to noisy inputs, establishing a continuous dependence of the gradient flows on the initial data and uniqueness of the flows. On the other hand, we prove the $Γ$-convergence of the perturbed interaction energy to the unperturbed one, leading to the convergence of the corresponding gradient flows. We complement these theoretical results with numerical experiments that confirm the predicted energy-dissipation identity and clarify the asymptotic behavior of the dynamics in both the autonomous-like (Ornstein--Uhlenbeck) and the genuinely non-autonomous (oscillating-weights) regimes.

</details>


### [301] [ZeroUnlearn: Few-Shot Knowledge Unlearning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18879)
*Yujie Lin,Chengyi Yang,Zhishang Xiang,Yiping Song,Jinsong Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ZeroUnlearn: a few-shot, model-editing approach to unlearn sensitive inputs by re-mapping them to a neutral state and enforcing representational orthogonality via a multiplicative update with a closed-form solution; extended to a gradient-based variant for multi-sample unlearning, achieving better baselines while preserving utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address privacy and safety concerns from training on large web corpora, where models retain sensitive information. Traditional unlearning via retraining or fine-tuning is expensive or harms model utility; a targeted, efficient method is needed.

Method: Formulates unlearning as a knowledge re-mapping problem. ZeroUnlearn overwrites sensitive inputs by mapping them to a neutral target and removes their original representations. It enforces representational orthogonality through a multiplicative parameter update with a closed-form solution. A gradient-based variant extends the approach to multiple samples.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows ZeroUnlearn outperforms existing baselines in achieving unlearning while better preserving general model utility.

Conclusion: ZeroUnlearn provides an efficient, targeted unlearning solution via model editing, with a closed-form multiplicative update (and gradient-based extension) enabling scalable handling of sensitive inputs without large retraining.

Abstract: Large language models inevitably retain sensitive information, defined as inputs that may induce harmful generations, due to training on massive web corpora, raising concerns for privacy and safety. Existing machine unlearning methods primarily rely on retraining or aggressive fine-tuning, which are either computationally expensive or prone to degrading related knowledge and overall model utility. In this work, we reformulate machine unlearning as a precise knowledge re-mapping problem via model editing. We propose ZeroUnlearn, a few-shot unlearning framework. It overwrites sensitive inputs by mapping them to a neutral target state and removing their original representations. ZeroUnlearn enforces representational orthogonality through a multiplicative parameter update with a closed-form solution, enabling efficient and targeted unlearning. We further extend ZeroUnlearn to a gradient-based variant for multi-sample unlearning. Experiments demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing baselines while preserving general model utility. Our code is available at the github: https://github.com/XMUDeepLIT/ZeroUnlearn.

</details>


### [302] [A Multi-Dimensional Clustering Approach for Identifying Inborn Errors of Immunity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18880)
*Nishad Kulkarni,Alexandra K. Martinson,Nicholas L. Rider,Michael Keller,Syed Muhammad Anwar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes ML-based pipeline to curate EHR data and cluster IEI patterns from a national registry to extract IEI-associated features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rare diseases like IEI suffer from delayed diagnosis due to data access barriers and limited ML methods; need systematic processing of complex medical data for pattern discovery.

Method: Data curation and formatting of EHR immunologic lab data into vectors; unsupervised clustering with hyperparameter tuning; development of toolkits for rare disease analysis; use of a national registry.

Result: Conceptual pipeline; no reported empirical results; aims to transform records into ML-interpretable vectors and enable disease pattern recognition.

Conclusion: The approach refines IEI feature awareness and expands data transformation capabilities for unsupervised ML in rare diseases.

Abstract: Rare diseases such as inborn errors of immunity (IEI) require early diagnosis to prevent end organ damage and improve quality of life. Hurdles in accessing and curating large scale electronic health record (EHR) data limit routine data driven analyses to remain on the forefront of IEI and other rare disease trends. Development of machine learning (ML) algorithms in IEI for pattern recognition as well as published methodology examining how to systematically process and integrate complex medical data is limited. Our proposed pipeline, including data curation and ML clustering algorithms, is designed to recognize novel rare disease patterns and extract IEI- associated features from a national data registry. Our methodology for EHR data formatting and processing presents the pipeline that transforms raw immunologic lab data into vectors. This is further combined with hyperparameter tuning for diseases pattern recognition via clustering. This study refines IEI feature awareness, develops data tool kits for rare disease populations analysis, and expands on transforming complex medical records in data structures interpretable by unsupervised ML.

</details>


### [303] [Emergence of a Flow-Assisted Casting Strategy for Olfactory Navigation via Memory-Augmented Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18881)
*Changxu Zhao,Dongxiao Zhao,Xin Bian,Gaojin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL agents in unsteady flows learn a flow-assisted casting strategy; memory length non-monotically affects search efficiency; optimal detection integration window aligns with a sector-search model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain how stochastic detections in dynamic flow fields enable efficient odor search and how memory length interacts with flow dynamics; connect RL-derived strategies to biological casting behavior.

Method: Train RL agents in simulated unsteady flows with varying memory lengths; no predefined navigation model; analyze trajectory geometry, casting threshold, success rate; compare to sector-search predictions.

Result: Agents develop flow-assisted, adaptive search trajectories and plastic casting thresholds; average speed toward source exhibits non-monotonic dependence on memory length; sector-search explains the non-monotonic behavior.

Conclusion: Memory and flow conditions shape search strategies; the sector-search framework provides mechanistic insight into the observed optimal integration window and could inform design of odor-tracking algorithms.

Abstract: In dynamic flow fields, various animals exhibit remarkable odor search capabilities despite relying on stochastic detections. Interestingly, there exists an optimal time window for integrating these detections that maximizes search efficiency. To understand the underlying mechanism, we investigate the navigation performance of Reinforcement Learning (RL) agents in unsteady flows under varying memory lengths and flow conditions. Without any predefined models, the agents develop a flow-assisted casting strategy and adaptively adjust both the geometry of their search trajectories and the concentration threshold for initiating casting to maximize the success rate. The agent's average speed toward the odor source exhibits a non-monotonic dependence on memory length, which can be explained by the "sector-search" model.

</details>


### [304] [To Call or Not to Call: Diagnosing Intrinsic Over-Calling Bias in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18882)
*Wei Shi,Ziheng Peng,Sihang Li,Xiting Wang,Xiang Wang,Mengnan Du,Na Zou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Intrinsic bias in LLM call/no-call decisions causes over-calling; Sparse Autoencoders reveal decision-aligned features; Adaptive Margin-Calibrated Steering cancels an activation-independent offset, improving overall accuracy with negligible loss in call accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain and mitigate persistent over-calling in LLM agents by proposing a mechanistic, causal account (Intrinsic Bias Hypothesis) and a corrective method (Adaptive Margin-Calibrated Steering).

Method: Train Sparse Autoencoders to learn feature bases for the call/no_call decision; reduce them to a signed activation margin; estimate an activation-independent offset; causally test IBH; apply AMCS along SAE decoder directions to cancel offset; evaluate on When2Call across six models from three families.

Result: IBH: model is decision-neutral only when no_call activation outweighs call activation; offset cancellation reduces over-calling, improving overall accuracy with negligible drop in call accuracy; performance moved into a higher overall accuracy band (55–70%).

Conclusion: Over-calling is a mechanistic bias that can be causally corrected; the approach yields a replicable, law-like correction and code is provided for implementation.

Abstract: LLM agents exhibit a consistent tendency to over-call, invoking tools even in situations where none is needed. On the When2Call benchmark, six models from three families show high call accuracy but much lower no-call accuracy, leaving overall accuracy in the 55%-70% range. We trace this to an Intrinsic Bias Hypothesis (IBH): the call/no-call decision mapping carries an activation-independent call offset, so the model favors call even at activation parity. Using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs), we recover behavior-aligned feature bases for the call/no_call decision, reduce them to a signed activation margin, and estimate the offset directly. Across all six models, the model is decision-neutral only when no_call activation outweighs call activation, consistent with IBH. We then causally test IBH with Adaptive Margin-Calibrated Steering (AMCS), a closed-form counter-bias shift along SAE decoder directions. Cancelling the diagnosed offset mitigates over-calling and improves overall accuracy with a negligible drop in call accuracy. Our work recasts over-calling from an empirical phenomenon into a mechanistic object amenable to causal correction. Code is available at https://github.com/SKURA502/agent-sae/.

</details>


### [305] [Prediction Is Not Physics: Learning and Evaluating Conserved Quantities in Neural Simulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18883)
*Andrew Bukowski,Aditya Kothari,Simba Shi,Ishir Rao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion-based Hamiltonian rollouts struggle to conserve energy; the study tests learning globally conserved quantities from trajectories using a structured energy model and Conservation Discovery Networks (CDN) acrossProjectile, pendulum, and spring-mass systems, with mixed success depending on temporal consistency, alignment losses, noise, and training duration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether neural networks can learn or select globally conserved quantities (like energy) from physical trajectories to enforce conservation laws in learned dynamics.

Method: Evaluate three Hamiltonian systems (projectile, pendulum, spring-mass) using: a structured T(v)+V(q) energy model, a black-box CDN, a polynomial CDN, and a conditional diffusion baseline; measure energy conservation via R^2 against analytical energy; study effects of temporal consistency, energy alignment loss (λ_align), and additive Gaussian noise; compare performance across systems and training regimens.

Result: Structured energy model achieves R^2 ≥ 0.9999 with clean data. CDN attains R^2 ≥ 0.996 when trained with temporal consistency plus λ_align=0.2; with λ_align=0 the CDN collapses to R^2 < 1e-3 on pendulum and spring-mass, showing temporal consistency alone is insufficient. Under 1% Gaussian noise, CDN outperforms the structured model on projectile and spring-mass, suggesting CDN can be more robust to noise in this setting. Polynomial CDN is highly sensitive to training configuration: ~R^2=0.78 with a short training schedule on pendulum, but R^2=0.9998 with longer training and more data, regardless of noise presence.

Conclusion: Temporal consistency alone is not enough to reliably identify the true energy. Incorporating inductive bias via a structured energy model or using alignment losses improves conservation discovery. CDN can be more robust to noise under suitable training, but its performance is highly dependent on training duration/data and the chosen CDN variant; training regime and inductive bias dominate conservation-learning outcomes.

Abstract: A diffusion model trained on Hamiltonian trajectories can achieve rollout MSE near $10^{-3}$, but the standard deviation of its energy over time is between 7500 and 36000 times larger than the ground-truth energy standard deviation, indicating a failure to preserve conservation laws. This gap motivates our central question of whether neural networks can learn or select globally conserved quantities from physical trajectories. We investigate this across three Hamiltonian systems: projectile motion, pendulum, and spring-mass. We use a structured $T(v)+V(q)$ energy model, a black-box Conservation Discovery Network (CDN), a polynomial CDN, and a conditional diffusion baseline. The structured network reaches $R^2 \geq 0.9999$ against analytical energy on clean data, while the black-box CDN reaches $R^2 \geq 0.996$ when trained with temporal consistency plus a small alignment loss to analytical energy at $t=0$ ($λ_{\mathrm{align}}=0.2$). With $λ_{\mathrm{align}}=0$, CDN Pearson $R^2$ collapses on pendulum and spring-mass ($< 10^{-3}$), showing that temporal consistency alone is not enough to reliably identify the true energy. Under $1\%$ additive Gaussian noise, the CDN outperforms the structured model on the projectile and spring-mass systems, suggesting that the CDN may be more robust to noisy inputs in this setting. However, the polynomial CDN is sensitive to training configuration: it achieves $R^2=0.78$ under a short training schedule on the pendulum system, but reaches $R^2=0.9998$ with more training time and data, regardless of whether noise is added.

</details>


### [306] [Navigating the Emotion Tree: Hierarchical Hyperbolic RAG for Multimodal Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18884)
*Zeheng Wang,Bo Zhao,Yijie Zhu,Zhishu Liu,Hui Ma,Ruixin Zhang,Shouhong Ding,Qianyu Xie,Zitong Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HyperEmo-RAG is a retrieval-augmented multimodal emotion recognition model that leverages hierarchical hyperbolic grounding and structured evidence injection to exploit emotion taxonomies, improving fine-grained recognition over flat-label baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal LLMs treat emotion categories as independent labels and lack external contextual knowledge, leading to misinterpretations of noisy cues and poor fine-grained emotion classification.

Method: 1) Hierarchical hyperbolic grounding: embed hierarchical emotion labels and multimodal samples in hyperbolic space (Poincaré ball) with hierarchical beam-search to retrieve from coarse to fine levels. 2) Structured evidence injection: build an evidence graph from retrieved info and inject knowledge via Tree-Aware Attention and an EmotionGraphFormer to the LLM, preserving graph structure.

Result: Experiments on multiple datasets show significant improvements over existing methods.

Conclusion: HyperEmo-RAG demonstrates effective use of hierarchical emotion knowledge and structured evidence, enhancing fine-grained multimodal emotion recognition and reducing susceptibility to noisy cues.

Abstract: Multimodal emotion recognition aims to integrate text, audio, and video sources to understand human affective states. Although multimodal large language models excel at multimodal reasoning, they typically treat emotion categories as independent labels, ignoring the rich hierarchical taxonomy of human psychology. Moreover, lacking external contextual knowledge makes them highly susceptible to over-interpreting noisy cues, further complicating fine-grained emotion classification. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{HyperEmo-RAG}, a retrieval-augmented generation framework that leverages a structured emotional knowledge base. Our framework introduces two key innovations. 1) Hierarchical hyperbolic grounding. Recognizing the inherent hierarchical tree structure of emotion taxonomies, we jointly embed hierarchical emotion labels and multimodal samples into a continuous hyperbolic space (Poincaré ball) and design a hierarchical beam-search deliberation process that progressively retrieves samples from coarse to fine-grained levels. 2) Structured evidence injection. Based on the retrieved evidence, we construct an evidence graph and inject the structured knowledge as explicit cognitive context into the LLM through a Tree-Aware Attention mechanism and an EmotionGraphFormer, preserving the integrity of graph-structured information. Experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that HyperEmo-RAG significantly outperforms existing methods.

</details>


### [307] [Soft Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18889)
*Mohammed Aledhari,Ali Aledhari,Fatimah Aledhari,Mohamed Rahouti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Soft Learning introduces a framework that pools heterogeneous specialists (linear, tree, kernel, neural) and optimizes their non-negative combination with cross-validated least squares, achieving provable guarantees to match or exceed the best weighted ensemble. It is fast on CPU, interpretable, scalable, and demonstrates strong empirical performance across 37 datasets, often ranking first and outperforming tuned deep nets and CatBoost without GPU or tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The common dilemma in ML is balancing accuracy and computation: deep networks are powerful but expensive; classical models are fast but limited. A principled approach is needed to select and combine diverse algorithms with guarantees, interpretability, and CPU efficiency.

Method: Maintain a library of heterogeneous specialists (linear models, tree ensembles, kernel machines, neural networks). Learn combination weights via cross-validated non-negative least squares to form a Soft Learning ensemble. The method provides provable guarantees that the ensemble performs at least as well as the best convex combination of its specialists. It requires no GPU/hyperparameter tuning and scales to large datasets by leveraging CPU execution.

Result: Empirically effective: across 37 datasets (25 classification, 12 regression) and nine competing methods, Soft Learning ranks first on 70% of tasks and achieves the best mean rank (Friedman test, p = 1.12e-12). It outperforms tuned deep networks and CatBoost, trains 72–435x faster on CPU, and handles both classification and regression well, while providing interpretability through learned weights and enabling incremental improvement by adding new specialists.

Conclusion: Shifts the paradigm from selecting a single “best” algorithm to finding the provably optimal weighted combination of diverse algorithms. The approach provides formal guarantees, interpretable weights, and future-proof scalability by simply adding more specialists, applicable across data modalities without GPUs or tuning.

Abstract: Modern machine learning forces practitioners to choose between powerful but expensive deep networks and fast but limited classical algorithms. Here we introduce Soft Learning, a framework that maintains a library of heterogeneous specialists -- spanning linear models, tree ensembles, kernel machines, and neural networks -- and discovers provably optimal combination weights through cross-validated non-negative least squares. Soft Learning is guaranteed to match or exceed the best weighted combination of its specialists, trains over two orders of magnitude faster than deep networks on CPU alone (72-435x faster across tested configurations), provides inherent interpretability through learned weights that reveal which algorithmic paradigm best fits the data, and is future-proof: adding specialists is mathematically guaranteed to maintain or improve performance. Across 37 datasets (25 classification, 12 regression) against nine methods including CatBoost and tuned deep networks, Soft Learning ranks first on 70% of tasks, achieves the best mean rank (Friedman test, p = 1.12 x 10^-12), and is the only method to simultaneously excel at both classification and regression -- all without GPU hardware or hyperparameter tuning. These results suggest a paradigm shift from "which algorithm is best?" to "what is the provably optimal combination?" -- a question Soft Learning answers with formal guarantees for any data modality.

</details>


### [308] [Auditing Reasoning-Trace Memorization Claims after Unlearning with Head-Conditioned Canaries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18891)
*Yanhang Li,Zhichao Fan,Zexin Zhuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper questions the reliability of bypass gaps as indicators of hidden weight-level memorization in reasoning models during unlearning audits; results show inconsistent effects across seeds and distillates; suggests decode-time template swaps as a cheap sanity check alongside canonical audits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To critically evaluate whether observed bypass gaps truly reveal memorized content stored in weights, rather than artifacts of decoding or prompting, and to propose practical sanity checks for unlearning audits.

Method: Empirical audits on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B with LoRA-memorized fictional authors and NPO unlearning, using a six-token canary head. The study manipulates the model's thinking trace by swapping it with short non-canary prefill prompts across seeds and distillates, and examines how answer rate and parser behavior (e.g., closing-tag detection) respond. It also contrasts results across different distillates to assess robustness.

Result: Findings show that a positive parser-split bypass gap does not uniquely indicate hidden memorization; swapping the thinking trace with prefill can both reduce or increase the answer rate depending on seed, sometimes aligning with the bypass gap, other times reversing it. In another distillate, the metric flips sign due to parsing limitations (closing tag). Collectively, bypass-gap metrics are unreliable alone.

Conclusion: A bypass gap is not a definitive indicator of hidden memorization. A decode-time template swap is recommended as a cheap, complementary sanity check alongside canonical auditing to improve robustness of unlearning evaluations.

Abstract: Evaluations of unlearning on reasoning models sometimes show a bypass pattern. The answer side looks unlearned, but the model's own thinking trace keeps emitting the forgotten content, and the gap is taken as evidence that the weights still remember. We audit this reading on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B with LoRA-memorized fictional authors and NPO unlearning, conditioned on a six-token canary head. On one seed, swapping the thinking trace for a short non-canary prefill on the same weights drops the answer rate by as much as the bypass gap itself, whether the prefill mimics the training template or not. On a second seed the bypass gap shrinks rather than vanishing, and the prefill swap reverses direction and brings the answer rate to ceiling. A positive parser-split bypass gap thus does not by itself identify hidden weight-level memorization, and does not rule it out either. On a different distillate the same metric flips sign because the parser cannot find the closing tag. We recommend a decode-time template swap as a cheap sanity check alongside the canonical audit.

</details>


### [309] [Data-Free Client Contribution Estimation via Logit Maximization for Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18892)
*Asim Ukaye,Nurbek Tastan,Mubarak Abdu-Aguye,Karthik Nandakumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CELM: a data-free, class-wise contribution estimation and aggregation framework for federated learning that mitigates class imbalance by weighting clients based on a cross-client evidence matrix derived from logit maximization, with simplex-constrained, momentum-smoothed aggregation compatible with standard FL workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address severe class imbalance and label skew in federated learning, where standard aggregation overfits dominant clients and hurts minority-class performance, while preserving privacy by avoiding raw data or auxiliary public datasets.

Method: Server-side CELM computes class-wise evidence scores from client updates via logit maximization, builds a cross-client evidence matrix capturing per-class competence and coverage, and derives contribution weights under simplex constraints with momentum smoothing to stabilize aggregation; integrates seamlessly with existing FL training pipelines.

Result: On representative vision benchmarks with controlled non-IID and pathological label splits, CELM-based aggregation improves robustness to imbalance and statistical heterogeneity, yielding better performance without any additional data exchange.

Conclusion: Data-free, class-wise evidence-based aggregation (CELM) is effective for improving minority-class performance and overall robustness in FL, and is compatible with standard training pipelines.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative learning of computer vision models, where privacy and regulatory constraints prevent centralizing data across devices or organizations. However, practical FL deployments often exhibit severe class imbalance and label skew, causing standard aggregation protocols to overfit dominant clients and degrade minority-class performance. We propose a data-free, class-wise contribution estimation and aggregation framework based on logit maximization (CELM) that does not require sharing raw data, client metadata, or auxiliary public datasets. The FL server probes client updates to obtain class-wise evidence scores and assembles a cross-client evidence matrix, which quantifies both per-class competence and class coverage. Using this matrix, we compute contribution weights that upweight clients providing strong, discriminative evidence for underrepresented classes. The resulting aggregation is stable due to simplex constraints and momentum smoothing, and it remains compatible with standard FL training pipelines. We evaluate the approach on representative vision benchmarks under controlled non-IID and pathological label splits, demonstrating that CELM-based aggregation improves robustness to imbalance and statistical heterogeneity, while yielding better performance without requiring any additional data exchange.

</details>


### [310] [Position: Graph Condensation Needs a Reset -- Move Beyond Full-dataset Training and Model-Dependence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18893)
*Mridul Gupta,Samyak Jain,Vansh Ramani,Hariprasad Kodamana,Sayan Ranu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph condensation needs a reset: current gradient-matching and model-dependent approaches are inefficient and poorly generalizable; the field should pursue lightweight, architecture-agnostic, deployable methods with robust evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As real-world graphs grow in size, current condensation methods aim to produce small synthetic graphs but rely on full-dataset training and architecture-specific designs, leading to scalability and generalization issues. The authors argue these systemic flaws hinder progress in the field.

Method: Position paper providing a critical analysis of existing methods, taxonomy of flaws, and concrete research directions—no new empirical experiments.

Result: Identifies core problems (contradictions of gradient matching, high overhead, poor cross-architecture generalization, reliance on misleading evaluations) and proposes a shift toward lightweight, architecture-agnostic, practically deployable condensation approaches with improved evaluation protocols.

Conclusion: To realize the promise of graph condensation at scale, the field should abandon full-dataset, model-specific training and pursue generalizable, efficient methods along with robust, application-relevant evaluation.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are powerful tools for learning from graph-structured data, but their scalability is increasingly strained by the size of real-world graphs in domains like recommender systems, fraud detection, and molecular biology. Graph condensation -- the task of generating a smaller synthetic graph that retains the performance of models trained on the original -- has emerged as a promising solution. However, the dominant approach of gradient matching introduces a fundamental contradiction: it requires training on the full dataset to create the compressed version, thereby undermining the goal of efficiency. Worse still, these methods suffer from high computational overhead, poor generalization across GNN architectures, and brittle reliance on specific model configurations. Equally concerning is the community's reliance on misleading evaluation protocols such as node compression ratios, which fail to reflect true resource savings, condensation overhead, and illusory application to neural architecture search. These shortcomings are not incidental -- they are systemic, and they obstruct meaningful progress.
  In this position paper, we argue that graph condensation, in its current form, needs a reset. We call for moving beyond full-dataset training and model-dependent design, and instead advocate for methods that are lightweight, architecture-agnostic, and practically deployable. By identifying key methodological flaws and outlining concrete research directions, we aim to reorient the field toward approaches that deliver on the true promise of condensation: efficient, generalizable, and usable GNN training at scale.

</details>


### [311] [A Two-Parameter Weibull Framework for Diagnosing Transformer Weight Distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18898)
*Tiexin Ding*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Weibull-based diagnostic for transformer weight magnitudes; k captures functional class, lambda tracks training progress; per-layer/component diagnostics across many models; robust across architectures; release of tooling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide an architecture-independent, component-wise diagnostic for training dynamics using weight-magnitude distributions fitted to a two-parameter Weibull distribution, enabling per-layer/per-component tracking beyond aggregate statistics.

Method: Fit Weibull distribution to absolute weight values |w| at initialization and at subsequent checkpoints. Use middle-80% probability-plot to estimate k (shape parameter). Classify weight matrices into Transmission Class (e.g., FFN output projection W_o) and Selection Class (W_q, W_k). Analyze across 12 models from 7 families and examine training-time evolution of lambda (scale parameter) relative to training budget metrics. Validate relationships with training dynamics and compare across architecture variants.

Result: Three findings: (1) Transmission Class weights cluster in a narrow k band: median terminal k in [1.186, 1.204] across 12 entries; (2) Selection Class weights deviate from Weibull with k ranges that depend on storage strategy: k in [0.76,0.99] for separately stored Q/K, and [1.10,1.16] for merged QKV; Pythia shows transitional behavior linked to training budget; (3) lambda grows during training and scales with sqrt(eta/lambda_wd); Pearson r = 0.94 across three Transmission kinds; k captures functional class while lambda tracks training progress.

Conclusion: The two Weibull parameters carry distinct information: k identifies the functional class (which substructure a weight belongs to), while lambda tracks training progress. The framework enables per-component, per-layer diagnostics and reveals architecture- and training-budget-dependent variations in weight-magnitude distributions. Public tooling (npm-weibull-py v0.4) and DATABASE_v9_1 released.

Abstract: We apply the Weibull distribution -- a two-parameter family from extreme-value theory -- as a diagnostic framework for element-wise weight magnitude distributions in transformers. At initialization, i.i.d. Gaussian weights give |w| ~ HalfNormal, yielding k ~ 1.20 via middle-80% probability-plot fit (the protocol used throughout this work). This anchor makes k a principled, architecture-independent measuring stick for training dynamics; fitting each weight matrix independently at every layer at every checkpoint enables per-component, per-layer, and per-step diagnostics that aggregate statistics cannot resolve.
  Applying this framework to 12 model entries spanning 7 architectural families (Pythia, OLMo-1/2, LLaMA-3, Mistral, Qwen2.5/3) reveals three findings. First, FFN modules and the attention output projection W_o -- the Transmission Class -- fall in a narrow k band: median terminal k in [1.186, 1.204] across 12 entries (cross-family CV = 0.51%), shared across SwiGLU/GeLU activations, Pre-LN/QK-Norm placements, and 70M-14B sizes. Second, the attention input projections W_q, W_k -- the Selection Class -- depart from the Weibull family, with severity shaped by storage: separately-stored Q/K (OLMo-1, OLMo-2) yields k in [0.76, 0.99] (deep); GQA models yield k in [1.10, 1.16] (mild); Pythia's merged W_qkv occupies a transitional zone tracking training budget T/tau monotonically. Third, lambda grows substantially during training and scales with sqrt(eta/lambda_wd) within the Pythia family (Pearson r = 0.94, three Transmission kinds), directionally consistent with Fan et al. (2025). The two parameters carry independent information: k labels the functional class, lambda labels training progress.
  We release npm-weibull-py v0.4 (Python library) and DATABASE_v9_1 at https://github.com/tiexinding/NPM-Weibull-public .

</details>


### [312] [Don't Let Bandit Feedback Pull Continual LLM-Recommender Updates Off Target](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18899)
*Taesan Kim,Hyeongjun Yun,Jaegul Choo,Chung Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Anchored Bandit Policy Optimization (ABPO) for continual LLM-based recommender updates mitigates exposure bias and feedback ambiguity by anchoring prior policy exposure in group-relative policy optimization (GRPO), applying self-normalized inverse propensity scoring to a fixed anchor, and asymmetrically weighting feedback with model-confidence tempering; evaluated across five domains (Amazon Reviews, MovieLens) with consistent post-update accuracy gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deployment logs for recommender systems provide only policy-shaped feedback (exposed items) leading to exposure bias and partial, asymmetric signals. There is a need for continual post-deployment updates that correctly interpret observed feedback and adjust policies without overreacting to ambiguous signals.

Method: ABPO framework: integrate GRPO with an anchored logging strategy. For each rollout group, insert the previously exposed action as a logged anchor to calibrate group-relative normalization against the actual prior-exposed action. Use self-normalized inverse propensity scoring (SNIPS) on the fixed anchor to correct for policy mismatch for both positive and no-response feedback. Treat positive responses as relatively direct endorsements while no-responses remain ambiguous; temper penalties from no-responses using self-certainty, i.e., the model's output-token confidence as a reliability signal.

Result: Empirical evaluation across five domains (Amazon Reviews and MovieLens) shows consistent post-update gains in recommendation accuracy and improved mitigation of prior-policy-induced exposure bias compared to baselines.

Conclusion: ABPO provides a robust framework for continual LLM-based recommender updates under exposure bias and feedback ambiguity by anchoring to prior policy exposure, using SNIPS on fixed anchors, and reliability-aware treatment of feedback; demonstrates generalizable gains across multiple domains.

Abstract: Generative LLM-based recommenders (LLM-Rec) require continual post-deployment updates, yet deployment logs provide only policy-shaped contextual bandit feedback: outcomes are observed solely for items exposed by a prior serving policy, inducing exposure bias and yielding partial, asymmetric signals consisting of relatively reliable positive responses and ambiguous no-responses. We propose an Anchored Bandit Policy Optimization (ABPO) framework for continual LLM-Rec updates that combines group-relative policy optimization (GRPO) with explicit treatment of exposure bias and feedback ambiguity. Specifically, we insert the exposed recommendation as a logged anchor into each GRPO rollout group, so that group-relative normalization is calibrated against the action actually exposed by the prior policy rather than against newly sampled rollouts alone. Because both positive- and no-responses are observed only through prior-policy exposure, we apply self-normalized inverse propensity scoring to the fixed anchor for both feedback types to correct for policy mismatch. At the same time, we treat the two feedback types asymmetrically in reliability: positive responses provide relatively direct endorsement signals, whereas no-responses remain ambiguous because they may reflect either true disinterest or unobserved external factors. To avoid overly aggressive updates from ambiguous no-responses, we temper their penalties with self-certainty, using the model's output-token confidence as a verifier-free reliability signal. Across five domains from Amazon Reviews and MovieLens, our method yields consistent post-update gains in recommendation accuracy while mitigating prior-policy-induced exposure bias more effectively than prior baselines.

</details>


### [313] [Reasoning Portability: Guiding Continual Learning for MLLMs in the RLVR Era](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18903)
*Qiuhe Hong,Yuyang Liu,Shuo Yang,Tiantian Peng,Fei Zhu,Yonghong Tian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Reasoning Portability (RP) and a dynamic balancing method RDB-CL for RLVR in Vision-Language Model continual learning, modulating per-sample KL regularization by RP; achieves +12.0% Last accuracy over vanilla RLVR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VL-CL models struggle with forgetting as they continually adapt to new multimodal tasks. Reasoning-level signals may be more stable than answer-level signals on out-of-distribution samples. The work formalizes portability as a sample-level measure of reuse of prior policy reasoning and leverages it to guide continual adaptation.

Method: Formalize portability and Reasoning Portability (RP); instantiate RP; propose Reasoning-based Dynamic Balance Continual Learning (RDB-CL) that modulates per-sample KL regularization in RLVR according to RP: strong anchors on high-RP samples to preserve reusable reasoning, weaker anchors on low-RP samples to enable exploration of new reasoning paths.

Result: Empirical evidence that RP signals are reliable on OOD samples while answer signals are not; RDB-CL yields consistent improvements over baselines, notably +12.0% Last accuracy over the vanilla RLVR baseline.

Conclusion: Reasoning-level signals offer a robust basis for dynamic balancing in continual learning with RL-based rewards. RP enables selective preservation of useful prior reasoning and exploration of new reasoning on low-RP samples, leading to improved final performance in VLM-CL tasks.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models in Continual Learning (VLM-CL) aim to continuously adapt to new multimodal tasks while retaining prior knowledge. The emerging paradigm that couples Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) with Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) calls for a new pattern to guide continual adaptation. Advances in reasoning capability now make it feasible to impose constraints at the reasoning level. We formalize portability, a sample-level measure of how reusable the previous policy's behavior is on a new task, and empirically show that reasoning-level signals remain reliable on out-of-distribution samples while answer-level signals do not. We instantiate this as Reasoning Portability (RP) and propose Reasoning-based Dynamic Balance Continual Learning (RDB-CL), which modulates the per-sample Kullback-Leibler regularization in RLVR according to RP: a tight anchor preserves reusable reasoning on high-RP samples, while a relaxed anchor on low-RP samples permits exploration of new reasoning pathways. Experiments show that RDB-CL consistently outperforms baselines, improving Last accuracy by +12.0% over the vanilla RLVR baseline.

</details>


### [314] [Dynamic Model Merging Made Slim](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18904)
*Guodong Du,Wanyu Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A slim dynamic merging framework (DiDi-Merging) that uses differentiable rank allocation in low-rank modules to balance shared parameters and task-specific experts, achieving strong accuracy with modest extra storage and data-free refinement across vision, language, and multimodal tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current dynamic merging either keeps a fully shared model with tiny experts or allocates too much capacity to experts, causing poor accuracy/efficiency trade-offs. There is a need for compact, flexible parameter budgeting for multi-task reuse of fine-tuned models.

Method: Formulates parameter budgeting as differentiable rank optimization in low-rank modules; introduces differentiable rank allocation to control the share/expert split; includes a data-free refinement step to recover task fidelity.

Result: Matches prior dynamic baselines at 1.24x the parameters of a single fine-tuned model and surpasses them at 1.4x; substantially more storage-efficient than >2x baselines; demonstrated across vision, language, and multimodal tasks.

Conclusion: DiDi-Merging provides a practical, compact dynamic merging framework that balances shared and expert parameters with differentiable rank budgeting, enabling efficient reuse of fine-tuned models across diverse domains.

Abstract: Model merging enables the reuse of fine-tuned models without joint training or access to original data. Dynamic merging further improves flexibility by selectively activating task-relevant parameters and efficiently composing experts across multiple tasks. However, existing dynamic methods either maintain a full shared model with tiny experts or allocate excessive capacity to experts, leading to suboptimal accuracy--efficiency trade-offs. To address this, we propose DiDi-Merging, a slim dynamic merging framework that leverages differentiable rank allocation to balance shared and expert parameters. By formulating parameter budgeting as differentiable rank optimization in low-rank modules and introducing a data-free refinement step to recover task fidelity, DiDi-Merging matches prior dynamic baselines at only 1.24x the parameters of a single fine-tuned model and surpasses them at 1.4x, substantially more compact than methods requiring > 2x storage. DiDi-Merging applies across vision, language, and multimodal tasks.

</details>


### [315] [Stability and Discretization Error of State Space Model Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18905)
*Abderrahim Bendahi,Adrien Fradin,Johan Peralez,Julie Digne,Madiha Nadri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This work provides discretization-error bounds and stability guarantees for neural operator approximations, deriving and applying a discretization-theoretic framework to SS-NOs and FNOs, and validating the theory with experiments across 1D and 2D benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap between continuous neural operator theory and its discrete numerical implementations. A formal link is needed to quantify how discretization affects accuracy and stability in practice.

Method: Derive analytical bounds that connect solution regularity to input discretization for neural operators. Prove a discretization-error theorem for SS-NOs and FNOs. Conduct an input-to-state stability (ISS) analysis to quantify discretization impact on SS-NO stability. Validate bounds and robustness through experiments on 1D and 2D benchmarks.

Result: Analytical discretization-error bounds are established for SS-NOs and FNOs; ISS analysis quantifies discretization effects on stability; empirical validation confirms the bounds and shows robustness of SS-NOs under varying resolutions.

Conclusion: The work provides theoretical guarantees linking continuous formulations to discrete implementations for neural operators, enabling reliable, discretization-aware design and deployment, supported by empirical evidence of robustness across resolutions.

Abstract: Neural operators have emerged as a powerful, discretization-invariant framework for solving partial differential equations (PDEs). Although established approaches like the Deep Operator Network (DeepONet) have successfully achieved universal approximation for operators, and architectures such as Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs) have shown algebraic convergence rates, a precise theoretical connection between the continuous theory and its discrete numerical implementation remains a challenge. Specifically, the relationship between the continuous formulation and the discrete numerical stability has yet to be fully explored. In this paper, we address this gap by establishing theoretical guarantees for the discretization error and stability of neural operator approximation schemes. We prove analytical bounds that link solution regularity to input discretization, providing a formal quantification of neural operator accuracy under real-world numerical constraints. We derive these bounds to the specific cases of State Space Model-based Neural Operators (SS-NOs) and FNOs, thus providing a new discretization error theorem for these models. Additionally, through an input-to-state stability (ISS) analysis, we formally assess the impact of discretization on the stability of SS-NOs results obtained in the continuous domain. Our empirical experiments on 1D and 2D benchmarks validate our theoretical bounds and show the robustness of SS-NOs under varying resolutions.

</details>


### [316] [Descriptive versus Regulatory Uncertainty in Bounded Predictive Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18909)
*Ahmed Gamal Eldin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Describes a structural distinction between descriptive and regulatory uncertainty, proving current transformers operate under descriptive uncertainty at inference; links uncertainty to energy via Landauer's principle; empirical entropy-accuracy decoupling across 3 models; entropy invariant while task performance varies; advocates physical coupling for genuine epistemic grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify whether uncertainty affects future behavior or merely outputs, and connect computational uncertainty to thermodynamic costs and energy dissipation.

Method: Theoretical formalism distinguishing uncertainty types; formal proof about transformers; thermodynamic argument via Landauer's principle; empirical evaluation on 3 models (3B, 8B, 70B) measuring token-level Shannon entropy across tasks; compare entropy and accuracy; assess scale invariance.

Result: Entropy remains invariant across tasks and models; pairwise p≥0.568; entropy ranges 0.011-0.028 nats; accuracy varies widely (0-100%); entropy and accuracy are orthogonal; larger models achieve higher accuracy but same entropy; inability to resolve uncertainty with more parameters or data; supports need for physical coupling.

Conclusion: Genuine epistemic grounding requires coupling between physical substrate state and information processing cost; current transformers are structurally limited to descriptive uncertainty; regulatory uncertainty would entail energetic costs tied to epistemic errors.

Abstract: Any system that models the world under finite representational capacity must compress; any compression entails a prior; and the prior is the system's bias. What has not been established is whether uncertainty participates in the dynamics governing future behavior, or merely describes the output distribution without consequence. We introduce a structural distinction between descriptive uncertainty, which does not recursively modulate the system's policy, and regulatory uncertainty, which directly enters the optimization landscape and drives persistent adaptive restructuring. We prove formally that current transformer architectures are confined to descriptive uncertainty at inference. We ground this in thermodynamics via Landauer's principle: for uncertainty to be regulatory, epistemic error must cost real energy; in a decoupled system, hallucinations and correct derivations dissipate identical energy. We test this empirically across three locally-deployed language models (3B, 8B, 70B parameters). Token-level Shannon entropy is statistically invariant across tasks spanning pattern retrieval, causal operator application, and out-of-distribution causal generalization in all three models (all pairwise p >= 0.568; within-model ranges 0.011-0.028 nats), while task accuracy varies substantially across the same conditions (0%-100%). Entropy and accuracy are orthogonal. The decoupling is scale-invariant: larger models achieve higher accuracy but identical entropy flatness. This structural incapacity is not resolvable by additional parameters or training data. Genuine epistemic grounding requires physical coupling between thermodynamic substrate state and information processing cost.

</details>


### [317] [Does Your Wildfire Prediction Model Actually Work, or Just Score Well?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18911)
*Yangshuang Xu,Yuyang Dai,Liling Chang,Qi Wang,Yushun Dong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes WILDFIRE-FM, a wildfire-specific foundation model, and a fixed-contract evaluation framework to address sparse wildfire events; shows evaluation design strongly influences transfer conclusions across Earth-FM baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need wildfire-specific foundation models and robust evaluation to avoid biased transfer conclusions due to data sparsity.

Method: Pretrain WILDFIRE-FM on weather, active-fire observations, topography, vegetation, and static data; introduce fixed-contract evaluation with fixed-output and fixed-feature checks; evaluate against ten Earth-FM baselines on occupancy, spread, retrieval, regression.

Result: Under matched contracts, conclusions about wildfire transfer depend heavily on evaluation design; method highlights the importance of evaluation frameworks and suggests WILDFIRE-FM as a baseline for wildfire-specific Earth-FM research; code available.

Conclusion: A robust evaluation framework is necessary for wildfire-specific Earth-FMs; WILDFIRE-FM offers a foundation for future research and benchmarking, with results emphasizing design sensitivity.

Abstract: Wildfire prediction is important for early warning and resource allocation, yet existing Earth foundation models (Earth FMs) are pretrained for general atmospheric and geophysical objectives rather than wildfire forecasting. To address this gap, we introduce WILDFIRE-FM, the first foundation model pretrained specifically for wildfire prediction using weather, active-fire observations, topography, vegetation, and static environmental data. However, introducing a domain-specific backbone alone does not solve the evaluation problem: wildfire events are sparse in space and time, making transfer conclusions highly sensitive to matching rules and evaluation settings. To address this problem, we introduce a fixed-contract evaluation framework with two controlled checks: a fixed-output check for matching-rule effects and a fixed-feature check for head-selection effects. Under matched contracts, we compare WILDFIRE-FM with ten Earth-FM baselines across occupancy, spread, retrieval, and regression tasks. Our results show that wildfire transfer conclusions depend strongly on evaluation design and task formulation. We hope this framework and WILDFIRE-FM provide a foundation for future wildfire-specific Earth-FM research and benchmarking. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Wildfire-fm-evaluation-contracts-5AE9/.

</details>


### [318] [HypergraphFormer: Learning Hypergraphs from LLMs for Editable Floor Plan Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18932)
*Nikita Klimenko,Hesam Salehipour,Parham Eftekhar,Amir Khasahmadi,Ramon Elias Weber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HypergraphFormer uses an LLM trained to output hypergraph-based textual representations of floor plans, enabling efficient, editable generation with strong generalization and data efficiency, outperforming rasterized/vector baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of rasterized/vectorized floor-plan representations by capturing complex spatial relationships and connectivity via a learnable hypergraph structure, enabling arbitrary boundaries, better editability, and improved data efficiency under distribution shifts.

Method:  Supervised fine-tuning of a large language model to generate a hypergraph textual representation encoding spatial relationships and connectivity. Training and evaluation on the RPLAN dataset, with a separate out-of-distribution dataset for generalization; comparisons against state-of-the-art rasterized/vectorized baselines.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple metrics, demonstrates improved data efficiency, and shows robust generalization under distribution shift. The hypergraph formulation enables generation for irregular, user-specified boundaries by decoupling footprints from functional/geometric subdivisions; offers high editability for design workflows.

Conclusion: A hypergraph-based representation learned via an LLM is effective for floor-plan generation, delivering superior performance, generalization, data efficiency, and editability, and is well-suited for LLM-assisted design workflows.

Abstract: In this work, we propose HypergraphFormer, a novel and efficient approach to floor plan generation based on learning hypergraph representations with a large language model (LLM). The model is trained via supervised fine-tuning to generate a hypergraph-based textual representation that encodes spatial relationships and connectivity information within floor plans. We train and evaluate our approach on the RPLAN dataset, and further demonstrate its generalizability on a separate out-of-distribution dataset, which we release in this paper. Our method outperforms state-of-the-art techniques based on rasterized or vectorized representations across a diverse set of metrics. We also show improved data efficiency, particularly under distribution shift. The hypergraph formulation enables the generation of floor plans for arbitrary, irregular, user-specified boundaries by decoupling apartment footprints from their functional and geometric subdivisions. Furthermore, we show that the proposed methodology offers a high degree of editability, making it particularly well suited to design-oriented workflows supported by LLMs.

</details>


### [319] [A Geometric Analysis of Sign-Magnitude Asymmetry in a ReLU + RMSNorm Block under Ternary Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18933)
*Lei Dong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Ternary weight quantization in pre-norm RMSNorm Transformers is theoretically explained: sign flips amplify transverse output energy by about 2.75x in a two-layer ReLU+RMSNorm with Gaussian weights, due to ReLU-induced directional asymmetry exposed by RMSNorm's transverse projection. Sign-quantization error is sign-preserving with angular alignment ~cos^2=2/π; ReLU is largely transparent to this error. Real-model deviations arise from outlier features (violating delocalization); energy amplification scales as R ~ nα^2. Empirical results on TinyLlama-1.1B in the linear regime (p≤0.5%) align with theory (≈10× NLL leverage), with a 5× all-column NLL ratio and heavy-tailed activation outliers. The Bussgang constant 2/π, RMSNorm geometry, and ReLU half-space structure together explain the observed sign-magnitude asymmetry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a geometric, analytic account for why and how ternary weight quantization with {-1,0,+1} incurs surprisingly small loss in pre-norm transformers using RMSNorm, by decomposing perturbations into sign and magnitude components and examining their propagation through ReLU and RMSNorm.

Method: The work analyzes a two-layer ReLU+RMSNorm network with i.i.d. Gaussian weights. It derives: (i) Theorem 3: sign flips produce ~2.75× more transverse output energy than equal-Frobenius-norm magnitude perturbations as flip rate p→0; (ii) Theorem 4: sign-quantization error acts as a sign-preserving perturbation with angular alignment cos^2→2/π; compares post-ReLU radial fraction to pre-ReLU value; (iii) Empirical verification on TinyLlama-1.1B in the linear regime, measurement of outliers and heavy-tailed α-distribution, comparison of NLL and PPL.

Result: Geometric explanation: sign-magnitude asymmetry from ReLU plus RMSNorm yields larger sensitivity to sign flips in transverse directions, with a predicted amplification factor of about 2.75; sign-quantization error aligns with ReLU behavior and remains largely transparent; empirical data on TinyLlama-1.1B confirms partial agreement in linear regime (≈10× NLL; all-column ratio ≈5×; R_col upper bound 19). Outliers with heavy-tailed activation values corroborate delocalization violations as a source of deviation from the idealized factor.

Conclusion: The Bussgang constant (2/π), RMSNorm geometry, and ReLU-induced half-space structure jointly explain the observed sign-magnitude asymmetry and tolerance to ternary perturbations in pre-norm models. Real-model deviations arise from outlier features that break delocalization; for typical inputs, the energy amplification scales as R ∼ nα^2, aligning with empirical measurements.

Abstract: Pre-norm Transformers with RMSNorm tolerate ternary {-1,0,+1} weight quantization with surprisingly small loss (Ma et al., 2024). We give a geometric explanation via sign-magnitude decomposition of weight perturbations. In a two-layer ReLU + RMSNorm model with i.i.d. Gaussian weights, sign-flips produce $π/(π-2) \approx 2.75$ times more transverse output energy than sign-preserving magnitude perturbations of equal Frobenius norm, as the flip rate $p \to 0$ (Theorem 3). The mechanism: ReLU creates a hidden-space directional asymmetry between the two perturbation types, which RMSNorm's transverse-projection Fréchet derivative selectively exposes. Sign-quantization error is itself a sign-preserving perturbation with angular alignment $\cos^2 \to 2/π$ (Theorem 4); its post-ReLU radial fraction ($0.365$) matches the pre-ReLU value $1-2/π$ within $0.4\%$, so ReLU is approximately transparent to ternary error. Multi-layer compounding of the $2.75\times$ factor is not experimentally supported; the gap to real-model sign sensitivity arises from outlier features violating delocalization. For an input dimension with amplitude $α$, a single sign-flip produces post-ReLU energy amplified by $R \approx nα^2$ relative to a delocalized entry. On TinyLlama-1.1B, at linear response ($p \leq 0.5\%$), count-matched NLL leverage stabilizes at $\sim 10\times \approx n\mathbb{E}[α^2]$, matching the per-entry theory; the all-column NLL ratio of $5.0\times$ falls within $R_{\mathrm{col}} \leq 19$ ($67\times$ PPL gap reflects metric nonlinearity). Measured outlier $α$ at layer 12 (median $0.024$, max $0.26$) confirms heavy-tailed concentration. The Bussgang constant $2/π$, RMSNorm geometry, and ReLU half-space structure together explain sign-magnitude asymmetry in pre-norm models, with $R \propto nα^2$ accounting for real-model deviations.

</details>


### [320] [FedMental: Evaluating Federated Learning for Mental Health Detection from Social Media Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18936)
*Nuredin Ali Abdelkadir,Anjali Ratnam,Zeerak Talat,Stevie Chancellor*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evaluates privacy-preserving ML for mental-health prediction using FL and DP-FL; FL nearly matches centralized performance, while DP-FL incurs large performance losses due to masking sparse informative linguistic cues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy concerns in sharing social-media mental-health data impede dataset growth; assess whether FL and DP-FL can enable safer data sharing without sacrificing accuracy for depression detection on Twitter and suicide crisis detection on Reddit.

Method: User-level non-IID FL simulations across varying client fractions, aggregation strategies, and privacy budgets; compare FL to centralized training; apply DP-FL with privacy parameter epsilon up to 50.

Result: Federated learning achieves performance close to centralized (centralized F1 = 85.63; best FL F1 = 83.16); Differentially Private FL shows a large performance-privacy trade-off (up to 27.01 F1 drop) even with moderate noise (epsilon = 50), due to distortion of sparse informative linguistic markers like health topics and emotion words.

Conclusion: Demonstrates both the potential and limitations of current privacy-preserving methods for mental-health inference; DP-FL currently imposes substantial accuracy costs, indicating a need for improved privacy mechanisms or task-specific adaptations to preserve informative linguistic signals.

Abstract: Social media text data are often used to train Machine Learning (ML) models to identify users exhibiting high-risk mental health behaviors. However, sharing this sensitive data poses privacy risks and limits the growth of benchmark datasets. We comprehensively evaluate whether privacy-preserving ML techniques can enable safer data sharing while preserving performance. Specifically, we apply federated learning (FL) and Differentially Private FL for two widely-studied mental health prediction tasks: depression detection on X (Twitter) and suicide crisis detection on Reddit. We simulate realistic data-sharing scenarios by treating each user as a client in a non-IID setting, evaluating across different client fractions, aggregation strategies, and privacy budgets. While FL achieves comparable performance to centralized training (centralized F1 = 85.63; best FL model F1 = 83.16) on depression identification, we find that Differentially Private FL has a large performance-privacy trade-off (up to F1 = 27.01 drop) even with low levels of noise (epsilon = 50). This is due to the distortion of highly informative yet sparse mental health linguistic markers related to mental health, like health topics and emotion words. This research empirically demonstrates the potential and limitations of current privacy preservation techniques for mental health inference tasks.

</details>


### [321] [Shaping the Prior: How Synthetic Task Distributions Determine Tabular Foundation Model Quality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18971)
*Mohamed Bouadi,Nassim Bouarour,Varun Kulkarni,Shivam Dubey,Aditya Tanna,Vinay Kumar Sankarapu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: O'Prior is a compositional realism prior for synthetic tabular data that improves the quality and robustness of tabular foundation models by explicitly modeling diverse, realistic, stress-inducing data distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tabular foundation models primarily derive inductive biases from synthetic pretraining distributions, but standard priors are too well-behaved and miss irregularities and failure modes that affect deployment robustness.

Method: Introduce O'Prior with four coupled components: a hierarchical SCM meta-generator spanning diverse functional families; a modular realism engine covering heterogeneous marginals, missingness, and target transforms; an explicit stress module injecting confounding and support-query mismatch; and a curriculum-governed, leakage-safe generation protocol. To isolate prior design, fix architecture/optimizer/compute and vary only the synthetic task distribution.

Result: O'Prior yields consistent and substantial improvements in downstream accuracy and robustness across real tabular benchmarks, with gains concentrated in regimes characterized by distributional irregularities. Ablations show mechanism diversity, realism composition, and shift-aware stress each contribute independently; their effects are not interchangeable.

Conclusion: Synthetic prior design is a first-order determinant of tabular foundation model quality; mechanism diversity, realism composition, and shift-aware stress are important contributors to robustness and accuracy.

Abstract: What determines the quality of a tabular foundation model? Unlike language or vision, tabular foundation models acquire their inductive biases almost entirely from synthetic pretraining distributions, yet the design of these distributions remains poorly understood. Standard synthetic priors are too well-behaved: they omit the irregularities and failure modes that determine deployment robustness. We introduce O'Prior, a compositional realism prior built around four coupled components: a hierarchical SCM meta-generator spanning diverse functional families; a modular realism engine covering heterogeneous marginals, missingness, and target transforms; an explicit stress module injecting confounding and support-query mismatch; and a curriculum-governed, leakage-safe generation protocol. To isolate prior design as the scientific variable, we hold architecture, optimizer, and compute budget fixed and vary only the synthetic task distribution. O'Prior yields consistent and substantial improvements in downstream accuracy and robustness across real tabular benchmarks, with gains concentrated in regimes characterized by distributional irregularities. Ablations confirm that mechanism diversity, realism composition, and shift-aware stress each contribute independently, their effects are not interchangeable. These results establish synthetic prior construction as a first-order and largely overlooked determinant of tabular foundation model quality

</details>


### [322] [TabQL: In-Context Q-Learning with Tabular Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18979)
*Qisai Liu,Zhanhong Jiang,Timilehin Ayanlade,Ashutosh Kumar Nirala,Yang Li,Aditya Balu,Soumik Sarkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TabQL replaces parametric Q-networks with a tabular representation and a sequence-to-sequence foundation model that performs Q-learning via in-context learning, enabling rapid adaptation from limited online experience and warm-started learning with DQN. It interpolates between vanilla Q-learning and DQN, with convergence and sample-complexity analysis and empirical gains in data efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce data inefficiency and reliance on large parametric networks in Deep Q-Learning by exploiting in-context learning and a tabularized Q-value representation, while leveraging a warm-up phase with standard DQN to bootstrap high-quality context.

Method: Represent Q-values as (state, action, Q-value) tuples in a tabular form and process them with a seq2seq foundation model. Use in-context updates conditioned on recent experiences to infer Q-values with zero- or few-shot learning. Include a warm-up phase using DQN to bootstrap context. Generate new transitions by executing actions suggested by TabQL with the DQN-predicted Q-values to improve context. Provide formal analysis of convergence and sample complexity and show interpolation between vanilla Q-learning and DQN with in-context learning. Conduct extensive benchmarks to evaluate performance and efficiency.

Result: TabQL achieves improved data efficiency and potentially faster adaptation than standard DQN by amortizing Bellman updates through in-context learning. It bridges vanilla Q-learning and DQN, with theoretical guarantees under mild assumptions and empirical validation on multiple benchmarks.

Conclusion: TabQL offers a continuum between vanilla Q-learning and DQN enhanced by in-context learning, delivering data-efficient Q-value estimation via a tabularized representation and a sequence-to-sequence model, with convergence and sample-complexity analyses supported by empirical results.

Abstract: We propose Tabular Q-Learning (TabQL), a reinforcement learning framework that replaces the conventional parametric Q-network in Deep Q-Learning (DQN) with a tabular foundation model endowed with in-context learning capabilities. The key idea is to represent Q-values through a sequence-to-sequence foundation model operating over a tabularized representation of state-action-Q-value tuples, enabling rapid adaptation from limited online interaction by conditioning on recent experience. TabQL departs from classical DQN by leveraging (i) zero- or few-shot Q-value inference via in-context updates, and (ii) a warm-up phase using standard DQN to bootstrap high-quality context. Particularly, to enhance the context quality, new transitions are generated by executing actions output by TabQL with predicted Q values from DQN. We formalize TabQL, analyze its convergence and sample complexity under mild assumptions, and show that TabQL interpolates between vanilla Q-learning and DQN with in-context learning. Our analysis demonstrates that TabQL achieves improved efficiency compared to DQN by amortizing Bellman updates through in-context learning. Extensive numerical experiments with several benchmarks showcase the effectiveness and efficacy of the proposed TabQL.

</details>


### [323] [Distilling Linearized Behavior for Effective Task Arithmetic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18993)
*Thomas Sommariva,Francesca Morandi,Simone Calderara,Angelo Porrello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid fine-tuning that enforces linearity via activation-space constraints by distilling from a curvature-regularized linearized teacher into a non-linear student, enabling linear-task-vector arithmetic without inference overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reconcile the efficiency and disentanglement of linear fine-tuning with the expressivity of non-linear models, enabling robust task vector composition in vision and language without extra inference cost.

Method: Construct a curvature-regularized linearized teacher around base weights; train a non-linear student with conventional fine-tuning while enforcing activation-space constraints that ensure linearity with respect to weight perturbations; use distillation of hidden representations from teacher to student.

Result: The student inherits linear-task-vector properties, enabling effective task vector composition and achieving strong performance on vision and language benchmarks with no inference-time overhead.

Conclusion: This work provides a practical mechanism to combine linear fine-tuning benefits with standard training, preserving task arithmetic capabilities while maintaining standard inference efficiency.

Abstract: Task vector composition has emerged as a promising paradigm for editing pre-trained models, enabling model merging through addition and unlearning through subtraction. Fine-tuning in the tangent space of a pre-trained model (linear fine-tuning) has proven effective, as it produces task vectors that are naturally disentangled and resistant to interference. However, linearized models suffer from limited expressivity during training and incur higher computational costs at inference time, which restrict their practical applicability. In this work, we bridge the gap between linear and standard non-linear fine-tuning. We show that linearity with respect to weight perturbations, a property defined in parameter space, can be enforced through constraints in activation space during training. Concretely, we distill hidden representations from a curvature-regularized linearized teacher into a non-linear student trained via conventional fine-tuning. We find that the resulting model inherits key properties of linearized models for task arithmetic, enabling effective composition of task vectors and achieving strong performance across vision and language benchmarks without incurring any inference-time overhead.

</details>


### [324] [Distance-Aware Muon: Adaptive Step Scaling for Normalized Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.18999)
*Yury Demidovich,Abhishek Chakraborty,Grigory Malinovsky,Angelia Nedić,Peter Richtárik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive scaling rules for Muon with three algorithms (Distance-Adaptive, Scale-Calibrated, Distance-Free) that adjust step size via trajectory radius, local descent certificate, or distance certificates; provides theoretical guarantees and empirical robustness across NLP/vision tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce sensitivity of Muon to step scale across general norm geometries and enable robust performance on smooth non-convex and star-convex objectives in practical deep learning settings.

Method: Introduce three complementary adaptive scaling algorithms for Muon. Distance-Adaptive Muon uses trajectory-explored radius as trust-region radius with a bounded-trajectory stationarity guarantee. Scale-Calibrated Muon preserves Muon's EMA but derives step length from a local descent certificate (current gradient and momentum) with a last-iterate O(1/T) objective-gap bound under a bounded initial sublevel-set. Distance-Free Muon recentered trust-region using a scalar distance certificate and a majorized 1D search to select the radius without needing the distance to a global minimizer.

Result: Theoretical guarantees for all three methods and empirical validation showing reduced sensitivity to scale tuning and competitive or improved performance versus tuned fixed-scale Muon baselines on Transformer language modeling (GPT-124M/WikiText-103) and Vision Transformer (ViT-Tiny/CIFAR-100).

Conclusion: Adaptive scaling rules for Muon in general norm geometries yield robust performance across problem regimes (smooth non-convex and star-convex) and reduce the need for manual scale tuning, matching or surpassing tuned baselines under tested budgets.

Abstract: Muon and related normalized optimizers decouple the choice of update direction from the choice of step scale, but their practical performance remains sensitive to the scale of the normalized step. We study adaptive scaling rules for Muon in general norm geometries and develop three complementary algorithms. For smooth non-convex objectives, we introduce Distance-Adaptive Muon, whose trust-region radius is set from the radius explored by the trajectory, and prove a stationarity guarantee under a bounded-trajectory assumption. We then turn to star-convex objectives, a tractable model of the favorable global geometry often used to reason about the empirical loss landscapes of deep neural networks, where objective-gap guarantees are possible. In this setting, we first introduce Scale-Calibrated Muon, which keeps Muon's exponential moving average but sets the step length from a local descent certificate computed from the current gradient and momentum. For this method, we prove a last-iterate O(1/T) objective-gap bound under a bounded initial sublevel-set assumption, where the corresponding radius parameter appears only in the analysis and not in the algorithm. Finally, we develop Distance-Free Muon, a recentered trust-region method that uses a scalar distance certificate and a majorized one-dimensional search to select the trust-region radius without requiring the unknown distance from the initialization to a global minimizer. Experiments on Transformer language modeling (GPT-124M/WikiText-103) and image classification (ViT-Tiny/CIFAR-100) show that the proposed adaptive scaling rules reduce sensitivity to manual scale tuning and match or improve tuned fixed-scale Muon baselines under the tested budgets.

</details>


### [325] [SAGA: A Sequence-Adaptive Generative Architecture for Multi-Horizon Probabilistic Forecasting with Adaptive Temporal Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19014)
*Gustav Olaf Yunus Laitinen-Fredriksson Lundström-Imanov,Hafize Gonca Cömert*

Main category: cs.LG
