### [402] [Exploiting Non-Negativity in DAG Structure Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19947)
*Samuel Rey,Madeline navarro,Gonzalo Mateos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A non-negative DAG learning method using augmented Lagrangian and a non-negativity constraint yields a simpler acyclicity characterization; it has a unique global minimizer in the population and superior empirical performance compared to other continuous DAG learners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DAG learning requires enforcing acyclicity, which is globally combinatorial. Continuous relaxations help but suffer from non-convex landscapes and degeneracy. Imposing non-negativity on edges offers a tractable structure to achieve better optimization properties and identifiability.

Method: Introduce a regularized non-negative DAG learning formulation and solve with an augmented-Lagrangian/multipliers algorithm. Leverage non-negativity to obtain a simpler acyclicity characterization and analyze the optimization landscape, showing benign (no spurious interior stationary points) properties in the population.

Result: The true DAG is the unique global minimizer of the augmented-Lagrangian objective in the population setting; no spurious interior stationary points; the true DAG is the only acyclic KKT point. Empirically, the method outperforms state-of-the-art continuous DAG-learning methods on synthetic and real data.

Conclusion: Non-negativity yields a simpler, favorable optimization landscape for DAG learning and enables a theoretically sound augmented-Lagrangian approach with strong population-level optimality guarantees and improved empirical performance.

Abstract: This work addresses the problem of learning directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) from nodal observations generated by a linear structural equation model. DAG learning is a central task in signal processing, machine learning, and causal inference, but it remains challenging because acyclicity is a global combinatorial property. Continuous acyclicity constraints have led to important algorithmic advances by replacing the discrete DAG constraint with smooth equality constraints. However, existing formulations still involve difficult non-convex optimization landscapes and may suffer from degenerate first-order optimality conditions. Here, we restrict attention to DAGs with non-negative edge weights and exploit this additional structure to obtain a simpler characterization of acyclicity. Building on this characterization, we formulate a regularized non-negative DAG learning problem and develop an algorithm based on the method of multipliers. We further analyze the benign optimization landscape induced by non-negativity. In the population regime, we show that the true DAG is the unique global minimizer of the proposed augmented-Lagrangian formulation; moreover, the landscape contains no spurious interior stationary points, and the true DAG is the only acyclic KKT point. Numerical experiments on synthetic and real-world data show that the proposed method improves over state-of-the-art continuous DAG-learning alternatives.

</details>


### [403] [Learning Orthonormal Bases for Function Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19959)
*Hamidreza Kamkari,Mohammad Sina Nabizadeh,Justin Solomon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes to learn infinite-dimensional orthonormal bases by parameterizing paths on the orthogonal group with neural-network-driven, finite-rank generators of skew-adjoint operators; proves universality (rank-2 suffices) and demonstrates transforming fixed bases (e.g., Fourier) into data-driven bases like PCA/eigenfunctions/DMD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed bases (Fourier, wavelets) may be suboptimal for a given dataset or PDE; a neural-network–driven, adaptive basis can align representations with structure in data or dynamics while preserving orthogonality.

Method: Model the target basis as the endpoint of a path on the Lie group of orthogonal transformations on a function space; the path is governed by an ODE with skew-adjoint integral operators. Neural networks parameterize finite-rank generators of these ODEs, enabling tractable learning. A universality result shows that even rank-2 generators yield density of endpoints in the orthogonal group under an appropriate operator topology. Empirically transform the Fourier basis into problem-relevant bases (PCA, eigenfunctions, dynamic modes).

Result: Theoretical: a universality/density result for learning bases via finite-rank-generated ODEs on the orthogonal group; Practical: a flexible framework to morph standard bases into data- or physics-driven bases across several settings.

Conclusion: Provides a principled, scalable approach to adapt basis functions in function spaces by combining Lie-group geometry, operator theory, and neural nets, with potential applications in data representation and physics-aware modeling.

Abstract: Infinite-dimensional orthonormal basis expansions play a central role in representing and computing with function spaces due to their favorable linear algebraic properties. However, common bases such as Fourier or wavelets are fixed and do not adapt to the structure of a given problem or dataset. In this paper, we aim to represent these bases with neural networks and optimize them. Our key idea is that any target infinite-dimensional orthonormal basis can be viewed either as a point on the Lie manifold of the orthogonal group, or equivalently, as the endpoint of a continuous path on that manifold that connects a reference basis, e.g. Fourier, to that target. Paths on the Lie manifold satisfy ordinary differential equations (ODEs) governed by skew-adjoint integral operators. Using neural networks to define finite-rank generators of such ODEs allows us to parameterize and optimize orthonormal bases in function space. While relying on finite-rank generators to model infinite operators might seem restrictive, we prove a universality result: even with a rank-2 generator, the integrated solutions of the ODE are dense in the orthogonal group under the appropriate operator topology. In other words, for any target orthonormal basis, there exists a path originating from a reference basis and driven by finite-rank generators that gets arbitrarily close to that target basis. We demonstrate the flexibility of our framework by transforming the Fourier basis into the principal components of a functional dataset, eigenfunctions of linear operators, or dynamic modes of energy-preserving physical simulations.

</details>


### [404] [Normative Networks for Source Separation via Local Plasticity and Dendritic Computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19965)
*Bariscan Bozkurt,Efe Ali Gorguner,Francesco Innocenti,Rafal Bogacz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Predictive Entropy Maximization (PEM), an online, biologically plausible BSS algorithm that handles structured and potentially correlated sources using local learning rules, achieving robust performance and competitive results with baselines; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable online, biologically plausible blind source separation for structured and potentially correlated sources, addressing the nonlocal dynamics of entropy-based approaches.

Method: Propose a surrogate entropy objective and derive a predictive neural architecture with locally updating components: feedforward, error-driven synapses; lateral inhibitory connections learned via local Hebbian plasticity; output nonlinearities enforcing source-domain constraints; derive explicit spectral bounds on the surrogate error to characterize approximation accuracy.

Result: Empirically, PEM remains robust to increasing source correlation and observation noise; it outperforms biologically plausible algorithms relying on stronger independence or decorrelation and is competitive with exact determinant- and correlative-information-based baselines.

Conclusion: Local plasticity and adaptive lateral inhibition can emerge from maximizing a regularized second-order entropy over structured source domains; the approach yields an online, biologically plausible BSS algorithm, with code available.

Abstract: Blind source separation (BSS) is a natural framework for studying how latent causes may be recovered from sensory mixtures, but deriving online and biologically plausible algorithms for structured (i.e., constrained to known domains) and potentially correlated sources remains challenging. Recent work has derived neural networks for BSS from maximization of an entropy measure, yet its online implementations involve complex and nonlocal recurrent dynamics. Motivated by this perspective, we propose Predictive Entropy Maximization, which achieves competitive performance in BSS, using only local weight updates. The method employs a close approximation of an entropy measure, yielding an objective function with easily interpretable components. Minimizing this objective leads to a predictive neural architecture in which feedforward synapses follow an error-driven rule (that can be realized through dendritic mechanisms), lateral inhibitory connections are learned with local Hebbian plasticity, and source-domain constraints are enforced through simple output nonlinearities. We derive explicit spectral bounds on the surrogate error, characterizing when the approximation is accurate. Empirically, Predictive Entropy Maximization remains robust under increasing source correlation and observation noise, outperforms biologically plausible algorithms that rely on stronger independence or decorrelation assumptions, and remains competitive with exact determinant- and correlative-information-based baselines. These results show how local plasticity and adaptive lateral inhibition can emerge from maximizing a regularized second-order entropy over structured source domains. Our implementation code is available at https://github.com/BariscanBozkurt/Predictive-Entropy-Maximization.

</details>


### [405] [Detecting Fluent Optimization-Based Adversarial Prompts via Sequential Entropy Changes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19966)
*Mohammed Alshaalan,Miguel R. D. Rodrigues*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CPD Online is a model-agnostic, training-free online detector for adversarial suffixes in LLM prompts, using online change-point detection on a token-level entropy stream with a robust system-prompt baseline and one-sided CUSUM; it localizes suffix onset and reduces gating overhead while outperforming windowed perplexity baselines across multiple models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adversarial suffixes can jailbreak aligned LLMs and evade static detectors; there is a need for online, robust detection that generalizes across models and localizes the onset of adversarial suffixes.

Method: Cast adversarial-suffix detection as an online change-point detection problem on the token-level next-token entropy stream. Use the LLM system prompt to estimate a robust baseline, standardize user-token entropies, and apply a one-sided CUSUM statistic. The resulting detector, CPD Online (CPD), is model-agnostic, training-free, runs online, and localizes the adversarial suffix onset.

Result: Evaluated on 1,012 optimization-based suffix attacks (GCG, AutoDAN, AdvPrompter, BEAST, AutoDAN-HGA) and 1,012 perplexity-controlled benign prompts across six open-weight chat models (LLaMA-2-7B/13B, Vicuna-7B/13B, Qwen2.5-7B/14B). CPD improves F1 over the strongest windowed-perplexity baseline on all models. On LLaMA-2-7B at canonical CUSUM (k=0), CPD achieves AUROC 0.88 and F1 0.82. CPD localizes 79.6% of triggers inside the adversarial suffix, compared to 17-46% for windowed perplexity. Additionally, when used as a lightweight gate for LLaMA Guard, CPD reduces guard calls by 17-22% in a high-volume, benign-dominated deployment while preserving guard-level detection quality.

Conclusion: CPD Online is effective, model-agnostic, and online, yielding strong detection performance, precise localization of suffix onset, and reduced gating overhead compared to windowed perplexity baselines.

Abstract: Optimization-based adversarial suffixes can jailbreak aligned large language models (LLMs) while remaining fluent, weakening static and windowed perplexity-based detectors. We cast adversarial suffix detection as an online change-point detection problem over the token-level next-token entropy stream. Using the LLM system prompt to estimate a robust baseline, we standardize user-token entropies and apply a one-sided CUSUM statistic. The resulting detector, CPD Online (CPD), is model-agnostic, training-free, runs online, and localizes the adversarial suffix onset. On a benchmark of 1,012 optimization-based suffix attacks (GCG, AutoDAN, AdvPrompter, BEAST, AutoDAN-HGA) and 1,012 perplexity-controlled benign prompts, CPD improves F1 over the strongest windowed-perplexity baseline on all six open-weight chat models (LLaMA-2-7B/13B, Vicuna-7B/13B, Qwen2.5-7B/14B). On LLaMA-2-7B at the canonical CUSUM setting ($k=0$), CPD reaches AUROC $0.88$ and F1 $0.82$. Beyond prompt-level detection, CPD concentrates 79.6% of its triggers inside the adversarial suffix, versus 17-46% for windowed perplexity. Finally, when used as a lightweight gate for LLaMA Guard, CPD reduces guard calls by 17-22% on a high-volume, benign-dominated deployment while preserving guard-level detection quality

</details>


### [406] [Your Neighbors Know: Leveraging Local Neighborhoods for Backdoor Detection in Decentralized Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19969)
*Sayan Biswas,Antoine Boutet,Davide Frey,Romaric Gaudel,Rachid Guerraoui,Maxime Jacovella,Anne-Marie Kermarrec,Dimitri Lerévérend,François Taïani,Martijn de Vos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Argus is a decentralized backdoor detector for distributed learning that operates without a central coordinator or prior trigger knowledge; it analyzes local model updates, shares candidate triggers with neighbors, and uses a structural similarity metric to distinguish true backdoors from heterogeneity-induced false alarms, with convergence guarantees and strong empirical defense against baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backdoor attacks in decentralized learning are understudied and existing defenses often assume centralized coordination or knowledge of triggers, which DL constraints violate.

Method: Honest nodes locally analyze received updates to identify potential backdoor triggers. Nodes share their triggers with neighbors and apply a structural similarity metric to separate true backdoors from false alarms caused by data heterogeneity. Suspicious updates are rejected and persistently malicious senders are evicted. The work provides theoretical DL-specific convergence guarantees for the detection mechanism.

Result: Empirical evaluation on three standard datasets against three baselines shows Argus reduces attack success rates by up to 90 points and preserves model utility within 5 percentage points of an oracle; effectiveness improves with increasing data heterogeneity.

Conclusion: Argus offers a DL-native backdoor defense with convergence guarantees, achieving strong defense without central coordination and robust performance under data heterogeneity.

Abstract: Decentralized learning (DL) is an emerging machine learning paradigm where nodes collaboratively train models without a central server. However, the collaborative nature of DL makes it vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where a model is taught to behave normally on standard inputs while executing hidden, malicious actions when encountering data with specific triggers. Backdoor attacks in DL remain understudied and existing defenses often overlook DL constraints. We introduce Argus, a novel backdoor detection framework native to DL that requires neither a central coordinator nor prior knowledge of the trigger. In Argus, honest nodes locally analyze received model updates to identify potential backdoor triggers. Nodes then collectively share their triggers with their neighbors and use a structural similarity metric to separate true backdoors from false alarms induced by data heterogeneity. A key insight is that false positive triggers exhibit inconsistencies across participants while true positive ones show consistent patterns. Model updates that fail this collaborative test are rejected, and persistently malicious senders are eventually evicted. We provide the first theoretical convergence guarantees for a DL-specific backdoor detection mechanism, showing that filtering out suspicious model updates with high probability preserves a convergence rate comparable to standard DL. We implement and evaluate Argus on three standard datasets and against three state-of-the-art baselines. Across settings, Argus reduces attack success rates by up to 90 points compared to no defense, while preserving model utility within 5 percentage points of an omniscient oracle. Furthermore, the effectiveness of Argus compared to baselines improves as data heterogeneity increases.

</details>


### [407] [Block-Sphere Vector Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19972)
*Heesang Ann,Joongkyu Lee,Min-hwan Oh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified theoretical comparison of rotation-based vector quantizers (EDEN, RabitQ, TurboQuant) under multiple distortion criteria; introduces Block-Sphere Quantization (BlockQuant) with spherical geometry; shows criterion-dependent advantages and practical gains for embeddings/LLM tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The lack of a consistent, criterion-agnostic framework to compare rotation-based quantizers, and the need to preserve geometric structure after random rotations for improved distortion guarantees and practical performance.

Method: Theoretical analysis comparing EDEN, RabitQ, and TurboQuant across MSE distortion, expected inner-product distortion, and high-probability guarantees; development of Block-Sphere Quantization (BlockQuant) that quantizes rotated vectors on the sphere by considering block-spherical geometry; proofs of reconstruction MSE and expected inner-product distortion improvements; empirical evaluation on real embedding datasets and long-context LLM inference tasks.

Result: Unified framework reveals criterion-dependent advantages: EDEN and TurboQuant excel under MSE; EDEN also benefits expected inner-product distortion; RabitQ provides strong high-probability guarantees. BlockQuant achieves theoretical improvements over baselines for both MSE and expected inner-product distortion with empirical results aligning with theory, showing practical gains on embeddings and LLM tasks.

Conclusion: The work clarifies when to prefer which rotation-based quantizer and introduces BlockQuant as a geometry-preserving alternative that improves both reconstruction and inner-product distortion; the approach has practical implications for memory-efficient embeddings and scalable inference, with potential extensions to other distortion criteria and block structures.

Abstract: Vector quantization is a fundamental primitive for scalable machine learning systems, enabling memory-efficient storage, fast retrieval, and compressed inference. Recent rotation-based quantizers such as EDEN, RabitQ, and TurboQuant have introduced strong guarantees and empirical performance, but the surrounding comparisons have been difficult to interpret because they rely on different distortion criteria, probability regimes, and implementation assumptions. As our first contribution, we provide a unified theoretical comparison of these methods and show that their relative advantages are criterion-dependent rather than absolute: EDEN and TurboQuant are favorable for MSE distortion, EDEN is also effective for expected inner-product distortion, and RabitQ provides strong high-probability control. This comparison further clarifies that EDEN provides particularly strong guarantees for expected distortion measures. As our second contribution, we introduce Block-Sphere Quantization (BlockQuant), a new rotation-based block quantization algorithm designed around the spherical geometry of randomly rotated vectors. Unlike coordinate-wise quantizers, BlockQuant quantizes blocks on the sphere, preserving the geometry of rotated embeddings more faithfully. We prove that this block-spherical design theoretically improves over the baselines considered in this paper for both reconstruction MSE and expected inner-product distortion. Our experiments on real embedding datasets and long-context LLM inference tasks show practical gains that are consistent with our theoretical improvements.

</details>


### [408] [Learning with Foresight: Enhancing Neural Routing Policy via Multi-Node Lookahead Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19975)
*Xia Jiang,Yaoxin Wu,Yew-Soon Ong,Yingqian Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MnLP is a training paradigm for neural routing policies that predicts multiple future nodes, enabling long-horizon planning with training-only modules and auxiliary losses, while preserving inference efficiency and improving generalization across problem sizes and distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current neural VRP policies rely on next-node prediction, which yields myopic decisions and poor long-horizon planning. There is a need to inject longer-range context into training without increasing inference cost.

Method: Introduce Multi-node Lookahead Prediction (MnLP) training: extend supervised learning to predict multiple future nodes simultaneously; incorporate causal and discardable MnLP modules that are active only during training; add multi-depth auxiliary supervision to the loss to encourage long-range contextual understanding; ensure no inference-time overhead and compatibility with various neural architectures.

Result: MnLP outperforms existing training methods, improving generalization of neural policies across varying problem sizes, distributions, and real-world benchmarks; it is plug-and-play with different architectures and introduces no additional inference overhead.

Conclusion: MnLP provides long-range planning ability by enriching the training signal with multi-step predictions, leading to better generalization while keeping inference-time efficiency and architectural flexibility.

Abstract: Neural policies have shown promise in solving vehicle routing problems due to their reduced reliance on handcrafted heuristics. However, current training paradigms suffer from a fundamental limitation: they primarily focus on next-node prediction for solution construction, resulting in myopic decision-making that undermines long-horizon planning capacity. To this end, we introduce Multi-node Lookahead Prediction (MnLP), a novel training strategy that extends the supervised learning paradigm to predict multiple future nodes simultaneously. We incorporate causal and discardable MnLP modules that operate exclusively during training, facilitating models to anticipate multi-step decisions while preserving inference-time efficiency. By incorporating multi-depth auxiliary supervision into the loss function, MnLP equips neural policies with the ability of long-range contextual understanding. Experimentally, MnLP outperforms existing training methods, improving the generalization capability of neural policies across various problem sizes, distributions, and real-world benchmarks. Moreover, MnLP can be seamlessly integrated into diverse neural architectures without introducing additional inference overhead.

</details>


### [409] [LLM Benchmark Datasets Should Be Contamination-Resistant](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19999)
*Ali Al-Lawati,Jason Lucas,Dongwon Lee,Suhang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes contamination-resistant, unlearnable benchmarks to preserve LLM evaluation integrity by leveraging training/inference asymmetry and enabling cross-architecture interoperability; a roadmap for research and community adoption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark contamination from pretraining data undermines reliable assessment of generalization; there is a need for benchmarks that resist memorization while still supporting valid inference.

Method: 1) Document the prevalence of contaminated benchmarks; 2) Define properties of contamination-resistant datasets; 3) Exploit Transformer inference-training asymmetry to enable protection; 4) Propose mathematical frameworks for interoperability across LLM architectures; 5) Call for community actions to adopt and develop methods and platforms.

Result: A conceptual framework and set of design principles, not empirical results—outlines how to construct and evaluate contamination-resistant benchmarks and how to integrate them into pipelines.

Conclusion: Adopt contamination-resistant benchmarks, develop supporting methodologies and platforms, and integrate into existing evaluation workflows to ensure reliable benchmarking of LLMs.

Abstract: Benchmark datasets are critical for reproducible, reliable, and discriminative evaluation of LLMs. However, recent studies reveal that many benchmark datasets are included in pretraining corpora, i.e., $\textit{contaminated}$, which diminishes their value as reliable measures of model generalization. In this paper, we argue that benchmark datasets should be $\textit{contamination-resistant}$, i.e., $\textit{unlearnable}$, but support $\textit{inference}$. To accomplish this, we first highlight the wide prevalence of benchmark dataset contamination and outline the properties of contamination-resistant datasets. Second, we highlight how the asymmetry between the inference and training pipelines in the Transformer architecture can be leveraged to support contamination-resistance. Third, we outline mathematical advancements to make these datasets interoperable across various LLM architectures. Based on the above, we call on the community to ensure the reliability of LLM benchmarking by: (i) advancing novel contamination-resistant methodologies, (ii) developing supporting methods and platforms, and (iii) adopting contamination-resistant benchmarks into existing evaluation pipelines.

</details>


### [410] [Fine-Tuning Without Forgetting via Loss-Adaptive Learning Rates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20005)
*Parjanya Prajakta Prashant,Jiongli Zhu,Aldan Creo,Babak Salimi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FINCH is a loss-adaptive learning-rate schedule for fine-tuning LLMs that bounds per-step forgetting by LR * sqrt(loss). It reduces forgetting dramatically while preserving task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning degrades pretraining-learned capabilities, especially when the target task has poor pretraining coverage. High-loss tokens are crucial for learning new tasks, so forgetting must be controlled without suppressing them.

Method: Proposes a per-step forgetting bound: forgetting ≤ learning_rate × sqrt(current_loss). Introduces FINCH, a loss-adaptive LR schedule that lowers the LR on high-loss batches and raises it as loss declines, without changing the fine-tuning objective.

Result: Across knowledge acquisition, science, and low-resource language benchmarks, FINCH reduces forgetting by 93% on average while maintaining the same task performance as standard fine-tuning. In Qwen3-4B knowledge acquisition, it cuts TruthfulQA degradation by 5x, reverses HaluEval degradation, and improves confidence calibration.

Conclusion: Learning-rate schedules are a powerful, general-purpose tool to shape model behavior during fine-tuning, beyond direct optimization of the target task.

Abstract: Fine-tuning large language models on new data improves task performance but degrades capabilities learned during pretraining, a phenomenon known as catastrophic forgetting. Existing methods mitigate this by modifying the fine-tuning objective to suppress high-loss tokens or sequences, but these tokens are essential for learning new tasks, especially those with poor pretraining coverage. In such settings, hard tokens should still contribute to learning, so forgetting must be controlled without suppressing them. We identify a simple mechanism for doing so: per-step forgetting is bounded by the product of the learning rate and the square root of the current training loss. This suggests that high-loss batches are especially prone to inducing forgetting. Motivated by this observation, we introduce FINCH, a loss-adaptive learning-rate schedule that reduces the learning rate on high-loss batches and increases it as the model converges, while leaving the fine-tuning objective unchanged. Across knowledge acquisition, science, and low-resource language adaptation benchmarks, FINCH reduces forgetting by 93% on average while matching the task performance of standard fine-tuning. On Qwen3-4B knowledge acquisition, FINCH cuts TruthfulQA degradation by 5x and reverses HaluEval degradation, while better preserving confidence calibration. Overall, our results show that learning-rate schedules are an effective tool to shape model behavior during fine-tuning, beyond just target-task optimization.

</details>


### [411] [Training Neural Networks with Optimal Double-Bayesian Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20009)
*Vy Bui,Hang Yu,Karthik Kantipudi,Ziv Yaniv,Stefan Jaeger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Double-Bayesian learning-rate framework for SGD: derives a theoretically optimal learning rate from two antagonistic Bayesian processes and validates its effectiveness across classification, segmentation, and detection tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperparameters for learning rate are critical yet often selected heuristically; a principled Bayesian derivation could improve training efficiency, stability, and generalization.

Method: Develops a double-Bayesian decision mechanism with two opposing Bayesian processes to derive an optimal learning rate; integrates this rate into stochastic gradient descent; conducts experiments across multiple tasks to test practical effectiveness.

Result: Empirical results corroborate the practical significance of the theoretically derived learning rate, demonstrating benefits across classification, segmentation, and detection tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed double-Bayesian framework offers a principled approach to learning-rate selection with potential implications for broader network training and performance, inviting comparison with existing adaptive/ Bayesian optimization methods.

Abstract: Backpropagation with gradient descent is a common optimization strategy employed by most neural network architectures in machine learning. However, finding optimal hyperparameters to guide training has proven challenging. While it is widely acknowledged that selecting appropriate parameters is crucial for avoiding overfitting and achieving unbiased outcomes, this choice remains largely based on empirical experiments and experience. This paper presents a new probabilistic framework for the learning rate, a key parameter in stochastic gradient descent. The framework develops classic Bayesian statistics into a double-Bayesian decision mechanism involving two antagonistic Bayesian processes. A theoretically optimal learning rate can be derived from these two processes and used for stochastic gradient descent. Experiments across various classification, segmentation, and detection tasks corroborate the practical significance of the theoretically derived learning rate. The paper also discusses the ramifications of the proposed double-Bayesian framework for network training and model performance.

</details>


### [412] [Training-Free Bayesian Filtering with Generative Emulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20028)
*Thomas Savary,François Rozet,Gilles Louppe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion-based emulators enable an optimal particle filter variant to scale to high-dimensional nonlinear dynamical systems without extra training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the scalability bottleneck of particle filters in high dimensions and address the practical challenges of implementing an optimal PF variant using traditional solvers.

Method: Leverage diffusion-based emulators of dynamical systems to realize, without additional training, an optimal particle filtering variant that has been difficult to implement with classical solvers.

Result: Empirical validation on nonlinear chaotic systems, including atmospheric dynamics, showing successful scaling of particle filtering to high-dimensional settings.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based emulators offer a practical and scalable route to applying optimal particle filters in high-dimensional, nonlinear dynamical contexts.

Abstract: Bayesian filtering is a well-known problem that aims to estimate plausible states of a dynamical system from observations. Among existing approaches to solve this problem, particle filters are theoretically exact for non-linear dynamics and observations, but suffer from poor scalability in high dimensions. In this work, we show that diffusion-based emulators of dynamical systems can be used to implement, without additional training, an optimal variant of particle filters that has remained largely unexplored due to implementation challenges with classical numerical solvers. Experiments on nonlinear chaotic systems, including atmospheric dynamics, demonstrate that the proposed approach successfully scales particle filtering to high-dimensional settings.

</details>


### [413] [Take It or Leave It: Intent-Controlled Partial Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20030)
*Salil Parth Tripathi,Bertrand Chapron,Fabrice Collard,Nicolas Courty,Ronan Fablet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IC-POT: a pointwise rejection generalization of partial OT with local rejection costs; dual formulation via local acceptance thresholds; reduces to balanced OT on augmented support; empirically benefits PU learning, open-partial domain adaptation, and multi-modal satellite ocean data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard OT enforces exact mass matching; partial OT uses global rejection; many applications require component-wise rejection guided by side information, geometry, and priors.

Method: Introduce intent-controlled partial OT with pointwise rejection costs over both measures; establish dual interpretation as local acceptance thresholds; cast as balanced Kantorovich OT on augmented support.

Result: Theoretical analysis and duality; algorithmic reduction to a balanced OT problem; empirical gains in PU learning and open-partial domain adaptation; geophysical case study demonstrates practical relevance.

Conclusion: IC-POT enables principled, structured rejection in OT leveraging side information; broad applicability and potential for improved signal extraction in complex sensing tasks.

Abstract: While optimal transport (OT) enforces a rigid constraint by requiring two measures to be matched exactly, partial optimal transport relaxes this requirement by allowing mass to remain unmatched through a global budget, scalar rebate, or uniform rejection rule. However, many applications call for more structured, pointwise rejection mechanisms, where the decision to leave mass unmatched depends on side-specific reliability, support geometry, or external information about which components should participate in the comparison. We introduce \emph{intent-controlled partial optimal transport} (IC-POT), a targeted generalization of partial transport that replaces the global rejection paradigm with pointwise rejection costs over both measures. We show that the resulting optimization problem admits a dual interpretation in terms of local acceptance thresholds and can be solved by recasting it as a balanced Kantorovich OT problem on an augmented support. Beyond theoretical analysis, we demonstrate the practical relevance of IC-POT in settings where rejection is driven by side information. In positive-unlabeled learning and open-partial domain adaptation, incorporating pointwise rejection rules that encode statistical structure improves fixed baseline pipelines. Finally, we motivate the use of IC-POT with a geophysical practical case: multi-modal satellite ocean measurements, for which physical and sensors priors naturally inform the rejection mechanism and define the retrieved comparable signal information.

</details>


### [414] [CAMERA: Adapting to Semantic Camouflage in Unsupervised Text-Attributed Graph Fraud Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20032)
*Junjun Pan,Yixin Liu,Yu Zheng,Lianhua Chi,Alan Wee-Chung Liew,Shirui Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CAMERA is an unsupervised text-attributed graph fraud detector using ego-decoupled mixture-of-experts to adaptively fuse multiple fraud-indicative cues, combined with one-class learning to exploit the rarity of fraudsters and combat semantic camouflage; it outperforms baselines on 4 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Semantic camouflage on text responses undermines conventional cue-based fraud detection, and unsupervised TAGFD struggles due to scarce fraudulent labels; there is a need for adaptive, multi-cue modeling that leverages rare fraudsters via one-class objectives.

Method: Case-Adaptive Multi-cue Expert fRAmework (CAMERA) with an ego-decoupled mixture-of-experts. Each expert captures a distinct cue type indicating fraud; a context-informed gating model integrates ego node and local neighborhood representations to weight cues; unsupervised one-class objectives encourage modeling benign patterns, leveraging fraud rarity.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 4 challenging datasets shows CAMERA consistently outperforms competitors, demonstrating robustness against semantically camouflaged fraudsters.

Conclusion: CAMERA provides effective unsupervised TAGFD under semantic camouflage by adaptively leveraging diverse cues and the rarity of fraudsters; code is publicly available for reproducibility.

Abstract: Text-attributed graph fraud detection (TAGFD) plays a critical role in preventing fraudulent activities on online social and e-commerce platforms. However, to evade detection, fraudsters continuously evolve their camouflaging strategies by deliberately mimicking textual responses of benign users, thereby concealing their malicious purposes. This phenomenon, referred to as semantic camouflage, fundamentally undermines commonly relied assumptions on how structural and attribute cues can be exploited to identify fraudsters, and makes it difficult to spot fraudsters with unsupervised TAGFD. To bridge the gaps, we propose a Case-Adaptive Multi-cue Expert fRAmework (CAMERA) for unsupervised TAGFD. CAMERA employs an ego-decoupled mixture-of-experts architecture, where each expert specializes in modeling a distinct type of fraud-indicative cue. A context-informed gating model is introduced to jointly consider the ego node representation and its local neighborhood context for adaptive integration of cues learned by different experts. Furthermore, CAMERA leverages the inherent rarity of fraudsters to support unsupervised one-class learning with expert-level objectives that encourage modeling dominant benign patterns, thereby enabling reliable unsupervised detection of camouflaged fraudsters. Experiments on 4 challenging datasets show that CAMERA consistently outperforms competitors, showing its effectiveness against semantically camouflaged fraudsters. Code available at https://github.com/CampanulaBells/CAMERA

</details>


### [415] [D$^3$-Subsidy: Online and Sequential Driver Subsidy Decision-Making for Large-Scale Ride-Hailing Market](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20036)
*Taijie Chen,Rui Su,Siyuan Feng,Laoming Zhang,Hongyang Zhang,Haijiao Wang,Zhaofeng Ma,Jintao Ke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces D3-Subsidy, a diffusion-based city-wide driver-subside controller for ride-hailing that respects caps and responds to shocks with low latency. Combines a prefix-conditioned diffusion model to generate plausible future trajectories from historical data, a context-conditioned inverse module to produce low-dimensional city-level controls, and a Lagrangian-dual mapping to enforce subsidy caps without iterative optimization. Enhanced by multi-city pretraining for robust cross-city transfer. Offline tests show gains in Rides/GMV and cap compliance; real-world A/B confirms uplift within budget constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Balance driver supply and passenger demand in dynamic urban environments under strict subsidy-rate caps and low-latency, city-scale constraints. Per-order optimization is too slow; need forward-looking, constraint-aware planning that can deploy at city scale.

Method: A hierarchical diffusion-based framework (D3-Subsidy). Uses a prefix-conditioned diffusion model trained on historical data to sample plausible future trajectories while preserving fixed history for deployability. A context-conditioned inverse module then decodes those trajectories into low-dimensional city-level subsidy controls. A Lagrangian-dual-derived mapping embeds subsidy-rate caps directly into order-driver incentives without iterative optimization. Includes multi-city pretraining with parameter-efficient fine-tuning for cross-city transfer and scalable execution that links city-level plans to per-order incentives.

Result: Offline evaluations show improvements in Rides and GMV and better adherence to subsidy caps. Real-world A/B testing demonstrates significant uplift while keeping budget-violation metrics within operational thresholds.

Conclusion: D3-Subsidy provides a deployable, scalable, constraint-aware city-level subsidy controller that improves core KPIs under cap constraints and transfers robustly across cities, with demonstrated production-ready performance.

Abstract: Ride-hailing platforms like DiDi Chuxing operate in highly dynamic environments where balancing driver supply and passenger demand is critical. Although driver-side subsidies serve as a primary lever to align these forces and improve key KPIs like completed rides (\texttt{Rides}) and gross merchandise value (\texttt{GMV}), optimizing them in production requires simultaneously meeting three constraints: (i) responsiveness to stochastic shocks, (ii) strict subsidy-rate caps, and (iii) low-latency execution at city scale. These requirements rule out expensive per-order optimization, calling for a forward-looking, constraint-aware city-level controller for online sequential decision making. To meet these requirements, we introduce D$^3$-Subsidy (Dynamic Driver-side Diffusion-based Subsidy), a hierarchical diffusion-based framework for deployable city-wide subsidy control. To bridge the train-inference gap, D$^3$-Subsidy employs a prefix-conditioned diffusion model that samples plausible future trajectories from immutable historical observations, ensuring the training protocol aligns with the fixed-history nature of online deployment. These generated plans are then decoded by a context-conditioned inverse module into low-dimensional city-level control signals. For scalable execution, we bridge the gap between city-level planning and fine-grained dispatch via a Lagrangian-dual-derived mapping, which embeds subsidy-rate caps directly into order-driver incentives without iterative optimization. Additionally, a multi-city pretraining strategy with parameter-efficient fine-tuning enables robust transfer across heterogeneous cities. Extensive offline evaluations demonstrate that D$^3$-Subsidy improves \texttt{Rides} and \texttt{GMV} while enhancing cap compliance, and a real-world A/B test confirms significant uplift while keeping budget-related violation metrics within operational thresholds.

</details>


### [416] [When Critics Disagree: Adaptive Reward Poisoning Attacks in RIS-Aided Wireless Control System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20037)
*Deemah H. Tashman,Soumaya Cherkaoui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Disagreement-Guided Reward Poisoning (DGRP) is a novel adaptive attack on SAC in RIS-assisted CRNs that corrupts rewards based on dual-critic disagreement, severely degrading RIS gains and network performance beyond baseline attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate DRL robustness against reward-poisoning attacks in RIS-enabled wireless networks and understand how critic disagreement in high-leverage states can be exploited.

Method: Introduce DGRP, an adaptive reward-poisoning strategy that triggers corruption when SAC's dual critics disagree; applied to a SAC agent optimizing SU transmit power and RIS phase shifts in a CRN with RIS.

Result: DGRP effectively diminishes RIS-induced performance gains and degrades transmission quality; it outperforms periodic-timing and exploration-triggered baselines; attack-parameter analysis reveals key influences on learning degradation.

Conclusion: Disagreement-aware threat models are essential for evaluating DRL robustness in RIS-assisted networks, and defenses should consider reward-poisoning that leverages critic disagreement.

Abstract: Reward-poisoning attacks present a significant risk to learning-based wireless control systems. Given this, we propose a Disagreement-Guided Reward Poisoning (DGRP) adaptive attack on a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) agent. In a Cognitive Radio Network (CRN) environment assisted by Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS), the SAC agent is tasked with maximizing the long-term secondary users' (SUs) rate by simultaneously optimizing the transmission power of the SU transmitter and the RIS phase shifts. DGRP corrupts rewards, particularly when the SAC dual critics exhibit substantial disagreement-especially in high-leverage, high-uncertainty states-resulting in distorted value estimations and guiding the policy towards suboptimal actions. Our findings demonstrate that DGRP substantially diminishes the performance improvements typically provided by RIS and degrades transmission quality. We further investigate key attack parameters and determine their impact on learning. In comparison to periodic-timing and exploration-triggered baselines, DGRP consistently causes greater damage, highlighting the necessity of considering disagreement-aware threats when evaluating the robustness of Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) in RIS-assisted networks.

</details>


### [417] [Active Context Selection Improves Simple Regret in Contextual Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20040)
*Mohammad Shahverdikondori,Jalal Etesami,Negar Kiyavash*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The work analyzes contextual multi-armed bandits with a finite set of contexts (subpopulations) and studies how sampling decisions across contexts affect context-weighted regret. They establish tight, instance-dependent regret rates for known context distributions under passive and active sampling, show substantial improvements with active allocation, extend to budgeted sampling, and propose the Explore-Explore-Then-Commit (EETC) algorithm for unknown distributions that achieves near the known-p rates; empirical results corroborate the theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In settings with subpopulations, it is crucial to design sampling strategies that allocate data collection effort where it most impacts performance. The problem blends contextual bandits with experimental design, seeking worst-case guarantees across reward distributions while exploiting the context distribution p to optimize regret.

Method: The authors derive worst-case regret bounds that depend on the context distribution p. For known p, they compare passive sampling (contexts revealed at random) with active sampling, showing regret rates: passive ~ sqrt(n/T · ||p||_{1/2}) and active with q_j ∝ p_j^{2/3} ~ sqrt(n/T) · ||p||_{2/3}. They extend to budgeted active sampling and identify when limited budgets suffice. For unknown p, they introduce Explore-Explore-Then-Commit (EETC), which estimates p and times the switch to active allocation to achieve, asymptotically, the same rate as the known-p active case. They validate these findings with experiments on synthetic and real data.

Result: Tight, context-dependent regret rates are established for both passive and active sampling with known p. Active sampling yields a substantial improvement over passive (up to Θ(k^{1/4}) in terms of the rate factor). Budgeted active sampling is characterized, with conditions under which a limited budget recovers the full active rate. The EETC algorithm achieves near-known-p performance when p is unknown, in the large-horizon regime. Experimental results support the theoretical claims.

Conclusion: The paper provides a principled framework for context-aware sampling in contextual bandits, revealing how optimal context allocation shapes regret. It demonstrates clear benefits of active sampling and offers practical algorithms (EETC) for unknown context distributions, with broad applicability to domains where subpopulation sampling can be controlled.

Abstract: We study the contextual multi-armed bandit problem with a finite context space (a.k.a. subpopulations), where the learner recommends a best action for each context and is evaluated by context-weighted simple regret. Our guarantees are worst-case over the reward distributions, while remaining instance-dependent with respect to the context distribution vector $p$. Akin to experimental design problems where the population of interest is fixed but the sampled subpopulation can be controlled, we allow the learner to actively choose which context to sample from. For a known $p$, we characterize tight regret rates: passive sampling where contexts are randomly revealed achieves regret of order $\sqrt{n/T \, \lVert p \rVert_{1/2}}$, whereas active sampling with allocation $q_j \propto p_j^{2/3}$ achieves the tight rate $\sqrt{n/T} \, \lVert p \rVert_{2/3}$. The resulting improvement can be as large as $Θ(k^{1/4})$, where $k$ is the number of contexts. We further extend the analysis to budgeted active sampling, characterize the corresponding tight rate, and identify when a limited active budget suffices to recover the fully active rate. When $p$ is unknown, we propose the Explore-Explore-Then-Commit (EETC) algorithm, which optimally balances estimating the context distribution and the time to switch to active allocation, such that for large horizons, it matches the known-$p$ active rate up to constants. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data support our theoretical findings.

</details>


### [418] [Smooth Partial Lotteries for Stable Randomized Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20069)
*Alexander Goldberg,Giulia Fanti,Nihar B. Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a Lipschitz-smooth design for partial lotteries in selection, introducing the Clipped Linear Lottery which scales selection probability linearly with estimated quality between an upper/ lower threshold, achieving near-optimal worst-case regret; improves stability and tradeoffs in empirical peer-review data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing partial lotteries yield unstable selection probabilities where small score perturbations can cause large changes near decision boundaries. A smoothness principle aims to make the probability mapping Lipschitz-continuous, stabilizing outcomes while retaining utility.

Method: Formalize smoothness as a Lipschitz condition on mapping review scores to selection probabilities. Propose Clipped Linear Lottery: probability scales linearly with estimated quality between an upper threshold (accept always) and a lower threshold (reject always). Derive regret bounds showing worst-case regret matches a lower bound up to (1−k/n). Compare to Individual Fairness and Differential Privacy notions. Empirically evaluate on real peer-review datasets (ICLR 2025, NeurIPS 2024, SNF) to assess stability and utility.

Result: Clipped Linear Lottery achieves near-optimal smoothness-regret tradeoff; worst-case regret matches a fundamental lower bound up to a factor (1−k/n). Existing lotteries are unstable to perturbations of a single score. Empirical results corroborate theory and show improved smoothness-utility tradeoffs over alternatives across multiple datasets.

Conclusion: Smoothness is a viable design principle for partial lotteries. The Clipped Linear Lottery provides a simple, tunable mechanism with strong theoretical guarantees and practical stability, suggesting broader adoption in selection and allocation tasks; further work could explore extensions and other smoothness notions.

Abstract: Competitive selection processes, from scientific funding to admissions and hiring, use evaluations to score candidates, and eventually choose a subset of them based on those scores. Recently, many organizations have adopted partial lotteries, which randomize selection based on evaluation scores. However, existing lottery designs are inherently unstable, as a small change to a single candidate's score can cause large shifts in their selection probabilities. This instability undermines a key goal of lotteries: reducing the influence of fine-grained score distinctions near the decision boundary. We propose smoothness as a design principle for partial lotteries, formalizing it as a Lipschitz condition on the mapping from review scores over candidates to selection probabilities. We introduce the Clipped Linear Lottery, a simple mechanism in which selection probabilities scale linearly with estimated quality between an upper threshold, above which we always accept, and a lower threshold, below which we always reject. We prove that the Clipped Linear Lottery's worst-case regret matches a lower bound for any smooth selection rule up to a factor of $(1 - k/n)$, where $k/n$ is the acceptance rate. We compare smooth selection to other stability notions like Individual Fairness and Differential Privacy, showing that the Clipped Linear Lottery achieves a better smoothness-regret tradeoff than alternatives. Experiments on real peer review data from ICLR 2025, NeurIPS 2024, and the Swiss National Science Foundation demonstrate that existing lottery designs are highly unstable in practice even under perturbations to a single score. Our experiments also confirm the tightness of our theoretical analysis and show that our proposed Clipped Linear Lottery achieves a better smoothness-utility tradeoff than alternatives in practice.

</details>


### [419] [Towards Distillation Guarantees under Algorithmic Alignment for Combinatorial Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20074)
*Thien Le,Melanie Weber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Distillation for combinatorial optimization is analyzed when the target model is a GNN aligned with a DP algorithm; under LRH and a sufficiently rich source model, distillation can be efficiently solved using DP transition complexity; provides a sufficient condition via algorithmic alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend distillation theory to structured prediction by aligning neural architectures with underlying DP structures and leveraging the linear representation hypothesis (LRH).

Method: Assume the source model is sufficiently rich (LRH). Let the target be a graph neural network whose architecture mirrors a dynamic programming algorithm for the task. Model distillation as exploiting a decision-tree distillation heuristic and analyze solvability in the complexity parameters of the DP transition function, represented as a decision tree.

Result: Derives a rigorous sufficient condition for successful distillation under algorithmic alignment; shows the distillation problem is efficiently solvable in terms of the DP transition function's complexity parameters.

Conclusion: Algorithmic alignment between the target architecture (DP-aligned GNN) and the problem structure enables reliable distillation for combinatorial optimization; LRH provides the representation theoretic foundation for the result.

Abstract: Distillation transfers knowledge from a large model trained on broad data to a smaller, more efficient model suitable for deployment. In structured prediction settings, prior knowledge about the task can guide the choice of a target architecture that is algorithmically aligned with the underlying problem. Building on recent learning-theoretic analyses of decision-tree (DT) distillation (Boix-Adsera, 2024), we study when distillation succeeds for combinatorial optimization tasks. We focus on the case where the target model is a graph neural network whose architecture is aligned with a dynamic programming (DP) algorithm for the task. Assuming that the source model is sufficiently rich, formalized through the linear representation hypothesis (LRH) (Elhage et al., 2022; Park et al., 2024), we show that the distillation problem can be solved efficiently in the complexity parameters of the DP transition function, represented as a DT. Our results provide a rigorous sufficient condition for successful distillation in the flavour of algorithmic alignment.

</details>


### [420] [INSHAPE: Instance-Level Shapelets for Interpretable Time-Series Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20088)
*Seongjun Lee,Seokhyun Lee,Changhee Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: INSHAPE is an interpretable time-series classification framework that discovers per-instance, variable-length, non-overlapping shapelets, models temporal dependencies among them, and aggregates to population-level prototypes, achieving state-of-the-art performance on UCR/UEA benchmarks while improving interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing shapelet methods emphasize population-level patterns, which can misalign with individual time series features and ignore temporal interactions among multiple patterns; this limits both predictive accuracy and interpretability.

Method: Per-instance discovery of discriminative, variable-length, non-overlapping segments within each time series; explicit modeling of temporal dependencies among the learned shapelets; bottom-up aggregation of instance-level shapelets into prototypical population-level shapelets to bridge local and global interpretability.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 128 UCR and 30 UEA benchmark datasets shows that INSHAPE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art shapelet-based methods and yields more intuitive, interpretable insights.

Conclusion: INSHAPE delivers strong predictive performance while providing clear instance-level interpretations and a principled way to derive population-level prototypes, addressing key limitations of traditional, population-only shapelet approaches.

Abstract: Discovering shapelets -- i.e., discriminative temporal patterns within time series -- has been widely studied to address the inherent complexity of time-series classification (TSC) and to make model decision-making processes more transparent. However, existing methods primarily focus on population-level shapelets optimized across the entire dataset, which leads to two fundamental limitations: (i) population-level patterns often misalign with instance-specific features, resulting in suboptimal performance and potentially misleading interpretations, and (ii) most methods treat shapelets as independent entities, overlooking important temporal dependencies and interactions among multiple patterns. To address these limitations, we propose INSHAPE, an interpretable TSC framework that discovers variable-length, discriminative temporal patterns specific to each time series. INSHAPE identifies these patterns as non-overlapping segments and models their temporal dependencies, thereby providing clear instance-level interpretations while achieving strong predictive performance. Furthermore, INSHAPE bridges local and global interpretability through a bottom-up approach, aggregating instance-level shapelets into prototypical (population-level) shapelets. Extensive experiments on 128 UCR and 30 UEA benchmark datasets show that INSHAPE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art shapelet-based methods while providing more intuitive and interpretable insights.

</details>


### [421] [Draft Less, Retrieve More: Hybrid Tree Construction for Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20104)
*Yuhao Shen,Tianyu Liu,Xinyi Hu,Quan Kong,Baolin Zhang,Jun Dai,Jun Zhang,Shuang Ge,Lei Chen,Yue Li,Mingcheng Wan,Cong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graft unifies pruning and retrieval in speculative decoding to compensate for coverage loss from pruning, achieving faster, training-free inference with no accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In speculative decoding, dense draft trees maximize acceptance rate but incur large VRAM and compute overhead. Pruning reduces latency but degrades coverage and acceptance, preventing attainment of the dense-tree Pareto optimum. A method that leverages the freed budget from pruning to strengthen retrieval can break this tradeoff.

Method: Sequential prune-then-graft: prune to free computational budget, then graft highly predictive retrieved tokens into the positions created by pruning, filling gaps with near-zero overhead. The approach is training-free and lossless, and is evaluated across short- and long-context generation and large models, including exploratory attempts to DFlash-style block drafting.

Result: Graft establishes a new Pareto frontier across practical deployment settings, delivering up to 5.41× speedup on short-context benchmarks and up to 21.8% average speedup over EAGLE-3 on Qwen3-235B. It is lossless and training-free, with broad applicability to short and long context and large models; preliminary experiments indicate potential extension to DFlash-style drafting.

Conclusion: Incorporating retrieval to compensate for pruning-induced coverage loss via a prune-then-graft workflow yields substantial, training-free speedups without sacrificing accuracy, signifying a robust approach to optimizing speculative decoding and suggesting avenues for extending grafting to other drafting paradigms.

Abstract: Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates large language model inference by leveraging a draft-then-verify paradigm. To maximize the acceptance rate, recent methods construct expansive draft trees, which unfortunately incur severe VRAM bandwidth and computational overheads that bottleneck end-to-end speedups. While dynamic-depth pruning can reduce this latency by removing marginal branches, it also discards potentially valid candidates, preventing the acceptance rate from reaching the upper bound of dense trees. In this paper, we identify a critical opportunity in resource allocation: the transition from dense to pruned drafting frees up significant computational budget. To break this Pareto tradeoff, we introduce Graft, a compensation framework that couples pruning and retrieval as mutually reinforcing operations. Pruning supplies sufficient budget for retrieval, while retrieval compensates for pruning-induced coverage loss and recovers accepted length. By employing a sequential `prune-then-graft' mechanism, Graft attaches highly predictive retrieved tokens into positions opened by pruning, filling the topological gaps with near-zero overhead. Graft is entirely training-free and lossless. Comprehensive evaluations show that Graft establishes a new Pareto frontier across practical deployment settings, including short-context generation, long-context generation, and large-scale models. On short-context benchmarks, it achieves up to 5.41$\times$ speedup and improves average speedup over EAGLE-3 by up to 21.8% on the large-scale Qwen3-235B. We also provide a preliminary exploration of applying Graft to the DFlash-style block drafting paradigm, offering initial evidence and insights for extending grafting beyond autoregressive draft trees.

</details>


### [422] [Optimal Representation Size: High-Dimensional Analysis of Pretraining and Linear Probing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20105)
*Valentina Njaradi,Clémentine Dominé,Rachel Swanson,Marco Mondelli,Andrew Saxe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A two-stage learning model analyzes how pretraining (structure extraction via PCA) and downstream linear learning interact. It derives exact training and test errors in the high-dimensional limit, showing that the optimal representation size depends on the balance between unlabelled and labelled data. Compression is best when unlabelled data is plentiful and labelled data scarce; with limited unlabelled data, higher-dimensional representations generalize better. An explicit trade-off quantifies how many unlabelled samples can substitute for one labelled sample. The same phenomenology appears in autoencoders and pretrained LLMs, underscoring the importance of choosing representation capacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and quantify how the size of the learned representation affects downstream generalisation in a two-stage learning pipeline (unsupervised pretraining followed by supervised learning) and to provide actionable guidance on when compression helps.

Method: Analytical high-dimensional model: structure extraction is PCA on unlabeled data; downstream task is linear regression on labeled data using the extracted features. Derive exact expressions for training and generalisation errors as functions of representation dimension D, unlabeled sample size N_u, labeled sample size N_l, and task alignment, in the large-D regime. Determine the optimal D under data-budget constraints and connect to autoencoders/LLMs.

Result: Pretrained representations strongly influence downstream generalisation. With abundant unlabeled data and scarce labelled data, maximally compressed representations (small D) optimise generalisation; with limited unlabeled data, higher-dimensional representations perform better. An exact trade-off is established quantifying how many unlabeled samples replace a single labeled sample. Phenomenology mirrors that seen in autoencoders and pretrained LLMs, suggesting a general principle: representation-size optimization is crucial for generalisation.

Conclusion: Optimizing representation capacity is critical in two-stage learning; the theory provides regime-dependent guidance for choosing compression levels during pretraining and clarifies when pretraining should be aggressive in compressing features versus preserving high dimensionality for supervision.

Abstract: Learning to generalise from limited data is a fundamental challenge for both artificial and biological systems. A common strategy is to extract reusable structure from abundant unlabelled data, enabling efficient adaptation to new tasks from limited labelled data. This two-stage paradigm is now standard in modern training pipelines, where pretraining is followed by fine-tuning or linear probing. We provide an analytical model of this process: structure extraction is formalized as principal component analysis on unlabelled data, and downstream learning as linear regression on a separate labelled dataset. In the high-dimensional regime, we derive exact expressions for training and generalisation error showcasing their dependence on representation dimensionality, unlabelled and labelled sample sizes, and task alignment. Our results show that pretrained representations strongly influence downstream generalisation, and we characterize the optimal representation size as a function of task parameters: with abundant pretraining data but scarce downstream data, maximally compressed representations are optimal, whereas with limited pretraining data, higher-dimensional representations generalise better. Furthermore, we establish an exact trade-off between pretraining and supervision, quantifying how much unlabelled data is required to replace a single labelled sample. Beyond our idealised model, we observe similar phenomenology in autoencoders and pretrained LLMs. Altogether, we highlight that optimising representation size is critical, giving conditions for when compression during pretraining improves generalisation.

</details>


### [423] [Beyond Isotropy in JEPAs: Hamiltonian Geometry and Symplectic Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20107)
*Robert Jenkinson Alvarez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: JEPAs' isotropic Gaussian priors introduce a geometry-dependent trade-off; fixed marginals cannot canonically align with all structured geometries. Propose HamJEPA: phase-space view encoding with Hamiltonian leapfrog cross-view coupling, yielding geometry-aware gains over baselines on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100; symplectic coupling drives gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate the impact of isotropy-inducing encoders in JEPAs and whether a fixed marginal target can accommodate adversarial downstream geometries. Show that geometry matters for optimal covariance and coupling, motivating a geometry-aware cross-view mechanism.

Method: (i) Theoretically characterize optimal covariance under a Hamiltonian energy budget for known H, yielding (c/d)H^{-1} and highlighting isotropy cost. (ii) Argue non-uniqueness of a fixed geometry-independent marginal when geometry is unknown. (iii) Propose HamJEPA: encode views as phase-space (q,p) states and learn a Hamiltonian leapfrog map for cross-view transitions; apply non-isotropic scales and spectral floors to avoid collapse. (iv) Evaluate on CIFAR-100 (headless token protocol) and ImageNet-100, with ablations (MLP predictor) to isolate the effect of symplectic coupling.

Result: HamJEPA improves over SIGReg on CIFAR-100: +4.89 kNN@20 and +3.52 linear-probe points at 30 epochs; +6.45 kNN@20 and +10.64 linear-probe points at 80 epochs. MLP predictor ablation shows symplectic coupling drives gains. On ImageNet-100: +4.82 kNN@20 and +7.52 linear-probe points at 45 epochs.

Conclusion: Structural bias in JEPAs should be integrated into cross-view coupling rather than fixed encoder marginals. The phase-space HamJEPA with a learned Hamiltonian leapfrog map provides geometry-aware improvements across datasets, with symplectic coupling identified as the key contributor to improved neighborhood geometry.

Abstract: JEPAs often regularize one-view embeddings toward an isotropic Gaussian, implicitly baking Euclidean symmetry into the representation. We show that this is not merely a benign default. For a known structured downstream geometry $H\succ0$, the minimax and maximum-entropy covariance under a Hamiltonian energy budget is $(c/d)H^{-1}$, and Euclidean isotropy incurs a closed-form price of isotropy. More importantly, when the downstream geometry is unknown, no geometry-independent fixed marginal target is canonical: every fixed covariance shape can be maximally misaligned for some structured geometry. We further show that even oracle one-view marginals do not identify the JEPA view-to-view predictive coupling. These results suggest that the structural bias in JEPAs should enter the cross-view coupling rather than a fixed encoder marginal. We instantiate this principle with \textbf{HamJEPA}, which encodes each view as a phase-space state $(q,p)$ and predicts view-to-view transitions with a learned Hamiltonian leapfrog map, while non-isotropic scale and spectral floors prevent collapse. In a deliberately headless token protocol, HamJEPA improves over SIGReg on CIFAR-100 by $+4.89$ kNN@20 and $+3.52$ linear-probe points at 30 epochs, and by $+6.45$ kNN@20 and $+10.64$ linear-probe points at 80 epochs, while a matched MLP predictor ablation shows that the symplectic coupling is the ingredient driving the neighborhood-geometry gain. On ImageNet-100, HamJEPA-$q$ improves by $+4.82$ kNN@20 and $+7.52$ linear-probe points at 45 epochs.

</details>


### [424] [Toto 2.0: Time Series Forecasting Enters the Scaling Era](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20119)
*Emaad Khwaja,Chris Lettieri,Gerald Woo,Eden Belouadah,Marc Cenac,Guillaume Jarry,Enguerrand Paquin,Xunyi Zhao,Viktoriya Zhukov,Othmane Abou-Amal,Chenghao Liu,Ameet Talwalkar,David Asker*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A single training recipe enables scalable time series foundation models; Toto 2.0 releases five open-weight forecasting models (4M–2.5B params) that achieve state-of-the-art performance on BOOM, GIFT-Eval, and TIME benchmarks; weights are Apache 2.0 licensed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Demonstrate scalability and reproducibility of time series foundation models and provide open, reusable weights to advance forecasting research; establish strong baselines across standard benchmarks.

Method: Apply a single training recipe to scale from 4 million to 2.5 billion parameters, detailing architecture, training data, and the u-muP hyperparameter transfer pipeline; release five base checkpoints under Apache 2.0.

Result: Toto 2.0 achieves new state-of-the-art results on BOOM, GIFT-Eval, and TIME benchmarks; provides reliable forecast-quality improvements across a wide parameter range; facilitates reproducibility via open weights.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates the scalability of time series foundation models under a unified recipe and provides open-access models and the u-muP tuning workflow, setting new baselines on multiple forecasting benchmarks.

Abstract: We show that time series foundation models scale: a single training recipe produces reliable forecast-quality improvements from 4M to 2.5B parameters. We release Toto 2.0, a family of five open-weights forecasting models trained under this recipe. The Toto 2.0 family sets a new state of the art on three forecasting benchmarks: BOOM, our observability benchmark; GIFT-Eval, the standard general-purpose benchmark; and the recent contamination-resistant TIME benchmark. This report describes our experimental results and details the design decisions behind Toto 2.0: its architecture and training recipe, training data, and the u-muP hyperparameter transfer pipeline. All five base checkpoints are released under Apache 2.0.

</details>


### [425] [TrajTok: Adaptive Spatial Tokenization for Trajectory Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20134)
*Zhen Xiong,Shang-Ling Hsu,Cyrus Shahabi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TrajTok introduces a multi-resolution hexagonal spatial tokenization and a factorized transformer with ST-RoPE for learning transferable trajectory embeddings via masked-token pretraining; achieves strong, generalizable performance on Porto dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome challenges in learning generalizable trajectory representations from noisy, irregular GPS data and from coarse vs. fine spatial tokenization that can obscure patterns.

Method: Multi-resolution hex grid tokenization of GPS points; a factorized transformer with per-modality self-attention, cross-attention fusion, spatiotemporal Rotary position embeddings (ST-RoPE); masked-token pretraining to reconstruct geometry and kinematics; frozen encoder with task adapters for downstream tasks.

Result: On Porto dataset, frozen TrajTok encoder with lightweight adapters achieves strong performance across trajectory similarity, classification, ETA, and travel-time regression, outperforming task-specific baselines; encoder shows transferability to both geometry-dominated and kinematics-dominated tasks.

Conclusion: Learned multi-resolution spatial tokenization combined with masked-token pretraining is a promising direction for general-purpose trajectory foundation models.

Abstract: Learning generalizable trajectory representations from raw GPS traces remains difficult because the data is continuous, noisy, and irregularly sampled. Spatial tokenization is also challenging: fine grids yield sparse cells with weak embeddings, while coarse grids merge heterogeneous movement patterns into the same token. We present TrajTok, a trajectory encoder with a simple pretraining recipe for transferable trajectory embeddings. TrajTok first learns a multi-resolution hexagonal cell partition from the spatial distribution of GPS points, converting noisy GPS sequences into discrete cell tokens. To capture both geometry and kinematics, it uses a factorized transformer encoder with early per-modality self-attention blocks, cross-attention fusion layers, and spatiotemporal rotary position embeddings, ST-RoPE, to encode where and when each token occurs. TrajTok is pretrained with masked-token modeling that recovers both geometric structure and kinematic patterns from partial trajectory observations. On the Porto dataset, a frozen TrajTok encoder with lightweight task adapters achieves strong performance across trajectory similarity search, classification, estimated time of arrival, and full travel-time regression, outperforming multiple task-specific methods. The same frozen encoder supports both geometry-dominated and kinematics-dominated tasks, suggesting that TrajTok learns transferable trajectory structure rather than task-specific shortcuts. These results indicate that learned multi-resolution spatial tokenization combined with masked-token pretraining is a promising direction for general-purpose trajectory foundation models.

</details>


### [426] [When Does Model Collapse Occur in Structured Interactive Learning?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20151)
*Yuchen Wu,Kangjie Zhou,Weijie Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Interactive multi-model learning with synthetic data is governed by the topology of the interaction graph; the paper provides a necessary-and-sufficient condition for model collapse, plus finite-sample results for linear regression and asymptotic results for general M-estimators, supported by experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical statistical learning assumes data from a fixed target population and independent training; in interactive AI ecosystems, models continually train on others' synthetic outputs, creating data leakage and complex dependencies that risk model collapse. A formal theory linking graph topology to stability/inference is needed.

Method: Formalize model interactions as directed graphs. Derive a necessary and sufficient condition for model collapse depending on the interaction topology. Prove finite-sample results for linear regression and asymptotic guarantees for general M-estimators. Validate findings with extensive numerical experiments.

Result: Characterization: model collapse occurs if and only if the interaction graph meets the derived condition (topology-dependent). Provides finite-sample bounds for linear regression and asymptotic results for M-estimators under the interactive setting. Empirical results corroborate the theoretical predictions across various graph topologies.

Conclusion: Graph topology critically governs stability in interactive learning with synthetic data. The work offers a rigorous criterion for collapse, enabling design principles to prevent degradation and guiding reliable inference in multi-model AI systems; it also lays a foundation for further empirical and theoretical exploration in non-i.i.d., correlated-data regimes.

Abstract: The proliferation of generative artificial intelligence has given rise to an interactive learning environment, where model parameters are continuously updated using not only data generated by natural processes, but also synthetic outputs produced by other models. This paradigm introduces two major challenges: (1) training data are no longer drawn exclusively from the target population, undermining a core assumption of classical statistical learning, and (2) model training processes become inherently correlated, as models interact with one another through repeated exposure to each other's synthetic outputs in a potentially complex manner.
